content: yo so im running my own campaign and i like the language system in qamis. how do bonus languages actually work?

Pretty straightforward. Every character gets three languages by default: the capital dialect of their national language (like Edran from Jia, or Qis from Qis'mos), plus one regional dialect variation. Then everyone gets one bonus language, but you have to justify how you learned it. That's the hook -- it ties backstory to mechanics.
okay so like, what counts as a valid justification? does the DM have to approve it or is it mostly freeform?

That's... actually not covered in the notes. The DM (me, in the Qamis campaign) just said 'you have to tell me how you know it,' which implies there's a gate there, but the notes don't lay out what passes and what doesn't. You'd probably want to decide that for your own table -- like, does a merchant character automatically get Trade Pidgin? Does a soldier get any allied nation's language? How much has to have happened in-game vs in backstory?
right. and like, what if someone wants two bonus languages instead of one? is there a cost or penalty?

Yeah, that's blank. The notes just say everyone gets one bonus language -- they don't address whether you can trade something for extras, or if it's strictly one per character. That's a house-rule call you'd need to make.
fair. what about nonstandard languages -- like, thieves cant, or ancient draconic, or stuff only specialist groups know?

Also not covered. The notes mention Old Draconic as the language of scholarship and treaties, but there's no guidance on whether that's accessible as a bonus language, or whether it costs extra, or how you'd even justify learning it unless you're a scholar or artificer. Same with any underground guild languages or religious lingoes. That's all DM discretion.
cool. so the framework is solid but the specific rulings are left to the table. that's actually a useful starting point for my campaign.
Yeah, that's the pattern with Qamis overall -- the worldbuilding is detailed and specific, but the DM has deliberately left blank spaces for you to fill in at your table. Languages are one of them.
okay so i missed last session, what's the deal with Luna? is that like, a thing in Qamis?

Luna is Earth's Moon -- it's not in Qamis at all. It's just the reference standard. When the notes describe Qaia's moons, they measure everything relative to Luna: how big, how bright, how much tidal force, all of it. Luna is the baseline that everything else gets compared to.
oh okay so it's just like, a math thing

Yeah, basically. So like, Primus is 0.037 times Luna's radius. Secundus is 0.06 magnitude dimmer than Luna. Quartus is about as big and bright as Luna itself. Once you know what Luna looks like, you can picture Qaia's sky.
so what do the moons actually look like from the ground? my drow character is still getting used to the surface, so...

Qaia has five freely-orbiting moons plus Primus, which is geostationary -- it doesn't move, just hangs in the sky. Biggest ones are Tertius and Quartus, nearly the same apparent size as Luna but slightly dimmer. Secundus is also pretty big but dark and iron-rich, so it looks dimmer even though it's large. Then there's Primus, which is small and faint -- about one-third Luna's size and 3.1 magnitudes dimmer. And two tiny distant ones, Sextus and Septimus, barely visible.

From the near-side hemisphere you see Primus fixed in place. From the far side, you never see it at all.
wait, one moon never moves? that's... wild

Yeah, it's magically anchored. Its orbit is geostationary -- takes one day to orbit, so it never rises or sets from the perspective of whoever's under it. And here's the useful part: that makes it a fixed longitude reference. Navigators can measure how high Primus is in the sky to figure out where they are, no chronometer needed. Kinda revolutionary for Qaian navigation.
huh. so it's actually useful

Very. Only works on the near-side hemisphere though -- anyone on the far side has to navigate the hard way, like Earth before chronometers. But if you're on the side where Primus is visible, you get a way to find longitude that no other world has.

so i'm trying to figure out Ryraxos -- coming from Forgotten Realms, a dragon powerful enough to wage a 30-year war and basically conquer a continent seems... pretty impressive? why does your setting describe him as 'not truly that impressive of a dragon'?

Good question! The key is context. Ryraxos is bronze and relatively young -- around sixty years old. Compare that to ancient dragons in other settings, and you've got a dragon who's still in his prime but not at the peak of power.

Moreover, the notes specifically say he's formidable, certainly, but "less so than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery." Part of his success came from being an excellent *military strategist and artillery commander* -- dragons are great at hauling guns -- rather than just raw martial power. He won a long war through coordination and tactics, not because he personally outmuscled the world.

So "not truly that impressive" is relative: he's dangerous, but he's not a world-ending ancient wyrm like you might find in FR.
wait, hold on -- is this the same Ryraxos from the Qaringil era, or are we talking about the one in the present campaign?

Good catch on thinking about the eras! Ryraxos is only in the *present-day Qamis* campaign, not in Qaringil. Qaringil is set about 200 years earlier, and Ryraxos wouldn't have been born yet -- he's part of the story after the Ryraxian War, which is already over when the Qamis campaign starts. That's why the notes say he plays no role in the campaign: he's accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon.
ohh okay that makes way more sense. so he's young relative to the actual dragon lords, not young relative to, like, a five-year-old green dragon in Waterdeep.

Exactly. In his own setting, sixty is solidly prime-of-life for a bronze dragon, but he's nowhere near ancient. And yeah, young relative to the *idea* of a draconic overlord -- not to humanoid timescales. The setting is being careful not to oversell him: he's a formidable military leader and a real problem *at the tactical level*, but he's not a reality-warping entity. Very different vibe from FR's oldest dragons.

so i've been reading about the bahamut schism and i'm confused. the church teaches that primus is this sacred anchor point, right? but it says only half the world can even see it. how does that work for believers on the far side?

That's a genuinely sharp question. Primus is geostationary -- it orbits directly above the equator and never rises or sets for the hemisphere beneath it. From the opposite hemisphere, it's permanently invisible. You literally cannot see it no matter what.

So yeah, believers in Forthyr or the far-side coast of Edrad have no direct celestial connection to what the church positions as the prime meridian anchor. That creates exactly the kind of theological friction you're picking up on.
right, so that feels... convenient? for whoever controls qama and the near side. like, how did the church justify that before the drow showed up?

I don't have doctrine on that in the notes -- how the pre-war church explained the visibility asymmetry isn't filled in. But your instinct about convenience is solid. The drow occupied Qama during the late war and installed Qe'lth IV as a rival high priest, and yeah, that happened in a setting where one hemisphere has a navigational and theological anchor the other literally cannot access.

Whether that asymmetry was part of why the schism happened, or got weaponized after it did, isn't specified. But it's exactly the kind of thing your table might want to explore.

hm okay so i'm not crazy for thinking that's weird. but like... does the schism even matter to me? qachu ii is in qis without the relics, qe'lth iv is in qama with drow backing. if i'm on the far hemisphere i can't even see primus anyway, so why should i care which one's legit?

That's the real question. The relics are in Qama under drow control, so institutional legitimacy currently favors Qe'lth IV -- at least materially. But Qachu II claims the original tradition and still operates from Qis. From the far side, yeah, Primus is invisible to you either way. Your faith doesn't depend on seeing the moon. What matters is which priest controls the church's power and resources.

The schism is real and ongoing, but it's political as much as theological. Primus visibility is a near-hemisphere advantage, not a universal foundation for the faith.
okay i'm less skeptical now. that actually makes sense. so if i play a character from the far side, i can just... not care about this?

You can, but it's richer if you don't. Even on the far side, the schism has political teeth -- Qe'lth IV and Qachu II represent competing claims to church authority that affect temples, tithes, and doctrine everywhere. You don't need to see Primus to feel the effects. But yeah, a character from Forthyr or the far coast has a fundamentally different relationship to Bahamut theology than someone in Qis'mos. That's a real detail worth leaning into.
So I've been reading about Qaia's moon system, and I'm curious about Primus. How big and bright does it actually look in the sky? Like, compared to Earth's Moon?

Good question! Primus is deceptively underwhelming in the sky despite being close. It's got an apparent angular diameter of about 10.4 arcminutes—roughly one-third the angular size of Earth's Moon. And brightness-wise, it's at magnitude −9.62, which makes it about 3.1 magnitudes dimmer than a full Moon. In terms of actual illumination, it provides only about 1/18th the light of Earth's full Moon. The reason it's so dim is its dark iron surface (albedo 0.06) combined with its tiny mass—just 0.0001 times the Moon's mass.
Okay so it's close but tiny and dark. Is it actually the smallest moon in the system?

By apparent size, yes—it's way the smallest. The ranking in the sky goes: Tertius at 33.7 arcminutes, Quartus at 29.6′, Secundus at 28.0′, then Primus at just 10.4′. So Tertius is more than three times larger-looking in the sky. But here's what makes Primus special: it's geostationary. It never rises or sets from the near-side hemisphere—it just hangs motionless at a fixed point in the sky. That unique property makes it incredibly valuable for navigation, even if it's not the most visually impressive of Qaia's moons.
alright so you've got six moons. that's some indie fantasy nonsense. brightest and dimmest when they're full?

Fair skepticism. Five moons actually -- Primus is geostationary and only visible from one hemisphere.

Brightest full moon: Quartus at magnitude −12.64, about 0.9 times as bright as Earth's full moon.

Dimmest: Septimus at magnitude −7.14, still bright enough to see clearly but about 1/167th of Earth's full moon.

Weird detail: Tertius and Quartus are nearly identical in brightness (−12.60 vs −12.64). Tertius is closer and larger in the sky, but its darker surface (albedo 0.09 vs 0.12) makes up the difference.
hold up. two moons that are basically the same brightness? that's lazy

Actually not lazy -- the notes call it out explicitly. Tertius orbits at 0.45 LD with a 33.7 arcminute diameter, Quartus at 1.05 LD with 29.6 arcminutes. The math works: closer and bigger should make Tertius brighter, but its darker surface cancels that out. It's deliberate, not hand-waving.
cool, cool. so what's the actual appearance? colors, detail you can see from the ground?

Blank space. The notes give you orbital mechanics, magnitudes, tidal forces, escape velocities -- very thorough on the physics side -- but not what they look like visually beyond brightness and size. That's left unfilled.
what about navigation? could you use Quartus to navigate like you use the pole star?

The navigation system in the notes is built entirely around Primus -- the geostationary one. It never moves, so measuring its elevation angle gives you longitude without a chronometer. Quartus swings across the sky on a 29-day cycle, which breaks that. The notes don't address whether Quartus could work as a secondary nav tool, but structurally? Primus already solved the hard problem. The others are probably backup at best.
hey, what's the deal with Qis'mos?

Qis'mos is the richest, most productive region of the continent - which also means it got absolutely torn apart during the thirty-year Ryraxian War. It's governed by a Potentate who is elected and impeachable by the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters (a system that traces back to the old Draconic empire). The Church of Bahumut has massive influence there, centered in the holy city of Qama. TL;DR: wealthy, politically complex, religiously important.
you mentioned the church, but I remember something about drow? that sounds complicated

Yeah, it's a mess. The drow were recruited as allies by Artkos near the end of the war, and they're still there - most visibly in Qama itself. When the drow occupied the holy city in the late war phases, they appointed their own high priest (Qe'lth IV) instead of recognizing the traditional leadership. So now there's a schism: the official high priest in Qama is drow-backed, but the legitimate candidate (Qachu II) is operating out of Qis without any of the actual relics and regalia of office. It's a political and religious mess that nobody's quite resolved yet.
hey, I'm trying to refresh on the language setup. In the current Qamis campaign, what's the language policy like under the Exarch Giovanni Pesaro?

Small correction -- Giovanni Pesaro is the Exarch of Qaringil, the island city-state from the prequel campaign about 200 years in the past. He's held that post for 19 years. In present-day Qamis, the political map is very different -- there's no Exarch there. Which era or region were you actually asking about?
Oh damn, I was mixing them up. Yeah, present-day Qamis. So like, what's the main language spoken across the continent?

No single "main" language -- Qamis is divided into nation-states, and each has its own. The big ones are Edran (Edrad), Artklan (Artkos and the Artklan League), Qis (Qis'mos and old imperial regions), and Forthrian (Forthyr). Plus Islas from the island traders.

But here's the key thing: these languages aren't standardized. Someone from the capital of Artkos would struggle to understand the regional Artklan dialect spoken out in the mountain towns of Rathakos. So "Artklan" usually means the prestige capital dialect unless you specify otherwise.
Okay, that makes sense. So if you're starting a character in Rathakos -- which sits between Edrad and Qis'mos -- would you speak Edran there, or Qis, or something else?

Good question, but the notes don't actually pin down Rathakos's primary language. It's a mountainous border state that borders Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, and others, so it probably has a linguistic blend -- but the exact makeup isn't spelled out yet. That'd be worth asking your DM about when building a character from there.
hey! so I'm a drow who stayed topside after the war, and I'm wondering about getting into the guild scene in Qaringil. what are the Great Guilds?

Quick clarification first -- Qaringil's the prequel city from about 200 years before the Ryraxian War. The drow-on-the-surface thing is more of a present-day Qamis detail. Are you playing in Qaringil or Qamis era? That'll change things.
oh dang, qamis era. my bad. so is qaringil even still around then?

Yeah, it should be -- city's been there for a couple centuries by then. But the notes don't really detail what Qaringil looks like in the present day, so that's a DM fill-in. What's your actual campaign location?
we're starting in rathakos. but my character's got history with qaringil, so i wanted to know about the guild structure

Gotcha. So in the Qaringil era, there were seven Great Guilds -- Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. Five major families mostly ran them. Whether that structure's the same two centuries later in Qamis time, your DM would have to say. But that's the historical baseline.
which family had the most power?

They were pretty balanced, actually -- Peixota led Shipping, Manos had Judicial, Qanto was Mercantile (though they lost it at some point), Volmer ran Banking, and Vitelli led Silk. All had fingers in multiple pies. The Gondolier and Wool guilds were the only major ones that stayed independent from the families.
Hey, I missed last session and everyone kept bringing up Ryraxos. I'm playing a gondolier in Qaringil and I'm totally lost. Is he relevant to our campaign or something?

Ryraxos is actually future history from your Qaringil perspective -- he's a major figure in the present-day Qamis setting, about 200 years ahead of when your campaign takes place. So he wouldn't show up in your game directly, but he's part of the setting's larger story.

He's a bronze dragon who rose to power as an artillery commander. During the Ryraxian War, he basically turned the entire conflict on its head. Everyone expected it to last about two months, but it went on for thirty years. After his eventual defeat, he accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon.
Okay, so he's future lore. That makes sense. But thirty years? What even started the whole war?

The Ryraxian War happened after the Keyberos dynasty fell and got replaced by a Revolutionary Council. When that looked too chaotic, Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire made a pact to march on Edrad, depose the Council, and restore the Keyberos dynasty. Everyone thought: done in sixty days. But Ryraxos was supporting Edrad, and his involvement kept escalating the conflict into this massive continental war that dragged on for three decades.

By the time it ended, the Keyberos were back in power like planned, but the political map had been completely redrawn. That's why it matters to the larger world -- the present-day Qamis campaign exists in the aftermath of all that.
Got it. So for my gondolier stuff I don't really need to worry about any of this?

Right -- your Qaringil campaign is set 200 years before all of that, so dragons and continental wars aren't a factor the way they are later. You're dealing with the Five Families, guild politics, that whole scene. Different world entirely. But good context to have for how the setting evolves.
okay so i'm reading about qaringil and the gondolier guild keeps getting mentioned but it sounds like it's just... boats? is that actually a major thing or am i missing something

Good catch -- it's more than just boats, but the notes are sparse. Here's what we know: the Gondolier Guild controls both the gondolas that crisscross the city and the maintenance of the two enormous bridges that connect Qaringil to the mainland via a volcanic island. That's a big infrastructure responsibility. They've also never been led by one of the five major families, which suggests they have some independence or at least a different power structure than the Mercantile or Shipping Guilds. But honestly, that's about all the DM has pinned down so far.
so like... what's their actual internal structure? do they have officers? who decides policy? are they rich?

Those are all blank spaces. The notes don't say. You've got the fact that they run critical infrastructure and that they're independent of the major families, but how they're actually organized, whether they're wealthy, what their internal politics look like, what their relationship is to the Exarch -- none of that is filled in. That's potentially a hook for backstory if your character has ties to them, or something the DM will develop as play unfolds.
wait, is this the starting city for the qamis campaign we're doing or is this something else?

Good question to clarify -- Qaringil is the Qaringil prequel campaign, which is set about 200 years *before* the Qamis present-day campaign you're joining. The Qamis campaign starts in Rathakos, a mountainous border state. So Qaringil is a separate adventure, different era, different political map. The notes I've got for present-day Qamis don't mention the Gondolier Guild at all.
oh okay that makes sense. so for the qamis one where we're starting, what are the guilds in rathakos?

The Qamis notes don't detail Rathakos's guilds. The city Rath is mentioned as the capital, and there's a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship in the region because the Red Mountains are dangerous and underexplored, but the internal guild structure and economy of Rathakos itself are blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. That's another potential hook for your character's background.
Hey, I missed last session and I'm thinking about pitching Qaringil as a one-shot for some folks. Quick question: what's the deal with the moons? Which ones are moving which way across the sky?

Good question. You've got three prograde moons (moving west-to-east like you'd expect) and three retrograde ones (rising in the west). Here's the breakdown:

Prograde: Tertius, Quartus, and Primus.
Retrograde: Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus.

The most important one is Tertius -- it's got an 8-day period, so it basically sets the week. Quartus is your monthly calendar (29.5-day period), so it's the natural clock for longer cycles.

Primus is a special case though. It's geostationary -- it never moves at all. Just hangs there fixed in the sky over the same spot on the equator. Tiny, dark, but critically useful for navigation.
Wait, Tertius is 8 days? So the week is just tied directly to a moon phase cycle?

Exactly. On Qaia, the "week" is just the Tertius cycle -- about 8.15 days. Your calendar runs on the actual celestial mechanics of the system rather than some arbitrary cultural convention. It's baked into the world.

That matters for Qaringil especially, since island city-states tend to be really attuned to lunar cycles and tides. If you're running the prequel, time might feel a bit different to your players just from the 8-day rhythm instead of a 7-day one.
Got it. And Primus being locked in place -- does that actually come up in Qaringil, or is it more of a present-day thing?

It's relevant in both eras. Primus doesn't move in Qaringil any more than it does in Qamis -- same moon system, just 200 years earlier. It's useful for navigation on the near side of the planet, especially for maritime city-states like Qaringil. You can use it to find your longitude without a chronometer, which is a huge advantage for someone running trade routes.

That said, whether your one-shot players will care about celestial navigation depends on what you're doing. If it's pure adventure, it might just be flavor. If there's travel or exploration involved, it becomes more practical.
yo, so there's this one moon that never moves? I keep seeing it in the same spot every night and it's weird.

Yeah, that's Primus. It hangs in the sky directly over the equator and never rises or sets for anyone on this hemisphere -- the same spot every single night, forever. It's called geostationary. Its orbital period matches Qaia's rotation exactly, so it's basically locked in place.
that's... actually kind of cool? but like, why does that matter? is it just a celestial novelty or something?

Oh no, it's genuinely useful. Primus is the reason navigation works on this side of the world. Because it's always in the exact same spot, you can measure your longitude -- your east-west position -- just by measuring its height in the sky. No chronometer needed. You already know your latitude from the pole star, so Primus's elevation pins down exactly where you are.
wait, you can just... figure out where you are? that seems insane. and you said 'this side of the world' -- does the other hemisphere not get this?

Exactly. On the far side, Primus is always below the horizon -- completely invisible. So yeah, they're stuck with dead reckoning and stellar methods, which is way harder. You're fortunate being on this side. The whole navigation method works through something called the primsine function -- it's a single table lookup that converts Primus's elevation into your angular distance from directly under it. With your latitude and that distance, geometry gives you your longitude.
huh. that's... pretty clever, whoever designed that. or did it just naturally work out that way?

Good question. Primus's properties aren't random -- its orbital plane exactly matches Qaia's equator because the moon's inclination to the ecliptic is precisely 23.5 degrees, which is the same as Qaia's axial tilt. They cancel out perfectly. And it's magically anchored in place, so it doesn't drift or migrate like the other moons do. So either this was deliberately engineered ages ago, or it's one hell of a cosmic coincidence. Either way, it's been incredibly stable for a very long time.
morning! so I've been reading about the families in Qaringil and I'm curious about the Manos family. they seem like they have their fingers in a lot of pies.

They do, yeah. Manos is one of the Five Families, and their main power base is the Judicial Guild -- they lead that. Beyond that they're also involved in silk and wool, which gives them influence across trade and commercial stuff.
okay, so the wool thing -- the notes say their entry into it is "hotly contested." what's the actual beef? are they trying to take over the Wool Guild or something?

That's the blank space, honestly. The notes say the contention exists but don't explain what it's about. But here's the interesting part: the Wool Guild has never been led by any of the Five Families -- it's run by someone from outside that circle. So if Manos is pushing into wool, they're definitely stepping on someone's toes, just... whose exactly isn't spelled out.
huh. so we don't know if they're trying to take over production, trade, or just muscle in on profits?
Nope. The Wool Guild covers both production and trade in wool, so it could be either or both. The notes only tell us the Manos entry is contested, not the shape of the conflict.
and nobody names who actually runs the Wool Guild?

Not in the campaign prep so far. It's definitely a non-major family or maybe a coalition of minor guilds, but the specific names and personalities aren't filled in yet. That's the kind of thing a DM could use for a PC backstory or a plot thread to discover.
so i'm thinking about my drow character staying on the surface after the war, maybe getting involved with some trading operations. how bad is navigation if we end up sailing to the far side?

It's pretty rough, honestly. Without Primus visible, you lose the geostationary longitude fix that makes navigation so easy on the near side. You'd have to fall back on dead reckoning, stellar methods, or lunar-distance calculations using one of the other moons against a star catalogue. It's basically the navigation problem Earth had before the chronometer -- you can still get latitude from the pole star, but longitude becomes a real headache.
right, so would the drow enclaves be based out of Qaringil then? that's the trade hub, right? or would we set up in Qama?

Ah, easy mix-up -- Qaringil and Qama are two different places. Qaringil is the island city-state in the central sea, a merchant republic with the big bridges. Your drow enclaves are actually in Qama, which is the holy city of Bahumat in Qis'mos on the mainland, plus scattered in a dozen other places. Both are trade-oriented and culturally mixed, so I see why you'd conflate them! The good news: Qama's on the near side, so Primus is visible and navigation is straightforward. The far-side problem only becomes real if you sail way west or venture to the opposite hemisphere.
got it, so i'm not going to be stuck doing dead reckoning from Qama. but if we did expand operations west, we'd really have to learn all that extra navigation stuff?

Yeah, if you went far enough west to lose sight of Primus, you'd be in that harder navigation world -- stellar transits, dead reckoning, maybe lunar distances with Tertius or Quartus if you've got the star charts. It's the kind of thing that made exploration slow and expensive on Earth. Doable, but you'd need skilled navigators and you'd lose a lot of precision. But from the settled drow enclaves where they are now, you've got Primus in the sky, so you're good.
hey, can you catch me up? i missed last session and apparently we talked about religion in westron?

Sure. So Westron is... kind of a blank space right now. The DM knows it's important and knows there are significant religious differences from the mainland, mainly that they don't worship Bahumat like most of Qamis does. But the specific details haven't been filled in yet -- it's left open for the campaign to explore.
gotcha. btw since we're talking about westron -- i'm curious about the language side. are there Q sounds in their languages like there are in ours? like the kh sound in qaringil

Good question. So Q is pronounced 'Kh' (like the Old Draconic sound) when it appears in place names and lore. Old Draconic -- or Old Qis -- is the scholarly language, used in treaties and academic stuff. It's where a lot of the place names come from, the ones with that 'q without u' pattern. But the actual spoken languages of Qamis are national: Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas. Those don't necessarily have the kh sound the way Old Draconic does.
so Old Draconic is like Latin -- the written prestige language -- and the national languages are the vernaculars?

That's a solid analogy. Yeah, Old Draconic (or Old Qis) serves that scholarly/diplomatic role. And the national languages do vary by region too -- someone from the capital of Artkos would struggle to understand Artklan as spoken in Rathakos, for example. But without qualification, the languages are understood as the prestige dialect: the one spoken in the capital and by the ruling class.

interesting -- so if westron does end up having its own language, would it maybe have different religious terms tied to the non-bahumat stuff? like how qis language connects to the qis religion stuff?

That's a really smart connection. And honestly, that's the kind of thing that could totally be part of what makes Westron distinct once the DM fills it in. But right now it's still mostly open. All we know is they have different religious traditions and they're not Bahumat-focused. The linguistic side of that -- whether it has its own language, what religious vocabulary exists, how that might connect -- that's blank space waiting to be explored.
yo, what's the deal with the criminal guilds in Qamis? like, the Thieves and Bandits?

Just to clarify -- the criminal organizations that style themselves as guilds (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses) are actually from the Qaringil era, the prequel campaign set about 200 years before the current Qamis setting. In present-day Qamis, criminal underworld stuff isn't documented yet -- that's a blank space. Which era were you asking about?
oh, Qaringil then. so the 'Bandits' guild thing -- that's a wild name to style as a guild, right? does it have Old Draconic roots? like the Q names you see everywhere?

Good linguistic instinct! Old Draconic is where you get those 'Q without U' place names like Qama, Qis, Qirkos -- all pronounced 'Kh'. But the criminal guild names (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses) -- the notes just call them by those common terms. Whether they have formal Old Draconic names or not isn't detailed. Could be a fun thing for your DM to flesh out, though!
wait, Qama is in Qamis too though right? i thought those crime guilds were happening there?

Qama does exist in present-day Qamis -- it's the holy city of Bahumut in the Qis'mos region. But the documented criminal guilds (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses) are specifically from the Qaringil prequel era. In present-day Qamis, there's no detail on whether criminal underworld organizations exist or how they're structured. Different campaigns, different times!
okay so everyone keeps talking about this setting but it sounds super complicated? like... too many nations, too much history. is it as messy as it sounds?

Fair question. It's definitely got moving parts -- thirty-year war, multiple nations, two campaign eras 200 years apart. But the core of it is actually pretty straightforward. Want me to talk about the biggest nation and you can judge?
sure, lay it on me. what's Edrad all about?

Edrad is the culturally dominant nation -- based in the coastal capital of Jia, which sits on a peninsula in the Central Sea. Ruled by a half-elven nobility; specifically the Keyberos dynasty, who've held the throne for four hundred years. They just got it back after the dragon Ryraxos ruled for thirty years and the Revolutionary Council ruled before that. Now the borders are back where they historically were.
okay, that's not too bad. so what's their economy? are they mercantile, agrarian, what?

Good question -- the notes don't actually detail that. They say Edrad is politically and economically dominant, and that Jia is the most culturally influential city on the continent. But the specific pillars of their economy? That's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
uh huh. and how does the half-elven nobility thing work? like, all the nobles are half-elf?
Close -- they have to be between one quarter and three quarters elf. So a mix of bloodlines, but within that range. That's the actual rule from the notes.
right. but like... why? there's gotta be a reason the Keyberos dynasty *has* to maintain that ratio. what's the history there?

Another blank space. The notes just state the rule itself, not the historical reason behind it. It's definitely something the DM could detail, but right now it's just... a constraint on the bloodline.
so there ARE blank spaces. you keep saying that like the DM is cool with it?

Yeah -- the DM literally marked them out in the notes. The document says 'There are a lot of blank spaces on the map that need to be filled. You can help with that.' So it's intentional. The core is there: Edrad is dominant, Jia is the cultural heart, the Keyberos dynasty just got power back. But economics, internal politics, specific cities beyond Jia, Etops, and Qeth -- those are open to fill in.
huh. okay so it's not *actually* super complicated, it just looks that way because there's all this stuff that's not written yet?

Pretty much. The setting has real structure and several detailed mechanics -- the moon system is *deeply* worked out, for instance. But the political and economic texture of most nations? Still being built. So you get these moments where you hit the edge of what's there.
ok wait, so primus just sits there? like it never moves in the sky? that can't be right

no it's real! primus is geostationary -- it orbits at 42,160 km out with a period that EXACTLY matches qaia's sidereal day. so it's locked in place above a single point on the equator. from edrad, it's just this motionless fixed point in the sky, day after day, year after year. never rises, never sets. you grow up staring at the same patch of sky and primus is always there in the exact same spot.
OKAY so that's wild enough, but-- people on the OTHER side of the planet can't see it at all right? that's what i'm reading

exactly! it's permanently below the horizon for the far-side hemisphere. you can only see primus if you're within roughly ±80° longitude of the prime meridian -- which is the sub-primus point, right at the equator where it hangs. so edrad gets this incredible navigational anchor that basically half the world doesn't have access to. no wonder edran navigators had such an advantage historically.

this is it. this is the detail that makes my character work. half-elf edran noble who's NEVER been off the continent, and primus is just background radiation, right? but then suddenly she realizes that's a POWER. that's why edrad dominated.

ohh i love that! edrad grows up with free longitude for everyone with a sextant and a clear night. the far side has to solve their longitude problem the hard way -- dead reckoning, stellar methods, all of it harder. and your character suddenly realizes that constant pinprick of light is why edrad's merchant fleets, why edran power projection even works. it's not just culture or wealth -- it's literally written into the moon system.

So I'm creating a character from Rathakos, someone who lived through all the Red Mountain Republic chaos. I keep seeing that Ryraxos got exiled to the second moon, and I'm wondering why specifically the second moon?

Great question - the notes actually don't explain the reasoning for that choice. They just say that after his defeat, Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon, and that he won't play any role in the campaign. But the 'why Secundus specifically' part - that's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
Alright, fair enough. But like... what even IS the second moon? Is it habitable? Can dragons even survive there?

The notes don't cover whether Secundus is habitable or how Ryraxos survives there - also blank spaces. Physically, Secundus is a solid moon: about 40% of Earth's Moon's mass with a dark iron-rich surface, nearly the size of our Moon in the sky (28 arcminutes across). Retrograde orbit, so it rises in the west. What's interesting is the wording - the notes say he 'accepted' exile, which suggests it might have been negotiated rather than purely forced. But the actual nature of his situation up there - is it livable, is it exile or sanctuary, what's he doing - that's all blank space.
so i'm seeing a lot of Q names in Qamis -- Qama, Qis'mos, Qirkos. but they're all pronounced with a 'Kh' sound? that's weird. is that like, a Draconic thing? how does it work in FR?

Yeah, Q is consistently pronounced 'Kh' in Qamis -- it's from Old Draconic, the language that shows up in all the scholarly publications and treaties. Place names like Qama, Qis'mos, Qirkos all follow that pattern. As for Forgotten Realms -- this is a completely homebrew setting, so the linguistic history is different. But the key thing here is that Old Draconic (also called Old Qis) is the academic language, so that's where the 'q without u' spelling convention comes from.
okay but *why* the Kh sound? did dragons introduce it? is there some historical sound shift? because in most settings the draconic influence is either a lot more alien or it's kinda glossed over.

That's a good question, and honestly the campaign notes don't go into the historical reason for the shift -- just that it exists and comes from Old Draconic. There's definitely something interesting there about why dragons would phonetically influence the local languages that much, but the DM hasn't filled in that blank space yet. It's totally the kind of thing you could build backstory around though.
so Old Draconic is still used? like, is it a living language or more like Latin in the setting?

It's more like Latin. Old Draconic is the language of scholarship and formal treaties -- you see it in official documents and academia. But the actual spoken languages are national: Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas. The Qis language is actually the closest modern descendant to Old Draconic, which makes sense given the cultural continuity in that region. Each of those national languages has regional variations too, so a scholar from the capital of Artkos might have trouble understanding the Artklan spoken way out in a mountain border region like Rathakos.
yo! quick question about setting continuity
Hey! What's up?
so I'm looking back at my notes from Qaringil, and Artkos back then was like... just the northwest, right? Queen Ezvia was expanding but it was still pretty small compared to the Holy Qis Empire?

Yeah, exactly. In the Qaringil era -- about 200 years back -- Artkos was just its historical heartland in the northwest. Ezvia was pushing to expand, but central Qamis was dominated by the Holy Qis Empire ruling from Ath'r. Fast forward to now and Artkos has grown massively. The Artklan Empire at its peak controlled about four tenths of the continent. High King Ezevir Arth is trying to consolidate that dominance.

okay so they basically won. But wait -- isn't the Artklan League fragmented now? Or am I mixing things up? Feels a little like the collapsed Netheril trying to reestablish in Forgotten Realms or something.

You're not mixing things up -- it IS fragmented. And yeah, there's a surface similarity to that FR arc, but this is homebrew, so the dynamics are different. The Artklan League isn't exactly a collapsed empire trying to reconquer. The war just ended thirty years ago, so Artkos DID dominate during the Ryraxian conflict. But now the newly independent states -- Rathakos and Acrokos especially, which literally didn't exist before -- have their own institutions and aren't just waiting to be reincorporated. Worse, Kard, which is Artkos's own southwestern enclave, is a nightmare: it's a major financial hub but the population is mostly Edran-speaking and actually welcomed the invasion twenty-eight years ago. So you've got Artkos trying to restore dominance over nations that just experienced autonomy, plus internal instability in your own territories.
Damn. That's way messier than I expected. So what about the characters -- is Ezevir related to Ezvia at all, or did that whole dynasty line change?

The notes don't specify. Ezvia was ruling Artkos 200 years ago and actively expanding, and now Ezevir Arth is High King in the present day, but the exact lineage and succession between them isn't filled in. Could be a good question for your DM if you're thinking about Artklan character backstory.
So what's the brightest moon you'd see from Rathakos? My character's from the Red Mountains and I'm trying to picture the night sky.

Qaia has six moons. The brightest at full is Septimus at magnitude −7.14, though it's still about 5.6 magnitudes dimmer than Earth's Moon. The dimmest is Quartus at −12.64. Sextus at −8.02 is second-brightest. From Rathakos up in the mountains you'd see whichever are above the horizon at any given time, and they vary a lot because of their different orbital periods — Septimus goes full roughly every 106 days.
Huh, so they're all dimmer than Earth's Moon? That seems like the night sky would be pretty dark on Qaia.

Actually, not necessarily — Tertius is almost identical to Earth's Moon in brightness (just 0.14 magnitudes dimmer), and you often have multiple moons in the sky at the same time since they're on different orbital schedules. Plus the inner moons cycle faster, so there's way more variety in the night sky than just one full moon every month. It would feel different from Earth — more moons, different cadences — but not darker overall.
so what's edrad actually want? just power grab everything?

Edrad's main foreign policy goal right now is taking Qeth and the whole region to the northeast. Currently it's all claimed by the Holy Qis Empire from their seat at Ath'r, but the Keyberos dynasty -- they run Edrad, been in power for a couple centuries -- they want it for themselves. Already the most powerful and wealthy nation on the continent, so yeah, it's basically consolidation of dominance.
does the qis empire actually control all that though? seems like a lot to hold

Yeah, that's the thing -- they claim it, but actually holding it is messier. The core Qis heartland is rich and well-controlled, but the broader empire has free cities like Lock that are basically independent and in constant cold conflict over their ancient rights. Mountains have independent strongmen, borderlands have local powers. The empire's more like a claim backed by regional influence than an iron grip everywhere. That's exactly why Edrad sees an opening.
interesting. so like, what does that mean for qaringil? do we gotta worry about edrad coming after us?

Not really -- you're an island republic with your own merchant navy and guild structure, you control your own trade. Edrad's interests are on the continent. They might try to court you with favorable trade deals, build alliances, get the guilds on their side with gold, but conquest doesn't make sense. You're way more useful as a neutral trading hub. The bigger deal for Qaringil is watching how the continental power balance shifts -- affects which nations are rising, what trade routes matter, where the money flows.
hey so I'm rolling a Forthyrian from the Dragon Emperor sect. What's the deal with all the Kh names -- like Qamis, Qis'mos, Qama? Is that a dragon language thing?

Good catch. Yeah, the Kh is a big signal of dragon-era history. Those names come from Old Draconic -- also called Old Qis -- which was the language of scholarly publications and treaties back when the dragons held power. The written form uses what looks like 'q without u', but it's pronounced 'Kh'. Qamis, Qis'mos, Qama -- all Old Draconic place names from that era, so they keep the old pronunciation.
So Old Draconic is like... the empire's official language? Do people still speak it or is it dead?

Not dead, but definitely not a daily language anymore. Old Draconic survives as a language of the academy and diplomacy now -- scholars, treaties, formal docs. The main spoken languages are national: Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas. These vary a lot by region too, no standardization.

That said, Qis'mos is kind of the exception. Since they were where the old imperial institutions lasted longest after the dragons left, the Qis language there is actually the closest to Old Draconic. But even that's more of a living regional language than the scholarly Old Draconic everyone else uses.
Interesting. So in Forthyr, with all the Dragon Emperor revival stuff... does the sect use Old Draconic in their rituals or ceremonies?

That's a blank space -- the notes don't detail the sect's practices or whether they use Old Draconic in their ceremonies or teaching. What I can tell you is that Forthyr is "somewhat backwards" and the sect calling for the Dragon Emperors' return gets "tacit encouragement, if not support" from the Dragoncaller dynasty. But the specific rituals, language use, or cultural details of the sect itself... that's up to you and your DM to develop.

Fortunately for a Forthyrian Dragon Emperor cultist, that's basically your character's origin story blank space to fill in with your backstory.

Fair, that's actually helpful. One more thing -- when you say Old Draconic is the language of treaties, was that because dragons literally made the rules? Like, is there a reason the Kh sound exists, or is it just historical?

The notes just say Old Draconic was the language of scholarly work and treaties during and after the dragons' era -- they don't explain *why* it took that form, or whether the dragons literally constructed the language, or if it developed in the imperial court, or what. The fact that Q is pronounced Kh 'from Old Draconic' is established, but the origin story of why that sound system exists... that's blank too.
OK so I'm looking at these campaign notes for Qamis and I'm seeing Qaringil all over the place. Is that like a province or region? How does the geography fit together?

Good question -- Qaringil is actually a city-state on an island in the central sea, so it's part of the geography either way. But here's the key thing: the two campaigns are set about 200 years apart. Qamis is the present day, right after the thirty-year Ryraxian War ended. Qaringil is the *prequel* set roughly two centuries earlier. Same world (Qaia), mostly same geography, but the political situation is completely different.
Ohhh OK. So like, in Qamis when Exarch Giovanni Pesaro is ruling Qaringil, that's the same person as in the present?

Not quite! Giovanni Pesaro *is* the Exarch in Qaringil, but he's the one from the prequel -- he's been in power for 19 years in that era and is considered to be on his deathbed. So he's not around anymore by the time of Qamis present day, two centuries later. Different Exarchs entirely, separated by two hundred years of history.
Right, right. So the big difference between the two campaigns is basically the political map changed a ton, especially after the war?

Exactly. In Qaringil's era (~200 years back), the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r, Artkos is just its historical heartland in the northwest, and places like Rathakos and Acrokos don't exist yet. Fast forward to Qamis present day: the Keyberos dynasty is restored in Edrad, the Artklan Empire has fractured into the Artklan League trying to hang on to power, and Rathakos and Acrokos are new mountain princedoms carved out after the war. Same continent, but it's been reshaped.
Got it. So if I wanted to play in both campaigns, I'm basically exploring the same world at two very different points in its history.

Exactly right. Both campaigns use the same moon system and continent, so the navigation rules and geography are stable. But the nations, rulers, and conflicts are two very different stories. It's a cool way to see how empires rise and fall over centuries.
yo, what's the quintus thing in the moon notes?

Quintus is basically a trace particle—effectively massless—sitting at the Sun-Qaia L4 point, which is 60 degrees ahead of Qaia in its orbit around the sun. It oscillates between 45 and 80 degrees from Qaia with a period of around 2,000 years. It's included for worldbuilding purposes, so not mechanically relevant to anything in play.
okay but why include something with such a weird specific oscillation? seems like oddly detailed for just background flavor

That specificity comes from the fact that the entire moon system was built from real orbital mechanics. L4 is a Lagrange point—a gravitationally stable zone where small bodies can naturally hang out indefinitely. The 2,000-year period is just what falls out of the math when you model it properly. The DM wanted the system to feel grounded in actual physics, even if the details never show up in the campaign.
makes sense as a design choice. but would anyone in the world actually know it exists? does Qaian astronomy go that deep?

The notes don't specify—it's a blank space. But here's what we know: Qaian navigators figured out how to use Primus for longitude without a chronometer, which means they've invested serious effort in understanding the sky. Discovering Quintus would take centuries of observation and wouldn't be immediately useful, but it's plausible someone could spot it eventually. It's the kind of thing that hints at deeper layers of knowledge waiting to be uncovered in the campaign.
Okay so I'm thinking about running Qaringil as a one-shot, and I'm reading about navigation and I just hit this line about 'no chronometer required' -- what's that about??

Oh man, this is such a cool detail! So Primus -- the little geostationary moon -- is locked in place directly over the prime meridian at the equator. It literally never moves in the sky. Which means any navigator can measure where it sits in the sky relative to their horizon, and that immediately tells them their longitude.
Wait, how does THAT work? I'm not following.

So the sub-Primus point is always at 0-0 (equator, prime meridian), right? Since Primus is fixed, when you measure its elevation angle, you're basically measuring how far away you are from that fixed point. Combine that with your latitude (which you get from the pole star the normal way) and you've got your position. There's a formula -- primsine(elevation) -- that reduces the whole calculation to a table lookup and a division. No need to know what time it is!
That's actually BRILLIANT. So Earth navigators needed chronometers because the moon moves across the sky?

Exactly! On Earth, the Moon orbits once a month, so its position constantly changes -- you need to know the time to figure out where it should be relative to you. But Primus is magically anchored in place. It's the same "thing" in the sky every single night. That's why the notes call it "a revolutionary longitude reference." Qaian navigators solved the longitude problem because of moon physics, not mathematics and chronometers.
Oh man, and you could actually use this in a one-shot mechanic. Characters on a ship, player has to navigate, instead of "roll navigation" it's "measure Primus's elevation" ...

RIGHT? And there's a built-in drama too -- Primus only works if you're on the near-side hemisphere. Get exiled to the far side? Navigators are flying blind, back to dead reckoning. It's a natural constraint that makes location matter tactically. The setting just gives you this elegant system and it immediately creates narrative friction.
I'm building a character for Qaringil and thinking about tying them to Forthyr for some backstory. But isn't that super far away? Feels like I'd be better off with local connections.

You're right that it would be distant. Forthyr is in the far northeast of Qamis, so it's not a natural home base for a Qaringil native. That said, the island city has merchant fleets and sailors who travel, so a connection isn't impossible if your character has that kind of background. But I'll be honest: the notes don't give you a lot to work with. Forthyr's ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong I, a half-dragonborn sorcerer-king, and there's a religious sect that wants the Dragon Emperors back -- the ruling dynasty tacitly supports them. Beyond that, it's described as "somewhat backwards," but that's mostly it. The DM hasn't filled it in much.
So... a dragon-blooded guy backing a cult that wants dragons to return? That honestly sounds pretty generic. Why not just make something up instead?

That's a fair read. And if it doesn't grab you, there's no reason to force it. Forthyr is one of the blank spaces -- the DM has left it sketchy on purpose. But here's what I'd say: it's not just "random cult," it's the *ruling dynasty itself* tacitly funding a Dragon Emperors restoration movement. That creates real cultural texture and potential conflict. If your character came from there, you've got built-in hooks -- loyalty to the family? Disillusionment with the cult? Refugee from political chaos? Any of those would give you substance to work with. You'd develop it *with* the DM, though, since the skeleton is there but the real detail isn't.
Okay that's actually pretty cool. So I could pitch something and the DM would probably fill it in? What kind of work would naturally bring someone from Forthyr to an island city?

Good question, but the notes are silent on that. There's no specified trade or migration pattern between Forthyr and Qaringil. That's another blank space. What I *can* tell you: Qaringil draws exiles, merchants, sailors, and adventurers from everywhere -- the city's built on that. If you pitch a concept (scholar fleeing a purge? Mercenary? Family merchant? Someone escaping dragon-cult complications?), the DM will almost certainly build on it. What kind of character concept did you have in mind?
so i'm reading about the war and i'm confused -- the intro says bookmakers had some betting line on how long it would last?

Yeah, that's a great detail. When Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire decided to march on Edrad to depose the Revolutionary Council and restore the Keyberos dynasty, bookmakers in Kard set the over/under at sixty days. It was supposed to be a quick political fix. The war actually lasted thirty years.
okay so that's a *massive* miss. like, half a century off. what went so wrong with their prediction?

The big factor was Ryraxos. He's a bronze dragon who started out as an artillery officer for Edrad, and he was really good at it -- dragons can actually help carry the guns. When Edrad looked like they were losing, Ryraxos turned the war around. But once he did, instead of going home, he kept fighting and waged aggressive war across the continent for the next two and a half decades. So the bookmakers were probably betting on a conventional political war between nation-states. They didn't account for a dragon overlord deciding to just keep the conflict going for his own reasons.
So you've got this Westron continent. What's the religious landscape look like over there? I've been playing since 1st edition and homebrew DMs always half-ass the religion stuff.

Fair question. The notes specifically call out that Westron has religious differences from the main continent -- mostly notably, they don't worship Bahamut the way Qamis does. That's basically it. The Church of Bahamut is huge in Qamis and Qis'mos, but Westron... doesn't share that. Beyond that the DM hasn't written anything up yet.
Okay, so what DO they worship? Elemental stuff? Some forgotten god? Dragonborn culture like in Forthyr?

The notes don't say. That's genuinely a blank space. The DM noted that Westron is "important" and marked it as unprepped material. So there's explicitly something there -- different pantheon, different institutions, different culture -- but none of the specifics are filled in yet.
Ugh. So it's a continent that's supposed to be important but the DM basically said 'I'll figure it out later.' That's classic homebrew right there.

That's a fair read. The DM's been pretty transparent about it though -- they explicitly call out blank spaces in the notes rather than leaving you guessing. For Westron, the only hard fact is that religious practice is different from Bahamut-worship. Everything else -- their gods, their institutions, their temples, whether they're hostile or friendly to Qamis -- that's unfilled.
At least they're honest about it, I guess. But 'there are religious differences' is pretty thin for a whole continent. What if my character has ties to Westron?

Then you'd probably end up workshopping it with the DM during character creation. That's actually a hook -- if Westron's unfinished and the DM says it's important, a character with Westron ties could help fill in those blank spaces. It's how homebrew worldbuilding sometimes works best, honestly: the DM sketches the outline and lets player backstories help flesh it out.
so real talk -- is there any chance Ryraxos breaks out of exile? like, mechanically speaking, what are we working with here

The setting notes just say he accepted exile to Secundus (the second moon) and will play no role in the campaign. There's no detail on *how* he's imprisoned or whether the exile is escapable -- that's one of the blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. But the fact that the DM explicitly removed him from the campaign setup suggests it's not something that's meant to be a returning threat in the present day.
okay so that's more of a "it's not a mechanical consideration for this campaign" thing than "he's magically locked up forever"

Yeah, exactly. The DM's made it clear Ryraxos isn't a factor. Whether that's because he's genuinely unescapable, or just because the DM decided to shelve him as a plot element, the notes don't say. It's a design choice more than a lore constraint -- the post-war Qamis campaign is about the power vacuum and political fallout he left behind, not about him as an active threat.
got it. that's actually cool -- lets the world move past "dragon overlord" as the main conflict. what's filling that vacuum then?

Basically all the traditional political mess: Edrad's trying to reassert dominance, the Artklan League is fracturing (the high kings want their old empire back but their subjects are clinging to independence), drow allies from the war are now permanently stationed in cities like Qama, and border states like Rathakos are struggling to find stable government. Way more complex and nuanced than "one dragon running everything."
so I'm looking at the map and there's a bunch of blank spaces. DM didn't finish building this world?

Those blank spaces are intentional. The DM explicitly marks them in both campaigns and invites players to help fill them in. Some examples: regions that can be incorporated into your backstory, plans to add more islands, and whole sections marked 'blank spaces that need to be filled' like most of Westron. But the core is solid -- the three major nations, the 30-year Ryraxian War that just ended, the detailed moon system, the major cities. The edges are where the DM wants contributions.
alright, fair enough. so are we just making stuff up, or do we have to keep it consistent with what's already there?

Keep it consistent. The foundation is real: the moon system has specific physics that affects navigation and tides; there are two linked campaigns 200 years apart with different political maps; the nations have established histories and current conflicts. Any blank spaces you help fill need to fit that frame -- not contradict the Edrad-Artkos dynamic, the post-war situation, or the astronomy. The DM's asking for contributions, not a total rewrite.
hey, building a half-elf from Edrad for a campaign. what's the whole deal with half-elves there? like do they actually run the place or is it complicated?

Edrad is ruled entirely by half-elf nobility -- so yeah, they run it. But there's a specific rule: anyone in the Edran nobility has to be between one quarter and three quarters elf. So you can't be full elf or mostly-human, it's that narrow band.

The Keyberos dynasty has ruled for four hundred years from Jia, the capital on a peninsula in the Central Sea. Edrad's the most culturally influential city in all of Qamis, so being half-elf nobility there is a pretty big deal.

interesting, so it's like a genetic requirement basically. but wait -- does that naming thing connect to this? like I keep seeing names with Q's in them. is that the Old Draconic thing or is that more of an Edran thing?

Good catch! The Q without a U comes from Old Draconic (also called Old Qis) -- that's the scholarly language used in treaties and academic writing. Place names picked up a lot of it historically.

Edran is its own language, the prestige dialect spoken in Jia and by the ruling class. It's not standardized though -- regional variation means an Edran speaker from the capital might have trouble with the dialect from further out in the kingdom.

So names could go either way: some might follow Old Draconic convention (Q = Kh sound), others just regular Edran. Depends on your family's history and where they're from within Edrad.
okay so if I'm making up a backstory for someone from like, the capital area, what would their family probably be into? like, what do Edran nobles actually do?

The notes don't spell out exactly what the noble families do day-to-day, that's one of the blank spaces for you to fill in with your backstory. But given that Edrad is the political and economic powerhouse of Qamis, you've got room to work with.

What the notes do say: Jia is hugely culturally influential, so your family could be patrons of arts, scholars, whatever. The Keyberos dynasty itself obviously focuses on rule and military power. You could play with the tension between the prestige Edran of the capital versus your family's regional roots if you want some local color -- maybe an ancestor from the countryside, some half-remembered old language variant, that sort of thing.
so random question but what's the math for primsine?

The formula is: primsine(e) = ρ cos²e + sin(e) × sqrt(1 − ρ² cos²e), where ρ equals about 0.1511 (the ratio of Qaia's radius to Primus's orbital radius). Basically, you measure Primus's elevation angle, look up the primsine value in a table, divide by your latitude's cosine, and you get your longitude without needing a chronometer. Elegant stuff.
okay but like, did the old Dragon Emperors have this figured out?

The notes don't say when primsine navigation developed or whether the Emperors came up with it. It's tied to Primus being magically anchored in geostationary orbit, so there's definitely dragon magic in the foundation. But whether they developed the actual navigation method or it came later in independent places like Qis'mos -- that's a blank space. Could be an interesting thing for your Forthyr character to speculate about, especially if your sect has ideas about lost imperial knowledge.
yeah I mean that tracks. my character's from the Dragon Emperor revival sect so anything that might be ancient empire tech is gonna catch their interest.

Makes sense. Forthyr's got heavy dragon mystique already -- the Dragoncaller line and the revival sect getting tacit support from the throne means that world's full of imperial heritage vibes. Navigation tech is a more practical side of it, but it's the kind of detail that could fit into a character's worldview, especially if they're trying to piece together what the old empire actually knew.
cool setting -- quick rules question on bonus languages. are those gonna cost currency or feats, or do I just get an extra language pick?

No cost -- you get one bonus language free. The only requirement is you justify it through your backstory. The notes say 'everyone gets one bonus language too but you have to tell me how you know it,' so it's narrative-driven but mechanically free.
got it. hard cap at one, or could I stack more if the backstory backs it?

One bonus language is what the notes specify as the baseline. The DM hasn't explicitly ruled on whether you could negotiate for a second with solid backstory justification, so that's worth asking at the table. But plan on one.

quick question -- so the Keyberos get restored after the whole Ryraxos thing ends, but they're basically back to square one? or did they actually gain something from having a dragon warlord running things?

They're technically restored but at a loss. The Keyberos ruled Edrad for four centuries before the Revolutionary Council coup. Then Qis'mos and Artklan invaded to put them back in power -- figured it'd take sixty days. Thirty years later, once Ryraxos gets exiled to Secundus, they are ruling again, but their borders got driven back to their historical lines. So yeah, restoration, but with territory lost.
Hold on, the Revolutionary Council thing -- how long had they actually been in power before the invasion? Was Edrad in chaos for decades or was this a relatively fresh coup?

The notes don't say. You get 'squabbling Revolutionary Council' as the description of their state when the decision was made to remove them, but nothing on how long they'd actually held power or when they took over. It's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
Alright. So Ryraxos shows up because the restoration invasion is going badly?

Exactly. The notes are pretty clear: Edrad was facing defeat when Ryraxos intervened and saved them. But then instead of just helping the restoration stick, he wages aggressive war across the continent for two and a half decades. So the dynasty gets restored, but they're basically living under a dragon occupation the whole time.
Yeah, that's the weird part. So once he gets exiled, the Keyberos are back in power but... everything's wrecked. How fragile is that restoration looking in the current setup?

Very fragile. The intro literally says 'Everything has been put back the way it was. Except everything has changed.' Borders are reverted, the dynasty rules like before, but thirty years of continental war and a dragon military dictatorship don't just vanish. The notes point out that 'it is easy to sign an edict reversing thirty years of change, but harder to enforce it.' You see it everywhere -- Forthyr's ignoring the southwest, the Artklan League is fractious, and in Rathakos there's open unrest in the Southern Reaches that were annexed from Edrad during the war, plus mountain communities still following old dragon cults.

So the Keyberos have nominal authority but actual enforcement is a mess. But what about their political position internally? Like, did the dynasty's alliance with a dragon cost them credibility with their own nobility?

That's where the notes get quieter. You know Edran nobility is half-elven and rules from Jia, and the Keyberos are still culturally dominant, but the specifics of internal political scars from the occupation, whether elements of the nobility resent how the dragon situation was handled, whether there's a successor crisis brewing -- that's mostly blank space. It seems like the kind of thing the DM is letting the campaign explore and define.

The notes don't really explore that angle. But what the Keyberos actually negotiated with him, what leverage they had or didn't have, whether they could've shortened the occupation, any back-channel diplomacy -- that's all blank. Probably intentional though. That whole political dimension of the dynasty during the war feels like rich material for the campaign to dig into.
So Edrad's like the main power on the continent, yeah? I'm trying to figure out where my Forthyrian stands with them.

Edrad is historically the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis, ruled by half-elven nobility from the coastal capital city of Jia. Jia is also the most culturally influential city in Qamis overall, so they set a lot of the tone for what's considered civilized or respectable. But after Ryraxos's defeat, the Keyberos dynasty was restored to the throne, and Edrad's borders were "driven back to their historical lines." Ryraxos though -- he's a dragon, right? And he *won* for Edrad before going to war for himself. So what's the vibe around dragons in Edrad now? Back home in Forthyr we've got the whole Dragon Emperor revival thing. Do Edradians care about that at all?

You know that Forthyr's Dragoncaller dynasty gives tacit support to the Dragon Emperor revival sect -- but I don't have details on whether Edrad has a similar thing going, or whether they view dragons as a threat, or something else entirely. That's a genuine blank space. What we do know is Ryraxos was relatively young for a dragon, bronze, and started as an artillery commander. But his acceptance of exile suggests he was defeated, not that Edradians are necessarily pro-dragon now.

Wait, so mountain communities in Edrad's territory are *still* following old dragon cults? Is that what's driving the unrest, or is it something else? And are we talking about actual dragon worship or ancestor veneration or what?

The notes mention those communities exist and that they follow "the old dragon cults," but they don't specify what that actually looks like -- whether it's worship, ancestor veneration, political allegiance to a dragon ruler, or something else. It's left as a genuine blank space. What is clear is that the unrest in those mountain areas exists and it's connected to these cults, and that post-war, the Keyberos dynasty is finding it "impossible to ignore" the southwest, which had been neglected during the long war. So there's definitely instability your sect could theoretically leverage or understand. But the mechanics of what "old dragon cults" means and what they actually want? That's DM territory still.

okay so I'm building an Edran noble navigator, and I'm reading about Primus for position-finding. But the notes keep saying atmospheric refraction tanks your precision near the horizon. Like... doesn't that kind of defeat the purpose?

Not quite, but it's honest to say it's a real constraint. Let me break it down: Primus gives you longitude *without* a chronometer, which is revolutionary. The catch is that your precision depends on *where* you are.

At the equator near the prime meridian, Primus hangs high in the sky and elevation changes dramatically as you move east or west. A small measurement error becomes a big precision win. But near the visibility limit—roughly 81 degrees away from the prime meridian—Primus is almost on the horizon. The geometry gets shallow, and now refraction becomes a real problem. Atmospheric layers bend light, so you're not measuring true elevation, and your position fix degrades.

The notes say this explicitly: 'near the visibility boundary, Primus is low on the horizon; atmospheric refraction is significant and precision is reduced.'
Okay, but that sounds pretty bad? If you're far from the prime meridian, you basically can't use it reliably. So why would anyone bother?

Because Edrad controls the central coast, Artkos controls the northwest, and Qis'mos sits in the heartland—all of them cluster close to the prime meridian. You're not *supposed* to be using Primus from the far side of Qaia; you'd use dead reckoning or stellar methods instead, just like navigators did on Earth before the chronometer.

For an Edran noble navigating the Central Sea or coastal routes? You're well within the range where Primus works beautifully. A good sextant (±0.05°) gets you ~6–7 km precision even accounting for some refraction. That's genuinely useful for trade routes, naval operations, anything that isn't exploring the far hemisphere.

The limitation is real, but it's *designed* into the system. Primus isn't a magic fix for everyone; it's an advantage for nations on the prime-meridian side of Qaia.
Huh. So Edrad basically has the home-field advantage on navigation. That's... actually kind of a bigger geopolitical thing than I realized.

Exactly. The Edran Keyberos dynasty controlled maritime trade for centuries partly *because* their position on the Central Coast makes Primus navigation native knowledge. That's worth building into your character—an Edran noble would grow up assuming Primus is just how you find your way on the sea, while someone from the far side (if they're even venturing that way) is working harder with different tools.

If your PC is a navigator or trades across the Central Sea, Primus is second nature. If they venture inland toward Forthyr or the unexplored interior, suddenly that advantage evaporates. Adds some nice texture to the setting.
alright so real talk—this setting feels needlessly complicated. can you explain the drow situation in Qama without making it worse?

Sure. Qama is Bahamut's holy city in Qis'mos. During the late Ryraxian War, drow occupied it and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. There's now a rival priest, Qachu II, in Qis with the legitimate claim but no relics or actual seat of power.
