They don't, actually. The notes say drow were recruited by Artkos as desperation allies during the war, and that they ended up in scattered enclaves afterward—Qama being the most prominent one. But the mechanics of how they took the city, or their long-term plans? That's a blank space.
so it's just 'drow are there, deal with it.' this is exactly what I'm talking about. the setting has so many loose threads.

You're identifying a real gap. The core fact is solid—drow occupation of Qama created a religious schism that still matters. But the *why* they took it, or why they're staying, is unexplained. Could be intentional mystery for the campaign, could be something the DM will develop. But as written, yeah, it's more of a premise than a complete story.
so this primsine thing... am i crazy or is this like, insanely overcomplicated? like, i get that longitude is hard, but why do you need a whole special function just to measure a moon's elevation?

Fair question. It does sound overkill at first. But here's the elegance: you're converting 'elevation of a fixed moon from my position' into 'how far west or east am I from the prime meridian' — that's a spherical geometry problem. Primsine just solves it without needing to know what time it is. Measure an angle, look it up in a table, divide by one cosine, and you have longitude. No chronometer. That's revolutionary for any navigator who's ever been stuck trying to keep a reliable clock at sea.
okay fine, but i want proof this isn't just theoretical sailor fantasy. give me a real worked example with actual numbers.

Sure. You're at 30 degrees north latitude, Primus measures 42.2 degrees above the horizon, slightly to the west. Primsine of 42.2 degrees comes out to 0.7504. Divide by cos(30°), which is 0.8660, and you get 0.8666. Arccos of that is 30 degrees. Since Primus appeared to the west, you're 30 degrees east of the prime meridian. Three table lookups, one division, done.
okay that actually... that works out. but why does this only work from one hemisphere? what's so special about how Primus orbits?

That's the whole trick. Primus orbits at a distance and period that exactly match Qaia's rotation — its orbital period is exactly one Qaia day. And its orbital inclination to the ecliptic, 23.5 degrees, exactly equals Qaia's axial tilt. Those two tilts cancel perfectly, placing Primus's orbit in the equatorial plane. Result: Primus hangs absolutely motionless over a single point on the equator, never rising, never setting from the near side. From the far hemisphere you never see it at all.
right. and so — every navigator carries around a printed table of primsine values? like, you don't recalculate it every time?

Right, it's computed once and printed in the almanac as a permanent reference column. Primsine only depends on one fixed constant: the ratio of Qaia's radius to Primus's orbital distance, which is 0.1511. That ratio never changes. So you compute primsine for every degree — or every 0.05 degrees if you want precision — put it in the book, and you're done. Every navigator just looks it up. Same as sine tables. You're not recalculating the math from scratch, you're using the almanac.
so when's the next time all three inner moons are gonna be new at the same time? that seems like it'd be pretty rare

Great question! According to the alignment calendar, the closest triple-new of the next decade is May 24, 2262 at 03 hours PMT, where all three inner moons -- Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus -- are within 0.1% of new phase simultaneously. That's about as perfect an alignment as you get. The moons follow a roughly 12-year cycle alternating between full-moon eras (where triple-full alignments cluster) and new-moon eras (where triple-new alignments cluster), so there are several strong triple-news in sequence during a new era.
wait, 0.1%? that's insanely precise. how does that even happen with three different orbital periods?

It's a resonance effect. Secundus has a 4.53-day period, Tertius is 8.15 days, and Quartus is 29.50 days -- they're not in a clean ratio, but the numbers still create windows where they line up almost perfectly. Within a few hours' window a couple times a year, all three can be within 1% of the same phase. The alignment calendar picks out those sweet spots. May 24 is special because it's the tightest alignment of the decade.
so like, what happens to the tides during one of those? must be absolutely brutal

Exactly right instinct. The three inner moons combined pull at about 4.4 times Earth's Moon's tidal force -- Secundus contributes 1.47x Luna, Tertius at 2.04x, and Quartus at 0.87x. When all three are new (opposite side of Qaia from the sun) or all full (same side), the forces reinforce massively. You'd get spring tides that dwarf the normal daily cycle. Sailors definitely consult the alignment calendar before planning long voyages.
are there any records of settlements being built specifically to take advantage of or avoid those big tidal events?

That's a really good question, and honestly the notes don't cover it. A culture aware of the moons would absolutely track it -- some places might exploit predictable tidal surges for fishing or mills, others would need to design harbors and fortifications around them. But the specific history of how Qaian settlements adapted to the alignment cycles isn't filled in yet. Sounds like worldbuilding space if you're curious about that angle.
Quick question: why are all of Edrad's nobles half-elves? Is that lore or just flavor?

It's a hard rule. Edran nobility has to be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf — that's the requirement across the whole ruling class. Edrad is governed from Jia, a capital city on a peninsula in the Central Sea. The Keyberos dynasty has ruled for four hundred years, even through the thirty-year Ryraxian War that just wrapped up. And yeah, Edrad is by far the most culturally influential nation on the continent, so you see that half-elf aesthetic everywhere in the setting.
So it's locked in. But *why* though? Historical reason, bloodline thing, some magical requirement...?

That's actually a blank space — the campaign notes don't explain the origin or the reasoning. It's been the rule for the entire four-hundred-year Keyberos dynasty, so it's definitely woven into Edran culture, but the actual *why* isn't filled in yet. Could be historical, could be magical, could be something else. Might be a hook for a character backstory if you're interested in poking at it.
so i keep seeing references to 'dragon cults' in the setting notes. are they like actual organized religion or more folk beliefs?

Both, kind of? The main one is in Forthyr -- there's a prominent religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, and the Dragoncaller dynasty (the sorcerer-kings who rule Forthyr) tacitly encourage it, or at least don't suppress it. So it has real institutional backing.

But there are also older, more grassroots dragon cults scattered around. In Rathakos especially, mountain communities in the interior still follow what the notes call 'the old dragon cults' -- those seem more like surviving local beliefs from before the dragons left.
wait, the dragons *left*? what happened to them?

The notes don't spell out exactly what happened or when -- that's one of the blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. But there was definitely a Draconic empire at some point. The key clue is that Qis'mos, while it was never actually the seat of that empire, is where the institutions of the empire 'persisted the longest after the dragons departed.' So something happened that made the dragons go away, but their government structures lingered in that region for a really long time.
oh interesting. so 'Draconic' -- that's the same root as Old Draconic? the language with the Q/Kh thing?

Exactly! Old Draconic (also called Old Qis) is the scholarly and diplomatic language -- the 'Q without U' you see in place names like Qamis, Qis'mos, Qama, Qeth, Qaringil. The Q there is pronounced 'Kh' from Old Draconic.

So yeah, there's a direct linguistic thread: Old Draconic was the language of the actual Draconic empire. When the dragons left and the empire fell apart, Old Draconic became the prestige language of scholarship and treaties -- kind of like Latin in medieval Europe. The place names with Kh/Q sounds are literal fossils of that language.
that's so cool. so in Forthyr where they're calling for dragon emperors to come back, are they using Old Draconic in their sect? like is that part of the revival?

The notes don't get into those details, so I can't say for sure. But it would make sense -- if you're a religious sect longing for a restored Draconic empire, using the language of that empire would be a pretty natural move. The Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr would probably be familiar with Old Draconic anyway, since it's the language of official discourse.

That said, I'm speculating there. The setting notes just say the sect exists and receives tacit support; the religious practice and theology are blank spaces you could probably help the DM fill in.

hey! so im considering running qaringil as a one-shot. one of my players wants to build a merchant/navigator type and im trying to get a feel for what that would actually look like locally

Nice choice for a one-shot -- Qaringil is a vivid setting. The city's got strong maritime flavor: it's criss-crossed with canals, ruled as an "incomprehensible republic" according to the notes, and the elites are mostly commercial rather than landed nobility. So a merchant-navigator would fit right in.

The city has seven major guilds, including Shipping and Mercantile, so your player could easily be embedded in one of those. The Five Families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, Vitelli) run most of the power, and there's a lot of intrigue around guild leadership. Armed merchant fleets fan out from the city pretty regularly.

What direction is your player leaning for the character?

he wants to play someone with navigator skills, maybe a cleric or bard who did time on merchant ships. im trying to figure out what the actual navigation game would feel like -- like, would they use stars? maps? is there actual navigation tech in the world?

Oh, this is a fun detail. Qaian navigation is pretty well thought out. Navigators use two main celestial anchors:

1. **The pole star** -- you measure its elevation above the horizon to get your latitude. Classic stuff.

2. **Primus** -- this is the clever one. Primus is a geostationary moon (it never moves in the sky, always fixed over the same point on the equator). By measuring Primus's elevation and consulting a table called the primsine function, you can calculate your longitude *without needing a chronometer*. That's a huge deal historically.

So a skilled navigator in Qaringil would likely know both methods. It's practical, grounded, and gives your player some flavor to work with -- maybe they're proud of their navigation skills, or there's drama around learning the primsine tables.
wait, primus stays in the same spot forever? so like... does everyone have access to it or

Good catch -- there's a major limitation. Primus is only visible from *one hemisphere* of Qaia. It's geostationary over a single point on the equator (the prime meridian), and observers can only see it from roughly within ±81° longitude from that point. Beyond that, Primus dips below the horizon and you can't use it.

So navigators on the far side of the planet -- the hemisphere where Primus is never visible -- have to use dead reckoning, stellar transit observations, or other slower methods. It's a huge navigational disadvantage.

For Qaringil specifically, the city's on the islands of the central sea, so I'd assume it's within Primus's visibility range and the local navigator culture relies on it pretty heavily. But if a character ever ventures to the far side or deals with far-side traders, that's a real complication.

ohhh so thats why having ships and islands matters so much. the hemisphere with primus can navigate way easier. so like, is the current exarch someone who's good at navigation, or would that even be a guild thing?

The current Exarch is Giovanni Pesaro -- he's been in office 19 years and is considered to be on his deathbed, notes say. He's from a minor family, but he's aligned most closely with the Peixota and Manos families.

Navigation expertise would likely fall under the Shipping Guild (which Peixota leads) or maybe the Mercantile Guild. The Exarch himself is more of a political figure elected by the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters -- so while he'd definitely understand the importance of navigation, his personal skill might be in politics rather than seamanship.

But that could be fun for your character's backstory. Is he rivals with someone in the Shipping Guild? Mentored by a ship captain? Ambitious enough to navigate his way up the Mercantile Guild ladder? The setup gives you lots to play with.
cool, that helps. one more thing -- if primus is so useful, are there stories about navigators stranded on the far side? seems like that would be dramatic

That's a great instinct for worldbuilding. The notes don't have specific stories about far-side stranding, but the setup definitely suggests it would be *dramatic* -- your navigator would essentially lose their main tool. They'd have to improvise with dead reckoning, star charts, or lunar-distance methods, which are way slower and less precise.

Sounds like a blank space the DM (you!) could fill in. If your player wants some character depth, maybe their navigator has a traumatic memory of being stranded or cut off on the far side. Or maybe they're obsessed with the problem -- developing backup methods, or seeking out rare maps, or something. Could be a nice character hook for the one-shot.
yeah i like that. so should i tell him primus is fixed in the sky now, or let him discover it? or is that like... basic geography for a qaingil person

Solid question. For a character *from* Qaringil or with maritime training, knowing that Primus is geostationary would be basic knowledge -- like how we know about gravity, it's just part of how the world works. Any navigator in the city would take that for granted.

But the *implications* -- the advantage/disadvantage between hemispheres, the far-side problem, the primsine tables and how to use them -- those are learned skills. So I'd say: let him know the world has a stationary moon that doesn't rise or set, because that's atmospheric flavor he'd automatically have. But when it comes to how to use it for navigation or what it means for the far side, let that come out in play or in his backstory workshopping. That way it feels earned rather than info-dumped.

so like, my character wants to navigate using Primus. can she just look at it anywhere on the map?

Pretty much! Primus is a geostationary moon -- it hangs motionless over a fixed point on the equator (the prime meridian, at 0° longitude). From the near-side hemisphere, you can measure its elevation above the horizon and that tells you how far away you are from that fixed point. So yeah, as long as you can see Primus, you can figure out your longitude without needing a chronometer. That's the whole trick.
okay cool. but like... what about when it's really far away and low on the horizon? does it get harder then?

Ah, good question! Yeah, it actually does get harder. Primus is only visible to about 81 degrees away from the prime meridian. When you're near that edge and Primus is sitting low on the horizon, atmospheric refraction becomes a real problem -- the air near the horizon bends the light and messes with your angle measurement. On top of that, the geometry gets "shallow," so small errors in your elevation reading turn into bigger longitude errors. Your DM would say precision is worst near the visibility boundary.
ohhhh so like if I'm trying to navigate as I get closer to the edge of where I can see Primus, my measurements get worse and worse?

Yeah, exactly. Sensitivity is highest when Primus is overhead (best precision) and lowest when it's near the horizon (worst precision). Near the edge, atmospheric refraction is eating your precision, and the geometry doesn't help you either. It's like trying to aim at something when your angle is really shallow vs. when it's straight up -- the straight-up shot is way easier to be precise with.
that's actually sick. so what if I'm way out on the far side of Qaia? can I even see Primus?

Nope, you're out of luck there. Primus is permanently below the horizon for the entire far-side hemisphere. Your character would have to use dead reckoning, stars, or lunar-distance methods with one of the other moons -- way harder. That's actually a big asymmetry in how navigation works on Qaia.

So my character's from Forthyr, and he's got this whole thing about the Dragon Emperors coming back. I'm curious -- how bad did the whole Ryraxian War actually mess with that? Like, did people think Ryraxos was gonna be the guy?

Good question. The war itself was wild -- bookmakers in Kard literally set the over/under on how long it'd last at sixty days. Spoiler: it went thirty years instead. So yeah, a lot of hope and chaos and shifting allegiances.

But here's the thing about Ryraxos -- he's a bronze dragon, relatively young (maybe sixty), and he's not one of the old Dragon Emperors. He did save Edrad from defeat when things looked impossible, which made him incredibly powerful for a time. He waged aggressive war across the continent for like two and a half decades. But he's not a resurrected emperor or part of the old Draconic empire -- he's just a powerful dragon who rose to power as an artillery officer and took over.

So for your character's sect in Forthyr, Ryraxos would be... complicated? He's a dragon, which is thematically aligned with what they want. But he's not what they're actually looking for.
Oh huh, so he's just a random powerful dragon, not like, connected to the old empire at all?

Right. And actually, he's no longer in the picture anyway -- after his defeat, he accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon. So he won't play any role in the current campaign.

Forthyr's Dragon Emperor revival sect gets tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty itself, so there's definitely institutional backing for that ideology. But what they're actually reviving is the *Draconic empire system* -- the old institutions, the sorcerer-kings, that whole structure. Ryraxos was never part of that; he was just a powerful individual dragon who showed up and took over during the chaos of the war.
Okay cool, that actually makes sense for my character's motivations then. So the sect is more about wanting the return of the old system rather than just any dragon taking charge.

Exactly. The sect is looking for the *restoration* of something that used to exist -- the Draconic empire institutions, the dragon-led government structure. Ryraxos showed that a dragon could be militarily powerful and reshape the continent, but he wasn't restoring anything; he was just doing his own thing. So yeah, that distinction matters for how your character might see things, especially now that the war's over and the Keyberos dynasty is back in power in Edrad.
so if i'm rolling a gondolier-guild character, what's the actual leverage there? like mechanically, is it just theme or does the guild actually have power?

Way more than flavor. The Gondolier Guild runs maintenance and control of the two massive bridges that cross the central sea -- the ones that form the wizard-made isthmus connecting Qaringil to the mainland. That's critical infrastructure. You're talking about the main physical chokepoint for overland trade between the city and the continent.
ohhh okay so they're basically logistics/infrastructure lords, not merchant lords like the shipping guild. that's a totally different power axis

Exactly. And here's the kicker: the Gondolier Guild has never been led by any of the Five Families, unlike every other major guild. So you've got this one critical infrastructure guild sitting totally outside the family power structure. Everyone needs them, but nobody owns them.
that's such a good pressure point for a PC. okay so if my character is mid-level in the guild and someone from, like, House Peixota comes asking favors...

Your character's in an awkward position immediately. Peixota leads the Shipping Guild and has heavy interests in Mercantile and the courts -- they absolutely care about bridge tolls and access. But if your character starts cutting side deals with one family, the other four notice. The guild's whole power comes from being neutral and indispensable. Play it right and you're a resource everyone courts. Play it wrong and you're a liability that got compromised.
love that. so the gondoliers are the only guild power base that doesn't have a built-in family handler. what's internal gondolier politics like? is there some faction thing?

The notes don't fill that in -- it's a blank space. But that's actually perfect for your backstory. The DM hasn't specified internal gondolier factions, leadership structure, any guild politics beyond the fact that they stay independent. You've got room to propose something or just leave it open for the DM to hook into whatever plot they're building.
okay so I keep forgetting -- didn't the bookmakers have this war pegged to end in like two months or something? and then it just... didn't?

Yeah, exactly. Bookmakers in Kard set the over/under at sixty days. The thinking was probably that once Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire marched on Edrad to restore the Keyberos dynasty and depose the Revolutionary Council, it'd be a quick campaign. Instead the whole thing went for thirty years.
holy shit. so like... what actually happened that made it drag on that long?

The notes don't really get into the specifics of how it all played out, but the broad strokes are: Ryraxos, a bronze dragon, initially saved Edrad from what looked like certain defeat. But then instead of things settling, he went on to wage aggressive war across the continent for about two and a half decades. That's basically the meat of the thirty years right there. The war ended with Ryraxos defeated and exiled to Secundus -- so he's not around anymore for the current campaign. Everything got put back to how it was before the war, except... everything's different now.
okay so I just read that Jia is the most culturally influential city in Qamis -- is that as big as it sounds??

YES, it absolutely is! Jia is the capital of Edrad, which is historically the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis. It sits on a peninsula jutting out into the Central Sea, and it's ruled by the half-elven Keyberos dynasty -- they've held power for four centuries. So as the heart of Edrad, Jia's basically the cultural center of gravity for the whole continent.
wait, the nobility is half-elven?? that's such a cool detail, i didn't expect that

RIGHT? Edran nobility is required to be between one quarter and three quarters elf. So the ruling class of the most culturally influential city has this specific half-elven identity baked in -- that's definitely part of what makes Jia distinctive. Four hundred years of the Keyberos dynasty ruling from a peninsula capital with that cultural character... yeah, that's huge.

hey, so for my Edran noble character — I'm trying to understand the whole dynastic thing, calendars, omens, that kind of stuff. What's the deal with Sextus and Septimus? Are those just random names or do they matter?

Good question. They're the outer two of Qaia's five freely-orbiting moons. Sextus has a period of about 57.69 days, and Septimus is longer at 106.22 days. They're physically identical — both small (about 374 km radius), rocky, and dim in the sky. Sextus is the brighter of the two at magnitude −8.0, but still dramatically fainter than the inner three moons. Septimus, at −7.14, is even dimmer. They rise in the west like Secundus — retrograde orbits.
okay, so dim outer moons. Do they have any cultural significance? Like, does Edran nobility use them for calendar things or... I dunno, astrology? Omens?

That's a blank space in the notes, honestly. The campaign docs don't cover the astrological or religious meanings of the moons — or even whether Edran culture attributes any special significance to Sextus and Septimus specifically. We know Tertius and Quartus drive the main calendrical cycles (Tertius is the basis for the roughly 8-day week), but the cultural lore around the outer moons isn't filled in yet. That could be a fun thing to develop for your character's backstory, though.
hmm. and like, are there any names for them? Or do people just call them Sextus and Septimus?

The notes use those ordinal names (Primus through Septimus) but don't specify what Qaians actually call them in the setting languages — whether there are poetic names, whether Edrans have different names than other cultures, etc. Blank space again. You could definitely invent something for your character.

fair. one more thing — the ~106 day period for Septimus, the notes mention that makes it useful for a long calendar. Who's actually using that? Like, does Edrad or other nations have a formal calendar based on it?

The notes mention that Septimus's ~106-day period "makes it a useful long-calendar body," but they don't go further — no detail on which nations use which moons for official calendars, or how formalized any of this is. The campaign docs are pretty sparse on calendrical systems. That's another area where you've got room to build.
where's jia, the edrad capital?
Jia sits on a peninsula jutting out into the Central Sea. It's the capital of Edrad and the most culturally influential city in Qamis. Ruled by the half-elven Keyberos nobility.
cool -- do the notes give me exact coordinates? i'm trying to work through primsine from different positions and jia seems like a natural test case

They don't, actually. Jia gets described geographically (peninsula, Central Sea) but no exact latitude/longitude. So you can estimate based on that geography, but you can't calculate precisely from primsine.
wait, even for a major city?

Yep. The campaign notes explicitly call out "blank spaces" -- details the DM hasn't worked out yet. Jia's exact position is one of them. You've got the geography narrowing things down (peninsula in the Central Sea), but the precise coordinates are open. Could actually be a good hook for your character's backstory, or something you and the DM flesh out together.
so quick question while I'm working through the primsine tables—what color is Ryraxos?

Bronze! And this is the fun part: he's only maybe sixty years old. Everyone assumes "dragon overlord" means ancient and apocalyptic, but Ryraxos is actually *dangerous as a commander*. The notes say a well-organized adventuring party could've taken him.
WAIT so the dragon that waged a 30-year war is something a party could've 1v5'd??

Yes! He got his power base from being an artillery officer—dragons are phenomenal at that because they can literally carry the guns. So his whole empire was built on logistics and tactics, not raw draconic magic. That's actually way more terrifying in a war context.
okay but if he's exiled to Secundus, doesn't the moon choice matter? like Secundus is retrograde with a completely different tidal signature

That's a really smart connection—you're thinking like a navigator already! But the notes don't explain why Secundus specifically. Could be meaningful, could be arbitrary. Definitely a blank space worth wondering about though.

I'm reading about Qaringil and noticed something. The campaign notes say the Islas dialect there has 'heavier than usual Qis vocabulary' because the city was founded by adventurers and laborers from Qismos. But... isn't that kind of superficial? Does that actually change anything about how you'd experience the city?

It could, depending on what level you're engaging with it. The notes give you the cause -- Qismos settlers -- and the effect -- Qis vocabulary is thicker than elsewhere. But they don't spell out what that concretely sounds or feels like: whether it's loanwords, grammar patterns, accent shifts, or all three. So as a reader, it's a signal that Qaringil's culture has real roots in Qismos, not just passing trade. As a player, it's a detail your character would know about their own city. Whether it's 'superficial' depends on how much weight you want to give it.
Okay, but doesn't the vagueness kind of undercut the point? Like, if you can't actually pinpoint *what's* different about the dialect, is it really a notable detail or just flavor text?

Fair question. The notes don't give you the specific flavor -- which words, which grammatical quirks, which phonetic shifts are actually Qis imports versus just Islas. That's genuinely left as a blank space for the DM to develop (or for a player to invent for their character). But the fact that the cause is specified -- founded by Qismos folks -- means the detail isn't just cosmetic. It tells you something real about Qaringil's history and cultural allegiance. The vagueness is actually intentional. It's enough to make the setting cohere logically without over-specifying every variation of every regional dialect.

That's... actually a fair point. So someone from the capital who speaks standard Islas would pick up on something off about Qaringil speech, but wouldn't necessarily know *why* until someone explained the backstory?

Yeah, exactly. Or they might hear the Qis terms and just place it immediately if they're educated enough to recognize the connection. The detail lives in the setting logic even if the DM hasn't written out the exact phonetic blueprint. It's the kind of thing that makes the world feel like it has history baked in rather than just being described from above.
So I'm reading about Rathakos and there's this Grand Council deliberating on something -- what's that about?

Good question! Rathakos was just declared a Princedom after the wars, but they don't have a Prince yet. The Grand Council is deliberating on who should become the monarch -- basically they're trying to figure out who can govern. Meanwhile, a regent named John Dunne is temporarily running things. The challenge is that Rathakos has a lot of internal problems right now: unrest in the Southern Reaches (which were annexed from Edrad and whose population isn't happy about that) and communities in the mountains that still follow the old dragon cults. So the Council is looking for someone who can actually handle all that.

okay so you've got this sorcerer-king guy in Forthyr called 'Dragoncaller Shanlong VI' -- that's a lot of title for one guy. What's the deal there? Is this like a Forgotten Realms knockoff or something?

Not knockoff, just homebrew. Dragoncaller is both a title and the dynasty name -- Shanlong VI is the sixth ruler in that line of sorcerer-kings. The key detail: the bloodline used to be dragonborn, but it's thinned down to just humans with sorcerous power. It's native to the setting, not imported.
Alright, so bloodlines thinning -- sounds like there's a story there. How does that actually work? And when did this dynasty start? The 'VI' makes it sound like a long timeline.

The notes don't specify the mechanics of how or why the bloodline thinned, or when the dynasty was founded. What we know: 200 years ago in Qaringil, Forthyr was ruled by a half-dragonborn sorcerer-king, Dragoncaller Shanlong I. Now it's Shanlong VI -- so roughly six rulers over two centuries, maybe one every 30 years. But the exact genealogy, whether intermarriage happened, whether there was magic involved -- that's all blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
Got it. So what about this Dragon Emperor cult thing? Is that like a threat Forthyr deals with, or does the crown actually want that?

There's a prominent religious sect in Forthyr calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, and the Dragoncaller dynasty gives it 'tacit encouragement, if not support' -- meaning they're not openly backing it, but they're not crushing it either. The implication is that the dynasty's goals align with dragon rule in some way. But the notes don't specify what the sect actually believes, how large it is, or what the political reality looks like on the ground.
So Forthyr is basically run by a guy maybe hedging on bringing dragons back. That's... actually a blank space I could see working as a campaign hook.

Exactly. The DM left room to fill in -- What does the sect want? Is Shanlong VI an actual believer or just managing factions? Are there dragons alive somewhere waiting to return, or is it pure ideology? Why is Forthyr 'backwards' in the first place? All of those could become plot threads or character backstory.
Working through some primsine calculations and I need to nail down Edrad's capital. What's the geog on Jia?

Jia's the capital of Edrad -- sits on a peninsula jutting out into the Central Sea. It's the most culturally influential city in all of Qamis, so it's a natural navigation hub. What era are you working in? And do you have a starting point in mind, or are you just mapping reference locations?
Present day, Qamis. I was thinking of running a route from Qeth across to Jia to work through the formula. Should be good practice.

Hold on -- in present-day Qamis, Qeth actually isn't part of Edrad. After the Ryraxian War ended, Edrad's borders got pulled back to their historical lines. Back in the Qaringil era, about 200 years ago, conquering Qeth and that region northeast of it was a major goal of Edran foreign policy, but they hadn't managed it yet. Are you thinking of the earlier period, or do you want to pick a different starting point in present-day Edrad?
Ah, I had those eras mixed. Okay, so present-day Qeth is off-limits. But Jia itself -- it's definitely coastal on that peninsula, right? That should give decent clear skies for sighting Primus.

Exactly. Jia's right on the coast, peninsula into the Central Sea, so you'd have open sky and stable horizon for taking your elevation readings. Pick any Edrad inland city as your start point and you're good to go.
okay so the DM keeps saying 'blank spaces' like I'm supposed to know what that means. What are they actually talking about?

The DM has explicitly left parts of the Qamis setting unfinished and calls them out directly in the campaign notes. Like, the Qamis document literally says "There are a lot of blank spaces on the map that need to be filled. You can help with that." It's not accidental -- they're telling you the gaps exist and that you could fill them with your backstory if you want.
wait, that's... intentional? I thought the DM just hadn't finished writing yet. You're telling me leaving stuff out is a feature?

Yeah, it's a feature. The DM is being explicit about it. Like, in the Qaringil notes it says the same thing -- blank spaces you can fill in. And then there's specific text like "Westron" where it literally says "The continent to the west. It's exotic or some shit, I guess. It is very large." That's not an oversight, it's a placeholder the DM left for world-building material. Same with the big island to the south -- mostly unexplored, and the notes say "Some say this is where the dragons went" but don't actually fill in what's there. The DM is inviting you to develop that.
huh. okay, but like... is there a full list of what's actually not filled in? Because I could work with that as backstory material.

Yeah, there are several explicit ones. In present-day Qamis: most of Westron is blank; the big island to the south beyond Freeport is mostly unexplored; and the Qamis map has some areas marked as ???. In the Qaringil prequel, similar story -- blank spaces marked for filling in. The DM's pretty clear that these are hooks for backstory if you want to use them.

wait, so you're saying *multiple documents* have the DM saying this? That's... yeah, that's definitely intentional then. I was worried the worldbuilding was half-baked but it sounds like the DM knows exactly what they're doing.

Right. The Qamis and Qaringil campaign notes both open with the same line about blank spaces. It's a deliberate design choice -- the DM built out the major nations, the navigation system, the moon physics, the two different eras, and then left room for you to contribute. Given that you're a navigation nerd, you could probably have fun with some of the underdeveloped territory, especially if it intersects with how Primus visibility works or changes exploration patterns.

oh damn, that's actually a good point. Primus is visible from one hemisphere... that means the far side is basically cut off from his longitude method, yeah? So blank spaces on the far side would have totally different navigational constraints than something in the Primus-visible zone.

Exactly. Navigators on the far side of Qaia can't see Primus, so they're stuck with dead reckoning or stellar methods -- way harder. The notes specifically mention that as an asymmetry: "Navigation there requires dead reckoning, stellar transit observations, or lunar-distance methods (using Secundus or Quartus against a star catalogue) -- a substantially harder problem." So if you're filling in blank spaces on the far side, you'd have a totally different exploration dynamic than somewhere Primus-visible.
hey! so I'm reading about Qaringil and I'm seeing something about CRIMINAL GUILDS styled as actual guilds and I need to know more

oh this is such a cool detail!! So in Qaringil there are legitimate guilds (Shipping, Silk, Mercantile, Banking, Judicial, Wool, Gondolier) that basically run the city, and then there are competing underground criminal organizations that *style themselves as guilds* too. That's the setup. The three that are called out are: Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses.
wait so they actually use the guild structure? like they have ranks and everything?

That's the thing -- the notes say they style themselves as guilds, which is such an evocative detail, but the specifics of how they're actually organized, their internal structure, how they compete with each other, who runs them -- that's all blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. It's a really interesting setup though, right? Like you've got this city where legitimate merchant and professional guilds have formal power, and then shadowy criminal versions operating in parallel.
Do they operate differently in different parts of the city or are they all mixed together?

Also a blank! The notes don't specify territorial control, how they divvy up the city, or where their operations are strongest. That could be a fun hook for a campaign or backstory though -- like, maybe different families or districts have different criminal guild allegiances, or maybe the guild structures are literally mirrored somehow.

so i'm making a character from Rathakos and i was wondering -- would they speak a noticeably different dialect than like, someone from Ath'r or Jia? i want to play up the accent or speech patterns or whatever

Yeah, absolutely. The notes explicitly call out that national languages in Qamis aren't standardized -- they have major regional variations. The example given is that most speakers from the Artkos capital would have trouble understanding Artklan as spoken in Rathakos. So your Rathakos character would definitely have a recognizable dialect.

For character creation you'd assume you speak the capital dialect (prestige Artklan from Ath'r) plus one regional dialect -- so you could pick Rathakos Artklan as your regional one and play it as your native speech.
ok cool, but like... what *is* the Rathakos dialect actually like? are there common phrases, does it drop consonants, is it faster or slower, any words that are different?

That's where I hit a blank space. The notes don't go into those specifics -- they establish that regional variation is real and significant (hence the capital speakers struggling to understand Rathakos), but they don't detail what the actual differences are.

Though here's something that might be relevant to your character: Rathakos has a complicated history. It was part of the Red Mountain Republic during the war, with internal unrest in the Southern Reaches (annexed from Edrad) and mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. That kind of regional and political fracture might suggest the dialect has some patchiness or mixed influences -- but that's me inferring from context, not from the notes.
ah damn. so the DM just hasn't written that out yet?

Right. The DM's literally marked language variation as a blank space you can help fill in with your backstory if you want. That's the deal with Qamis -- there are a lot of intentional gaps. You could work with your DM to nail down what your character's speech sounds like, and it might become part of the wider Rathakos dialect going forward.
hey what's the deal with half-elves in edrad

So the Keyberos dynasty has ruled Edrad for four hundred years, and the whole nobility is half-elven. That's been the case the whole time. The capital is Jia, sits on a peninsula in the Central Sea, really culturally influential place.

wait the ENTIRE nobility is half-elf? like, that's a lot of moving pieces. does the setting really need a whole hereditary half-elf ruling class? feels like you're adding complexity for complexity's sake

I get the skepticism, but it's actually pretty elegant. The rule is: Edran nobility has to be between one quarter and three quarters elf. You can't be full human or full elf, and you can't be too extreme in either direction.

okay so... you HAVE to have elf blood but you also can't be a full elf? okay that's actually interesting. so what stops a noble family from breeding with humans until they're almost human, or with elves until they're almost elves?

That's the constraint! You CAN'T. If your kid ends up outside that 1/4 to 3/4 range, they're ineligible for power. So any noble house making marriage alliances has to think really carefully about bloodlines and heritages. You can't just marry whoever you want.
ohhh okay yeah that actually creates real political drama. succession crises, contested legitimacy, forbidden marriages. that's genuinely good.
EXACTLY! It's such a clean rule for generating conflict. It's not random flavor, it actually makes political mechanics work differently than a typical fantasy kingdom.
quick question -- is Qamis the continent or a city? I'm trying to get my head around the geography here

Qamis is the continent itself. It's got a bunch of major nations and regions on it -- Edrad, the Artklan League (which includes Artkos, Rathakos, Acrokos, and Lock), Qis'mos, and Forthyr in the northeast. There's also some island city-states in the central sea, Westron is the continent to the west across the sea, and a big unexplored island to the south where Freeport sits.
interesting. so I keep seeing place names with Q in them -- Qamis, Qis'mos, Qama. How are these actually pronounced? Is it like an English Q?

Nope! Q is pronounced 'Kh' -- kind of a guttural sound. It comes from Old Draconic, which is also called Old Qis. That 'Q without u' pattern in place names is straight from Old Draconic spelling. It was the scholarly and diplomatic language across the continent back in the day, so those formal names stuck even as spoken languages evolved.
so Old Draconic was really influential? I'm curious why it stuck around in place names and formal writing but not in everyday speech. Does that actually happen in real language history?

Totally realistic pattern. Old Draconic persisted longest in institutions -- it became the language of treaties, scholarly work, and official names. Even though people now speak Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, or Islas in daily life, the old formal names never got updated. It's like how English still uses Latin for scientific terms or keeps French legal vocabulary even though nobody speaks Vulgar Latin or Middle French anymore. Qis'mos in particular kept those old institutions alive longer than anywhere else, so the connection stayed strongest there.
so modern Qis is descended from Old Qis then? Is that why the spelling and pronunciation stuck, or did the language actually retain Old Draconic features?

The notes tell us that modern Qis is the most similar of all the current languages to Old Qis -- which makes sense, since the Qis'mos region was the cultural heartland where those old institutions were strongest. But whether modern Qis is a direct descendant or whether speakers just refused to do a spelling reform on formal language, the notes don't specify. Could be both -- a language that retained more of the old grammar and phonetics plus scribes who refused to update place names. That's the kind of detail that would be fun to flesh out in actual play.
hey, what's the criminal underworld like in Qaringil? coming from FR and Greyhawk I'm curious how your setting does organized crime

Good question. Qaringil's a commercial republic with legit guilds controlling most of the economy -- Shipping, Mercantile, Banking, all the big ones -- so there's also an underground scene. But honestly, it's pretty sparse in the notes right now. There are three criminal organizations that style themselves as guilds: Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses. They're competing with each other but the DM hasn't fleshed out how they operate, their structure, territories, rivalries, any of that. It's marked as a blank space.
ah so they literally just call themselves guilds to mimic the legit ones? that's actually pretty smart

Yeah, that's the vibe -- they're aping the guild structure as cover or legitimacy. Whether that's tongue-in-cheek, actually fooling anyone, or something else entirely is left open. The DM explicitly said there's room for backstory stuff to fill in the blank spaces, so if you've got ideas about how crime works in a city like that, you could probably work it into your character.
cool. are there any other guilds that are worth knowing about?

For the legit ones? Seven major guilds: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. The Five Families run four of those and have fingers in everything else. There are also smaller guilds relevant if you're an adventurer type -- Adventurer, Alchemist, Mage. At fifth level you'd be a full member of any of those pretty easily. The notes mention there are many minor guilds too, but they're not detailed.
who runs artkos these days?

High King Ezevir Arth. He's directly in charge and pretty fixated on one goal: getting Artkos back to where it used to be. The empire controlled about two-fifths of the continent historically, but the Ryraxian War knocked it down hard. Now he's looking at the rest of the Artklan League as something he can bring back under control.
So what does he actually want to take over?

Mostly the central territories. His historic heartland is West Artkos in the northwest, but he also controls exclaves including Ath'r, the capital, which sits in the center. The real prize is Kard in the southwest -- major shipping and finance hub. Except there's a catch: most people there speak Edran and actually welcomed the Edran invasion twenty-eight years ago. So he's got a valuable city that actively doesn't want to be under his rule.
That's... not ideal for empire building. Is he stuck, or does he have a move?

The notes don't detail a specific strategy, which is telling. The problem is structural. Rathakos, the border state between Artkos and Edrad, is currently fragmented -- a regent's in charge while a Grand Council looks for a prince. So Ezevir doesn't have a stable buffer zone. Kard sits exposed between his territory and Edrad, with no reliable support behind it. It's a weak position.

Wait, let me think about this in terms of navigation. Kard's a shipping hub, and you mentioned Primus gives massive navigational advantages on the near side. Wouldn't control of Kard's harbor mean Ezevir controls key maritime knowledge?

Good instinct, but not quite. Primus-based navigation is crucial for maritime trade on the near side -- any captain with basic instruments and primsine tables can find their longitude. Kard would definitely have skilled navigators. But the knowledge isn't proprietary. All Qaian maritime powers understand how to use Primus. Kard's real power is physical infrastructure: harbors, shipyards, banking networks, mercenary fleets. Knowledge spreads; good facilities don't.
Okay, but doesn't the hemisphere asymmetry give Ezevir a latent advantage? I mean, Primus is only visible on one half of Qaia. Isn't that a structural power imbalance he could exploit?

You're thinking strategically, which I like. But the notes don't actually specify whether Qamis is on the Primus-visible hemisphere or the far side. The campaign's set on Qamis and involves maritime powers, so it seems to assume Primus navigation is relevant -- but it's not spelled out explicitly. Even if Qamis is near-side, the real constraints on Ezevir aren't astronomical. They're political: Edrad is still the dominant power, Rathakos won't fall into line, and Kard's population resists Artkosian rule. Celestial mechanics don't fix those problems.
So what's the actual path to power for Ezevir? Like, what would have to happen for Artkos to actually dominate the League again?

That's where it gets interesting because the notes don't give you a clear roadmap. He'd need to either convince Kard's Edran-speaking population to switch loyalty, stabilize Rathakos by supporting whoever becomes prince there, or prevent Edrad from interfering while he's weak. The notes mention that 'The high kings of Artkos see in the new Artklan League a pale shadow of their old empire while their nominal subjects cling to a tenuous quasi-independence' -- so his bet seems to be that the League members are too divided to resist. Whether that holds up is an open question. There's room there for actual gameplay to determine whether his dominance returns or falls apart.
hey, so I've been reading about Qaia's moon system and I'm confused about something. the notes say Tertius defines an 8-day week, but like... why Tertius and not one of the other moons?

Good question. Tertius orbits every 8.15 days, which is why it anchors the week. Think about it: it's long enough to be meaningful (unlike a daily cycle), but short enough to subdivide the month nicely. Quartus, the big bright moon comparable to Earth's Luna, has a 29.5-day period - that's the month. So Tertius at about 8 days naturally divides the month into roughly four weeks. It's also bright enough to track easily (magnitude −12.60, nearly as bright as Quartus), and it's prograde, so it moves predictably west-to-east across the sky. It's the obvious choice for a calendar body.
Okay, so it's convenient - I get that. But wait, what about Secundus? That one's closer, and it's got like, a 4.5-day period? Why isn't that the week instead?

Secundus orbits every 4.53 days - too short to be practical as a week anchor. If you tied the week to Secundus, it would cycle through its phases way too fast; you'd get almost two full Secundus weeks in a single Tertius week. Tertius at 8.15 days hits the goldilocks zone: long enough that people can actually plan around it, but still short enough to subdivide the longer Quartus month. Plus Secundus is retrograde and has an eccentricity of about 0.19, so its brightness varies noticeably as it swings between periapsis and apoapsis. Tertius is way more stable for timekeeping.

That makes sense from a calendar perspective. But I'm also wondering about the tidal angle - Tertius has a 2.04x tidal force compared to Earth's Luna. Does that mean the 8-day week actually corresponds to observable tidal cycles? Like, is what people see in the sky lined up with the actual physical tides?

Exactly - you're hitting on something really deep. Tertius contributes 2.04 times Earth's lunar tidal force, and combined with Secundus (1.47x) and Quartus (0.87x), the three inner moons drive tidal forces roughly 4.4 times stronger than what Earth experiences. Because Tertius orbits every 8.15 days, the tidal bulges created by that moon cycle across Qaia with that same rhythm. So when a Qaian observer watches Tertius march through the sky every 8 days, they're literally tracking the major tidal cycle happening in the oceans below. The visible week and the physical tides are the same phenomenon - you can't separate them. It's why the 8-day week works so well: it's grounded in actual physics that people experience daily.

hi! i've been reading the navigation guide and i'm confused about something. the notes say primsine is analogous to haversine, but i don't really get why. they look like completely different formulas.

Good question! The analogy isn't about the specific math -- it's about what they *do*. Haversine reduces a spherical geometry problem (finding distance between two points on a sphere using the law of cosines) down to a function of a single variable. You plug in one angle and get a result, and that's it -- no extra calculation needed. Primsine does the same thing for Primus navigation. Instead of wrestling with the full spherical law of cosines (which has multiple variables), you collapse the problem into a function of just elevation e. One lookup, one result.
okay, so they're both designed to be easy to table? but why not just... use the full equation with a 2D table or something? why does it matter that it's a single variable?

Space and practicality. A 1D table fits in a ship's almanac. Primsine with 0.05° resolution (matching sextant precision) is roughly 3,600 entries -- totally reasonable. A full 2D table mapping (elevation, latitude) to longitude would be orders of magnitude larger and much slower to use at sea. Plus you don't even need to know latitude to calculate primsine -- you look it up first, then divide by cos φ. The single-variable function lets navigators solve longitude with three quick table lookups and one division instead of consulting a massive 2D grid.
that makes sense from a practical angle. but mathematically, what's the magic? the primsine formula has ρ cos²e plus a square root term. is that just... what falls out of the geometry?

Exactly. It falls out of the geometry. You start with the spherical law of cosines: cos λ = cos φ × cos ψ, where ψ is the geocentric angular distance from you to the sub-Primus point. The key insight is that ψ depends on your elevation angle e to Primus, and that relationship includes both a direct geometric component (the ρ cos²e term, which accounts for the radius ratio and projection) and a correction term (the square root) that accounts for the curvature of the sphere. Primsine *is* that expression for cos ψ as a function of e. It's not arbitrary -- it's the inevitable result of projecting a fixed point on the equator onto an observer's local horizon.

ohh, i think i get it now. so primsine(90°) = 1 because if Primus is directly overhead, you're *at* the sub-Primus point, and cos(0°) = 1. and at the horizon, primsine(0°) = ρ because... you're at the visibility boundary?

Perfect. You've got it. When Primus is directly overhead (e = 90°), the angular distance to the sub-Primus point is zero, so cos ψ = 1. At the horizon (e = 0°), you're at the maximum geocentric angle where Primus is visible, which is arccos(ρ) ≈ 81.3° -- and primsine(0°) = ρ is exactly cos(81.3°). Those boundary conditions are built right into the formula. The whole thing is a elegant way of encoding the projection geometry into a single function so navigators can solve for longitude just by measuring elevation and looking up a table.
so im trying to figure out what guild my character should be part of. are the five families like... controlling everything? bc that sounds limiting lol

Good question -- they're powerful but not *everything*. The Five Families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, Vitelli) do run four of the seven major guilds, plus they're involved in a bunch of other stuff. But there are some guilds that have stayed independent of them.

And Peixota didn't stop there -- they've also got leverage in Mercantile and Judicial, so they can influence what gets traded and how disputes shake out. That's institutional dominance.

If you want to learn primsine tables, understand the harbor masters' charts, study how working navigators solve for longitude on the Central Sea, you're going to Peixota connections. They have the patronage networks, the experienced captains, the accumulated knowledge from centuries of shipping routes. And with Primus visible from the near hemisphere, Qaringil navigators have a real advantage -- they can use that longitude fix in a way far-side navigators can't. That's potentially a core part of Peixota's power.

ok so my friend won't stop talking about this world and apparently the MOONS move in different directions?? is that actually a thing in real astronomy or is your DM just making it up

YES okay so this is so cool -- both things are true! Real astronomy has retrograde moons (way less common, but they exist), and the DM designed this specific system with it. So on Qaia you've got three moons moving west-to-east normally -- those are prograde. That's Tertius, Quartus, and Primus. But then you've got THREE MORE that go backwards -- Secundus, Sextus, Septimus are all retrograde, rising in the west and sweeping across the sky east-to-west. So you're standing there at night and half the moons are doing the normal thing and half are going the weird way.
wait wait wait -- there are SIX moons?? and they're all doing different things?? how does that not look insane

I KNOW RIGHT?? But here's the thing -- they're not all visible at once and they're not all the same brightness. The three you'd notice most are Tertius, Quartus, and Secundus -- Tertius and Quartus are huge in the sky, like just under the size of Earth's moon. Secundus is almost as big but darker because it's made of iron. Then Primus is a tiny fixed point that never moves (it's geostationary), and the other two (Sextus and Septimus) are way out and pretty dim. So the night sky isn't chaos, it's actually this weird elegant thing where you've got a few bright moving moons and one tiny fixed anchor point.
primus never moves though? how is that even possible

Okay so Primus is magically anchored -- the DM notes specifically say that. It orbits at exactly the right distance and speed so it stays fixed over one point on the surface (the equator at the prime meridian). From one hemisphere you see it hanging in the exact same spot forever -- it never rises, never sets, never drifts. Which is WILD because then navigators can use it to figure out their longitude just by measuring its angle above the horizon. No chronometer needed.
so the moons are navigation tools?? that's such a cool detail oh my god

RIGHT?! Primus is specifically designed for that. The whole point is that it fixes longitude without needing a clock -- which was actually Earth's huge navigation problem for centuries. But on Qaia? Solved, at least if you're on the right hemisphere. The people on the far side of the planet can't see Primus at all so they're out of luck. That kind of asymmetry in a world is SO good for worldbuilding.
so what do those outer moons actually look like? sextus and septimus?

Quick version: they're small but genuinely visible to the naked eye. Sextus looks like a tiny disc, about 4 arcminutes across (roughly 5x the apparent size of Jupiter), and shows up at magnitude −8, so it's dramatically brighter than any planet but nowhere near as bright as a full moon. Septimus is a bit smaller and dimmer — about 2.7 arcminutes and magnitude −7 — because it's farther out. Both are retrograde, so they rise in the west and move west to east, opposite to the inner moons. Sextus has a roughly 58-day period, Septimus about 106 days, so Septimus is useful as a long-calendar marker. In practice they're clear navigational landmarks — brighter than any star, obviously not planets, obviously not the main show.
wait, retrograde means they're actually moving backwards?

Exactly. Most of Qaia's moons orbit west-to-east with the planet's rotation. Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus orbit backwards — east-to-west. So you see them rise in the west, move across the sky backwards, set in the east. It's weird but stable — retrograde orbits don't decay as long as they stay beyond about 70% of the Hill sphere, which these do.

Hey! So I'm reading up on Qaia's moon system and I'm seeing retrograde and prograde thrown around. In Forgotten Realms, Selûne is just one moon, and Greyhawk's moons are kind of in the background. Does this retrograde/prograde thing actually matter for Qamis, or is it just astronomical flavor?

It matters quite a bit, actually. So here's the breakdown: you've got five freely-orbiting moons plus Primus, which is magically anchored and geostationary. The prograde ones move west-to-east across the sky like you'd expect -- that's Tertius and Quartus. The retrograde ones -- Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus -- rise in the west and set in the east, moving backward relative to Qaia's rotation. But the really important one is Primus. It hangs motionless in the sky of one hemisphere, never rises, never sets. It's your longitude solution without a chronometer.
Hold on -- Primus is geostationary? That's basically a permanent navigational landmark. But wait, if it never moves in the sky... does that mean half the planet can't see it at all?

Exactly. Primus is fixed at the equator over the prime meridian, so it's only visible from one hemisphere -- roughly within ±81° longitude of that point. The far-side hemisphere can't see it at all. It creates a wild asymmetry: navigators on the near side get easy longitude fixes just by measuring Primus's elevation above the horizon. Navigators on the far side have to use dead reckoning or stellar methods, just like Earth before the chronometer. The notes specifically flag this as a major navigation divide.

That's fascinating and kind of brutal for anyone on the far side. But I'm also curious about the three retrograde ones. If they're all moving in the opposite direction, what does that look like in the actual sky? And with five other moons cycling on different periods... how do cultures even keep track of time?

The three retrograde moons rising in the west create this strange visual: from the near hemisphere, you see Primus stationary overhead, then Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus all sweeping east-to-west, while Tertius and Quartus move the normal way. It's layered and complex. As for timekeeping, Tertius has about an 8-day period and's the basis for the week. Quartus is nearly lunar -- 29.5 days -- so it anchors the month. The moons also create insane tidal complexity: you've got Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all pulling at different frequencies, which means the tides don't follow a simple pattern. And there are these rare triple-alignment eras where all three inner moons reach full or new together, which seems like the kind of thing cultures would build prophecies and traditions around.

Okay, that triple alignment thing is interesting -- so there are calendar epochs where all three hit syzygy simultaneously? That seems like it would be huge religiously or politically. Does the campaign material hint at how much Qaian culture orbits around these alignments? Like, are we talking 'used for divination' level or full-on 'religious calendar structured around them'?

The notes mention that the triple alignments happen roughly five times a year, with the closest ones being essentially exact. There's definitely a precomputed alignment calendar for 2253–2263 in the materials, which suggests it's important enough to track precisely. But honestly, the campaign notes don't fill in how much cultural weight this carries yet. That's one of the blank spaces the DM hasn't detailed. What's clear is that the complexity alone -- three incommensurate cycles overlaid on a geostationary reference -- gives the world a fundamentally different relationship to the sky than single-moon settings. Whether that's baked into religion, prophecy, navigation guilds, or all of it is something your DM can build on.

primus is geostationary -- it orbits in sync with qaia's rotation, so it stays fixed over the equator. it never moves relative to the ground. from the near hemisphere you see it all the time; from the far side it's permanently below the horizon.
