so i've been reading about qaia's moons and noticed these dates keep getting highlighted -- may 24 2262 and feb 13 2253. what's the deal with those? is it like, major eclipse events or something? in forgotten realms the celestial stuff is pretty hand-wavy but it sounds like you guys have actual physics here

Good catch. Those dates are the closest triple-moon alignments in a decade-long cycle. On May 24, 2262 at 03h, all three major moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) hit new phase simultaneously -- within 0.1% of each other, which is about as close as it gets. Feb 13, 2253 is the counterpart: all three full at once, also within 0.2%. They're pretty rare and significant enough that navigators and scholars would definitely mark them. The moons actually have an 8-12 year cycle where full-moon alignments dominate for a stretch, then new-moon alignments take over.

wait, so the moons have actual incommensurate periods like earth's? that's way more complex than most d&d settings. does that affect tide patterns? because in some forgotten realms books i read they kind of handwave the tides

Exactly -- three of the five moons have periods that don't align nicely (Secundus at 4.5 days, Tertius at 8.15 days, Quartus at 29.5 days). Their tidal forces add up to about 4.4 times what Earth's Moon produces, but hitting from different angles at different times. There's also Primus, the geostationary moon, which doesn't create oscillating tides at all -- it just raises a permanent 2cm bulge in the oceans. So yeah, Qaia has genuinely complex tidal dynamics, not handwaved at all.

ok this is nerdy but im fascinated -- the place names have this weird 'Q without U' thing, Qamis, Qaringil, Qis'mos, Qama. the notes say that's Old Draconic. how does that relate to the modern Q/Kh pronunciation thing? like, was there a sound shift in the language family?

Good observation. Q is pronounced 'Kh' (like the German guttural), and that comes from Old Draconic. The notes call it 'Old Draconic (or Old Qis)' -- so the Q spelling comes from how scholars write Old Draconic in treaties and academic publications, and the Kh sound is how modern Qaians pronounce it. It does suggest a historical sound shift, or at least a difference between how Old Draconic was written versus how the modern languages evolved from it. But the notes don't actually detail the linguistic history -- whether it was a gradual shift, when it happened, or how the other languages relate to Old Draconic. That's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.

huh, so you can't tell me like, whether edran or artklan or qis borrowed it differently, or if there was a proto-language situation? because in forgotten realms they at least sketch out draconic as a root language and then you get common and all these offshoots

Right, the notes don't go there. What we know is: there's Old Draconic used for treaties and formal scholarship, there are modern national languages (Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas), those languages have major regional dialects, and the Q/Kh sound comes from Old Draconic. But the historical linguistics -- how they split, which language family each belongs to, whether Old Draconic was literally the dragons' language or just a scholarly convention -- none of that's filled in. Could be something your DM is building as you play, or it could be a hook for character backstory.

gotcha. last thing -- the notes mention Old Draconic is used in treaties. does that mean there's actual Old Draconic *speakers* alive now, or is it purely scholarly? because that would be a pretty different vibe from forgotten realms where draconic is just... a language anyone can learn

The notes only say it's used for scholarly publications and treaties -- so it's at least a written standard that educated people read. Whether anyone speaks it as a living language, or whether it's purely a formal/ceremonial thing, the notes don't say. Given that it's tied to 'Old Draconic' and the actual dragons seem to have mostly departed the world by the time of Qamis, I'd guess it's more like Latin than a living tongue -- but that's inference on my part, not something the setting specifies yet.
so what's with the Dragoncallers - are they actually dragonborn or is that just flavor

They used to be, but not anymore. Dragoncaller Shanlong I, who ruled about two hundred years ago, was half-dragonborn. But over time the bloodlines ran thin, and the current ruler, Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, isn't dragonborn. They kept the title anyway.
so they're basically just sorcerer-kings calling themselves Dragoncaller for the lore, got it

That's the idea. Forthyr is noted as 'somewhat backwards,' and there's a religious sect there pushing for the return of the Dragon Emperors -- which the Dragoncaller dynasty tacitly supports. So the dragon branding probably ties into that, even if the actual bloodline is gone.

hey! so my friend won't shut up about this D&D setting and apparently the sky is like... wild? something about multiple moons? I've been reading fantasy novels forever but never actually played tabletop, so idk if this is normal lol

Yeah, Qaia has a pretty complicated moon system! There are five moons total, and they're all pretty different from each other. The big three that people usually notice are Tertius, Quartus, and Secundus -- they're all pretty large in the sky and drive the tides. But there are also two smaller ones that are kind of weird and interesting.
ooh okay, so what's the deal with the smaller ones? do they even matter in the sky or are they like... I dunno, invisible?

Not invisible, just way more subtle. They're called Sextus and Septimus, and they're both retrograde -- which means they move backwards across the sky compared to the other moons. Sextus is the brighter of the two. It's small, about a fifth the diameter of Earth's moon, but it's bright enough that it's dramatically brighter than any planet in the sky. Like, if you're looking up at night, you'd definitely notice it as a distinct disc, but it's still clearly not one of the main moons. It takes about 58 days to orbit. Septimus is even smaller and dimmer -- it's physically pretty much identical to Sextus, just farther out and takes longer to go around, about 106 days.
wait so if they're retrograde they move like... the opposite direction? how does that work visually?

Right -- so most of the moons rise in the east and set in the west, like you'd expect. But Sextus and Septimus rise in the west and set in the east. So if you're watching the sky over a few nights, you'd see them moving in the opposite direction compared to the main moons. It's definitely a weird effect if you're paying attention to the night sky. And because they're retrograde, they have these higher orbital inclinations too, so their paths across the sky aren't as neat and tidy as the other moons.
that actually sounds really cool for atmosphere honestly. like imagining the sky just has all this complexity going on

Yeah! That's kind of the idea -- Qaia's night sky is pretty chaotic compared to Earth. You've got these five moons all moving on different schedules with different brightnesses, some retrograde, and the whole thing creates this complex tidal system. It actually matters for navigation too -- the big geostationary moon Primus is used to find longitude without a chronometer. But yeah, just from a "what does the sky look like" perspective, Sextus and Septimus add this extra layer of strangeness that makes it feel properly alien.
hey, so I'm familiar with FR and the Underdark drow situation there. What's the deal with the drow in Qamis? Are they similar to the FR setup?

Pretty different, actually. In Qamis, the drow weren't some ancient underworld civilization that conquered the surface -- they were recruited as military allies by Artkos during the Ryraxian War. This was a desperation move when things weren't going well. They fought alongside Artkos forces and, as the war wound down, they just... stayed on the surface. Now they occupy a dozen scattered enclaves, with Qama being the most prominent.
Okay, so they're occupiers rather than native Underdark dwellers. That's interesting. But Qama -- that's the holy city of the Church, right? How'd that work out?

Qama is indeed the holy city of Bahumat. The drow occupied it during the late phases of the war, and the notes say they haven't despoiled it, but they did appoint their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. This created a full schism -- there's an alternate high priest candidate, Qachu II, operating out of Qis with the traditional relics of office, but he has no actual city or control. It's a messy religious-political split.
Wait -- Qama, Qachu, Qis -- lots of Q names here. That's not how you'd transliterate Drow Elvish. What's the linguistic deal? Is Qis like a separate language from the 'Q without U' you mentioned?

Good ear! Q is pronounced 'Kh' -- it comes from Old Draconic (also called Old Qis). Those place names and the language Qis itself all trace back to that Old Draconic root. Qis is the modern national language, and the notes specifically say Qis is the closest to Old Draconic among the modern tongues. Old Draconic is used for scholarly publications and treaties now. So Qama, Qis, and Qachu all have that 'Kh' sound at the front.
Oh interesting -- so the drow didn't bring their own language, they're using names that derive from the dragon empire that used to rule? That actually makes sense if they moved in as occupiers.

Exactly. The notes don't spell out what language the drow speak or whether they've adopted Qis or one of the other national tongues, but you're right -- Qama and the other place names predate their arrival. They're inheriting institutional names and the religious structure that's already there. The setup is definitely more 'occupying power settling into an existing system' rather than 'ancient underground civilization with its own culture.'
yo so quick lore check -- I'm making a navigator PC and trying to figure out the primsine function. What's primsine(0) actually equal?

primsine(0) = ρ, which is roughly 0.1511. That's the value when Primus is sitting right on your horizon -- it marks the edge of Primus visibility. Beyond about 81 degrees of longitude from the prime meridian, Primus drops below the horizon permanently.
Oh so like, there's a hard geographical line where Primus just... stops being usable? That's actually pretty cool.

Exactly. And it creates a real navigation divide on the planet. Navigators on the near side get this elegant, chronometer-free longitude system using Primus elevation. The far-side hemisphere can't see Primus at all, so they had to develop completely separate methods -- dead reckoning, stellar transits, lunar distances using Secundus or Quartus. Basically the same problem Earth solved with the chronometer, but Qaian navigators would've split into two different traditions.

okay wait, this is actually perfect for my PC backstory. What if my navigator learned from someone who trained on the far side? Like, they know the hard classical methods AND the Primus tables. That's way more interesting than just 'I memorized charts.'

That's a solid angle. Someone trained in both traditions would definitely be unusual -- could've happened on a mixed crew, or maybe learned from a far-side captain who settled in a cosmopolitan port. The island city-states especially would draw navigators from everywhere.
Qaringil -- that's the one on the central sea with the bridges, right? What's the navigation scene like there? Would they lean Primus-heavy or develop their own thing?

Qaringil sits on the western shore of the central sea, a thalassocracy of armed merchant fleets. They'd absolutely use Primus when visible -- it's too good a tool to pass up. But as an island city dealing with routes that venture far enough west to lose Primus, they'd also maintain deep knowledge of classical navigation. Your PC could've trained in exactly that environment: Primus as the primary system, but also fluent in the harder methods for when you sail beyond the visibility boundary. That's a credible Qaringil skill set.
Dope. So in play, if I need to navigate far enough west that Primus drops, I've got real methods instead of just failing. Love that for the character arc potential too.

Exactly. Mechanically it gives you options depending on the campaign's scope. Near-side play, you've got the Primus toolkit. Adventure pushes west or toward the far side, you're not suddenly navigation-useless. Plus solid flavor -- your PC isn't a book-learner, they're someone who actually trained the old ways from a mentor who knew both systems.
wait so does the Exarch have like favorite families he's working with? my friend was going on about Qaringil politics and i'm honestly totally lost

Oh yeah, this is actually a cool detail. The current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is from a minor family himself, but he's clearly aligned with two of the big Five Families: Peixota and Manos. Peixota runs the Shipping Guild, so they control the trade routes. Manos runs the Judicial Guild, so they basically control justice in the city. The Exarch's basically working WITH the families that have shipping and law locked down.
okay that's actually so smart though?? he's leveraging their power to actually govern. but like, what about the other families? are they just... mad about it?

Pretty much, yeah. The other three major families--Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli--each have their own power bases. Qanto actually JUST lost control of the Mercantile Guild to a minor gnomish family more aligned with the Exarch, so they're definitely not thrilled. Volmer controls the Banking Guild and Vitelli runs the Silk Guild. So you've got this five-way political dynamic where the Exarch is balancing all of them, but he's clearly closest with Peixota and Manos.
yo, so what level are we starting at in the Qaringil thing?

You're fifth level. That's high enough that you can all easily be full guild members in any of the major guilds in Qaringil, or the minor ones if you want -- Adventurers, Alchemists, Mages, whatever fits your class. So you're not grinding up from zero; you're already established enough to have some real standing in the city.
nice. so what does that actually let us do? like mechanically, what's the diff from level 3 or whatever

Depends on your class, but you've got more powerful spells, better combat chops, maybe class features kicking in. More importantly for Qaringil specifically: you're not newbies. You can hold your own in a real fight, so you're trusted with actual guild work -- not errand-boy stuff. The DM hasn't specified exactly what encounters look like, but being fifth level means you're in a position where people take you seriously as professionals, not just hopefuls.
ok cool. so like, I'm thinking street urchin turned... something. What's the city actually like? Any neighborhoods I should know about?

The notes don't detail specific neighborhoods yet -- that's one of the blank spaces you could help the DM fill in, actually. But here's what you get: Qaringil is criss-crossed with canals, so picture Venice-ish vibes. Population around 100,000. The economy runs on fishing and importation, so waterfront is huge. The city is ruled by five major families -- Peixota (dolphins, shipping), Manos (hand, judicial), Qanto (dove, mercantile), Volmer (crow, banking), Vitelli (moth, silk). There's also Tiddwar, a gnomish family leading the Mercantile Guild. The elites these days are commercial traders, not landowners, which shapes everything.
so if I was a street kid before I leveled up, where would that have been? docks? and who would I be trying to avoid -- family enforcers or something?

Docks makes total sense for a street kid, yeah -- fishing and importation means constant traffic, chaos to hide in. As for who to avoid: there are underground criminal orgs in the city styled as guilds -- Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses. Those would be real problems. The family enforcers could be too, depending on which family's territory you were in and how much they cared. But honestly, the notes don't specify how rough the streets are or what kind of childhood trauma shaped the city's poor. That's yours to invent -- the DM will work with it.
is there like a rough quarter or a rich quarter, or is it all mixed with the canals?

The notes don't map that out yet. That's another blank space. The DM mentions the families control territory and have guild interests spread around, so you could probably justify any geography -- maybe certain families own wharf zones, maybe the banks cluster in one area, maybe poor housing is crammed on the smaller islands. This is actually great material for your backstory workshopping session with the DM. You could say "I was raised in the Peixota docks" or "the Thieves Guild operated out of this neighborhood" and the DM can either affirm it or give you a correction that builds the world.
hey, quick question—how does navigation work differently on Qaia versus Earth? I know there's something special about the moons but I don't want a 30-minute lore dump.

TL;DR: Qaia solved the longitude problem 200 years ago through orbital mechanics. Earth needed a chronometer; Qaia uses geometry and a geostationary moon called Primus. Latitude works the same way (pole star elevation), but longitude is a game-changer: you measure Primus's angle in the sky, plug it into a table, done. No clock required.
wait, one of the moons is geostationary? that's... actually wild. so it just sits over one spot?

Exactly. Primus orbits at 0.11 light-distance (about 42,000 km up—geostationary orbit, if that helps) with a period matching Qaia's 23-hour-56-minute rotation. Its orbital inclination is 23.5°, which exactly cancels Qaia's axial tilt, so it ends up hanging over the equator at a fixed longitude. Never rises, never sets, never drifts. From the near hemisphere, it's always at the same spot in the sky.

okay, that makes sense geometrically. but how does measuring an angle in the sky tell you where you are? Earth's moon moves, so measuring it at different times gives you time zones, but Primus is static...

Right—it's a parallax problem. You measure Primus's elevation above your horizon. That elevation angle, combined with knowing you're at the equator (Primus hangs there), creates a cone of positions at that angle. Intersect that with your latitude (which you already got from the pole star), and you've narrowed down to two possible longitudes. Check which side of your local meridian Primus appears on, and you're done. One function called primsine(elevation) does the heavy lifting—it's precomputed in almanacs as a static lookup table.
so you just need two angle measurements and a table. that's... surprisingly elegant. what's the catch?

Three catches. One: Primus is only visible from one hemisphere—it sits on the far side of Qaia from the far-side hemisphere. Observers there are stuck with dead reckoning, just like pre-chronometer Earth. Two: Primus is small and dim (about a third the apparent size of Earth's moon and notably fainter), so you need clear skies and decent instruments. Three: the visibility boundary is roughly ±81° longitude from the prime meridian, narrowing closer to the poles. So even on the near side, you're not getting a fix if Primus is too low.
got it. so half the world is navigationally disadvantaged, and the other half has a huge advantage. that's gotta have shaped the whole geopolitics and exploration patterns.

Exactly—you're thinking like a DM. The notes don't spell out all the historical implications, but it's a natural consequence. Anyone doing serious maritime trade or exploration from the near hemisphere has a decisive advantage over far-side rivals. The central sea has these island city-states that rely on powerful navies and mercenary work—having accurate navigation would be a major resource for them.

ok so i'm looking at running qaringil as a one-shot for some forgotten realms people. does it feel that different? and like, who are some of the power players i should know about? i was looking at the tiddwar family

It's genuinely different from FR in flavor -- no gods walking around, way heavier on trade guilds and political intrigue, moon system that actually *works* for navigation. And the Qaringil power structure is really mercantile-focused rather than aristocratic.

Tiddwar is a gnomish family that leads the Mercantile Guild. Their sigil is Gears. The interesting part: they're described as "prominent" and aligned with the Exarch, and they recently took control of the Mercantile Guild from the Qanto family, who held it until recently.

But here's the thing -- the notes don't actually detail *how* they rose to power or what their rivalry with Qanto looks like. That's a deliberate blank space the DM left open.
wait, so the DM just... didn't fill that in? that seems incomplete. in FR you've got centuries of lore about these dynasties

Yeah, the DM explicitly calls it out -- there are "a lot of blank spaces on the map that need to be filled." For Qaringil specifically, the Five Families all have basic stats (sigil, guild leadership, other interests), but the *narrative* of how they got there or how they're competing is open ground.

For a one-shot though, that's actually kind of useful? You can either pick a ready-made family drama from the notes (like Manos's hotly-contested entry into the wool trade, or the Qanto-Tiddwar Mercantile handoff), or you can leave it vague and let it stay in the background. Depends on how deep you want to go.
huh. so compared to waterdeep or the sword coast, this is way more like... mercantile republic vibes? like Venice?

Exactly. Qaringil is explicitly a criss-crossed-with-canals city ruled as "an incomprehensible republic," and the power structure is guild-based, not noble houses. Lots of merchant fleets, fishing, imports. The elites are "primarily commercial, not landowners."

It's got the same political intrigue as Waterdeep's guilds, but grounded in actually caring about trade routes and imports instead of just having guilds as flavor. Population is around 100,000, so it's substantial but not a megacity.
ok that's cool. so for a one-shot would you need to know the five families in detail? or is that too much setup for a single session

Too much setup if you're listing all five families and their histories. But you absolutely *could* run a one-shot where one or two families matter -- drop the party into a guild conflict or a specific crew's problem and keep the rest as NPCs they bump into. The Tiddwar-Qanto handoff, or the Manos-wool-guild controversy, either one is a ready-made hook without needing to memorize the whole power structure.

The nice thing: because the full history is blank, you're not fighting against established lore. You can play loose with it.
Hey, so I'm looking at building a cleric or something connected to Bahamut, and I keep seeing this 'primsine' thing in the navigation stuff. Is that a Church of Bahamut concept, or...?

Not explicitly, no — the notes describe primsine as a navigation technique, but don't tie it to the Church of Bahamut specifically. Though it's cool that you're connecting them! So primsine is basically a solution to a really hard problem: how do you find your longitude at sea without a chronometer? Primus, the moon, hangs geostationary over the equator — it never moves. If you measure how high it appears above your horizon, and you know your latitude, you can calculate your east-west position. Primsine is the function that makes the calculation work. Navigators would have lookup tables of primsine values pre-printed so they don't have to do the math every time.

Okay, that's cool. But like — who teaches this? In my head, the Church of Bahamut feels like it should be involved in knowledge preservation and transmission. And with the schism right now in Qis'mos — Qe'lth IV in Qama with the drow, and Qachu II running the rival faction — would that affect access to navigation training or knowledge? Like, does one faction control navigation schools?

That's a really good question, and the notes don't actually cover it. We know the Church of Bahamut has enormous presence in Qis'mos and Qama is their traditional seat. We also know the drow occupied Qama in the late phases of the Ryraxian War and appointed Qe'lth IV as their chosen high priest, while Qachu II leads the rival faction. But the notes are silent on: how religious institutions transmit knowledge, whether the Church formally teaches navigation, whether drow control of Qama has disrupted scholarly traditions, or who's even responsible for passing down navigation knowledge in the first place — guilds, the military, merchant houses, the Church. That's all blank space.

So essentially the notes explain 'here is how primsine works, here is why it's useful,' but the whole institutional side — who knows it, how it spreads, whether the schism has fractured knowledge — that's all DM backstory territory?
Exactly. The mathematics and the utility are all there. The social and institutional context is completely open — great territory for your backstory or DM material.

hey, I saw this thing in your notes about primsine being like haversine - that's for navigation, right? Never seen that comparison before and I'm trying to understand if I could steal the idea for my own world

Yeah, primsine is the Qaia-specific version. Haversine is a navigation formula Earth navigators use to find distances on a sphere; primsine is the analogous formula for finding longitude using Primus. The key similarity: both reduce a complicated spherical geometry problem down to a single function you can precompute and stick in a table. On Earth, haversine lets you look up distances without needing trig. On Qaia, primsine lets you look up longitude without needing a chronometer. The notes have the full derivation and the primsine function: cos²e times a constant, plus sin(e) times a square root. That lets navigators calculate longitude with just elevation measurements and three table lookups.
ok that's clever. but I'm wondering - is this actually *used* by captains and navigators in Qamis campaigns? Like, do they carry primsine tables on their ships, or is it more theoretical?

That's the blank space, honestly. The notes describe the system in detail - how primsine tables would work, different accuracy levels (coarse 1-degree tables vs fine 3-minute ones), the whole procedure - but there's no actual play examples of a navigator pulling out a primsine table during a Qamis campaign. It's framed as something Qaian navigators *would* have, and there's even mention that it's printed in 'the almanac' as if it's standard equipment. But whether PCs ever interact with it, or whether it's just atmospheric detail, that's not in the notes.
hmm, and on the opposite side - you said Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. So half the world can't use it at all?

Exactly. Primus is geostationary over a fixed point on the equator - the prime meridian at 0 degrees. Visible only from the near-side hemisphere within roughly ±81 degrees of longitude (narrower at higher latitudes). The far-side hemisphere never sees it. The notes mention this creates a pretty massive navigational asymmetry - navigators there need dead reckoning, stellar transit observations, or 'lunar-distance methods' using Secundus or Quartus instead. It's explicitly compared to Earth's pre-chronometer longitude problem. But the notes don't actually detail what those alternative methods look like in practice, or how far-side navigation culture developed differently.
that's actually a great hook for worldbuilding. Do the notes say anything about how the far-side cultures react to that disadvantage? Trade routes, cultural attitudes?

Nope, that's blank. The Qamis and Qaringil notes mention various nations and islands, but there's no detail on far-side geography, politics, or how the lack of Primus shaped their development. You could definitely fill that in yourself though - that seems like exactly the kind of thing a DM would want to invent based on the constraint the system gives you.
sup, missed last week -- what's the actual tidal situation on qaia? i remember the dm mentioning something about three moons lining up?

Yep, pretty neat system. Qaia has five freely-orbiting moons plus a geostationary one called Primus that never moves. The three inner ones -- Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus -- are the tidal heavyweights. Together they produce roughly 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's Moon. And yes, they line up: all three come within 5% of full or new about five times per year, and within 1% about once per year. There's actually a whole alignment calendar if you want the exact dates.
oh nice. so when did this alignment happen in the qaringil campaign, during exarch pesaro's time? was that important for any of the political stuff?

Good question, but the lunar alignment calendar is for the present-day Qamis campaign (starts in 2253). Qaringil, the prequel campaign, is set roughly 200 years earlier, so the alignment cycle would've been completely different back then. The notes don't detail Qaringil-era moon phases or whether they mattered politically. Exarch Giovanni Pesaro is a Qaringil detail, so if you're asking about that era, the triple-moon stuff isn't really relevant. Were you asking about Qaringil-era politics, or about the present-day campaign?
oh right, i mixed those up. so in the present campaign, do the alignments actually affect anything, or is it just calendar flavor?

The notes don't specify whether alignments have mechanical or narrative effects -- that's a blank space your DM hasn't filled in yet. Could be religious observances, tidal magic, seasonal events, whatever. But yeah, they happen frequently enough (roughly five times a year, with the tightest ones within 1%) that they'd be easy to use as a campaign hook if he wants them to matter.
So I'm thinking about playing a drow who stayed on the surface after the war. The notes mention a drow presence in Qama -- what's that about? Is that where I should be from?

Qama is the holy city of Bahamut -- the traditional seat of the Church of Bahamut, which has enormous influence across Qis'mos. It's not just a major religious center; it's *the* major religious center for the region. During the late phases of the Ryraxian War, the drow occupied it. And here's the thing: they're still there. They didn't despoil the city, but they installed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. That caused a pretty significant split -- there's now an alternate candidate, Qachu II, running things from Qis without any of the relics of office. It's an active schism.
Oh wow, so the drow just... stayed? Even though the war's over and Edrad got back on top?

That's exactly the situation. The notes say the drow were recruited as allies by Artkos in a desperation move during the war, and after Ryraxos went down and the Keyberos dynasty was restored, nobody managed to push them out. They've ended up scattered in a dozen enclaves across the continent, but Qama is the most prominent one -- a whole drow-controlled holy city is hard to miss. So yeah, you could absolutely be from there. The aftermath of the occupation, the schism with the human church, the tension of drow staying on the surface in a city that's religiously significant to the whole region -- that's rich ground for a backstory.
What's the day-to-day like though? Like, is it openly hostile, or is there some kind of uneasy coexistence? What would my character actually *experience* living there?

That's actually a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet -- the notes don't specify the tone of the occupation, what the broader Qis'mosi attitude is toward Qama under drow control, or what daily life looks like for a drow or a human there. You've got some solid hooks though: Qe'lth IV is in charge of the holy city, Qachu II is running a schismatic church from Qis claiming the true authority, and Bahamut's church is deeply woven into Qis'mos culture. That friction is definitely there. Your DM could go anywhere with it -- genuine integration, cold war, outright hostility, religious chaos. That might be worth workshopping with them, or it could be something you and your character are figuring out as you play.
Okay, and just to check -- Bahamut is... what exactly? Like, what do people worship about that god?

The notes mention Bahamut as a god with a church and a holy city, but they don't spell out the theology or what domains/aspects Bahamut represents. Standard D&D lore would give you a lot of detail there, but for this setting's campaign notes, that's another blank space. hey so my friend won't shut up about this D&D world she's running called Qamis and apparently Qis'mos is like the big deal. what's the deal with that place?

It's also got serious religious and institutional weight. The Church of Bahamut is huge there, and it's where the oldest governmental traditions from the old Draconic empire lasted the longest.
wait, when you say richest and most productive... like what makes it rich? farms? trade? gold mines?

okay, and what's the government like there?

They haven't wrecked the place, but they did install their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. This created a schism -- there's now a rival high priest, Qachu II, operating from the city of Qis without any of the relics of office. So the church is split right now.
so the drow just... stayed there after the war ended?

Yeah, they're still there. They were actually brought in as allies by Artkos during the war (as a desperation move), and when the fighting ended and Ryraxos got exiled, the drow didn't leave. They're in scattered enclaves across the continent, but Qama is one of their most prominent settlements.

So you've got this interesting situation now where the richest, most important region is dealing with an occupation that's not hostile but definitely a major political complication -- religious schism, foreign power in the holy city, uncertain what happens next. Your friend's campaign could easily explore that tension.
So I keep seeing Primus mentioned... what's the big deal with it? Is it like, special somehow?

Primus is one of Qaia's moons, and it's genuinely unique. It stays perfectly fixed in the sky -- like, it never rises and never sets. It just hangs there in the same spot forever, which is actually incredibly useful. Most navigators use it to figure out longitude without needing a chronometer, which is a huge deal. Back on Earth that took centuries to solve, but Qaian navigators got it basically for free.
Wait, it NEVER moves? Like, at all? That's actually insane. Is that magic, or like... physics?

It's magic. Primus is magically anchored -- that's what keeps it geostationary. And it's tiny compared to the other moons (about a third the size), plus it's dark and iron-rich, so it's not super bright. But that fixed position? Pure magic, and it completely changes how navigation works.
OK so everyone just... learns to use Primus to navigate? Are there like navigators' guilds or something? And what about people on the other side of the planet? Can they still see it?

Good question about the far side -- Primus is only visible from roughly half the planet. Observers on the other side of Qaia literally can't see it, so they're stuck with dead reckoning or stellar methods. That's a huge disadvantage.

As for navigators' guilds or how widespread the knowledge is, the campaign notes don't actually specify that. It's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
Hmm, interesting. But like... who even set up the magic in the first place? Is there a story there, or...

That's another blank space, yeah. The notes just say Primus is magically anchored, but how it got that way, who did it, whether there's history behind it -- none of that's detailed. It's definitely something the DM could expand on, or maybe even tie into the campaign.
hey -- building a character from Westron. what's the religious situation there vs the mainland?

Honest answer: Westron is mostly a blank space. The DM's notes literally say "The continent to the West is important and I have not yet prepared any material!" But there's one concrete detail -- they're not Bahamut worshippers, unlike most of the mainland. That's actually pretty significant since the Church of Bahamut is *enormous* in Qis'mos and has major influence everywhere else. So whatever religion Westron practices, it's different enough to be religiously distinct from the Bahamut consensus. Might be a good hook for your backstory if you want to lean into it.
interesting -- so if I'm from Westron, am I like, a missionary type, or am I fleeing that religion? or do I just not care about the religious stuff?

All of those work. The notes don't specify enough to rule any of them out. You could be someone from a Westron religious tradition who's exploring the mainland and its Bahamut worship. You could be someone who rejected your homeland's faith and came east. Or you could just be a merchant or adventurer who doesn't care much either way. The DM's left it pretty open specifically so backstories can fill the gaps. If I were you, I'd nail down your *personal* relationship to religion first, then figure out what Westron tradition that puts you at odds with or aligned with.
what's the vibe of the place otherwise? like, is it organized kingdoms, scattered tribes, merchant cities?

Also blank. The notes say it's "exotic" and "very large" but that's it. No details on government, cities, culture, none of it -- that's all for the DM to fill in or for you to suggest as part of your character's backstory. If your PC is from a merchant republic like Qaringil's style, or feudal kingdoms like Edrad, or something completely different, you can propose it. The DM's got room for it.

hey so i'm reading about qaringil and there's mention of bridges crossing the central sea -- i'm super curious about those. with your geostationary moon setup and all that celestial navigation stuff, do the bridges have any astronomical significance? like, is their placement tied to primus or the tidal window?

Good question! The notes say there's a wizard-made isthmus that crosses the central sea near its northern end, helped by two enormous bridges. One of them is called the Great Western Bridge. But honestly, the campaign notes don't go into detail about their original purpose or why they were placed where they are -- whether it's tied to Primus's longitude line or tidal dynamics, that's blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. All we know is they're wizard-made construction, and the Great Western Bridge dominates Qaringil's fortifications.
wait, wizard-made? so like, magically constructed? and you said they connect to a volcanic island? i'm trying to picture the geography here.

Yeah, the isthmus itself is explicitly wizard-made, and the two enormous bridges support it. There's mention of a volcanic island involved with the bridges, and Qaringil sits on the western shore of the island *between* the bridges. So the picture is: volcanic island in the middle, two bridges spanning from it (one is the Great Western Bridge), and the wizard-made causeway tying it all together to create that northern crossing of the central sea. But the notes don't spell out which bridge is which or give their full names beyond the Great Western Bridge.
hey, so my friend says you know about this D&D world they're running? I've read a ton of fantasy novels but I'm pretty new to tabletop. What's this place called?

Yeah, I can help! The world is called Qaia, and the current campaign is set in a continent called Qamis -- pronounced Khah-mis, the Q is from Old Draconic. Right now it's set in the aftermath of a massive 30-year war called the Ryraxian War. Things just stabilized: a dragon named Ryraxos who led the war got exiled to the second moon, and the old Keyberos dynasty is back in charge of Edrad, the dominant kingdom. It's tense because nobody's quite sure what the new normal looks like yet. There's also a prequel campaign set 200 years earlier, but sounds like your friend is running the present-day version.
okay cool. so like, where does the campaign actually start? In a tavern or whatever?

The campaign is expected to start in a city called Rathakos in the Red Mountains. It's this mountainous border region sandwiched between Edrad, Qis'mos, and the powerful nation of Artkos. The thing is, Rathakos just became its own independent nation -- it hasn't even found a permanent prince yet. A guy named John Dunne is acting as regent while a grand council figures it out. And because the Red Mountains are still wild and underexplored, there's this strong tradition of adventuring there. So it's a solid place for a party to get started and actually have impact.
wait, it just became its own nation? That sounds messy. What actually went down?

Yeah, it's complicated. During the 30-year war, the mountains got occupied by Edrad at one point and were organized as the Red Mountain Republic. But when Edrad lost ground elsewhere and had to pull back, the local resistance took over -- they purged the faction that had been friendly to Edrad and created two new independent nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So the war's officially over, but it's not settled. There's internal unrest in a lot of places, especially in the southern parts that used to be directly controlled by Edrad, and some mountain communities still follow old dragon cults. It's unstable enough that there's real work for adventurers to do.
okay so about this Ryraxos -- is he still like a threat? Can he come back from the moon?

No, he's gone. Part of the peace deal was he accepted exile to Secundus -- he doesn't play any role in the present-day campaign. But here's an interesting detail: Ryraxos wasn't actually the most powerful dragon ever. He's bronze and only about sixty years old. What made him formidable was that he was an exceptional military commander -- like, dragons make excellent artillery officers because they can literally carry the guns themselves. That's how his rise to power started. So he lost, accepted exile, and that's the end of it.
wait hold on -- how do you have a second moon? And doesn't that completely mess up how navigation and calendars work? I'm imagining the sky must be bonkers.

Qaia actually has seven moons. The big ones are Secundus -- dark iron, retrograde, roughly Earth's moon in size -- Tertius, which is slightly larger than Earth's moon, and Quartus, which is basically identical to Earth's moon. Then there's Primus, this tiny geostationary moon that sits directly over the equator and never moves. Primus is actually huge for navigation because its position is fixed, so you can measure its elevation and calculate your longitude without needing a chronometer. That's something Earth took centuries to solve. The moons also drive much stronger tides than Earth -- about four times stronger if you add them up. So yeah, the night sky is definitely more crowded and the ocean is way more dynamic.
okay so if navigation is actually solvable because of Primus, that means sea trade must be way more advanced than in typical fantasy? Like the coasts are probably connected?

Exactly. There's a network of islands in the central sea run by merchant lords who basically sell mercenary naval services to continental powers -- it's called a thassolocracy. One of the island city-states, Qaringil, is built with canals and runs as this chaotic republic that's actually the setting for the prequel campaign. The point is: because navigation is actually viable, sea power matters way more than it does in most fantasy worlds. You've got genuine maritime civilization and trade networks. That said, the interior -- especially the Red Mountains where your campaign's based -- that's still wild frontier. So you get both: sophisticated coast and islands, and genuine unexplored wilderness in the mountains.
Hey! So my character is from Forthyr and follows the Dragon Emperor revival cult. What's the big picture here? Is Dragoncaller Shanlong actually backing this movement?

Good setup. Yes -- Forthyr is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, the latest in a line of sorcerer-kings (the dynasty used to be dragonborn, but bloodlines ran thin). The Dragon Emperor revival sect gets tacit encouragement, if not outright support, from the dynasty itself. So there's definitely some political cover for a character like yours. The broader picture: Forthyr is somewhat backwards compared to the rest of Qamis, and this sect has real cultural weight there.
Okay cool. And Ryraxos -- he was a dragon, right? Is he helping push the revival or...?

Ah, I should clarify the timeline here. Ryraxos *was* a bronze dragon, but the Ryraxian War -- the thirty-year conflict he waged across the continent -- ended before the campaign starts. He accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon, so he's not active in the present-day setting. He'll play no role in Qamis. If you're curious about what things were like *during* the war or before it, that's a different story, but the campaign kicks off after all that is resolved.
Wait, the war's already over? I thought the setting was, like, in the middle of all this conflict.

Not quite. The Ryraxian War lasted thirty years and has just ended. The present-day Qamis campaign happens *after* that -- so the world is in a weird state of trying to return to how things were, except everything has changed. The Keyberos dynasty is restored to Edrad, Ryraxos is exiled, and there's a lot of political fragmentation. If you wanted to play in a prequel era with a totally different political map, that's the Qaringil campaign, set about 200 years *before* all of this. But for Qamis proper, you're starting in the post-war period.

So I'm rolling a cleric for Qaringil. What's the party level? In Forgotten Realms the Church of Bahamut gets politically interesting around mid-levels, and I want to know if the same thing happens here.

The Qaringil party starts at fifth level, which is a solid entry point for guild membership and religious authority. That said -- Qamis is a homebrew setting, so the structures are pretty different from Forgotten Realms. At fifth level you'd be a full member of the Church of Bahamut (which has a major presence in Qis'mos and its holy city Qama), but what opportunities that opens up is something the DM has kept pretty open.
I also read something about a schism in the Church? Like factions fighting for control? That kind of thing happens in a lot of settings.

There is a schism, but it's important to note it's in the present-day Qamis campaign, not in Qaringil. Qaringil is set about 200 years earlier, when the Church is unified. In present-day Qamis, though, Qama was occupied by the drow during the Ryraxian wars. They didn't destroy the city, but they installed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. That created a power split: an alternate candidate, Qachu II, is leading the Church from Qis without access to the traditional relics and symbols of office.
Got it, so Qaringil's Church is unified at least. What's the internal hierarchy like? Different orders, ranks, that kind of thing?

That's mostly blank space -- the DM hasn't detailed the Church's governance structure, hierarchy, or internal orders yet. You know it's massive in Qis'mos and centered on Qama, and it inherited institutions from the old Draconic empire. But the specifics of how it's organized, what kind of ranks or orders exist -- that's something you could help define through your character's background or discover as you play.
Yo so I'm building this nation for my campaign and I need a sorcerer-king dynasty, but everything I look at is just Forgotten Realms copypaste. You got anything original I can borrow?

Dragoncaller Shanlong VI runs Forthyr -- an enormous nation in the northeast of Qamis. Sorcerer-king dynasty, so that's in your ballpark.

The interesting bit: the dynasty used to be dragonborn, but the bloodline's thinned out over generations. And there's a religious sect in Forthyr calling for the return of Dragon Emperors. The Dragoncallers tacitly support it, maybe even encourage it. So you've got this sorcerer-king line watching a cult try to resurrect what they used to be.
Ehhh. A sorcerer-king dynasty with a Dragon Emperor cult? That's pretty standard fantasy. What actually makes this different?

Honest answer? The notes don't spell it out. Forthyr's kind of a blank space -- you get the skeleton (sorcerer-king, fading dragonborn heritage, Dragon Emperor cult) but not the endgame. Does Shanlong want to resurrect dragons? Control the cult? Prevent the resurrection? No idea.

But that blank space is actually useful for borrowing. You get the rough shape and you fill in what the Dragoncaller actually wants.
Wait, Dragoncaller VI? That means there were multiple before him.

Yeah. There's a prequel campaign set about 200 years earlier, and it mentions Dragoncaller Shanlong I ruling Forthyr. Also a sorcerer-king with a Dragon Emperor cult around.

Twist: Shanlong I was half-dragonborn. But by the time you get to Shanlong VI in the present day, the bloodlines have run thin. The notes don't detail Shanlong II through V or how the degradation happened.
Actually... you could do a lot with that. Build a campaign arc around why the bloodline faded, or what role the cult played in it. That's actually useful.

Exactly. You've got historical anchors -- the dynasty's dragon heritage fading over centuries, a cult with their tacit backing, the whole generational arc -- but the specific why is yours to decide. That's more useful than a finished antagonist.

Okay I'm trying to build a character backstory but looking at the map I'm kind of overwhelmed. There's like... what, eight nations? Nine? Isn't that going to make the politics super confusing to track?

It looks like a lot at first, but the good news is you don't need to master the whole continent to build a solid character. The setting is designed so that the complexity matters -- especially because the Ryraxian War just ended thirty years ago -- but it mostly matters *locally*. What's your character from?

I was thinking Rathakos, since that's where the campaign starts. But I want the backstory to feel like it's actually rooted in something, you know? Not just "I'm from X country." What makes Rathakos feel like a place?

That's the right instinct. Rathakos has a lot to work with. It's a mountainous border state -- the capital, Rath, sits in the northern foothills of the Red Mountains. The thing that defines the culture there is that it has a *strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship*. The Red Mountains are super dangerous and underexplored, so adventuring is kind of baked into how people think about the world.

Also, Rathakos just got made a Princedom, but hasn't actually found a Prince yet. It's being run by a Regent named John Dunne while a Grand Council figures out who should rule. So there's this fresh, unsettled feeling to the place.

Interesting, so there's that instability. But I'm also reading that it's caught between Edrad and Artkos and other nations. Won't that make it feel kind of precarious? Like, would someone from Rathakos even be stable enough for a fun backstory?

That's actually where it gets good. Rathakos is *designed* to be precarious -- it's the whole point. During the war it actually fought on both sides at different times. It was the Red Mountain Republic under Edran occupation, then when Edran forces pulled back, it split into Rathakos and Acrokos. So nobody's roots are super clean.

Plus, the Southern Reaches of Rathakos are culturally Edran and actually welcomed the Edran invasion. And some mountain communities still follow old dragon cults. So there's all this internal tension baked in. That tension is *great* character material if you lean into it.
Oh wait, that's actually really good. So if I made my character from the Southern Reaches, they'd be Edran but also technically Rathakos now. That feels like there's a real story there.

Exactly. The notes specifically mention that the Southern Reaches are Edran-speaking and that the population there welcomed the Edran occupation -- so there's a whole chunk of Rathakos that's culturally Edran. Your character could have family divided by that, or be figuring out their own loyalty, or lean fully into one side. The fact that it's not clean gives you a lot to work with.
Nice. One more thing though -- you mentioned dragon cults in the mountains? Is that a Rathakos thing or does that come up everywhere?

The notes just mention it for Rathakos's mountain communities, without getting into how widespread dragon reverence is in the rest of the world. It's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. But Forthyr has a whole religious sect that wants to bring back the Dragon Emperors, so *something* dragon-nostalgic is in the water post-war. Whether Rathakos's cults are connected to that or their own thing -- that's open. Could be a cool angle if you want to explore it with the DM.

hey so I'm trying to understand how Primus works theologically - like, does it relate to the Bahamut schism at all? In Forgotten Realms, Selûne's position in the sky is tied to divine authority and prophecy. Is Primus like that for the Church of Bahamut?

Good question, but Primus is actually more of a navigation tool than a theological object. It's geostationary -- fixed in the sky at the prime meridian, never moving -- so navigators use it to find longitude without a chronometer. That's a huge practical advantage, but the notes don't describe it as a source of religious authority or prophecy the way Selûne is in the Realms.

That said, Primus does have visibility limits that matter: it's only visible from one hemisphere of Qaia. From the equator, you can see it from roughly 81° east or west of the prime meridian. At higher latitudes, that range shrinks -- at 60° latitude, you're down to about 72° in each direction. The far-side hemisphere never sees it at all.
Wait, so half the planet can't even see it? That's pretty dramatic. In FR, both hemispheres see Selûne... I think. How does that work with the schism? Like, is Qama's authority tied to seeing Primus?

The notes don't connect Primus visibility to the schism itself. The schism is political: Qama was occupied by drow during the late Ryraxian Wars, and they appointed their own high priest (Qe'lth IV), while an alternate candidate (Qachu II) operates from Qis without the relics of office. It's more about control of the city and the sacred objects than about celestial observation.

But you're right that the hemisphere split is significant. It means navigators on the near side of Qaia have a huge advantage -- they can pinpoint longitude on the fly. The far side would need dead reckoning or stellar methods, which is much harder. That's a real asymmetry in capability.

Hmm, so it's a navigation thing, not theology. That makes sense. But here's what I'm wondering -- the Bahamut church was supposedly based on Old Draconic institutions, right? And Old Draconic had to deal with this same moon system. Did dragons ever use Primus the way sailors do?

The notes don't specifically say dragons used Primus for navigation. What we know is that the Church of Bahamut has an enormous presence in Qis'mos and their traditional seat is in Qama, the holy city. The Old Draconic language was used for scholarly and diplomatic texts. But there's no mention of dragons as navigators or any connection between Primus and dragon culture.

That's actually a blank space in the setting -- something you could explore with your DM if it matters for your character's backstory.
Fair enough. One more comparison though -- in Forgotten Realms the moon's position gets tracked for spells and rituals. Does Qaia's Bahamut worship involve tracking Primus phases or position?

Not according to the notes. The schism focuses on institutional control and the relics of office, not celestial events. And unlike moons that go through phases, Primus is always visible from the same point in the sky on the near side -- it's stationary. The phases and positions that matter for Qaia's calendar are Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus, the three inner moons that actually oscillate and create tides. But again, the notes don't describe any religious use of moon-tracking in the Bahamut church.

So while Primus is huge for navigation, it doesn't seem to have the theological role you're thinking of from the Realms.

hey, got a minute? working on some backstory stuff for my gondolier and i'm confused about something. if Qaringil was already thriving 200 years ago, where were Rathakos and Acrokos? did they have different names back then?

They didn't exist yet. In the Qaringil era—about 200 years before now—the Red Mountains were just that: mountains. Various non-state peoples lived there, and the Holy Qis Empire claimed the territory on paper but didn't actually govern it. No unified nations, no city-states like you're thinking of.
so they just... popped into existence? when?

During the Ryraxian War that just ended. Here's how it went: Edrad occupied the mountain provinces and organized them into the Red Mountain Republic. But when Edrad got stretched thin militarily and had to pull back, the mountain people organized their own resistance. The Red Mountain Assembly split into factions—pro-Edran and anti-Edran—and the anti-Edran side won out. When the dust settled, you had two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So they're literally just a few years old.
oh damn, that explains why Rathakos seems so chaotic right now

Yeah. They're still figuring it out. Technically it's a Princedom now, but there's no Prince yet—just a Regent named John Dunne while the Grand Council searches for one. And there's a lot of tension: the southern regions that used to be Edran-controlled don't trust the new government, and some mountain communities still follow the old dragon cults. Brand-new nation trying to hold itself together.
yo, need some intel on the church of bahamut for a backstory. what's their deal in qamis?

The Church of Bahamut is huge in Qis'mos -- like, enormous presence. Their traditional seat is in the holy city of Qama (pronounced Khama). But here's the current mess: the drow occupied Qama during the late Ryraxian Wars and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. Meanwhile, an alternate high priest, Qachu II, is operating from Qis with the traditionalist faction -- but without the actual relics of office. So you've got a schism. Qis'mos is still rich and powerful, so the church's reach and influence remains substantial despite being split.
okay but if i'm making a cleric from qis'mos, how does that tension actually hit? like am i getting looks from other clergy depending on which faction i'm tied to?

Absolutely. If you're ordained under Qachu II in Qis, the Qama priesthood might see you as lacking legitimacy -- you've got the theology but not the relics or the seat. If you trained under Qe'lth IV in Qama, traditionalists elsewhere might view you as tainted by drow influence. The schism is real and would affect how other members of the church treat you. That said, the notes don't detail the exact theology of the split or how far the church's authority extends beyond Qis'mos proper, so there's room to develop exactly what your character's position means within the larger organization.
would that reach extend to like kard or the drow enclaves? or is the church mostly a qis'mos thing?

The notes emphasize the church's presence in Qis'mos specifically, with the schism rooted in Qama's drow occupation. Kard is in the Artklan League's southwest enclave -- historically Edran-speaking, different political sphere. The notes mention drow remain in scattered enclaves across Qamis after the war, most prominently in Qama, but don't detail how the church's authority or the schism plays out in those other locations. That's blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet -- could be a fun angle for your backstory, though.
hey, I missed most of last week's session. someone was talking about the moons and how some are way denser than others? what's that about?

Oh dude, yeah! So Qaia's moon system has this really cool composition split. Primus and Secundus are both insanely iron-rich and dense — we're talking 6,689 kg/m³, which is nearly twice as dense as moons like Quartus that run at 3,344 kg/m³. Dark iron surfaces too, both of them.

The theory is they're probably fragments from the same differentiated progenitor — like, pieces of the iron core from something that got shattered at some point. Tertius is kind of in the middle at 5,351 kg/m³, so it's iron-enriched but not as extreme. The rest of the system is standard rocky bodies.
Wait, so we're talking pieces of something that broke apart? That's wild!

Right?? That's the best explanation for why Primus and Secundus are so similar in density and composition. They're too much of a match to be coincidence — both at 6,690 kg/m³, both dark iron. If they're fragments of an iron core from some larger differentiated body, that explains everything. The rest of the system — Quartus, Sextus, Septimus — they're all normal rocky at lunar density. So there's definitely a story there.
Does that show up in how they orbit? Like, are they doing anything weird?

Actually yeah! Secundus is retrograde, which is pretty significant — it comes at the system from a different direction than the normal moons, and that kind of fits with the "fragment from elsewhere" theory. It's also got a notable eccentricity around 0.19.

Primus is geostationary because it's magically anchored — someone during the dragon empire era locked it in place. Secundus though, no magical anchor. It's actually slowly spiraling inward toward Qaia over geological time from tidal forces.

But here's the thing: the notes don't explicitly spell out HOW they ended up in their current orbits. We know they're likely fragments and we know how they're moving now, but the full origin story? That's still a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. Could be sick material for a backstory hook though!
I'm building a character from Rathakos. What's actually going on there right now?

Rathakos is a mountainous border state along the Red Mountains, and it's in a tense spot. The Ryraxian War just ended, and Rathakos was formally made a Princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r, but there's a problem: they haven't actually found a Prince yet. Right now it's being governed by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council hunts for a new monarch. The region has serious internal unrest—especially in the Southern Reaches that were annexed from Edrad, and in mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. Geographically, you're in a tough spot: bordered by Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, Qirkos, Lock, and Acrokos. You're trying to hang on to independence from all sides. On the bright side, the Red Mountains are dangerous and underexplored, so there's a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship.
Wait, you said it was 'newly made a Princedom'—was it something else before?

Yeah. During the Ryraxian War, after Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire, the mountain provinces fell under Edran occupation and were organized as the Red Mountain Republic. When the war swung the other way and Edran forces pulled out, the Red Mountain Assembly split. The pro-Edran faction got purged, and what remained fragmented into two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So you're dealing with a region that went from independent → occupied → a unified republic → split into two new nations, all in about thirty years. That kind of upheaval leaves scars—that's a lot of why you're seeing the internal unrest and the persistence of the old dragon cults now.
hey, what peoples live in the Red Mountains?

The notes don't go into specifics about particular peoples. What we know: the Red Mountains are dangerous and underexplored. Some mountain communities there still follow old dragon cults, which contributes to unrest in Rathakos. In the Qaringil prequel era (~200 years earlier), the mountains were claimed but never really controlled by the Holy Qis Empire. It's marked as a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
Dragon cults? That's wild. Are those older than Ryraxos, or related to him?

Almost certainly older. The notes mention an old Draconic empire and Dragon Emperors—the dragons have since departed. So these mountain cults are probably rooted in reverence for that ancient era. But the specifics (beliefs, organization, who they worship, how hostile they are) aren't detailed in the campaign notes. That's another blank space the DM can develop.
ok quick question -- im workshopping a PC from Forthyr and the name Dragoncaller Shanlong keeps coming up. is that like, a specific person or a dynasty title

It's a title that runs in the family. The current ruler is Shanlong VI -- that's the sixth one in the line. They're all sorcerer-kings, which is pretty much what it sounds like: they rule Forthyr through sorcery. Fun historical note: the first Dragoncaller, Shanlong I, was actually half-dragonborn, but the bloodlines thinned out over the centuries, so VI and the more recent ones aren't dragonborn anymore.
lol ok so there's a whole dragon empire angle to this? what's the vibe in Forthyr around that

Pretty strong, actually. There's a religious sect in Forthyr that's all about bringing back the Dragon Emperors, and the Dragoncaller dynasty basically tacitly supports them -- no suppression going on. The notes describe Forthyr as "somewhat backwards," so this reverence for the old dragon age probably colors the whole culture pretty heavily. If your PC grew up there, they'd definitely be swimming in that dragon-nostalgia vibe.
cool that helps a lot. last thing -- im into the navigation stuff (been working through some primsine calcs lol). Forthyr's northeast, right? that's on the side where you can see Primus?

Yep, Forthyr's on the near hemisphere, so Primus is visible from there. It's geostationary, so it just sits in one spot in the sky and never moves -- would be a solid constant reference point if your character's into that kind of thing.
wait, I'm confused -- I thought Rathakos and Acrokos were part of the Red Mountains? why don't they exist in the Qaringil era?

Good catch -- they're way newer than Qaringil. In the Qaringil era, about 200 years ago, the Red Mountains were just... non-state territory. Various peoples lived there, and the Holy Qis Empire claimed the area but didn't actually govern it. No formal nations yet. Rathakos and Acrokos only became real countries after the Ryraxian War, when the region got reorganized. The war created the Red Mountain Republic as an Edran-occupied territory, and when Edrad withdrew, that faction split into the two separate princedoms we see now in Qamis present-day. So they're products of recent history, not old settlements.
yo so i missed last week - what was everyone talking about with the moons?

The party was discussing the next triple-full alignment -- when Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all reach full moon simultaneously. It's pretty rare and considered significant for a lot of reasons depending on who you ask: navigators care because the triple-full messes with tidal patterns, merchants watch for it because there's extra light at night for trade runs, and various religious types see it as auspicious or inauspicious. As a gondolier, you'd definitely know the importance of the tides and the moons.
ok when IS the next one though?

There's one coming up at the beginning of Tertius's next cycle -- February 13th. All three moons will be within a hair of full, basically exact. That's the closest one happening for a while. After that there are a couple more later in the year -- July 2nd and August 29th -- but they're slightly less precise. The February one is considered the "great" alignment of this era.
what were they planning to do with it?

That's where I'd need to know what actually happened in your session. The alignment itself matters for different reasons depending on who you talk to -- could be navigation-related, could be religious, could be merchant-related given Qaringil's focus on trade. What context did people seem most interested in before you had to drop?
hey, I'm building a character from Qaringil. what's the story with those two bridges?

There's a wizard-made isthmus crossing the central sea near its northern end, supported by two enormous bridges connected to a volcanic island. Qaringil sits on the western shore between them. One bridge is called the Great Western Bridge—Qaringil's fortifications dominate it. As for who built them, when, or what the second bridge is called, that's blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
cool, could I work that into my backstory? like my family helped build them?
That's worth pitching to your DM, but since the notes don't specify who built the bridges, there's definitely room there. Session zero would be the place to explore it.
ok so i dropped early from worldbuilding, what's the deal with primus and tides? people were talking about static bulges or something

Primus doesn't make oscillating tides like a normal moon - it makes a permanent static one instead. It's geostationary, meaning it stays fixed in the exact same spot in the sky and never moves. Since it doesn't orbit, the tidal bulge it raises in the ocean just stays locked in place too - about 2 centimeters of permanent sea level rise along the prime meridian axis. The actual daily tides come from the three other inner moons that do move around.
okay but like... why does this matter? feels like we're getting really into the weeds with orbital mechanics. this is D&D, not an astronomy lecture

Fair point on the complexity. But here's why it actually matters: Primus being stationary is what makes celestial navigation work on Qaia. You measure its elevation to find your longitude without needing a chronometer - which is genuinely a big deal for a pre-industrial world. So the static bulge is kind of a side effect of a feature that's central to how the world functions. The detail exists for a reason.
huh, okay that's actually useful. so like... the bulge just stays put then? never moves?

Right, it's always there - locked in place. Meanwhile Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all orbit normally, so they sweep across the sky and their gravity creates the daily oscillating tides that actually change throughout the day. So you've got this permanent static feature from Primus plus dynamic tidal loading from three other moons with incommensurate periods.
okay yeah that makes sense actually. the weird moon does the weird thing, normal moons do normal moon stuff. got it
Exactly. Which also means navigators on the near hemisphere have a huge advantage over the far side - they can use Primus for longitude fixes. Far side, you're stuck with dead reckoning.

Hey, quick question -- I'm ripping off some elements from Qamis for my own game, and I'm trying to understand how Primus navigation would actually work in practice. Like, where does it break down? Are there big parts of the world where you just can't use it?

Good question. Primus is genuinely revolutionary for navigation, but it has a pretty hard limit: you can only see it from roughly half the planet. It's geostationary over the prime meridian at the equator, so if you're on the far side of Qaia, it's permanently below the horizon. You're stuck with dead reckoning or lunar-distance methods, which are way harder.

Even on the near side, there's a visibility boundary. The maximum angle from the sub-Primus point at which Primus stays above the horizon is about 81.3 degrees. So at the equator, you can roughly see Primus if you're within ±81 degrees of longitude from the prime meridian. It gets tighter at higher latitudes -- at 60 degrees latitude, you're down to about ±72 degrees.
Okay that makes sense. So half the world just loses the trick entirely. But on the near side, if I'm near that boundary, is it still usable or does it get sketchy?

It gets sketchy, yeah. As you approach the visibility boundary, Primus sinks lower and lower toward the horizon. And here's the thing: your longitude precision is directly tied to how accurately you can measure its elevation. A good cross-staff might get you ±11 km of accuracy on longitude if Primus is well-positioned, but near the horizon where the geometry is shallow, you lose a lot of that precision. Atmospheric refraction becomes a real problem too.

So technically still usable, but if you're navigating near the edge of Primus's visibility, you'd want to be pretty careful about it. You might drop to ±55-70 km of accuracy instead of ±11 km.

That's actually perfect for what I need. So basically: one hemisphere gets magic longitude, the other hemisphere is back to square one, and the boundary region is... squinty. Do the Qaians have some kind of convention for how to handle that boundary issue? Or is it just a fact of life?

The notes don't really go into that. It's marked as a blank space. Presumably navigators just know their instruments' limits and act accordingly -- if you're doing serious navigation work, you'd want to be well inland from that boundary with clear Primus sightings. But how different maritime cultures actually *handle* it, whether there are local conventions or whether some explorer figured out a clever workaround -- that's all open ground for your own setting.
Okay so I'm still confused about the drow. Didn't they used to be way underground? How'd they end up all over the surface now?

Artkos recruited them as allies during the Ryraxian War -- kind of a desperation move on their part. Once the war ended they just never left. Now they've got a dozen or so scattered enclaves on the surface, with the biggest one being Qama -- the holy city of Bahumat.
Whoa, wait -- Qama? The main temple city? What's happening there?

The drow occupied it during the late war phases. Didn't trash the place, but they did appoint their own high priest, a drow named Qe'lth IV. That's caused a serious schism -- the original priesthood has an alternate candidate, Qachu II, who's holed up in Qis without any of the relics of office. So you've got two competing heads of the Church of Bahumat now.
Hi! I'm working on a Qaringil character and I'm curious -- what's the deal with half-elves in Edrad? Are they like, the ruling class or something?

Good question! Yeah, in Edrad they basically are the ruling class. The Keyberos dynasty -- which has run Edrad for the past couple centuries -- is half-elf, and their whole nobility is structured around it. You have to be somewhere between one quarter and three quarters elf to be Edran nobility. It's a pretty strict thing.

Why are you asking? Are you thinking your Qaringil character has an Edran connection?
Maybe! I like the idea of like, a younger child of an Edran merchant family who came to Qaringil to try their luck. Would that make sense? Like, what would bring someone from Edrad to the islands?

That tracks! Qaringil is built mostly by adventurers and laborers from Qismos originally, but it's a major trade hub with powerful merchant navies. Edrad's pretty culturally dominant in Qamis -- Jia is the capital and it's super influential -- so you'd definitely have Edran merchants and traders in Qaringil. Your character coming over to build their own fortune away from home? Very reasonable.

Plus, Qaringil is run by five major families who are almost all mixed human-elf ancestry anyway, so a half-elf wouldn't stand out. You wouldn't have the same formal requirements Edrad does, but you'd fit right in with the commercial elite.
Oh nice. So what are these five families? Could my character actually be from one of them?

The Five Families are Peixota (Shipping), Manos (Judicial), Qanto (used to lead Mercantile but just lost it), Volmer (Banking), and Vitelli (Silk). All of them have merchant or noble ties, and yeah, they all have some elf ancestry. You could totally be a junior member of one of them, or maybe you came TO Qaringil to work your way into one of their operations.

Peixota runs shipping, which would make sense for an Edran merchant family. Manos and Volmer also have shipping interests. What kind of character were you imagining?

I was thinking maybe someone with some skill in talking and money -- like a face who's trying to climb the ladder in Qaringil commerce. Is there a guild for that, or would they have to just navigate the families?

There's a Mercantile Guild -- that would be perfect for you. It just got taken over by a gnomish family aligned with the current Exarch, which actually creates some interesting politics if you want to play into it. The Qanto family used to run it and they're NOT happy about losing it. If your character is an ambitious merchant, that internal struggle is right there to navigate.

You could be a guild member trying to rise through the ranks, or play the family politics, or both. The elites in Qaringil are commercial first and foremost, so that's exactly the kind of character who fits. You'd also have access to the Adventurer Guild if you want combat skills, plus Alchemist and Mage guilds if magic appeals to you.
OK this is helpful. One more thing -- in Qaringil, what language would my character actually speak day to day?

The main language is Islas -- the island lingua franca. But interestingly, the Qaringil variant of Islas has heavier Qis vocabulary than usual, because the city was originally founded by adventurers and laborers from Qismos.

If you're coming from Edrad, you'd speak Edran as a home language (and probably the capital prestige dialect, since your family's got merchant status). You could reasonably know Islas pretty well by now if you've been in Qaringil a few years, and having some Qis would give you an edge -- it's the academic language across Qamis, so knowing it marks you as educated or at least cosmopolitan.

Ah, quick clarification -- Qaringil is the main setting for the prequel campaign, which happened about 200 years before the present-day Qamis campaign.
