oh wait, Qaringil is from 200 years ago? I thought it was a current city

Yeah, the detailed campaign notes for Qaringil are set in that earlier era. The DM hasn't mapped out what happens to it in the present day. If you're in the modern Qamis campaign and want an island trading hub, you'd need to ask the DM about what's actually available.
makes sense. but if the prequel is a thing, what's Qaringil actually like? the setup sounds interesting

It's an island city-state in the central sea, about 100,000 people. Ruled as a republic by five major families -- Peixota (Shipping), Manos (Judicial), Qanto (Mercantile), Volmer (Banking), and Vitelli (Silk). Each family leads one of the major guilds. The city's criss-crossed with canals and connected to the mainland by wizard-made bridges. There's an Exarch (Giovanni Pesaro currently, in his 19th year) as the nominal leader. The whole place is basically merchant-driven -- elites are commercial, not landowners. There are Adventurer, Alchemist, and Mage guilds too if you're exploring characters for the prequel.
So I just heard about this D&D world and my friend mentioned some dragon named Ryraxos? Something about him being exiled to the moon? That's wild. Why the moon specifically?

Yeah, Ryraxos is kind of a big deal in the setting's recent history. He's a bronze dragon -- pretty formidable, though not unbeatable by a competent adventuring party or even a well-commanded artillery unit. He actually got famous as an artillery officer, since dragons are excellent at carrying heavy guns. Anyway, he took over Edrad when the Ryraxian War was going badly and turned things around, but then spent thirty years waging aggressive war across the continent. When he was finally defeated, he agreed to exile on Secundus, which is Qaia's second moon. As for why that moon specifically -- the notes don't actually say. That's one of those details the DM hasn't filled in yet.
Huh. Okay, so he like... accepted going to the moon? He didn't fight it?

Right, the notes specifically say he 'accepted exile to the second moon.' So yeah, he didn't resist or get forced. Whether he negotiated that as part of a peace deal, or if there was some other reason he agreed to it, isn't covered though. The DM could fill that in whenever.
Wait, is the moon even... livable? Like, can a dragon actually survive there?

That's a great question. The notes give detailed physical specs on Secundus -- it's about 471 km in radius, dense iron-rich surface, that kind of thing -- but they don't actually say anything about whether it's habitable or what the conditions are like for a dragon living there. It's definitely a blank space. All we know for certain is that Ryraxos is there now and that he won't play a role in the present-day campaign.
Okay, so is this a permanent thing? Could he theoretically come back?

Not something the notes specify. He 'accepted' the exile, which could mean anything from a binding magical agreement to just a voluntary retreat. Whether there are escape clauses, whether someone could negotiate his return, whether magic could bring him back -- none of that's covered. It's another blank space the DM can fill in however fits the story they want to tell.
So what's the story on those two big bridges in Qaringil? Seems like something that'd have major lore behind it.

They're part of a wizard-made isthmus that crosses the central sea near its northern end -- two enormous bridges connected to a volcanic island. That's honestly all the notes give us. The DM hasn't detailed who built them, when, or the history. Blank space for now.
What, really? I thought the Edran Empire built them when they were expanding. That was before the Ryraxian War, right?

A couple things to untangle: Qaringil is set about 200 years *before* the Ryraxian War, not right before it. And the notes don't credit the Edran Empire with building the bridges -- they're just described as wizard-made. In present-day Qamis (after the war), they're still there, still mysterious. Could be mixing in lore from another setting, or the DM just hasn't filled that part in yet.
Okay, okay. So the same bridges exist in both Qaringil and present day, they're just... wizard work and nobody knows who the wizard was?

Yep. They're established infrastructure in both timelines, 200 years apart, but the builder and backstory are a blank slate. Good hook if you want to ask your DM about it, especially if your character has any connection to the islands or the central sea.

wait so if primus is geostationary, that means it's like... the perfect longitude reference forever, right? my drow character just realized this and i'm genuinely losing it because that's SO different from earth

YES right?? on earth finding longitude without a chronometer was literally impossible for centuries - people got lost, whole fleets went down. on qaia it's just... solved. primus never moves. you measure its elevation angle, look up primsine on a table, and boom - you know your longitude. no chronometer needed.

your drow character is basically working with navigation tech that earth didn't develop until the 1700s, and qaia has it for free just because of orbital mechanics.

ohhh good catch. so here's the thing - primus only works if you're on the near hemisphere. the far side of qaia never sees it at all. permanently below the horizon.

that's actually insane - so far-side drow basically just... had to be really good at navigation the hard way?

exactly. structural inequality written into the planet itself. far-side sailors have to use dead reckoning, stellar transits, maybe lunar-distance methods using the other moons against a star catalogue - way harder than just reading primus's elevation. so either the far-side enclaves have seriously elite navigation specialists, or they're just not doing long ocean voyages. gives the near-side drow a built-in advantage that's been there since qaia formed.
yo so quick question for my backstory -- i'm thinking my character has Edran noble ties. what's the whole half-elf thing there? is it a big deal?

It's a *big* deal. In the Qaringil era, Edran nobility is almost entirely half-elven -- not just preferred, it's basically required. The rule is you have to be between one quarter and three quarters elf to be part of the ruling class. The Keyberos dynasty has been in power for two hundred years, so they've basically locked in that half-elf identity as the mark of Edran prestige. And because Edrad is culturally dominant across the continent, that carries weight everywhere.
okay so if my half-elf is from an Edran noble family, why would they even be here in Qaringil? seems like they'd have everything at home.

Good question for workshopping. Qaringil is a merchant republic full of adventurers and guild money -- completely different power structure than Edrad. You could be there for a ton of reasons: running from family politics, adventuring before settling into noble life, business for a merchant branch, or just being a black sheep. The cool part is Edran prestige would help you (cultural cache is real) but wouldn't automatically make you important here. The five families that run Qaringil are mostly human with some elf ancestry themselves, so they'd respect the bloodline but not defer to it.
wait, so edran is the LANGUAGE or the nationality? i'm confused.

Good catch -- it's both. Edrad is the nation; Edran is the language and the cultural adjective. So 'Edran nobility' means 'nobility from Edrad', and 'Edran language' is what they speak there. The five families in Qaringil would speak Islas (the primary language of the island), but educated merchants and nobles would probably have Edran as well. Main languages on Qaia are all national: Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas.
alright alright, so if my character knows both Edran and Islas, what would their backstory reason be? just grew up multilingual?

Most people grow up speaking their home language plus one regional dialect. But everyone also gets a bonus language -- however they justify it. Could be: Edran parent, merchant family with Qaringil ties, educated at court, or just 'I spent time here'. Since your character is an Edran half-elf *in* Qaringil, knowing both makes tons of sense. Maybe a parent was a merchant trader, or they came here as a young adult and stayed. What sounds right for your character?
is Lock a place where a drow could actually make something of themselves? I heard it's got industry going on.

Lock is worth considering. The kingdom as a whole has a burgeoning industrial presence, which means opportunity if you're willing to work. The city of Lock itself and a couple other major cities are designated as free cities—they've got old perogatives that give them some independence from the kingdom's direct rule, which historically they've guarded pretty fiercely. So there's a bit more autonomy in those places, less rigid hierarchy than you'd find in a straight-up kingdom city. That said, the notes don't spell out exactly what the current atmosphere is toward drow in Lock specifically. You'd need to scout it yourself to know if you'd be welcomed or tolerated.

The notes just say Lock has a 'burgeoning industrial presence'—they don't get specific about what kind. That's one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. Could be anything from weapons forging to textile mills to something tied to river trade. You might have to ask your DM or figure it out when your character actually gets there.
hey! quick question - I'm a new player and the DM mentioned West Artkos. What's the deal there?

West Artkos is the historic heartland of Artkos -- the original territory in the northwest. What makes it interesting is that Artkos has expanded way beyond that over the centuries. They now control multiple exclaves scattered across the continent, including their capital city Ath'r, which is actually the ancient seat of empires from way back. So you've got this situation where Artkos is trying to rebuild dominance over the rest of the Artklan League after the Ryraxian War shattered things.
wait, they control the ancient imperial capital??

Right?? That's huge. Ath'r has been the seat of central Qamisi empires since well before Artkos even conquered it two hundred years ago. So it's not just some city Artkos owns -- it's *the* historic capital with all that legitimacy and institutional weight behind it. Combined with West Artkos being their original heartland, it's a strong power base for High King Ezevir Arth to claim that Artkos should be dominant again.
so my character is from Forthyr, right? and I keep hearing about this Hill sphere thing in the setting. what even is that and why should I care?

Good question. The Hill sphere is basically Qaia's gravitational boundary relative to the sun -- it's the region where Qaia's gravity is strong enough to hold moons in orbit. Qaia's is about 3.89 lunar distances (LD) wide. All seven of Qaia's moons have to fit inside it or they'd get yanked away by the sun. Why it matters: the moons aren't evenly distributed. Quartus hangs out pretty far, taking up 27% of the sphere, while little Primus sits really close at only 2.8%. The outer retrograde moons -- Sextus and Septimus -- are pushing toward the boundary at 42% and 63% respectively, which is why the notes mention they're stable but getting close to the limits for retrograde orbits.
wait, retrograde limits? how does that work? And why are three of the moons going backwards anyway?

Retrograde means they orbit the "wrong way" relative to Qaia's rotation -- they rise in the west instead of the east. The notes don't specify why the moons have that orbit, but retrograde moons generally need to stay closer to their primary (Qaia) than prograde ones do, or they escape. The Hill sphere is the hard limit. Secundus is retrograde and only at 7.7%, totally safe. But Sextus and Septimus are both way out there -- if they drifted much farther, the sun's gravity would overpower Qaia's pull. The notes mention retrograde stability maxes out around 70% of the Hill sphere, and Septimus is at 63.3%, so it's genuinely on the edge. That's probably not accident -- the DM left this detail in for a reason.
huh. as someone from Forthyr with the Dragon Emperor revival thing... is this cosmic stuff documented anywhere? like old prophecies or something about the moons being fragile?

That's a good instinct, but the notes don't cover that. There's no lore yet connecting the moon system to the Dragon Emperor cult or any prophecies -- that's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in. Could be a great hook for your character though: maybe the sect has old draconic texts about the moons that your character could uncover, or maybe this fragile system is supposed to mean something spiritually. Worth asking your DM if there's hidden lore there, or if it's something you could develop as part of your character's backstory.
ok so where did all the dragons actually go? theres gotta be lore on this

Good question! The setting has exactly one rumor on this: there's a big, mostly unexplored island to the south with a city called Freeport at the shore, and the residents "try to not look too hard at the jungle pressing up against the city." The rumor is "some say this is where the dragons went." That's the entire in-world hint.
ok but like... what's the actual history? the notes mention old Draconic empire, institutions persisting in Qis'mos. So dragons ruled, then they just... left?

Yeah, the timeline is sparse. You get that there WAS an old Draconic empire, and after the dragons departed, institutions from that empire persisted longest in Qis'mos. Some of those institutions still survive in modified form. But the notes don't actually explain *when* the departure happened, *why*, or *where* they went. The southern island rumor is all the lore offers.
so the jungle on that island is just... blank? no hints at all about what's in there?

Blank. The DM's notes explicitly flag it as a blank space they haven't filled in yet. Same with most of Westron (the western continent -- the notes literally say "I have not yet prepared any material!") and a lot of the interior geography. The southern island is ripe for a player backstory or a future campaign hook, but the setting doesn't define what's in that jungle yet.
that's actually kind of cool from a worldbuilding angle though? leaving space for players to fill it in?

Yeah, looks intentional. The DM explicitly mentions in both the Qamis and Qaringil docs: "There are a lot of blank spaces on the map that need to be filled. You can help with that." So it's a collaborative sandbox rather than a finished world bible. Ryraxos being exiled to the moon is locked down, the political map post-war is solid, but dragons' ultimate fate? That's still open.
ok so i gotta ask - why is the whole world called Qaia? like did the DM just take Earth and slap a Q in front of it?

Fair question. The notes don't actually spell out the etymology or the in-world reason for the name, so I can't tell you a deep lore answer. But there's context: Q comes from Old Draconic, which is the language of scholarly publications and treaties across Qamis. You see it all over the place in proper nouns - Qis, Qama, Qeth, and so on. It's pronounced 'Kh' (hard sound), not like a regular Q. So the name Qaia probably has Old Draconic roots, but the DM hasn't filled in the actual story of why or what it means. That's one of the blank spaces.
so like... no one actually knows? that seems lazy lol

The DM explicitly marked it as a blank space - they know it's unfilled. Your character might not know either. As a Qaringil gondolier, you'd speak Islas mostly, with Qis vocabulary mixed in since Qaringil has Qis roots. The scholars and priests use Old Draconic, and they'd know the naming conventions better than a merchant or dockworker. So it's actually realistic that your character *wouldn't* have the answer. That could be a fun hook if you want to dig into it - maybe there's a mystery there, or it's just settled lore that nobody really questions. But yeah, the actual reason the world is called Qaia? DM hasn't written it yet.
hey! so what's the Qamis campaign actually about?

Qamis is the present-day setting -- the Ryraxian War just ended after thirty years. The dragon Ryraxos is exiled to Secundus (the second moon), the Keyberos dynasty is restored to the throne in Edrad, and everything's supposed to be back the way it was. Except it's not -- nations are fracturing, old powers are resurfacing, and the continent's still a mess. The campaign kicks off in Rathakos, a mountainous border state that's trying to hang onto independence between Edrad, Artkos, and Qis'mos.
wait wait wait -- you said Secundus? the second moon? hold on, the campaign world has MULTIPLE moons?

YES! Qaia has seven moons! This is so cool. Primus is geostationary and magically anchored over the equator -- it never rises or sets, just sits there in the sky. Then you've got Secundus (retrograde, dark iron surface), Tertius (prograde, about 8-day period), Quartus (basically Earth's Moon), and the outer pair Sextus and Septimus. They're all influencing the tides and the calendar.
okay but GEOSTATIONARY -- does that mean what I think it means? fixed point for navigation??

OH you're going to LOVE this. Primus is literally a fixed longitude reference. Its orbital inclination (23.5°) exactly matches Qaia's axial tilt, so it sits in the equatorial plane and never drifts. You can find longitude just by measuring Primus's elevation angle above the horizon -- no chronometer needed! They use a function called primsine that relates elevation to your geocentric angle from the sub-Primus point.
okay I need the formula. what's primsine??

YES! It's defined as primsine(e) = ρ cos²e + sin(e) × sqrt(1 − ρ² cos²e), where ρ = R_Qaia / r_Primus ≈ 0.1511. Once you have primsine(e) from your measured elevation, you solve cos λ = primsine(e) / cos φ, where φ is your latitude. The sign of λ (east or west) comes from Primus's azimuth relative to due south. No time measurement, pure geometry!
this is insane. so like, primsine(90°) = 1 because you're directly under Primus at the prime meridian??

Exactly! And primsine(0°) = ρ ≈ 0.1511, which gives you the visibility horizon -- Primus can be seen from roughly ±81° longitude from the prime meridian (though the range narrows at higher latitudes). The whole thing is SO elegant because ρ is just a fixed physical constant of the Qaia-Primus system. One primsine table, precomputed once, works forever. No updates needed.
so random question -- do weeks work the same way here as like real life or
Nope, they're different! Qaia has 8-day weeks. It's based on one of the moons called Tertius -- it orbits every 8 days or so, so that's what people use for their weekly cycle.
oh thats actually kinda cool. so like is tertius the main moon or something

It's one of the main ones, yeah. When you look up at Qaia's sky, Tertius is slightly bigger than Earth's Moon and almost the same brightness. Pretty noticeable. There are other moons too but Tertius is the one that matters for timekeeping because of that 8-day orbit.
wait how many moons does qaia even have lol

Five main ones! Tertius is the one that defines the week. There's also Quartus which is kind of like our Moon, Secundus which goes backwards in its orbit, and then Primus which is tiny and doesn't move in the sky. Sextus and Septimus are smaller and farther out. It's a lot but the main one you'd notice is probably Tertius.
okay so I'm building a character from Rathakos and I keep hearing about dragon cults in the mountains. what's that all about? are there actually people worshipping dragons still?

Yeah, there are. The notes mention mountain communities in Rathakos that still follow old dragon cults -- these are hanging on in the Red Mountains, especially after all the upheaval during the Ryraxian War. So if your character is from the mountains or the Southern Reaches, running into dragon cultists wouldn't be weird at all.
huh, interesting. so like... is this a holdover from when there was actually a dragon empire or something?

Sort of. There *was* an old Draconic empire, though it wasn't actually centered where you might think -- Qis'mos is where its institutions lasted the longest after the dragons departed. But yeah, these mountain communities seem to be keeping the old religion alive. And it's not just Rathakos -- Forthyr has a whole religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, and the Dragoncaller dynasty there gives them tacit encouragement.
wait, the Dragoncaller dynasty? I thought they were sorcerer-kings or something?

Right -- Forthyr is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, the latest in a line of sorcerer-kings. The dynasty *used to* be dragonborn, before the bloodlines ran thin. So even though they're not dragons anymore, they've got that draconic heritage and there's this whole sect in Forthyr pushing for the dragons to come back. The Dragoncallers aren't stopping it.
so are the cults in the Rathakos mountains connected to the ones in Forthyr, or is it separate stuff?

That's where the notes get fuzzy -- they mention both exist but don't say how connected they are. The Rathakos cults seem to be localized mountain communities keeping old traditions alive, while Forthyr's sect is more organized and politically tied to the Dragoncaller court. Whether there's overlap or communication between them, whether they worship the same things or different aspects of the empire -- that's blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. Could be a good hook for your character's backstory though.

okay so I'm working on an Edran noble merchant character, and I'm trying to get a feel for Qaia's whole deal before I lock in the backstory. What's the actual tidal contribution from each moon? I figure that would matter a lot for someone from a major port city like Jia.

Good instinct -- tides absolutely matter for a port power. Here's the breakdown: the three inner moons do most of the oscillating work. Tertius pulls at about 2.04 times lunar strength, Secundus at 1.47 times, and Quartus at 0.87 times. Combined that's roughly 4.4 times Earth's tidal force, which is intense. The outer moons, Sextus and Septimus, are too far out to matter much.

But there's something weird: Primus, the small geostationary moon, doesn't contribute to the oscillating tides at all. It just raises a permanent 2 centimeter static bulge in the ocean and stays there.
Hold on -- Primus doesn't create tides? How is that even possible? It's still a moon, right?

Yeah, it is, but here's the catch: Primus is geostationary. It orbits perfectly in sync with Qaia's rotation, so it hangs fixed over one point on the equator and never moves across the sky. Because it doesn't sweep around, there's no tidal bulge chasing it -- instead it raises a permanent static bulge at that one spot, kind of like how the Sun does a much smaller version on Earth. All the daily tidal rhythm on Qaia comes from Tertius, Secundus, and Quartus moving across the sky. So from Jia's perspective you get complex, incommensurate tidal cycles from three different moons, but at least Primus is predictable.
okay so I'm reading about Qaia's moon system and Quartus is just... Earth's moon, right? Same size, same distance, same everything. Doesn't that feel kind of lazy?

You're reading the notes correctly. Quartus is literally described as 'a clone of Earth's Moon at roughly the same distance and mass' -- same radius, same density, same albedo. The period is 29.50 days, matching Luna's monthly cycle, and it orbits at 1.05 LD versus Luna's 1.00 LD. So yeah, it's intentionally cloned.

But here's the thing: it's not lazy. It's a deliberate choice. Quartus serves a specific function -- it's *the* calendar body. The 29.5-day cycle gives Qaia a natural monthly rhythm for timekeeping. On a world with five moons on wildly incommensurate periods (Tertius at 8.15 days, Secundus at 4.53, Sextus at 57.69, Septimus at 106), having one predictable moon that anchors a stable calendar is actually useful worldbuilding.

I guess, but it still feels weird to have one moon that's identical to a real moon while the others are all alien. If you're designing a world, why make half the system realistic and half fantastical?

That's a fair criticism, and it points to a real design tradeoff. But consider: Quartus isn't the only 'realistic' moon. Tertius is also fairly dense and rocky. Secundus and Tertius together produce 3.5 times the tidal force of all Earth moons combined. Primus is the truly fantastical one -- a magically anchored geostationary moon.

The actual design philosophy seems to be: start with a realistic moon system that creates interesting, workable tides and calendars, then layer the fantastical bits on top. Quartus is there because once you commit to five incommensurate orbital periods with a triple-moon alignment calendar, you *need* one boring anchor that doesn't require constant recalculation. It makes the rest of the complexity actually navigable for a culture.
Huh. Actually that makes sense. I didn't think about it as an anchor for the rest of the system.
Right. The moment you add five moons with five different cycles, your calendar becomes a nightmare unless you have something boring to fall back on. Quartus is the boring part. That's its job.

okay so i've been working through the primsine formula for like two hours and then i read that ryraxos is 'not truly that impressive of a dragon' -- how is that possible if he literally controlled most of the continent for thirty years??

Great question! So Ryraxos is bronze and relatively young for a dragon -- maybe sixty years old. And the notes are explicit: he's less impressive than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery. Formidable, sure, but not some ancient reality-warping threat. The wild part is how he actually rose to power -- he started out as an artillery officer. Dragons just make excellent artillery officers because they can help carry the guns. So his whole rapid ascent came from that practical military advantage, not raw draconic power.
WAIT. He became a continental threat because he was good at LOGISTICS?? Like dragons can actually carry artillery and that alone got him to the top??

YES! That's exactly it! His rapid rise literally began with his exploits as an artillery commander. And yeah, when you think about it, that's kind of genius -- forget magic and ancient power, he just figured out that dragons + heavy guns = unstoppable. It's such a grounded reason for a dragon to become a major threat. Most dragon overlords are about ancient curses or world-ending magic; Ryraxos is just really good at moving cannons around.
yo so I'm hearing about this Great Western Bridge thing. isn't that where the drow are camped out or something? or am I mixing stuff up

Slight mix-up -- the drow did get involved in the Ryraxian War and some ended up staying on the surface, but the Great Western Bridge isn't their base. That's in Qaringil, which is an island city-state out in the central sea. The bridge is one of two massive wizard-made bridges that help cross the sea to connect the islands.
ok but like... why does it matter. it's a bridge. is it just flavor or does it actually do something

Fair question. Qaringil's whole power comes from controlling sea trade through the central sea, right? The Great Western Bridge is part of that -- Qaringil's fortifications literally dominate it. Plus the Gondolier Guild (one of the major power centers in the city) maintains and controls both bridges. So if you want to move cargo, armed forces, merchants, whatever -- through that corridor, you're either paying Qaringil or dealing with them directly. It's leverage.
wait but you said this is an island city. how does a bridge matter if they're already surrounded by water

Good catch. Qaringil sits on an island between the two bridges, and the bridges cross the central sea to the mainland. So yeah, it's on an island, but the bridges mean people don't *have* to take a boat -- they can march an army across if they're willing to cross Qaringil's fortified territory. That's exactly why controlling the bridges matters. You can deny passage, levy tolls, or hold off armies. That's real power in a trade-focused city.
hey, been reading through your notes and Qama keeps coming up. what's the deal with it? why is it such a big deal?

Qama is the holy city of Qis'mos and the traditional seat of the Church of Bahamut. So basically the religious capital of the continent -- Bahumut worship is huge in Qis'mos, and Qama is where it all centers. That alone makes it important, but there's more to it now.
okay but the "now" sounds ominous lol. what happened?

During the late phases of the Ryraxian War, the drow occupied Qama. They didn't wreck it -- the city itself wasn't despoiled -- but they stayed. So now there's a drow high priest there named Qe'lth IV that the drow appointed. Meanwhile the legitimate alternate candidate, Qachu II, is operating out of Qis with what's left of the religious authority, but without any of the actual relics of office. So you've got this religious schism -- legitimate authority but no relics, versus relics-in-hand but under drow occupation.
oh damn, that's juicy. so it's basically a contested holy city, great for tension.

Exactly. Lots of potential there. You've got religious factions, drow politics, Qis'mos trying to figure out what to do about it, the broader question of whether the drow are actually leaving or settling in... it's a powder keg.
so I keep hearing about dragons being artillery or whatever -- like why would that even matter?

Straight answer: dragons can carry the guns. Artillery pieces are heavy and cumbersome, right? A dragon can help transport them to where they need to be, position them, move them quickly if tactics shift. That alone gives you a massive tactical advantage over forces stuck hauling cannons by hand or ox-cart.
oh huh, okay. so like, heavy lifting basically

Exactly. But there's more to it than just the grunt work. A good artillery officer needs to think about positioning, sight lines, trajectory calculations, coordinating with ground forces. A dragon gives you all that *plus* they can actually fly and scout positions, do reconnaissance. And of course they can fight themselves if needed. It's not just logistics -- it's force multiplication at the strategic level.
wait, are you talking about someone specific?

Yeah, Ryraxos. He's a bronze dragon, relatively young for his kind -- maybe sixty years old. His rise to power in the recent Ryraxian War actually *started* with his work as an artillery commander. He got so good at it, so effective, that he eventually became the military leader of the whole war effort. Dragons aren't invincible in this world -- the notes say a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery can take one down -- but having one that understands both magic and firepower? That's a huge advantage, especially in a long, grinding conflict like the thirty-year war.
and he's... gone now?

Exiled to Secundus, our second moon. He accepted it after his defeat, so he's out of the picture for the current campaign. But yeah, that's how significant dragons can be -- one officer changed the entire course of a continent's war for decades. That's why the concept of a dragon as an artillery commander is such a big deal in this setting.
quick question -- why does Edran nobility have to be half-elf? that seems super arbitrary

It's not arbitrary in the sense that it's a hard rule -- Edran nobility must be between one quarter and three quarters elf. But you're right that the notes don't explain *why* this constraint exists. It's been the standard for four hundred years under the Keyberos dynasty, but the reasoning behind it isn't documented.
okay but like... that's so weird. is it magic? a law? did some king just decide elves were cool and make it official? what's the worldbuilding reason?

Honestly, we don't know. The notes state it as a fact but don't explain the reasoning. It's one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. It could be historical, magical, cultural, genetic -- there's room for a lot of explanations. But yeah, it's the kind of detail that should have a story behind it, and it's just not written down in the campaign material yet.
So the DM just... hasn't documented it? They're not being lazy about it?

Right. The setting material has some intentional gaps, and this is one of them. The rule is definitely real and canon, but the reasoning is still in the DM's head. It could actually be a great hook for a player to explore -- finding out the history behind it.
yo so I missed last session and everyone kept talking about this Primus thing for navigation? But like... wouldn't the atmosphere just mess that up? Especially if you're at the horizon?

Good question -- and yeah, atmospheric refraction is a real problem. The notes actually call this out directly. Near the visibility boundary, Primus is low on the horizon, and that's where atmospheric refraction becomes significant and precision drops. It's not magic, it's legit limited.
so like... is it even useful then? If you can only use it when it's high up, when can you actually see it?

From the equator, Primus is visible for about 162° of longitude -- roughly from the 81st degree west to the 81st degree east of the prime meridian. That's most of the hemisphere. At higher latitudes the range narrows (60° north gives you about 144° of visible longitude), but you still get a usable window. And here's the thing: precision is *best* when Primus is high in the sky anyway -- high over the equator, overhead at the prime meridian. That's when the elevation change per degree of longitude is steepest, so your measurement errors matter less. Near the boundary it gets shallow and hairy, but that's usually the edge case, not your main navigation zone.
okay but what if you're unlucky and stuck close to the horizon? like, how bad does it get?

Honest answer? It gets worse. The notes say sensitivity is highest at the equator and prime meridian, and lowest near the visibility boundary. So yeah, if Primus is low and you're near the edge of where you can see it, atmospheric refraction + shallow geometry = your measurement errors translate into bigger position errors. But practically speaking, a careful sextant observer gets about 6-7 km longitude accuracy in the *sweet spot* (high Primus, good latitude). Even degraded, that's still navigable. You just accept that you lose precision as Primus sinks toward the horizon and plan your observations accordingly.
ahhh okay so it's not broken, just... you gotta be smart about when you use it

Exactly. Primus still solves the longitude problem without a chronometer, which is huge. Atmospheric refraction is a real constraint, but it's a constraint you work around -- good instruments, good observation geometry, and awareness of when Primus is actually usable for precision work.
so uh, I've been reading about Primus and it sounds like Qaia's version of a geostationary satellite? is that basically right?

Yep, exactly right. Primus is in a geostationary orbit -- it's fixed over a single point on Qaia's surface and never moves from the sky of observers beneath it. Its orbital period is 1 Qaia sidereal day, just like Earth's geostationary satellites match Earth's rotation. The coolest part is that Primus's orbital inclination (23.5°) exactly cancels Qaia's axial tilt, so it sits in the equatorial plane. Truly locked in place.
okay but why magically anchor it? i mean, irl geostationary sats stay put naturally because of orbital mechanics.

Good question. The notes say Primus is magically anchored because without it, tidal forces would cause it to migrate over geological time (same as Earth's Moon drifting away from us very slowly). By magically fixing it, the DM keeps it as a permanent, stable reference point. Also, Primus is pretty small -- about 0.0001 Earth's moon mass, dark iron surface (albedo 0.06) -- so it's not as dynamically robust as a real sat. The magic is basically insurance that your navigation anchor doesn't quietly spiral away.
okay that makes sense. but wait -- is it actually *useful*? earth geostationary sats are amazing for weather and comms. what does Primus do?

Navigation. It's fixed at 0° latitude, 0° longitude -- the prime meridian. Because you can measure its elevation angle from any point, you can calculate your longitude without a chronometer. You measure the pole star's elevation for latitude, then Primus's elevation to get a longitude fix. It's the solution to the longitude problem that took Earth centuries of ships and dead sailors to solve. Navigators literally carry a lookup table (the "primsine function") and can triangulate their position from two angle measurements.
oh damn, that's actually clever. so it's like they got an instant navigation system built into the sky. what's the catch?

Visibility. Primus is only visible from one hemisphere -- the near side, out to roughly 81° longitude from the prime meridian. The far side of Qaia? No Primus. They have to use dead reckoning or stellar methods, which is way harder. So there's this built-in navigational asymmetry: half the world gets a navigation goldmine, the other half is sailing blind. Pretty cool world-building constraint.
hey, quick question - i'm reading about Qaringil's whole economy and i see criminal guilds mentioned. how do they actually operate in a city that's so guild-structured?

Good question - the notes mention three criminal organizations (Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses) that style themselves as guilds to blend into the system. But that's really all that's detailed - how they operate internally, their leadership structure, specific operations, all that's blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. The clever bit is just the framing: they mimic the legitimate guild structure.
wait, is this the main Qamis campaign or Qaringil?

Qaringil - the prequel set about 200 years earlier. The present-day Qamis campaign notes don't detail criminal organizations at all. But Qaringil being a city-state so heavily dependent on guild structure makes it a natural place for underground organizations to hide in plain sight by copying the guild framework.
ah okay, that makes sense. so they're like... shadow guilds competing with each other?

Yeah, basically. They adopt the guild framework (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses) and presumably operate through similar structures. Whether they actually register as official guilds or just use the organizational model as cover isn't spelled out - that's a worldbuilding gap the DM can fill in. But the idea of criminal organizations mimicking a city's economic structure to stay hidden is a solid detail.
okay so i just heard that the Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr used to actually BE dragonborn?? like as in actual dragonborn rulers?

YES! So the dynasty has been ruling Forthyr as sorcerer-kings for generations, but back in the day—we're talking like a couple centuries ago—the Dragoncallers were legitimately dragonborn. Now though, the current Dragoncaller Shanlong VI is the latest in the line, but the bloodlines have just... run thin over time. So they're no longer actually dragonborn anymore, even though the dynasty kept the title.
wait that's so cool though!! like the entire dynasty identity is built on a bloodline that's basically faded out? how is that not like... a huge problem for them politically?

Right? And here's the really interesting part—Forthyr itself is kind of backwards compared to the rest of Qamis, and there's actually a whole religious sect there pushing HARD for the return of the Dragon Emperors. The Dragoncaller dynasty doesn't exactly shut that down either, if you know what I mean. They seem pretty okay with it, at least tacitly. So instead of it being a liability, the faded dragonborn bloodline might actually feed into the whole mystique—like, "the dragons will return through us," that kind of thing.
oh damn, so it's less of a weakness and more of... propaganda fuel? that's kind of genius actually

Pretty much! As long as people believe the bloodline still matters—even if it's not literally dragonborn anymore—the Dragoncallers keep their authority. And with a whole religious movement backing the idea that dragons SHOULD return? That works in their favor.
yo, so I'm thinking about running a one-shot in Qaringil and I'm wondering -- how much is actually defined for Westron? Could I use it as a setting?

Westron is... pretty much a blank slate, honestly. The DM explicitly says in the notes: "The continent to the West is important and I have not yet prepared any material!" So you've got total freedom there, but no established lore to build on. The only real detail is that the religions are different -- mostly they don't worship Bahumat like the rest of Qamis does. Other than that, it's wide open.
huh okay, so completely blank. what about the big island to the south -- Freeport? that seems more developed?

Freeport is defined *just* a little bit more, but not by much. It's described as a city of exiles and pirates on the shore of a big, mostly unexplored island, and there's a jungle pressing up against it. The notes mention the running joke that people in the city try not to look too hard at the jungle -- hint being that maybe the dragons went there. But that's really it. No detail on the interior, the politics of Freeport itself, or what's actually in that jungle.
okay so basically if I wanted to do something set in Qaringil proper, I've got solid material, but anything exotic is on me to invent?

Exactly. Qaringil itself is detailed -- the Five Families, the seven major guilds, the politics, the city layout. The islands in the central sea have some character. Edrad, the Holy Qis Empire, Artkos, the Red Mountains -- all have enough meat to run with. But Westron, most of the big southern island, anything beyond the mapped nations? Total blank spaces. The DM's explicitly flagged those as things players can help fill in with their backstories.
fair, that actually works for a one-shot. i can keep it contained to the city and the other island powers and not worry about the bigger world building. thanks!
yeah that makes sense -- Qaringil's got enough intrigue with the Five Families and the guild politics that you could easily run a tight one-shot without ever leaving the city. good luck with it!
what's the deal with the Ryraxian War? like is it a Spellplague moment for the setting?

Not quite -- this is homebrew, so no Spellplague analogue. The Ryraxian War was a 30-year conflict that just ended before the Qamis campaign starts. It began when Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire pacted to march on Edrad, depose the Revolutionary Council, and restore the Keyberos dynasty. Bookmakers actually bet it would end in 60 days. Took 30 years.
30 years?? and a dragon was in it? like is this a Mystra's-death-type thing or...

There's a dragon involved -- Ryraxos, bronze, maybe 60 years old. He actually saved Edrad from losing, but then waged aggressive war across the continent himself for about two and a half decades. He's now exiled to Secundus (Qaia's second moon), so he's out of play going forward. The setting changed, sure, but it's not cataclysmic in the FR sense.
okay so if it changed that much, how's that affect Qaringil? my character's a gondolier there and I'm trying to figure out what everyone's so tense about lately.

Ah, I should clarify -- if your character is a Qaringil gondolier, you're in the Qaringil prequel campaign, set about 200 years before the Ryraxian War. Qaringil is an island city-state with its own rich politics back then: five major families, seven guilds, a republic with an Exarch. The war is future history from your character's view. As for how the war shapes the present-day islands, that's actually a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
oh gotcha, so Qaringil is the prequel. so in present-day Qamis the islands are just mercenary robber barons selling their navies?

Yep. In present-day Qamis, the islands are a thassolocracy -- nautical power brokers selling their military services to whoever's paying. The notes don't go into specific detail about individual cities or their politics in that era, but that's the general picture.

ok so i'm seeing this thing in the notes about full-moon eras and new-moon eras for like a 12-year cycle? what's that actually mean? i'm trying to plan some navigation work and i want to understand the lunar geometry here

Good question. On Qaia, the three inner moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—go through natural alignment cycles. A "full-moon era" is a stretch of time (usually a couple years) where the three moons align to all-full almost constantly; a "new-moon era" is the opposite, where all-new alignments dominate. They alternate with a full cycle of about 12 years.

So in a full-moon era, you'll get multiple triple-full-moon alignments a year—some of them almost exact, all three within 0.1% of syzygy simultaneously. In a new-moon era, same thing but all-new instead. Outside those windows, you'll get scattered alignments maybe 5% off, but the "era" is defined by which type dominates.

huh. that's way messier than Forgotten Realms, right? i remember in FR the moon just kind of does its thing on a regular cycle. how does this even work mathematically? like why would they synchronize like that?

Qaia's not Forgotten Realms—different system. The alignment happens because Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus have incommensurate orbital periods (4.53 days, 8.15 days, 29.50 days), so their phase angles evolve at different rates. Every few years their orbital geometry converges into a configuration where they're all close to the same illumination state (all full or all new) for an extended window. Then it drifts apart again and a different alignment type dominates.

It's not designed in—it's just what the orbital mechanics produce. The DM ran a simulation of the system, so the calendar in the notes reflects what actually happens.

okay so like, i'm actually hand-working primsine calculations for navigation, and i'm wondering—does being in a new-moon era vs a full-moon era change how useful Primus is for longitude work? or is Primus kind of independent of this whole thing?

Primus is independent. It's geostationary and doesn't phase like the other moons—it's always at the same illumination from any given location (its full-moon brightness is always about −9.62 magnitude). So whether you're in a new-moon era or a full-moon era, Primus is a stable longitude reference.

The three inner moons' alignment cycles matter more for calendars and predictive work. If you're doing primsine navigation, you measure Primus's elevation whenever it's visible and clear—era doesn't change the math. Though honestly, the brightest nights for general observation would be the full-moon eras, just because you'd have three bright moons in the sky instead of three dark ones.
wait, so in a new-moon era, all three moons are new at the same time? that seems dark as hell. how does anyone navigate without them?

Yep—in a new-moon era, all three inner moons are invisible for extended stretches. But you still have Primus (if you're in the near hemisphere), and you still have stars. The pole star gets you latitude any night; Primus gets you longitude if it's clear. Primus is small and dim (about 10.4 arcminutes, magnitude −9.62), but it's steady and never rises or sets. You don't need Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus to navigate—they're useful for calendars and for lighting up the night, but Primus does the heavy lifting.

That said, an all-new era would make for dark, quiet nights for ocean travel. Probably why different maritime traditions developed different customs around era timing.
huh, that's interesting. how often do you get those exact triple-full or triple-new moments? like how tight do the alignments actually get?

The notes give a calendar for 2253–2263. The tightest triple-fulls and triple-news in that decade are essentially perfect. For example, Feb 13, 2253 at 18h is a triple-full with all three moons at 99.8–100%, and May 24, 2262 at 03h is a triple-new with all three within 0.1% of each other.

Within a given era, the best alignments are that tight—all three within 0.1% of syzygy simultaneously. It's not common (maybe once a year per era), but it happens. Outside the era, you might get alignments within 5% a few times a year, but they're not as clean.

okay but like... how does that even show up in primsine work? like obviously the three moons don't figure into primsine directly, but when you're out on the ocean in a new-moon era with all three moons completely dark, you're relying entirely on Primus for lunar longitude, yeah?

Exactly. Primus is your only lunar reference for longitude in a new-moon era. You measure its elevation, look up primsine(e), divide by cos(latitude), reverse-lookup to get λ, and you're done—same as any other night. The darkness of Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus doesn't touch that calculation.

Now, Primus is small and dim, so measuring its elevation precisely (to the 0.1° you'd want for good accuracy) requires a decent instrument and clear sky. But mathematically, the era doesn't matter. You just need to see Primus and nothing else is required.
so i've been tracking the triple moon alignments over a few campaigns now, and i noticed they go in cycles. why do the eras keep flipping between full and new?

It's the orbital mechanics. Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus have periods that don't divide evenly -- about 4.5, 8.15, and 29.5 days respectively. Since they're incommensurate, their alignment geometry cycles over time. You get stretches where all three tend to bunch up near full simultaneously (a full-moon era), then stretches where they cluster near new together (a new-moon era). The whole pattern repeats roughly every 12 years. And when alignments do cluster in an era, they cluster *tight* -- sometimes all three within 0.1% of syzygy at the same moment.
ok so 12-year cycle makes sense. where are we in that right now? we're in 2262 in the current campaign.

You're sliding into a strong new-moon era. The decade started in a full stretch (2253-2254), dipped into new (2255-2258), popped back up to full (2259-2261), and then entered new again. May 24, 2262 at 03h is actually one of the closest triple-new alignments of the decade -- all three moons within 0.1% of syzygy. So yeah, you're right in the thick of a new-moon era.
so i'm looking at running qaringil as maybe a one-shot or short campaign. who are the dragon emperors? i see them mentioned in relation to forthyr and there's some sect that wants them back?

oh yeah, this is a cool thread! the dragon emperors are historical -- they used to rule qaia (or at least large parts of it), but they've departed by the time qaringil takes place. what's left is the institutions and remnants of the draconic empire, especially in qis'mos, where old draconic language and traditions stuck around the longest after the dragons left.

but here's the fun part: there's an actual religious sect in qaringil's era that wants them back. and in forthyr? the dragoncaller dynasty -- the sorcerer-kings who rule -- they're tacitly encouraging it. maybe even actively supporting it. this sect has some real political backing, which is wild.
oh that's GREAT. so the dragoncallers are themselves descended from dragons or something?

exactly! the dragoncaller line started out as dragonborn, but by the time of the qaringil campaign, bloodlines have run thin -- they're mostly human now, just with that sorcerous legacy. that actually makes their support for "bring back the dragon emperors" even juicier, right? like, they're clinging to this dragon heritage through magic and political backing of a religious movement.

so in qaringil you've got this undercurrent of "what if the dragons returned" and a dynasty in forthyr that would probably *welcome* it. that's a hell of a hook for a campaign.
and this is specific to the qaringil era right? not something present in qamis?

good question -- the sect and dragoncaller support for dragon return show up in both eras. but the context is really different. in qaringil (~200 years earlier), it's more of an active political force. in qamis present day, it's still there but the world has moved on a lot -- ryraxos just got exiled to the moon, the keyberos dynasty is restored, everything's being reorganized.

so yeah, if you're running qaringil, the dragon emperor return movement is a living, breathing thing with real institutional backing. that's way more immediate as a plot hook than the present day.
Yo! My character's from Forthyr and follows the Dragon Emperor revival sect. Is High King Ezevir Arth gonna be cool with that or what?

Ha, okay so just to clear this up -- Ezevir Arth is the High King of Artkos, not Forthyr. Artkos is rebuilding its empire but not specifically about dragons. The Dragon Emperor revival sect is in Forthyr, and that's actually where your Dragoncaller (Shanlong VI) gives it tacit support. Which era are you playing in -- present-day Qamis or the Qaringil prequel?
Oh wait, there's different eras? Yeah I'm confused now. Which Ezevir am I even asking about?

Ah, good question! So:

**Qamis present** (post-Ryraxian War): High King Ezevir Arth rules Artkos, trying to restore empire dominance.

**Qaringil prequel** (~200 years earlier): Ezevir Arth II was King of Artkos -- he doubled the kingdom's size, and then his daughter Ezvia Arth I took over and aimed to expand further.

If you're in present-day Qamis, it's just Ezevir Arth (the current one). The Dragon Emperor sect support comes from your Dragoncaller in Forthyr, not from Artkos.
Okay yeah that makes sense. So current Ezevir is doing his own thing with Artkos, not the dragon stuff. Got it.

Exactly. Ezevir's focused on consolidating Artkos's power after the thirty-year war. Your sect stuff is happening back home in Forthyr with Dragoncaller Shanlong VI -- he's the one giving it the quiet nod of approval, though Forthyr as a whole is pretty isolated and 'somewhat backwards' compared to the rest of Qamis.
So my character's from Forthyr and getting into that Dragon Emperor revival theology. Where's Ath'r at? Is that somewhere this whole movement would actually have traction?

Ath'r's an interesting choice for that kind of character. It's a central enclave of Artkos - geographically in the middle of the continent - ruled by High King Ezevir Arth. But here's the hook: it used to be the capital of the Holy Qis Empire, which ruled central Qamis maybe two hundred years ago. So there's real imperial history baked into the place, which could definitely speak to someone interested in the old Dragon Emperor stuff.
So it was actually the seat of an empire? What happened - did Artkos just roll in and take it over?

Artkos did conquer it about two hundred years ago, and it's been the capital of various empires going back even further than that. But the notes don't actually detail *how* that conquest happened - whether it was military, a coup, negotiation falling apart, any of that. You get that Artkos had a King Ezevir Arth II who expanded their holdings significantly, and his daughter Ezvia is the current High King, but the specific story of how Ath'r became their seat isn't fleshed out.

Okay so is Ath'r still got that imperial culture going? Like, dragon worship, Dragon Emperor loyalists, that kind of thing? Would my character find people who'd actually care about the revival movement?

That's a blank space. The notes mention that the old Draconic empire's institutions 'persisted the longest' in Qis'mos after the dragons left - and Ath'r was the capital of the Holy Qis Empire, so some of that history is definitely there. But there's nothing on whether that translated into actual Dragon Emperor factions, whether there are imperial loyalists still around, what the religious or cultural scene looks like now under Artkos rule. That could be a perfect thing for your character to discover or dig into once the campaign kicks off.
Got it. What about the city itself - size, population, layout? Anything like that?

Nope. We know it's important - important enough to be Artkos's capital and to have been the seat of empires for centuries - but actual details about what it looks like, how many people live there, how it's organized... the DM's left that open. More blank space for you to work with or for the campaign to fill in.
yo, so like -- national languages are hard to understand across regions? that sounds kinda artificial for a campaign tbh

It's not artificial actually. The notes are explicit: speakers from the capital of Artkos would have trouble understanding someone from Rathakos even though both speak Artklan. So you speak the prestige dialect fluently as your baseline, plus you pick one regional dialect. Outside those, comprehension gets real hard. It's how languages actually work with regional fragmentation.
okay but like... the war ended thirty years ago. wouldn't dialects naturally converge back together? seems like there'd be pressure to understand each other

Fair point. But the notes don't say how fast that's happening. Think about what went down: thirty years of war, borders redrawn, territories reshuffled, populations fragmented. That kind of chaos actually locks dialect differences in place instead of smoothing them out. You're not in stable peacetime where merchants can standardize over time -- you're right after massive upheaval. The language barriers are a consequence of the post-war situation.
huh. okay that actually makes sense. my forthyr dragon emperor sect guy would struggle understanding edran or kard people then. could be good rp tbh

Exactly. Could make for some good RP actually -- your character from the northeast having genuine communication friction with coastal merchants or Edran nobility. Makes those scenes more interesting when people don't just automatically understand each other.
so like, i saw there are like 7 moons? does each one do something with the tides?

Good question! Not all of them contribute equally. The big tidal drivers are Tertius, Secundus, and Quartus -- together they create about 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's Moon, so you get some pretty dramatic ocean tides.

Primus is special: it's geostationary, so it hangs motionless in the sky and doesn't sweep across the horizon. Instead of creating daily tides, it just raises a permanent ~2 cm bulge in the oceans -- think of it as a permanent geographic feature rather than a daily cycle.

Sextus and Septimus are so far out that their tidal contribution is basically negligible.
wait, 4.4 times as strong? isn't that... like, a lot? would the ocean be different?

Absolutely, yeah. Earth's tides are mostly driven by one moon (Luna), and Qaia's three inner moons are creating way more tidal stress than that. You'd definitely see higher tides, more dramatic coastal effects, and the tidal currents would be more powerful. It probably shapes a lot of how people navigate and build along the coasts. The exact mechanics of how much higher the tides get depend on local geography and the orbital geometry, but it's definitely noticeable.
cool. so like, do the moons all pull at the same time or?

Good instinct -- they don't. Each moon has a different orbital period, so they're constantly in different positions relative to each other. Tertius goes around every ~8 days, Secundus every ~4.5 days, and Quartus takes about a month. So sometimes they all tug in roughly the same direction and you get a really big tide (called a 'spring tide' on Earth), and sometimes they're pulling against each other and you get a smaller tide. The notes actually have a whole calendar of when the three moons align -- the really strong alignments happen a handful of times a year.
hey, thinking about running qaringil as a one-shot. what's the significance of qama to bahamut worshipers?

Qama's the holy city of Bahamut -- it's where the Church of Bahamut has its traditional seat in Qis'mos. So spiritually, that's the center for Bahamut worship across the region. Makes sense as a focal point for religious stuff.
ok so qe'lth iv runs things there, right? thought I read that somewhere

Ah, that's a present-day Qamis detail, not Qaringil. In the current campaign (after the Ryraxian War), the drow occupied Qama during the late war and installed Qe'lth IV as high priest. That actually created a schism because the legitimate Bahamut clergy, including Qachu II, went to Qis with the relics. But in Qaringil, that's 200 years earlier -- none of that's happened yet. Qama is just the traditional holy city, no complications. You're good for the prequel.
oh damn, yeah I mixed those up. so for qaringil it's just... the spiritual center, and that's all the notes say?

Yeah, exactly. For Qaringil, Qama is the traditional seat of the Church of Bahamut -- that's what's in the notes. Beyond that it's a blank space. Depending on what your one-shot needs, you've got room to develop the city and what's happening there religiously. Could be a good anchor point for Bahamut-focused plots.

okay so real quick: Artkos. In the Qaringil thing it's just a little kingdom in the northwest minding its own business. But then in the present-day campaign it's this big continental power with exclaves all over the place. What's the story there? Did I miss something?

No, you got it right. Two hundred years is a lot of time for expansion. In Qaringil, Artkos was confined to its historical heartland in the northwest. King Ezevir Arth II had already doubled their territory, and his daughter Queen Ezvia Arth I was pushing for more expansion, but they were still a regional power. By the present day though, Artkos has grown massively -- they control multiple exclaves, including a central one with the capital at Ath'r and a southwestern one centered on Kard. High King Ezevir Arth (a later descendant) rules directly from Ath'r. So yeah: small regional kingdom to continental power over two centuries.
Okay, but Ath'r is this ancient city though, right? Like, not something they built. So how'd they actually get control of it?

Yeah, Ath'r is definitely ancient -- it was the seat of central Qamisi empires long before Artkos touched it. But about two hundred years ago, Artkos conquered it. That move was game-changing: suddenly they held the historical capital of a fallen empire, and they made it the center of their own power. So Ath'r became the engine of their continental expansion. It wasn't native territory they held all along; they took it by force.
Got it. So what's the catch in the present day? The notes make it sound like things didn't go smoothly after the war ended.

Yeah, the Ryraxian War was brutal and lasted thirty years. When it finally ended, Artkos had lost ground. The Artklan League is now barely holding together -- the notes say it looks like a 'pale shadow' of what Artkos claims to be, and all the nominally subject nations are clinging to quasi-independence. You've got Rathakos and Acrokos, new nations that emerged from the old Red Mountain provinces. Those territories used to be Artklan, then got occupied by Edrad during the war, and when Edrad pulled out, they declared independence. So yeah, Artkos is trying to restore dominance, but they're in a weaker position than they were before the war shook everything up.
so i keep seeing this triple-full moon alignment thing mentioned in the setting notes. is that actually mechanically relevant or just flavor text that the dm threw in?

It's in the actual almanac, so the DM definitely has plans for it. The next really tight triple-full alignment happens Feb 13, 2253 at 18h -- all three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) within 0.2% of full simultaneously. That's genuinely rare. The calendar shows you get a few pretty good ones in the decade, but that Feb 13 one is the closest.
okay but like... mechanically. does three full moons at once actually *do* anything? or is it just a celestial event that looks cool?

Fair question. Here's what the notes actually specify: those three moons drive most of Qaia's tidal load -- about 4.4 times what Earth's moon does. When all three are full and pulling in the same direction, the tides are extreme. Primus adds a permanent static ~2 cm bulge, but doesn't oscillate, so the daily rhythm comes from the other three. A triple-full alignment creates peak tidal stress. Whether that triggers earthquakes, opens planar rifts, affects monster behavior, or just makes nautical navigation a complete nightmare -- that's the DM's call. But the mechanical fact is real and in the notes.
hmm. so if the dm hasn't called it out explicitly, it's just a neat calendar detail?

Pretty much, yeah. The notes give you the table and the physics, but don't specify any mechanical trigger or event tied to it. It's a *loaded* detail though -- the kind of thing a DM would include if they're planning something. Extreme tides, synchronized full-moon light flooding the landscape, planar thinness, monster migration cycles keyed to tidal stress -- any of those could matter. But the notes themselves are silent on which, if any.
that actually makes sense. so may i ask -- when's the absolute closest one in that decade? could be a good plot hook.

Feb 13, 2253 at 18h is the closest triple-full: all three within 0.2% of full. But if you're looking at pure geometric perfection across the whole calendar, **May 24, 2262** is the closest alignment period-wide -- that's a triple-**new**, all three within 0.1%. Total darkness, all three moons dark simultaneously. Could be an even spookier hook depending on what the DM wants.
so wait, how do you actually navigate on Qaia? i know there's a geostationary moon thing, but what about like, basic latitude?

Latitude's straightforward on Qaia -- you measure the elevation of the pole star above your horizon, and that angle equals your latitude. So if the pole star is 30 degrees above the horizon, you're at 30 degrees north. It's the same principle as Earth, actually, and it's one of the oldest navigation tricks there is.
huh, okay. is that like how it works in Forgotten Realms? i feel like i've never heard anyone talk about star navigation there

I don't have details on how FR handles navigation, but Qaia's system is pretty grounded in real celestial mechanics. The key is that Qaia has a fixed rotational axis like Earth does, so there's a stable north pole star to navigate by -- observers on the same latitude will always see it at the same elevation. That's the whole trick.
wait, okay, so the pole star never moves? that's wild. and this is in the notes as like, actual navigation lore?

Yep, it's in the navigation guide. The pole star is fixed in the sky because Qaia's axis points toward it constantly. In the northern hemisphere it hangs at a steady elevation above the north horizon; in the southern hemisphere you'd use the south celestial pole the same way. It never rises or sets -- it's as constant as you can get.

that's actually really elegant. oh btw, quick question -- all those place names with Q in them, like Qama, Qis... those are pronounced with like a 'kh' sound right? i noticed the notes say that. is that from Old Draconic?

Yep, Q is pronounced 'Kh' -- it comes from Old Draconic. Old Draconic is the language of scholarly publications and treaties, and it's where a lot of the place names get their roots. That 'q without u' pattern you see throughout the setting (Qaia, Qamis, Qis, Qama, etc.) is all Old Draconic. The prestige dialects of modern languages like Qis are closest to Old Draconic, since Qis'mos was a historical center for the Draconic empire institutions.
oh nice, so it's like how Latin roots show up everywhere in... wait, which setting has the dragon empire stuff? is that Forgotten Realms too or is that Greyhawk?

Qamis is a homebrew setting, so I can't speak to FR or Greyhawk lore. But in Qamis, there was definitely a dragon empire way in the past -- Qis'mos retains some of those institutions even now. Old Draconic is basically the legacy language of that era.
makes sense. so back to latitude -- what if you're in a place where you can't see the pole star? like, do they have a backup method or is that just tough luck?

The notes don't cover a backup method for finding latitude if the pole star is obscured. Cloud cover or bad weather would definitely be a problem. The basic principle relies on having that clear sight to the pole, same as on Earth historically.
huh, weird. so i guess in Qamis you just don't navigate on cloudy nights. at least you have that weird geostationary moon for longitude, which is pretty sick.

Right -- and that's a major advantage for navigators in the near hemisphere. Primus gives you longitude without needing a chronometer, which is something Earth navigators struggled with for centuries. Combine that with pole-star latitude and you've got a solid position fix. Observers on the far side of Qaia never see Primus though, so they'd have to rely on dead reckoning or stellar methods.
yo so uh... I'm trying to understand the whole Qama situation. why do the drow even have it?

Okay so the short version: the drow were recruited by Artkos as allies during the Ryraxian War — a desperation move, basically. During the late phases of that war they occupied Qama, which is the holy city of Bahumat and the traditional seat of the Church of Bahumat. They're still there now.

They did appoint their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, which caused a schism — the official Church split and an alternate candidate, Qachu II, set up in Qis with the relics. But the drow haven't actually despoiled the city, so it's... stable? For now?
wait wait wait. so Artkos recruited them as a desperation move but then just... let them stay? with a holy city? and now there's a split church? that seems like a lot of threads

Yeah okay, fair. The notes don't actually explain *why* Artkos is okay with them staying, or whether this was negotiated after the war ended, or if they just kind of dug in and nobody's managed to shift them yet. The notes are pretty clear that the drow remain in about a dozen scattered enclaves, with Qama being the biggest one, but... the *how* or *why* that arrangement is stable? That's blank space.
blank space. okay. so what about the whole "they were recruited as allies" thing — like, where did the drow even come from? were they already on the surface? this setting is getting complicated

Yeah, the notes don't say. They mention the drow were recruited as a desperation move by Artkos, but not where they came from originally, how long they've been on the surface, or what their own agenda is. That's all unfilled.

I get it though — a lot of the interesting politics right now (drow enclaves, the schism, Artkos's post-war stability, why nobody's fought them over Qama) just... isn't written yet. It's a lot of moving pieces and the DM's left it open.
so basically the DM has a hook and hasn't filled in the scaffolding. makes sense I guess

Yeah, exactly. The main facts are solid — drow hold Qama, there's a religious schism because of it, they're in scattered enclaves — but the *why* and *how stable* and *what happens next* are all yours to work with or ask about. Could be adventure hooks, could be your character's backstory. Blank spaces are features, not bugs.
hey, what's the dragon emperor cult thing in Forthyr? totally zoned out last session

So Forthyr is ruled by a sorcerer-king dynasty called the Dragoncallers -- currently Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. There's a religious sect there calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors from way back, and here's the weird part: the Dragoncaller dynasty actually *supports* this sect, or at least doesn't shut it down. Tacit encouragement.
lol wait that seems backwards. why would the ruling dynasty be cool with a sect wanting to bring back the emperor?

Right, that's the tension. The notes don't spell out their exact motivations, but there's context: the Dragoncaller dynasty used to be dragonborn, but the bloodline thinned out over time. So maybe they still feel tied to dragon culture, or it's ideology, or nostalgia. Also Forthyr is just described as "somewhat backwards," so the sect probably fits the cultural vibe. But the *why* is something your DM will have to develop.
so they're not dragonborn anymore?

Not in the present day, no. Shanlong VI is a sorcerer-king. The dragonborn heritage basically faded out as the bloodline thinned over generations. Still sorcerers with dragon connections, but not literally dragonborn anymore.
hey so i missed last session, can someone explain who ryraxos is and like... how a dragon just takes over the whole continent? seems kinda complicated lol

Okay so Ryraxos is a bronze dragon -- relatively young, maybe sixty years old. He started as an artillery officer, which is actually a thing in this world because dragons are good at carrying guns. He got really good at it and rose through the ranks, and at some point during the Ryraxian War, Edrad was about to lose. Ryraxos showed up, turned the war around, and then... kept going. Waged aggressive war across the continent for about thirty years total. Bookmakers had bet the war would last sixty days; it lasted thirty years. Then he lost, and accepted exile to the second moon, Secundus. So he's not around anymore in the campaign.
wait why does that sound so cartoonish. dragons doing artillery? that's insane. and he just... ruled everything for 30 years? isnt that supposed to be really hard? i feel like im missing something

The artillery thing is just how he built his reputation -- he was good at logistics and command, not because he's some all-powerful godlike dragon. The notes actually call him "not truly that impressive of a dragon" for a dragon. He's formidable, sure, but a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery could take him. He can shapeshift, which helped, but he was never this unstoppable force. The thirty-year war was long partly because he was good at it, but also because the whole world was involved and it took time to mobilize against him and actually defeat him. So yeah, he ruled for a long time, but he's not as impossible as the name "dragon overlord" makes it sound.
okay so he's strong but not like god-tier strong... so he just got lucky? or is it more that everyone else was too divided to stop him?

Both probably. When Edrad needed him most -- they were losing -- he showed up and saved them. That bought him loyalty, reputation, and a base of power. And yeah, the various nations around him were probably not unified enough to stop him immediately. But eventually they did organize and beat him. Now he's exiled to Secundus and doesn't matter for the campaign you're in, which starts post-war in a place trying to figure out what peace looks like.
why is there an 8-day week? is that because of a moon?

Yeah, exactly. Tertius is one of Qaia's moons, and it orbits the planet every 8.15 days. That's close enough to 8 days that people use it as the basis for their weekly calendar -- when you can see the same moon in the same phase every roughly 8 days, it's a natural rhythm to organize your life around. Plus Tertius is bright enough and visible enough to make it useful. It's roughly the same size and brightness as Earth's moon.
cool, does Tertius matter for navigation or anything?

Not really for finding your way -- that's Primus's job. Primus is geostationary, which means it hangs in the same spot in the sky forever, and navigators can use its position to figure out their longitude without needing a chronometer. Tertius does pull on the oceans and is part of what drives Qaia's tides, but for actual navigation? Primus is the key.
So I'm looking at Forthyr. How exotic is this place? Is it just another forgettable sorcerer-king kingdom, or is there actual meat there?

Forthyr's in the northeast. It's ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, a sorcerer-king, and there's a religious sect that wants Dragon Emperors to return. The setting notes describe it as 'somewhat backwards.' That's what's publicly on the record so far.
Dragoncaller. So that's a title thing, not just flavor. What's the story with the name?

The dynasty title 'Dragoncaller' has carried through generations. The founder, Shanlong I, ruled about 200 years ago and was explicitly half-dragonborn. But by the present day, Shanlong VI is a sorcerer-king with bloodlines that have run thin -- no longer dragonborn.

Oh. So the entire premise of the dynasty is they *used* to be part-dragon and now they're not. And meanwhile the locals are actively pushing for the dragons to come back. That's actually solid -- there's real friction there.

Exactly. The religious sect agitating for Dragon Emperors gets tacit support -- even encouragement -- from the dynasty itself. Whether that's genuine nostalgia, political strategy, or something else, the notes don't say. But yeah: a ruling family slowly losing the trait they're named for, while their own people are demanding dragons in charge.
So what else has changed in Forthyr over those 200 years, or is that still blank space?

The religious sect and the 'somewhat backwards' characterization are consistent between both eras, but beyond that -- how the nation itself has grown, shifted politically, changed militarily -- that's mostly unwritten. The DM's flagged it as a blank space that could be filled in.
okay so I keep hearing about all these moons in the sky on Qaia? I'm completely new to D&D so this is all kind of overwhelming. How many moons are even up there?

Hey, no worries! So Qaia has seven moons total. The good news is you don't need to memorize all of them -- most of the time you'll only interact with a few. Three of them in particular are bright and visible pretty regularly, one stays fixed in the sky in the same spot, and two smaller ones hang out in the background.
Wait, one stays fixed? Like, doesn't move? That's weird.

Yeah! It's called Primus and it's genuinely wild. It's magically anchored in a geostationary orbit -- that means it hangs above the same spot on the equator and never rises or sets. If you're in the right hemisphere, you look up and it's just... there. Always in the same place in the sky. Never moves.
that's actually kind of cool. what's the deal with the other three bright ones?

So you've got Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Tertius and Quartus look almost identical in size -- both nearly as big as Earth's moon if you've ever seen that. Secundus is also pretty sizable, though a bit darker. All three of them move across the sky on different schedules -- Tertius takes about 8 days, Quartus takes about 29 days, and Secundus orbits backward and takes about 4.5 days. Because they're all moving on different cycles, you get different combinations of them in the sky on different nights.
8 days? Does that mean your week is 8 days instead of 7?
Good catch! Tertius's 8-day period is the basis for Qaia's week, so yeah -- weeks are 8 days long on this world. It's just one of those things that's different from Earth.
okay, cool. so what about that fixed moon -- Primus? Why does it matter that it doesn't move?

So this is where it gets really practical for adventurers. Because Primus never moves and always hangs above the equator, you can use it to find your longitude without needing a clock or chronometer -- you just measure how high it is above the horizon, and do some geometry.
