Not quite, though there's a parallel. A Potentate is an elected ruler who leads one of the independent regions in Qis'mos. Each region's Potentate is elected by three groups: the nobility, the clergy, and the guildmasters. And those same three can vote to impeach and replace the Potentate if they lose confidence.

Here's the interesting bit: it's a direct holdover from the old Draconic empire. When the dragons left, most of their institutions collapsed, but Qis'mos held onto theirs far longer than anywhere else—so what survives now is basically a modified version of that ancient system.
so the clergy has as much power as the nobility and guilds? that's not how it works here. why is the Church so influential in Qis'mos?

The Church of Bahamut basically inherited a seat of power from the old Draconic empire institutions. Their holy city is Qama, and they're not just a religious authority there—they're actual co-rulers, with formal governing power alongside the nobility and guilds. That's the key difference: in most places the clergy advises or ministers, but in Qis'mos they're institutional powerholders.
so real talk -- Qaia? is that just Earth with a Q slapped on it, or is there actual lore behind the name?

Good question. The notes don't actually explain the origin of the name Qaia itself -- it's just what the planet is called. One detail though: Q is pronounced like 'Kh' (from Old Draconic), so it's not the same letter-sound as Earth's Q. But yeah, the campaign notes are silent on whether there's a deeper reason for the name. Blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
huh, okay. reminds me of like, how Forgotten Realms just has Toril and Faerûn and you kinda... accept it. so Qaia is the planet, and the continent where we play is Qamis, yeah?

Exactly. Qaia is the planet. The continent where both Qamis (the present-day campaign) and Qaringil (the prequel, ~200 years earlier) are set is called Qamis. There's also a big unexplored island to the south where Freeport sits, some western continents mentioned but not detailed, and a bunch of central sea islands. The moons have their own names too -- Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, etc.
wait, moons plural? how many are we talking about? Forgotten Realms only really cares about Selûne...

Seven moons, actually. Primus (geostationary, stays fixed in the sky), Secundus, Tertius, Quartus (roughly Earth-Moon sized), Sextus, Septimus, and Quintus (which is basically a trace particle at the L4 point). The DM worked out the whole orbital mechanics -- albedos, periods, tidal forces, everything. It's pretty detailed, especially for navigation purposes.
okay that's wild. so like, this is definitely homebrew from the ground up then, not a reskinned Realms campaign?

Yeah, this is a proper homebrew setting. The notes are pretty explicit about being original -- different political map, different history (the Ryraxian War, the dragon Ryraxos, the Keyberos dynasty, the Holy Qis Empire 200 years ago), unique celestial mechanics. Definitely not Forgotten Realms, Greyhawk, or anything like that.
so i'm planning a qaringil one-shot. does the far-side navigation stuff apply? i'm looking at my notes and it seems like that's a prequel rule?

Ah, I see the mix-up! The far-side navigation rules aren't era-specific—they're part of the world physics and apply to both campaigns. 'Far-side' and 'near-side' refer to which hemisphere of the planet Qaia you're on, not which campaign timeline. Both Qamis (present-day) and Qaringil (~200 years earlier) share the same world and moon system. In Qaringil, if your characters are on the islands or continent, they're on the near-side and can use Primus for navigation just fine. The far-side rules would only matter if someone travels to the western continent or beyond.
right, ok. so in qaringil, everyone's on the near-side of the planet? or do i need to worry about this?

Pretty much. The Qaringil campaign is based on the islands of the central sea and involves travel to the mainland continent—all of which are on the near-side hemisphere where Primus is visible. The far-side of the planet (where Primus is permanently invisible) includes the western continent, Westron, and beyond. Unless your one-shot specifically sends players there, they won't be dealing with far-side navigation challenges. For far-side navigators elsewhere on the world, they'd have to rely on dead reckoning, stellar methods, or lunar-distance observations instead.
ok that makes sense. so like, if my rogue ends up on the far side somehow, they just can't navigate at all without someone trained?

Not quite—far-side navigation is just harder and different. A far-side navigator would use dead reckoning (tracking your heading and distance traveled), stellar transit observations (watching where stars cross the horizon), or lunar-distance methods (measuring angles between Secundus or Quartus and stars). It's more labor-intensive than measuring Primus's elevation, but it's still possible. So if your rogue ends up on Westron or the far-side, they wouldn't be helpless—they'd just need someone trained in those alternate methods, or they'd be worse at positioning than a near-side navigator would be.
yo so ive been reading about the bahamut schism and wondering if the moon eras have any religious significance? like could that be part of it?

A full-moon era is when Qaia's three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) frequently align near full simultaneously -- about five times a year. A new-moon era is the opposite, where they line up near new instead. They alternate roughly every twelve years. The calendar shows a full-moon era in 2253-2254, then a new-moon era from 2255-2258, then another full-moon era starting in 2259.

As for connecting this to Bahamut theology and the schism -- the setting notes don't spell that out explicitly. But it's totally plausible the Church might ascribe spiritual meaning to lunar eras. Could be a rich angle for your character to explore.
got it. so which era are we actually in right now?

The notes don't specify what year the Qamis campaign is set in, so I can't tell you which era you're currently in. But the alignment calendar runs through 2263, so the campaign is probably somewhere in that range. Your DM would know the exact year.
so what's the adventure scene like in Rathakos? seems like it's supposed to be a good hub

Rathakos has a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship, mostly because the Red Mountains are one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions in the interior of Qamis. That's the core of it—there's enough unexplored territory and risk that adventuring has become a cultural and economic mainstay.
ok but like, from a game perspective—what are we talking? Is this a place where you're running rat basements at level 1, or is there actual dungeon density? What's the typical treasure progression?

That's where I have to be honest: the notes don't specify encounter difficulty, dungeon density, or loot scaling. They tell you why Rathakos is adventure-forward—dangerous, underexplored mountains—but not the mechanics of what that actually looks like at table. That's a blank space your DM would need to fill in.
fair enough. what about the adventurer infrastructure? Guild structure, contacts, that kind of thing?

Again, not specified. The notes mention Rathakos is a mountain princedom with its capital, Rath, in the northern foothills, governed by Regent John Dunne. You'd reasonably expect some kind of adventurer guild, tavern networks, information brokers—all the typical infrastructure—but the notes don't detail any of it. That's another blank space.
one more thing—the notes mention dragon cults in the mountain communities. Is there mechanics on what those are? Are we talking like organized religion stat blocks, or...?

The notes just mention that some mountain communities still follow the old dragon cults, which is part of why Rathakos has internal unrest. But what those cults actually are—beliefs, practices, mechanical implications—isn't covered. Another blank space your DM would define.
wait who's Regent John Dunne?? keeps coming up when i read about Rathakos

John Dunne is the regent running Rathakos right now! So Rathakos got made a Princedom after the Settlements of Ath'r, but they still haven't actually found a Prince yet -- so Dunne's in charge while a Grand Council figures it out. He's dealing with some serious unrest though: there's instability in the Southern Reaches (annexed from Edrad, so people are still angry about that) and mountain communities that follow the old dragon cults. Not an easy position.

okay but okay but — rathakos is literally hemmed in by mountains right? edrad, artkos, qismos on the borders, red mountains everywhere... doesn't that absolutely wreck celestial navigation visibility? i've been working primsine calculations by hand and i'm realizing mountain terrain would be a NIGHTMARE

OH MAN yeah you just nailed it! The Red Mountains are explicitly called out as one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions in the interior. So valley floors, deep terrain, all that -- moon visibility is gonna be spotty as hell. Primus especially could be blocked depending on which way a valley runs. But here's the cool part: since Primus is geostationary and NEVER moves, you don't need to wait for ideal conditions or a specific time. You just need one clear sightline to measure its elevation angle, punch primsine, and you've got your longitude. That's why it's such a game-changer. And that brutal terrain is also exactly why Rathakos has such a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship -- people who can navigate that stuff are genuinely valued.
RIGHT so you could just climb to a ridge at any time and get a fix!! that's so sick. would the mountain communities have figured that out or are they more dead-reckoning types

The notes don't actually say! That's one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. But yeah, you'd think mountain communities trading with lowland merchants would've picked up celestial navigation pretty quick, especially with Primus being *right there* on the equator, fixed in the sky. Whether they prefer it over dead reckoning or local route knowledge is totally open. Could be a cool detail to explore if your campaign goes there.
yo, so there's a ton of nations on Qamis right? like, Edrad, Artkos, Rathakos... is it gonna be confusing keeping track of all of them?

Yeah, there are a lot of players. The big ones are Edrad (ruled by the Keyberos dynasty from Jia), the Artklan League (Artkos under High King Ezevir, plus Rathakos and Acrokos), Qis'mos, Forthyr, Lock, and the island city-states. The war just ended thirty years after the dragon Ryraxos got exiled, so everyone's trying to figure out the new power balance. There's definitely some friction -- the Keyberos dynasty is back in charge in Edrad, Artkos wants its old dominance back, and places like Rathakos are trying to hold onto independence. So yeah, a lot of moving parts.
okay cool, that makes sense. uh, random question -- does the tidal situation make things more chaotic? like, with all seven moons pulling at once, maybe that's part of why everything's so messy?

Ha, I like where your head's at. The tidal dynamics are actually wild. You've got Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all oscillating at different rates -- they're the big tidal drivers. Secundus has a 4.5-day period, Tertius is about 8 days (that's actually your week), and Quartus is 29.5 days. Combined they hit Qaia with about 4.4 times the tidal force Earth's Moon exerts. Plus Primus sits geostationary over the equator and just... stays there, creating a permanent 2-centimeter bulge. It's honestly a pretty complex tidal system.
wait, Tertius is 8 days? so your week is literally tied to a moon cycle? that's actually sick. and these three moons are never in phase with each other because their periods don't line up?

Exactly -- the week follows Tertius's period, which is a natural calendar anchor. And yeah, the three don't sync up neatly. But here's the wild part: they DO occasionally align. A few times per year all three are close to full or new simultaneously, and the best alignments can be nearly exact -- all three within 0.1% of syzygy at the same time. There are whole eras where full-moon alignments dominate, then it switches to new-moon alignments. The cycle flips roughly every 12 years.
okay that's insane. do people use those alignments for anything? like timekeeping or rituals or whatever?

The notes don't spell that out, but it would make sense. The alignment calendar definitely tracks when they happen, so navigators and timekeepers definitely know about them. As for rituals or cultural significance -- that's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. Could be something cool for your backstory if you wanted to lean into it.
hi, so i'm writing a character from rathakos and i'm trying to understand the actual history. like, was ryraxos really as unstoppable as everyone makes it sound?

Ryraxos is a bronze dragon, maybe sixty years old, so relatively young for a dragon. Not actually superhuman in the way legends make him sound -- the notes specifically say he's less formidable than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery. What made him powerful was that he got his start as an artillery officer. Dragons make excellent artillery officers because they can physically help carry the guns. So his rapid rise to power came from that military advantage, not from being an unusually powerful dragon.
okay but that doesn't explain the thirty-year war. if he's not that impressive, how did he control the continent for that long?

Fair question. The war didn't start because he conquered everything -- he actually saved Edrad from what looked like inevitable defeat. So he started as a hero, not a conqueror. Once he had Edrad's backing and their resources, he used that advantage to wage aggressive war across Qamis for the next two and a half decades. It's less about him being a unstoppable force and more about him having institutional backing, military innovation (the artillery thing), and the political momentum of being the guy who prevented a total collapse. Bookmakers thought the whole conflict would be over in sixty days, so clearly nobody expected a thirty-year grind either.
so edrad created the problem? and what does any of this have to do with the red mountain republic?

Basically, yes. When Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire during the war, the former mountain provinces -- which had been under Edran occupation -- were reorganized as the Red Mountain Republic. So you had this weird situation: Edran military control, but nominally a republic. When Edran was forced to withdraw later in the war, the opposition faction of the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran faction and split the region into Rathakos and Acrokos. So the Red Mountain Republic itself was short-lived -- it was basically the administrative structure Ryraxos imposed while Edrad still had the military muscle to hold it.
hold on. so the region where my character is from was *occupied* during the war, and now we're supposed to have independence? how stable is that actually?

Not very, honestly. Rathakos is currently governed by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council deliberates on finding an actual prince -- so there's no real leadership yet. The notes specifically mention internal unrest in many places: the Southern Reaches that Edrad occupied and then had to give back, and mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults. So you're in a region that's figuring out its own government after thirty years of war and foreign occupation, with factions that still have competing loyalties. That's unstable enough to be interesting for character backstory -- your character probably lived through the transition, or their family did.
okay, that actually makes sense. so ryraxos himself isn't really around anymore?

Right. He accepted exile to Secundus -- that's Qaia's second moon -- after his defeat. So he plays no role in the current campaign. He's gone, which is why the post-war political landscape is about nations trying to restore the old power structures, not about anyone fighting a dragon anymore.
I keep seeing Quintus mentioned in the moon tables but I don't see an actual moon there. What is Quintus?

Good catch -- Quintus isn't a moon in the traditional sense. It's a trace particle orbiting at the Sun-Qaia L4 point, which is 60 degrees ahead of Qaia in its orbit around the sun. It librates (wobbles) between roughly 45 and 80 degrees from Qaia over a 2,000-year cycle. The notes explicitly say it's "effectively massless" and "included for worldbuilding purposes."
So it's not actually a moon? Is it like... a rogue asteroid or something that drifted into an orbit?

It's more stable than that. The L4 and L5 points (the leading and trailing Lagrange points) are gravitational equilibrium points in the Sun-Qaia system -- objects there are naturally stable even without being bound to either body. Quintus sits at L4 and librates around that point because of perturbations from the other planets. But because it's "trace" (massless), it doesn't actually affect anything gravitationally. It's there, but it doesn't contribute to tides, orbital mechanics, or anything else.
That's a really specific detail to include if it doesn't do anything. Why would the DM put that in?

The notes say it directly: "included for worldbuilding purposes." It's the kind of detail a worldbuilder adds for texture and realism -- Qaia has a complex moon system, and if you're modeling that system carefully, the L4/L5 Trojans are a natural part of that picture. Even if Quintus never affects a plot or game mechanic, it exists in the world. It's also a signal that the DM is thinking about orbital mechanics at a pretty sophisticated level.
And the 2,000-year libration cycle -- is that connected to anything else in the system? Like, does it sync up with other long-term dynamics?

That's not spelled out in the notes, but it's worth noticing. The Moons document mentions that Primus is magically anchored (no tidal migration), and the freely-orbiting moons show stability over at least a 2,000-year simulation. So a 2,000-year libration cycle for Quintus fits aesthetically with that timescale -- it's the period over which the broader system evolves or cycles. Whether the DM intends a deeper connection between Primus's magic and Quintus's libration, or whether they just happen to share a timescale, isn't clear from what's written. But the DM clearly thinks in terms of multi-millennial rhythms.

That actually makes sense. It sounds like the moon system isn't just there for flavor -- there's real thought about long-term orbital dynamics and magical stability. Is there anything else hiding in the notes like this?

A few things stand out: the static 2 cm tidal bulge from Primus (which never oscillates, unlike real tides), the triple-moon alignment eras that shift over roughly 12-year cycles, and the detailed navigation tables for Primus-based longitude finding. Most of these have clear game-world utility -- you can actually navigate with Primus, the alignments could trigger events or calendar systems. But Quintus is different: it's elegant orbital mechanics with no immediate practical payoff. It suggests the DM is building a world that works on its own terms, whether or not players ever engage with those details.
hey just got back from a trip and missed last session. can you give me a recap on the Five Families of Qaringil? trying to remember who controls what

Sure! Here's the breakdown. The Five Families are the most powerful merchant families in Qaringil and control most of the major guilds.

Peixota (dolphin sigil) leads the Shipping Guild and has interests in mercantile and judicial. Manos (hand sigil) leads the Judicial Guild and is involved in silk and wool. Qanto (dove sigil) used to lead the Mercantile Guild but recently lost that to a minor gnomish family — they still have fingers in judicial and banking. Volmer (crow sigil) leads the Banking Guild with shipping interests. Vitelli (moth sigil) leads the Silk Guild and is also involved in shipping and mercantile.

There's also Tiddwar, a prominent gnomish family with a gears sigil, which just took control of the Mercantile Guild. That's a pretty significant shift.

The current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is from a minor family but is aligned most closely with Peixota and Manos.
ok cool. wait — Qanto. That Q — is it pronounced like a C or something else? Why do some of these names have that?

Good ear! Q is pronounced "Kh" — it comes from Old Draconic, the language used in scholarship, treaties, and formal publications in the setting. Qaringil itself is "Kharingil." It's baked into the historical naming conventions.

Not all the Five Families use Q names — Peixota, Manos, Volmer, and Vitelli don't. But families like Qanto ("Khanto") tend to mark themselves as old merchant stock with deep classical institutional ties. Different families have different naming traditions, probably based on regional or ethnic roots.
is Old Draconic still actually used or is it more like Latin — just for old texts?

It's still active. Old Draconic is the language of the academy, scholarly publications, treaties, and formal diplomacy — so anyone with learning or rank would be expected to know it. It's not conversational, but it's definitely a living language in those formal contexts.

The everyday speech is national languages — Qis, Islas, Edran, Artklan, Forthrian depending on where you are. In Qaringil specifically, the primary language is Islas, though it has unusually heavy Qis vocabulary because the city was originally founded by adventurers and laborers from Qismos.

So you get Old Draconic in contracts and ceremonies, modern languages in daily life, and those Q names as kind of linguistic fossils — a reminder of the classical period.
so if the campaign starts in Rathakos, who even runs that? Is there a Prince or...?

Regent John Dunne. Rathakos was made a Princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r after the Ryraxian War ended, but they haven't actually installed a Prince yet -- there's a Grand Council still deliberating on who that should be. So Dunne's holding the seat as Regent in the meantime.
Okay so there's like, a power vacuum situation brewing. What's the vibe -- is Dunne trying to consolidate, or is he just caretaker?

The notes don't really specify Dunne's personal ambitions, so that's honestly a blank space your DM can fill in. What *is* clear is that Rathakos has serious internal unrest right now -- the Southern Reaches were annexed from Edrad pretty recently and aren't happy about it, and there are mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults. So Dunne's dealing with a fractious territory regardless of whether he's eyeing the Prince's chair himself or just trying to keep things from falling apart.
mountain communities with dragon cults? that could get weird fast lol

Yeah, the notes specifically flag that as a problem area. The Red Mountains have a strong tradition of adventuring for a reason -- they're described as "one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions." And these old dragon cult communities never got the memo that Ryraxos is exiled to the moon, so there's definitely potential friction there.
so wait, the Five Families of Rathakos -- the DM keeps mentioning those. which house is the most powerful?

Just to clarify -- the Five Families are from Qaringil, an island city-state. That's from the prequel campaign set about 200 years in the past. In present-day Qamis where you're starting (Rathakos), the political setup is completely different. Rathakos is a mountainous border region that's actually pretty new as a nation -- it was just made a Princedom after the Ryraxian War ended, and it's currently governed by a regent while they look for a prince. So no Five Families there.
ohhh wait no i'm thinking of the wrong thing. yeah the Families are the island merchant families yeah? what are their names again

Yep, that's the one. The five main families of Qaringil are Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli. Each one leads a major guild -- Peixota does Shipping, Manos handles Judicial stuff, Qanto used to run Mercantile but lost it to a gnomish family called Tiddwar, Volmer controls Banking, and Vitelli runs the Silk Guild. They're all human-descended with some elf ancestry, and they've got a lot of political drama between them.
cool. and these are the people running things in our campaign right?

Nope, they're only in the Qaringil prequel. Since that campaign is set 200 years in the past, these families would be long gone by the time your Qamis campaign starts in Rathakos. But if you wanted to tie your character's backstory to Qaringil somehow -- like maybe you're descended from one of those families or you have some connection to the island -- that could be a cool detail to work out with your DM. The setting has a lot of room for that kind of stuff.
makes sense. so is Qaringil still around as a city in the present day, or did it get destroyed or something

Good question -- the notes don't actually say what happened to Qaringil between the two campaigns. It's one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. So it could still exist, it could have been destroyed, something else could have happened. Definitely something worth asking the DM about if you want to tie your backstory to it.
hey so im prepping some downtime stuff and got curious -- when's the next great triple-full alignment gonna happen? do i need to mark it on my calendar or is this flavor

But I gotta be honest: the DM hasn't specified whether alignment events have mechanical effects. It's flavor-rich worldbuilding, but whether it *matters* to the campaign is blank space.
hm ok so basically "cool if the DM wants to build around it but doesn't move the mechanical needle." was hoping there'd be like tide-locked spell slots or something

So there's *scaffolding* for mechanics if the DM wants to hang stuff on it. Alignment events especially make sense as narrative beats or divine intervention triggers. But as written, it's worldbuilding depth, not mechanical load.

That's honestly a solid play. A character who notices or cares about alignment events is latching onto something the world actually does. And if you're the one bringing attention to it, the DM might build stuff around it later. Narrative space is yours to fill.
FR background here. So drow on the surface in Qamis—that's a trip. What's their deal? Why would they ever leave the Underdark?

Artkos brought them in as military allies during the Ryraxian War, back when things looked desperate. The weird part is they never left. They've got a dozen scattered enclaves on the surface now, with Qama—the holy city of Bahumat—being the most prominent one.

Hold on. Drow just... stay on the surface after the war ends? That doesn't track. Either they're actually integrating into surface society, which seems nuts for drow, or there's a hidden agenda. Which is it?

The Qama situation is the wild part. They're not just occupying it—they appointed their own high priest there, a drow named Qe'lth IV. That caused an immediate schism with the traditional Church clergy; there's an alternate candidate, Qachu II, operating out of Qis with the official relics. So they're entrenched in the religious hierarchy, not just squatting as conquerors. But the campaign notes don't actually explain *why* a drow military contingent would convert to Bahumat worship or what their long-term goals are. That's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
So basically this is a powder keg and nobody really knows which way it goes.

Yeah. The setup is there—they're integrated into a major religious center, they've got political footholds across multiple enclaves—but whether they're genuinely adopting surface culture, maintaining a parallel organization, or planning a longer play is unknown. Could go any direction. Definitely something a DM would want to develop.
hey so I'm making a half-elf Edran noble and reading up on the dynasty stuff. Did the Keyberos ever get restored in the earlier history, like when Giovanni Pesaro was around?

I think you might be mixing up timelines there. Giovanni Pesaro is the Exarch of Qaringil, an island city-state, and that's from about two hundred years *before* the Ryraxian War. The Keyberos dynasty was restored *recently*—that's the present day. Two hundred years ago when Giovanni was running Qaringil, the Keyberos were already ruling Edrad (they'd been in power for centuries), but there was no restoration event like what happened after the Ryraxian War. Which era were you curious about?
oh, right. So in the present day, the war just ended and Keyberos is back in power. How long did that restoration war actually last? I remember reading something about bookmakers setting odds on it.

Good catch! Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire signed a pact to march on Edrad and restore the Keyberos dynasty to the throne. Bookmakers in Kard set the over/under on the war's length at sixty days—they expected a quick restoration. It ended up lasting thirty years instead. The dragon Ryraxos delivered Edrad from defeat when things looked really bad, but then he waged aggressive war across the continent for about two and a half decades. Once he was defeated, he accepted exile to Secundus, and the dynasty finally got restored for real.
wait, sixty days versus thirty years? That's such a wild miss. What happened between those early days when Ryraxos helped Edrad and him taking over the whole war?

Yeah, the swing is massive. Unfortunately, the notes don't really spell out exactly when or how Ryraxos transitioned from fighting to help Edrad into waging his own aggressive campaign across the continent. What we know is he's a bronze dragon, relatively young—maybe sixty years old—and he'd originally made his name as an artillery officer. (Dragons are apparently great at carrying guns.) But the specific political or military turning point? That's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. Might be interesting to ask about for your character's backstory.
hey, i'm building this sea captain character for qaringil and i keep seeing primus mentioned everywhere. what am i looking at here?

primus is a geostationary moon -- it's basically a fixed point in the sky that hangs directly over the same spot on qaia's equator at all times. never rises, never sets, just stays there. that makes it incredibly useful for navigation because you can measure its elevation above the horizon to figure out your longitude without needing a chronometer. just need a sextant or cross-staff, a clear night, and a primsine table.
okay so that sounds super useful for sailing around the islands. but like... are there any catches? nothing's that convenient lol

ha, fair point. couple things. first, primus is only visible from one hemisphere -- the side facing it. sail far enough west or east and you lose sight of it entirely. you'd have to navigate by dead reckoning or stars instead.

second: precision tanks near the horizon. when primus is low in the sky, atmospheric refraction bends the light and makes your elevation measurement noisy. you can still measure it, but if you're trying to pin down your exact position for something important, you're better off waiting until it's higher up.
so like, if i'm trying to make landfall at night and primus is barely above the horizon, i can't trust it?

right. the refraction gets significant and precision drops. so as a sea captain you'd know to either wait for better conditions, stick to daytime piloting near shore, or just accept the uncertainty if you're in a hurry. it's one of those practical sailing things that makes for good character knowledge -- knowing when your tools work and when they don't.
Hey, quick question -- is "thassolocracy" a typo for "thalassocracy" or is the setting doing something intentional with the spelling?

Fair catch. The notes use "thassolocracy" consistently, but I don't have an explanation for whether that's intentional worldbuilding, a variant spelling, or just how the DM spells it. I can't rule out it being a typo. What I can confirm: Qaringil and the islands of the central sea are described as thassolocracies -- sea-based merchant republics with powerful mercenary navies they sell to continental powers. The governance model is clear, even if the spelling is a bit mysterious.
Okay, but are you making a guess about the spelling being connected to Old Draconic, or is that actually a rule in the setting? Because you mentioned Q is pronounced "Kh" -- is that real?

No, I'm not guessing on that part. The notes explicitly state "Q is pronounced 'Kh' (from Old Draconic / Old Qis)" and it's applied consistently to place names like Qamis, Qaringil, Qis'mos, Qirkos, and character names like Qe'lth. Old Draconic is the setting's scholarly and diplomatic language. So that's definitely a real rule. The Q/Kh thing and the "thassolocracy" spelling might be connected, but the notes don't explain the etymology of the word itself, so I'd be speculating beyond what's documented.
Hmm, so if Old Draconic uses Q for that Kh sound, wouldn't "thassolocracy" have a Q in it if it came from Old Draconic? The "th" spelling doesn't fit.

That's sharp logic, and you're right -- if "thassolocracy" were originally Old Draconic, you'd expect it to have a Q. The notes don't explain where the term comes from, so it could be borrowed from a different language in the setting, a real-world term, or something the DM chose without deep etymological work. The blank space is the word's history. What's confirmed is that thassolocracies are a governance model -- merchant republics ruling by sea power -- but not the origin of the term itself.
Alright, that makes sense. So which places in Qamis actually count as thassolocracies?

Qaringil is the main one. It's a city-state in the central sea islands, ruled as a republic, criss-crossed with canals, with armed merchant fleets. The broader island culture of the central sea is described as thassolocracies -- nautical robber barons who sell their powerful mercenary navies to mainland powers. Beyond those, the notes don't explicitly call out other thassolocracies. Kard is a major financial and shipping center, but it's not labeled as one. Lock has "free cities" but not the thassolocracy label. So Qaringil and the island cluster are your clear answers.
ok so i keep hearing about like triple-full alignments and i have no idea what that means. is that a qamis thing?

Yeah, it's a moon thing. Qaia has three inner moons that really matter -- Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus -- and they all orbit at different speeds. Sometimes they all line up so they're all full at the same time, or all new at the same time. That's a "triple alignment." A "triple-full" is when all three are full simultaneously. They don't happen super often in perfect sync, but they cluster into what navigators call "eras" where one type dominates for a few years at a time.
okay that makes sense i guess. when's the next one? like, are we about to have one?

Depends on when 'now' is for you, but the alignment calendar shows we're actually heading into a new-moon era pretty soon -- those start clustering in 2255. The *next great triple-full era* doesn't start up again until around 2259. The May of that year, 2259 May 11, is going to be absolutely perfect -- all three moons within a fraction of a percent of full at the same time. That's probably the best one you'll see for years.
wait so we're in like 2253 or something? and we have to wait six years? that's... a lot. why do people care so much about this?

Well, six years in-game might be a lot, but triple alignments are genuinely powerful. When all three moons are full at once, the tidal forces add up -- you're looking at way more extreme tides than normal. For sailors and navigators, that's huge. And for anyone living on the coasts? Flooding, resource changes, it ripples through everything. Plus there's probably spiritual significance too, given how much Bahumat worship ties into cosmic cycles. People plan around these events.
oh damn so like if i'm a sailor i should be thinking about 2259. but also like... is there weird lore about what *happens* during these? like do crazy things go down?

That's a good question and honestly the notes don't spell out specific lore about what happens during alignments -- that's the kind of thing your DM would probably fill in, maybe with NPCs telling stories. But think about it: Qaian culture is built around the moons, the Church of Bahumat is everywhere, Primus lets you navigate by longitude... these alignments are *major* celestial events. Whether it's prophecy, ritual, or just logistical chaos, yeah, people would remember them and talk about them. Your DM might have plans for 2259.
So I get a bonus language as a PC, right? How does that even work in this setting?

Yeah, everyone gets one bonus language. The catch: you have to tell the DM how you know it. It's not automatic -- you need a backstory reason. Maybe you grew up in a merchant family and picked up Islas from traders, or you spent time as a mercenary in Forthyr and learned Forthrian. But you need to justify where that knowledge came from.
OK but what about race-based languages? Like, aren't dwarves supposed to speak Dwarvish or whatever? Is that a thing here?

Nope. There are no racial languages in this setting -- no Dwarvish, no Elvish, none of that. Languages are national, not racial. The five main ones are Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, and Islas. You speak human languages because you grew up in a place, not because of what you are.
Wait, so an elf character just... learns Edran like anyone else? That seems weird. How do you roleplay that?

Yeah, exactly -- if your elf character grew up in Edrad, they speak Edran just like any human from there. The setting treats language as a cultural thing, not a racial thing. Elves exist in this world and they're woven into the societies. Half-elf nobility rules Edrad, for example. So there's no special 'elf language' -- your character's background and upbringing determine what they speak, regardless of race.
OK, I get that. But I'm hearing these languages aren't standardized -- so Edran from the capital city is different from Edran from, like, Rathakos or whatever?

Right. Each language has major regional variations. The prestige dialect -- what gets taught in academies and spoken by the nobility in the capital -- is the 'official' version, but street-level Edran in different cities can be pretty different. The notes specifically mention that a speaker from the capital of Artkos would have trouble understanding Artklan from someone in Rathakos. So when you pick your starting languages, you're speaking the capital prestige dialect PLUS one regional variation. That regional one is yours to define -- where in the world are you from?
So Old Draconic -- the thing scholars use -- that's standardized across the whole continent? Or does that also have regional flavors?

Good question. The notes don't actually spell out whether Old Draconic has regional variation. It's described as 'the language of scholarly publications and treaties' -- the academic standard -- which suggests it's standardized, but the DM hasn't explicitly confirmed that. It's probably one of those blank spaces. What I can tell you is it's definitely not a PC starting language. You'd need a serious backstory reason to know Old Draconic as a bonus language -- like you were trained as a scholar or clergy, or spent years in a monastery. It's not something you just picked up.
so i've been reading about edrad and i noticed the nobility has this weird requirement about being half-elven. is that just a flavor thing or is there actual worldbuilding logic behind it?

It's definitely a hard rule, not just flavor. The notes specify that Edran nobility must be between one quarter and three quarters elf -- so half-elven is the sweet spot, but there's a range. okay so that's *what* the rule is, but like... why? did some ancient Edran king marry an elf and decide all heirs had to be mixed? or is it about maintaining magical bloodlines, or political alliance with elves, or what?

That's exactly the question the notes don't answer. The campaign materials state the rule exists and has for centuries, but they don't explain the historical or political reasoning behind it. Whether it started as a deliberate policy, a practical necessity, a magical requirement, or something else entirely -- that's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.

huh. so we don't know if there's actually a *population* of half-elves in edrad, or if it's just a rule that nobles have to follow? like, are we talking about a blended society or is it purely an elite constraint?

Another blank space. The notes don't say whether the half-elven requirement reflects the overall demographics of Edrad or if it's an artificial constraint on the ruling class only. You could imagine either: a kingdom where half-elves are common and naturally ascend to power, or one where the nobility maintains an exclusionary rule that has nothing to do with the general population. The notes don't specify which.
and there are no racial languages in this world, right? so it's not like the half-elven thing is tied to some inherited magical language or scholarly tradition?

Correct -- the setting explicitly has no racial languages. No elvish, dwarvish, draconic as a spoken tongue, any of that. Old Draconic exists as a scholarly language for treaties and publications, but it's not inherited by race. So if the half-elven requirement in Edran nobility *is* tied to something magical or cultural, it'd have to be something other than language. Though again, the notes don't explain what that something is.

so we know the rule exists, we know it's been in place for four centuries across the whole dynasty, and we know it's not about language... but literally nothing else. that's a genuinely interesting gap.

It really is. The specificity of "one quarter to three quarters elf" suggests careful thought -- it's not just "have some elf ancestry." But the *why* behind that precision is completely open. Whether it's biological, political, magical, diplomatic, or something nobody's thought of yet, you've found a rich blank space the DM can fill in however makes sense for the campaign.
yo so im building a character in qaringil and ppl keep saying its ruled by an incomprehensible republic lol. whats that mean?

Qaringil is a city-state on an island in the central sea, ruled by a system built around seven major guilds -- Shipping, Mercantile, Judicial, Silk, Banking, Wool, and Gondolier. ok so like after the ryraxian war stuff got stabilized right? so does the republic get more organized or

Ah, quick clarification -- the Qaringil campaign is set about 200 years *before* the Ryraxian War. You're in the prequel era. So the Ryraxian War, Ryraxos's exile, the restoration of the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad -- all that happens way in the future from where Qaringil's story takes place.

ohhhh okay that makes more sense actually. so like could my character be from one of those five families then?

so Forthyr is described as backwards -- what does that actually mean in terms of the setting mechanics?

That's a fair question. The notes call Forthyr "somewhat backwards" but don't really spell out what that means. that's frustrating lol. does it matter for character building? like if I'm making a Forthyr native would that affect how I approach subclass choices or assumptions about what my character knows?

hey, just got back from missing a session. what was everyone talking about with Qama and the Bahumat stuff?

Qama is the holy city of Bahumat and the traditional seat of the Church. It's got huge religious significance in Qis'mos. The catch is that during the Ryraxian Wars the drow occupied it. They didn't destroy the city, but they appointed their own high priest there, a drow named Qe'lth IV. That caused a schism -- an alternate high priest called Qachu II has been running things from the city of Qis instead, but without access to the relics of office, so that's kind of a legitimacy problem for him.
wait, why do the drow even care about a Bahumat holy city? did they convert or something?

Good question, and honestly the notes don't explain that. The drow were recruited as allies during the war and some of them stayed on the surface afterward -- there are scattered drow enclaves all over, with Qama being the most prominent one. But I don't have details on whether they worship Bahumat themselves, or what their actual motive was for taking control of the city.
okay, and what makes Qama actually holy? like, is there a famous shrine or artifact or something that happened there?

That's blank space. The notes just say it's the traditional seat and that Bahumat worship is huge in Qis'mos generally, but they don't get into the history of why Qama became the sacred center or what specific relics or holy sites are there. Could be something your DM develops, or could work into a character backstory.
so like what smaller guilds would our party actually interact with? my drow stayed on the surface after the war

In Qaringil, the island city-state, the party-relevant guilds are the Adventurer Guild, Alchemist Guild, and Mage Guild. There's also an underground scene - Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses guilds if the party goes that route. These operate alongside the Seven Major Guilds that run the city. Now, Qaringil is from the prequel campaign (~200 years before present-day). In present-day Qamis where your drow character fits - the setting starts in Rathakos, a mountain frontier region - guild structures aren't detailed yet. That's blank space your DM could develop.
so if we're in rathakos do guilds just not exist there

Not necessarily - the DM just hasn't detailed them yet. Rathakos is described as having a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship, so it definitely has an Adventurer community. Beyond that, the notes don't map out specific guild structures. It's one of the blank spaces where your party's story could help shape what exists.

hey, I'm thinking about running the Qaringil prequel as a one-shot. Quick question: which of the five families are actually aligned with the current Exarch? I want to understand the political landscape.

The current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is from a minor family but is considered aligned most closely with the Peixota and Manos families. Peixota controls the Shipping Guild and has the dolphin sigil; Manos leads the Judicial Guild and uses the hand sigil. So those two are your power centers supporting him.
Okay, so Peixota and Manos are his backers. What about the other three? Are they neutral, hostile, or do they have their own agendas?

The other three major families are Qanto (dove sigil, formerly led the Mercantile Guild), Volmer (crow sigil, leads the Banking Guild), and Vitelli (moth sigil, leads the Silk Guild). The notes don't explicitly say whether they're hostile to Pesaro or just independent, but there's a telling detail: Qanto recently *lost* control of the Mercantile Guild to a minor gnomish family (Tiddwar, the gears sigil) that is more aligned with the Exarch. That loss is described as "hotly contested" — so there's definitely tension there. Beyond that, the notes don't fill in their stances clearly.

Interesting. So Pesaro is using his power to elevate smaller families over the established major ones? And Qanto is probably resentful. But you mentioned he's on his deathbed — how unstable is the succession situation?

Yeah, Pesaro has held the post for 19 years and is explicitly described as "on death's doorstep." That's a real ticking clock. The notes don't specify what happens when he dies — who takes over, how the succession works, what the major families do. It's left blank. But given that he's been consolidating power toward Peixota, Manos, and now Tiddwar at Qanto's expense, his death could trigger a succession crisis. That's a huge one-shot hook.

So the power balance is fragile, and there's going to be a vacuum soon. If I'm running a one-shot, I could seed conflict between the families — like, Qanto nursing a grudge, or Volmer and Vitelli deciding to move. What's Qaringil's day-to-day like for regular folk? Is it a city on the knife's edge, or does it function smoothly despite the elite politics?

The city itself seems functional: it's built on canals, has seven major guilds running different sectors (shipping, judicial, mercantile, silk, wool, banking, gondolier), and the gondolier guild also maintains the bridges and the wizard-made isthmus that connects to the mainland. The population is around 100,000 and they're primarily a commercial society — food comes from fishing and importation, and the elites are merchant-focused, not landowner feudal types. So it's probably stable day-to-day. But the underbelly you'd want to hint at: three competing underground criminal organizations (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses guilds) that are definitely jockeying for power while the major families focus on the succession. That's good one-shot texture — the streets don't know which way the wind is blowing once Pesaro's gone.

That's solid. So for a one-shot, I'm thinking the party stumbles into this via a guild commission or a heist that touches the succession mess. But I want to make sure I'm reading the family dynamics right. You said Tiddwar is gnomish and got the Mercantile Guild recently — are any of the five major families not human, or do they all have that same mix of human plus elf ancestry?

The five major families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, Vitelli) are all "mostly human, though all have some elf ancestry." The notes don't give you individual family breakdowns beyond that. Tiddwar is explicitly gnomish, and they've broken into the major-family game by taking the Mercantile Guild from Qanto. So Tiddwar is your interesting exception — a gnomish family clawing their way up the ladder, presumably with Pesaro's backing. That could be another angle for your one-shot: resentment from the human-plus-elf families toward this upstart gnome family changing the power structure.

Perfect. This is shaping up. One last thing: for a one-shot adventure, what's the tone? Is Qaringil a grimdark intrigue-fest, or more of a swashbuckling trader-republic vibe? The whole "city of exiles and adventurers" feel suggests the latter, but the three shadow-guilds and the succession knife-fight suggest the former.

You're reading it right — there's both. Qaringil was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qismos, and the city's criss-crossed with canals and ruled as an "incomprehensible republic" with armed merchant fleets and powerful fortifications. That's the swashbuckling foundation. But underneath, you've got the major families playing a sophisticated political game, underground criminal syndicates carving out territory, and a succession vacuum about to open up that could destabilize everything. So I'd pitch it as swashbuckling-on-the-surface-with-real-stakes-underneath. The party can be doing something fun and mercenary (a heist, a shipping job, guarding a guild commission) and accidentally brush up against the succession crisis. That's a one-shot sweet spot — adventure with weight.
hey, just got back from a trip and missed the last session. what'd I miss? anything important come up?

Welcome back! The notes don't track specific session details, so I can't tell you what happened at your table. But if you want to get up to speed on the Qamis setting itself, I'm happy to brief you on whatever interests you -- nations, cities, the political situation after the Ryraxian War, the moons, anything. What sounds useful?

Right, yeah. The moons thing is wild. So we've got seven of them, and Primus is geostationary, which means it's locked over the equator. That should make trade routes really predictable, right? Like, navigation is solved. But I'm noticing the campaign materials mention regional language barriers... that seems weird if trade is easy.

Good catch! So yes, Primus being geostationary is a massive navigation advantage -- you can find your longitude without a chronometer, just by measuring Primus's elevation above the horizon. Completely revolutionary compared to pre-chronometer Earth navigation.

But here's the thing: even though navigation is easier, the languages haven't standardized. The notes explicitly call this out. There are five main national languages -- Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, and Islas -- but within each one, regional dialects vary so much that a speaker from the capital might struggle to understand someone from a mountain province. A speaker of capital-dialect Artklan would have real trouble with Artklan from Rathakos, for example.

So trade might flow easier, but that doesn't automatically erase regional isolation or language drift.

Hmm, so the geography must be enforcing it somehow. Rathakos is in the Red Mountains, right? That's a natural barrier. What about the island city-states? They're accessible by sea if you've got good navigation...

Exactly -- Rathakos is hemmed in by mountains, so that makes sense for dialect fragmentation. The islands are trickier.

In the present day (post-Ryraxian War), the central sea islands are governed by competing thalassocracies -- mercenary navies run by island robber barons. They're accessible by sea, sure, but politically fragmented and focused on selling military services to continental powers rather than spreading a single lingua franca.

In the earlier Qaringil era (about 200 years back), Qaringil itself is described as an island city-state ruled by an incomprehensible republic with seven major guilds and five prominent families all competing. The primary language there is Islas, with heavier-than-usual Qis vocabulary because the city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qismos.

So even on the islands, you get regional variation. Trade happens, but it doesn't seem to homogenize speech the way you'd expect.

That's wild. I guess mercenary politics and guild rivalry don't really incentivize a common tongue. Everyone's protecting their own turf. The tidal complexity might play into it too -- with Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all pulling at different periods, ocean currents and shipping routes probably shift seasonally in complex ways. Trade wouldn't be this steady predictable flow, it'd be opportunistic.

That's a really smart connection. The oscillating tides from Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus -- plus their different orbital periods (4.5 days, 8.15 days, and 29.5 days) -- do create complex interference patterns. Shipping routes and seasonal windows would definitely be complicated and probably require local knowledge to navigate efficiently.

The notes don't explicitly detail how currents work or what seasonal windows exist, but yeah, if sailing is complex and route-dependent, that reinforces regional knowledge silos. You need local pilots who know the tides and currents of *their* coast, and that expertise wouldn't transfer neatly across regions. Over generations, that local knowledge sticks, and language drifts further.

So even though Primus solves longitude, the tidal regime and geographic complexity keep regions semi-isolated enough that languages don't converge.
hey, I'm reading up on the moons for Qaringil and I keep seeing Luna mentioned. Isn't that Earth's moon? Is Qaia connected to Earth somehow?

Luna is Earth's moon, but it's just being used as a reference standard here -- Qaia isn't connected to Earth. The point is that most people know what Earth's moon looks like, so it makes a convenient yardstick for understanding Qaia's moons. All the technical information in the notes is given as comparisons to Luna: how big they appear in the sky, how bright they are, their mass, orbital periods, everything. So when the notes say Tertius has an angular diameter of 33.7 arcminutes and appears 1.09× Luna, you instantly know it looks about 9% larger in the sky than Earth's moon. And when it says Tertius is only 0.14 magnitudes dimmer than Luna, you know it would look almost as bright. It's just a way to make those measurements concrete and relatable.
Oh I get it. So Primus is way dimmer then -- like three magnitudes? I thought that was the moon important for navigation or something.

Good question. Primus is definitely much dimmer -- about three magnitudes dimmer than Luna, which makes it significantly fainter in the sky. But it matters for navigation not because it's bright, but because it's geostationary: it hangs in exactly the same spot in the sky forever, never rising or setting. That fixed position is what makes it so valuable for determining longitude without needing a chronometer. It's dim enough that it's not a spectacular sight, but it's always there in the same place for observers on the near side of Qaia, and that reliable position is worth more than brightness for navigation purposes.

so my drow character stuck around after the war, and im thinking of setting them up in Qaringil. im looking at the Gondolier Guild as a thing to join, but i only know they maintain the bridges and run gondolas. what else is actually there? what's the scope

okay so fair heads up -- the detailed info i have on Qaringil's guilds is from about 200 years ago, during the Qaringil prequel campaign. the present day Qamis notes mention the bridges still exist and the islands are thassolocracies of mercenary naval powers, but they don't detail what the guild structure looks like now. so i don't know if the Gondolier Guild still exists as described, or what it's like in the present day.

that said, if the guild is still around as it was: they're one of seven major guilds, they maintain and control those huge wizard-made bridges. they're also one of only two major guilds never led by one of the Five Families, so they've stayed independent.

but i'm honest -- i don't know if that structure held or changed after the war.
ok ok, well let's say it's still there or at least something like it. so what do they actually *do* besides bridge work?

the prequel notes don't say. like, day-to-day operations, what kind of work members do, internal structure, whether there's ancillary work beyond maintenance -- all blank. if the guild is staffed with mages or engineers, whether they do security work, whether rogues have a role in there -- none of it's documented.
hmm. and the independence thing -- why are they independent of the families? is it leverage, or what

no idea. the notes just say they've stayed independent, never led by one of the major families. no explanation for why. could be they control something the families need and use that as leverage, could be they're just too valuable to mess with, could be politics played out that way. the notes don't say.
alright, and real talk -- if my rogue doesn't have bridge-maintenance skills, could i even join? like what would i do

the notes say party members at your level could become full guild members of anything relevant to your class, so joining is possible. but what a rogue would actually do in the Gondolier Guild -- whether they have stealth work, security gigs, or if it's all just hard labor -- that's blank. your dm would need to figure that out.
hey! so i'm looking at the gondolier guild in qaringil and i'm curious -- do they literally just run boats or is there more going on?

oh dude, yes! okay so the obvious answer is gondolas, right? but here's the cool part -- the Gondolier Guild *also* maintains and controls the bridges. both of them. the Great Western Bridge and the eastern one connected to the volcanic island. that's a *massive* amount of infrastructure power in their hands, and it's one of the reasons they're interesting -- along with the Wool Guild, they're the only one of the major guilds that's never been led by one of the Five Families. they've managed to stay independent.
wait hold on, they control *both* bridges? that's huge leverage over literally anyone trying to cross the sea

exactly! you get it. that's why it matters. for a thassolocracy that depends on sea trade and mercenary navies, control of those bridges is... it's not just infrastructure, it's politics. especially the Great Western Bridge -- that's your main connection to the western continent and the wizard-made isthmus. you cut off access, you've got leverage over everyone. the fact that they've managed to stay out of the Five Families' hands for this long is pretty wild.
quick question -- what does the Gondolier Guild actually do besides run boats? and is the Q sound everywhere because of Old Draconic?

The Gondolier Guild runs gondolas, but they also handle maintenance and control of the bridges -- the two enormous wizard-made bridges that span the central sea near Qaringil. Pretty significant responsibility. They've also stayed independent from the Five Families that control most of Qaringil's other major guilds, which makes them unusual.

And yeah, the Q is Old Draconic -- pronounced 'Kh' (so Qaringil is 'Kharingil', Qanto is 'Khanto', etc). Old Draconic is the scholarly and diplomatic language of Qamis, so you see that sound all over place names and formal terms.
so Old Draconic is like Latin for Qamis? was the Q sound always there, or is that a historical shift?

Good way to think about it. But here's the thing -- the notes don't actually cover whether the Q sound is original to Old Draconic or shifted at some point. We know it's used formally everywhere, but the linguistic history behind it isn't filled in yet. Definitely fertile ground for a character backstory if you wanted to dig into it though.
I've been reading about Qis'mos and I keep seeing it mentioned as important to the Draconic empire somehow. But the notes say it wasn't actually the seat? What's that about?

Good catch -- that's the interesting part. Qis'mos wasn't the capital of the Draconic empire, but it's where the empire's institutions persisted the longest after the dragons departed. The Potentate system of government (elected by nobility, clergy, and guildmasters) is basically a remnant of that imperial bureaucracy, still in place in the Qamis present day. And the Church of Bahamut has an enormous presence there, with its main seat in the holy city of Qama. So it's less about being the political center and more about being the continuity center.
Interesting. Hey, quick question though -- I keep reading 'Qis' but I'm not sure how to pronounce it. Is it like 'Kee-iss' or something else?

Good instinct to ask. 'Q' in Old Draconic (also called Old Qis, the scholarly language) is pronounced 'Kh' -- kind of like the German 'ch' sound in 'Bach'. So 'Qis' sounds like 'Khis'. You'll see that 'Q without U' convention in a lot of place names across Qamis -- Qama, Qirkos, Qaringil -- all Old Draconic names. Most modern languages that came later abandoned that sound.

Ohh, so the 'Q without U' thing is a linguistic signature of Old Draconic? So theoretically, if I see that spelling pattern, I know it's from the empire era or at least named by people who cared about that convention?

Exactly right. Old Draconic was the language of the academy and diplomacy, so place names using that convention typically mark either ancient settlements or ones important enough to the empire that they got official 'imperial' names. It's why the Qis language itself is described as being the closest to Old Draconic among modern languages -- Qis'mos preserved not just the political institutions but even the linguistic markers of that era.
Wait, so if Qis'mos wasn't the imperial seat, why did those institutions stick around *there* specifically? Seems like the capital would be the place you'd expect to preserve things most strongly.

That's the real puzzle. The notes don't fully explain the mechanism, but there's a clue in the Church of Bahamut being so entrenched there. The religious institutions might have been the continuity thread -- the Church probably maintained institutional memory and bureaucratic know-how in a way that survived the dragons' departure. The Potentate system of checks and balances (nobility, clergy, guildmasters all having a say) also looks designed to prevent any one power from dominating, which might reflect lessons the empire's survivors wanted to preserve. So Qis'mos became less about being the seat of power and more about being the custodian of how power should be balanced.
yo so whats the deal with rathakos and acrokos? theyre like totally missing from qaringil right

Good catch. Rathakos and Acrokos don't exist in the Qaringil era -- that's about two hundred years before present-day Qamis. Back then, the Red Mountains were just claimed territory controlled by the Holy Qis Empire, but they weren't actually administered -- it was mostly just various non-state peoples living there. Both nations formed during and after the Ryraxian War. When Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire, the mountain provinces that had been under Edran occupation got reorganized into the Red Mountain Republic. Then when the Edrans withdrew due to guerrilla resistance and setbacks elsewhere, the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran faction and split the region into two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So they're pretty recent additions to the political map.

Hey, quick question about the setting. I'm looking at the moon data and saw something about 'Hill sphere' -- 3.89 LD. Is that something I need to understand for running Qaringil as a one-shot, or is it just flavor?

Good instinct to ask. The Hill sphere is the gravitational boundary -- the region where Qaia's gravity holds sway over the moons. At 3.89 Lunar Distances (LD), it defines how far out the moon system can stretch before the sun's gravity pulls a moon away entirely. The moons' distances and periods are all listed in relation to it. For a one-shot? It's mostly worldbuilding flavor, but it does tell you the system is stable and well-thought-out. The cool part is that navigation actually uses the moons, especially Primus, so understanding why the moon system exists mechanically can deepen how you describe the sky.
Okay, so Qaia's got enough gravity to keep them all. But are the moons themselves stable? Like, if I'm setting a game in Qaringil, can I assume the night sky was basically the same two centuries ago?

The moons are stable. The notes explicitly say Primus is magically anchored (no migration), and the five freely-orbiting moons -- Secundus through Septimus -- have been run through a 2,000-year simulation showing they don't crash or escape. That said, there is slow drift over geological time. Secundus, which is retrograde, gradually spirals inward. But over 200 years? You're talking negligible change in orbital positions or periods. For Qaringil's timeline, assume the sky looks essentially the same.

Got it. So the moons are shared between Qaringil and Qamis. But I'm curious -- what does Hill sphere actually *tell* me about why these moons are where they are? Like, is Secundus at 0.30 LD because it needs to be there, or is that arbitrary?

That's a great question, and the notes don't explicitly say. What I can tell you: each moon's Hill sphere fraction (its distance as a percentage of the overall Hill sphere) correlates with its Roche margin -- the closest it can orbit before tidal forces tear it apart. Secundus is at 7.7% and has a Roche margin of 15.4x. Quartus is at 27% with a margin of 42.5x. The outer moons like Septimus push to 63.3% of the Hill sphere. So the placement respects orbital stability, but whether these specific distances were *chosen* for the system or just happened to work out is a blank space the DM hasn't filled in.

Interesting. One last thing: if I'm a player in Qaringil and I know my latitude from the pole star, could I actually use Primus to find longitude back then too, or is that navigation trick unique to Qamis?

You could use it, absolutely. Primus is geostationary -- it's magically anchored and never moves, making it a fixed reference point in the sky. The longitude-by-Primus method works because Primus's sub-satellite point is always at (0° N, 0° E), regardless of era. So in Qaringil, 200 years ago, the math is identical. Whether Qaringil's navigators back then *knew* about this method or had the tables to use it is another blank space -- the notes give you the physics and the method, but not the in-world history of who discovered it or when it became common knowledge.

So I'm reading through the moon mechanics and I see Quintus mentioned as a 'trace particle' at L4. That's the Lagrange point, right? But I gotta ask — if it's massless and does nothing, why is it even in the system?

Yep, L4 point — 60 degrees ahead of Qaia in its orbit around the sun. And you've spotted the key detail: it's effectively massless, so it exerts zero tidal force and has no mechanical impact on Qaia or the other moons. The DM's own note says it's 'included for worldbuilding purposes.' So to be completely honest, you're right to be skeptical — mechanically it does almost nothing.
But wait — if it's massless and at L4, wouldn't it just drift away eventually? Or is there some magical anchor on it like Primus has? Because that seems... suspicious.

No magic on Quintus, and it doesn't drift away — L4 is a *naturally* stable equilibrium in orbital mechanics. Objects naturally stay there without any help. It does move though: it librates (oscillates) between about 45 and 80 degrees from Qaia over a 2,000-year period. So it wobbles slowly, but the L4 point itself keeps it trapped in place.
Okay so it's stable, it oscillates every two millennia, and it affects literally nothing. I'm still not seeing why it exists if no one's ever going to notice it or interact with it.

That's the honest truth of it — you probably won't interact with it. But the DM included it because L4/L5 Lagrange points are real, and adding that detail makes the astronomy of Qaia feel complete and grounded in actual physics, even if it's never relevant to gameplay. Some worldbuilders like those touches — flavor that nobody notices but that makes the world feel thought-through. If your character becomes an astrophysicist, Quintus exists in the sky. Otherwise? Yeah, you can safely ignore it.
yo quick question — are the moons different in Qaringil or is it the same sky

Good question! Actually they're the same seven moons. The campaign notes are set up so both Qaringil (the prequel, ~200 years ago) and Qamis (present day) share the same moon system. So whether you're in Qaringil or Rathakos or anywhere else on Qaia, you're looking up at the same sky.
ok cool cool. so what's the full list then from closest to farthest

From closest to farthest:

1. **Primus** — geostationary, stays fixed in the sky, small and dark with an iron surface
2. **Secundus** — retrograde, medium-sized, also dark (Ryraxos got exiled there)
3. **Tertius** — prograde, one of the bigger ones in the sky
4. **Quartus** — prograde, basically a twin of Earth's Moon
5. **Sextus** — retrograde, small and faint
6. **Septimus** — retrograde, very far out and even fainter
7. **Quintus** — basically a trace particle at the Sun-Qaia L4 point, not really a proper moon

Primus is the special one for navigation because it literally never moves — the most reliable thing in the sky.
wait so Primus is the biggest one then right since its closest

Actually nope — Primus is small! Only about 10 arcminutes across, so roughly a third the size of Earth's Moon. It's close but has a tiny mass. **Tertius** and **Quartus** are way more prominent in the sky — they're both nearly the same size as Earth's Moon or even slightly bigger. Primus just feels important because it never moves, which made it insanely useful for ancient navigators. But size-wise, Tertius and Quartus are the real headliners.
so wait, what's this schism thing with the church of bahamut? qe'lth and qachu still fighting it out?

Yep, it's still a mess. So the drow occupied Qama -- the holy city of Bahamut in Qis'mos -- during the Ryraxian wars and basically never left. They appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, which created a schism. There's an alternate candidate, Qachu II, operating out of Qis, but the problem is all the actual relics of office stayed in Qama with Qe'lth IV. So you've got two competing high priests: one with the drow backing and the sacred relics, one without anything.
okay so the drow are just... still there? they didn't go back underground or whatever?

Yeah, they're staying put. Artkos brought them in as allies near the end of the war out of desperation, and once they got topside they basically just remained. There are a bunch of drow enclaves scattered around the continent now, but Qama is the most prominent one. So the drow are occupying the holiest site of one of the biggest religions. Weird situation.
hey, quick question — I'm making an Edran noble and want to understand the world better. How many moons does Qaia have and what's the order from closest to farthest?

Qaia has seven moons. From closest to farthest: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, Septimus. They're pretty different from each other — different sizes, orbits, even composition. Depends on how deep you want to go, but the short version is that Primus, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are the big players in the sky and daily life.
What do they actually look like? Like, how big are they compared to Earth's moon?

Good question. By apparent size in the sky: Tertius is the largest at about 33.7 arcminutes (slightly bigger than Earth's moon), then Quartus at 29.6 minutes (nearly the same), Secundus at 28 minutes (huge but really dark), and Primus at only 10.4 arcminutes — about a third the size. Sextus and Septimus are tiny, barely visible as discs.

Brightness tells a different story though. Secundus looks much dimmer despite being nearly Luna's size because it has an iron-rich surface with low reflectivity. Tertius and Quartus look almost equally bright to the eye despite Tertius being visibly larger. And Primus, despite being super important for navigation, is actually the faintest of the five visible ones.
Wait, Primus is geostationary? Like it never moves?

Right. It hangs in one fixed spot in the sky and never rises or sets. It's magically anchored in a geostationary orbit — its orbital period matches Qaia's rotation exactly (one sidereal day). So from one hemisphere it's a permanent unmoving landmark. The other hemisphere never sees it at all.

This is actually huge for navigation. In cultures with access to Primus, you can find your longitude just by measuring its elevation above the horizon — no chronometer needed. That's a massive advantage for seafaring and trade.
So Edrad gets that advantage since it's on the Central Sea? My character's family would absolutely leverage that for trade.

Exactly. Edrad's position puts them firmly in Primus's visible hemisphere. The island city-states like Qaringil are even more dependent on sea trade, so Primus is basically part of their economic infrastructure — it's baked into how they navigate and conduct commerce.

Meanwhile, the far-side hemisphere — where Primus never rises — is at a real navigational disadvantage. No direct longitude reference. They'd have to rely on dead reckoning, stellar methods, or way more complex techniques. One of the world's great geographic divides, honestly.
That makes sense. So why is Primus geostationary but the others aren't? Is it just a distance thing?

Good instinct but no — Primus is way too small to naturally be geostationary at that distance. Magic is holding it in place. The elegant part is that its orbital inclination (23.5 degrees to the ecliptic) exactly matches Qaia's axial tilt. Those two angles cancel each other out, placing Primus's orbit directly over the equator — exactly where you need it for true geostationary orbit.

The other moons follow regular orbital mechanics. Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus orbit freely without magical anchoring. All five are currently stable, but Primus is the only one being held in place by anything other than gravity.
yo, I missed last session. apparently there's some drama with the church in Qis'mos? something about a schism?

Yeah, there's a big split in the Church of Bahamut. So Qama is the holy city and traditional seat of the church in Qis'mos -- huge deal religiously. During the late phases of the Ryraxian War, drow occupied the city. They didn't trash it, but they appointed their own high priest: Qe'lth IV. That created a schism -- now there's an alternate candidate, Qachu II, who's operating out of Qis (the city, not the region) but without any of the actual relics of office. So you've got competing claims to religious authority, one with the sacred city and one with... legitimacy, maybe?
wait, I've been nerding on Old Draconic lately. Both those names have Q in them. Are they drow names or...?

Good catch. The Q is from Old Draconic -- that's the scholarly language across Qamis, used for treaties and academic stuff. The "Q without U" is the Old Draconic spelling convention, which shows up everywhere in place names. So Qe'lth and Qachu are Qis names (the region/language) influenced by that Old Draconic academic tradition. Not drow names per se, though Qe'lth IV being a drow appointed by the drow occupiers is... definitely a complicated signal about who has legitimacy in Qama right now.
oh okay, so the Kh sound -- does that shift affect how people actually pronounce these names?

Exactly. Q is pronounced "Kh" (like in Old Draconic). So Qe'lth is "Khe'lth" and Qachu is "Khachu" -- that guttural Kh sound. In the Qis prestige dialect at least, those Kh sounds would be really distinctive. Gives both names that older, more formal weight, which makes sense for religious titles.
yo so im making a character from rathakos and im trying to understand navigation up in the mountains. what's this thing about two celestial anchors?

Good question -- this is actually how Qaian navigators solve two of the hardest problems. The two anchors are: the pole star (for latitude) and Primus (for longitude). Pole star elevation above the horizon tells you your latitude directly. Primus is a geostationary moon that sits fixed in the sky over the equator, so by measuring its elevation, you can calculate longitude without needing a chronometer. That's revolutionary compared to Earth-style navigation.
wait so primus never moves? like ever?

Exactly. Primus orbits Qaia in exactly one day, synchronized with Qaia's rotation, and its orbit is aligned with the equator. So from anywhere in the near-side hemisphere, it hangs in the same spot in the sky forever -- never rises, never sets. The sub-Primus point is always at longitude 0, latitude 0. It's about 35,800 km up, smaller in the sky than Luna (only about 10.4 arcminutes), and pretty dim (magnitude -9.6), but once you know it's there and how to use it, you've basically got longitude for free.
okay but like... rathakos is in the mountains. how does that work if you've got clouds or terrain blocking sky view? did the red mountain republic have navigators?

That's the practical problem for mountain folk, yeah. You need clear sky to use these anchors at all, so high peaks and open plateaus would be better than valleys. No specifics in the notes about how the Red Mountain Republic handled navigation during the war -- that's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in.
