But Rathakos does have a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship, so local characters would probably be pretty comfortable with terrain navigation and dead reckoning too. What kind of backstory are you building?
thinking maybe someone who used to work for the old Red Mountain Republic? like a scout or guide who helped people navigate after the war when everything was chaos

That could work well. The Red Mountain Republic was the name for those mountain provinces when they were under Edran occupation during the war. Then when the Edrans withdrew and the guerrillas took over, one faction purged the pro-Edran people and split the territory into two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So a scout from that era would have lived through that upheaval. Plus, the notes mention the Southern Reaches (the parts Edrad annexed) still have internal unrest, and there are mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. Plenty of local color there if you want a character shaped by frontier instability.
dragon cults? in the mountains? that's sick. do we know what they believe or who they follow?

The notes just mention that old dragon cult communities still exist in the mountains -- it's noted as a source of unrest for Rathakos's regent, John Dunne. But specifics about which dragons, what they believe, what they want now -- all blank spaces. Could be a hook for your backstory though. Maybe your character grew up near one, or had dealings with them as a guide/scout.
quick question -- what's a full-moon era vs a new-moon era? do I need to worry about that for running a qaringil one-shot?

A full-moon era is a multi-year stretch where the three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) tend to hit full phase together, multiple times per year. A new-moon era is when they're all new together. They alternate on a roughly 12-year cycle. You don't need to worry about it unless you want to use moon phases for atmosphere -- just pick one and go with it.
ok, cool. so looking at that triple-moon alignment table... if my one-shot is set around 2260, which era is that?

That alignment table (2253–2263) is actually for the Qamis present-day setting -- the main campaign that starts after the Ryraxian War. Qaringil is the prequel, set roughly 200 years *before* that. So the table won't apply to your one-shot. But you can just pick a full-moon or new-moon era for Qaringil and go with it.
oh wait, so the main campaign is set in 2260-ish then? I thought I was confused about when it starts.

The main campaign starts right after the Ryraxian War ends -- when Ryraxos is exiled and the Keyberos dynasty is restored. The notes don't specify an exact year, but the alignment table is provided as a world-building reference for that era. So yeah, it's probably somewhere in that 2253–2263 window, but Qaringil is roughly 200 years *earlier*. For your prequel one-shot, just pick a moon era that fits your story.
hey, so I'm building out Forthyr for my campaign and I keep seeing 'Dragoncaller Shanlong' -- is that a name or a title? what's the setup there?

Both. Dragoncaller is the title, Shanlong is the name. The current ruler is Dragoncaller Shanlong VI -- he's the latest in a line of sorcerer-kings ruling Forthyr, this enormous nation in the northeast. One key detail: the dynasty originally was dragonborn, but the bloodlines have run thin over time, so Shanlong VI is presumably no longer actually dragonborn.
So he's number six of these sorcerer-kings, and the dragon heritage is fading? That's a really interesting setup. How deep does that dynasty go?

The notes don't give you an exact founding date, but from the prequel era 200 years ago, it was Dragoncaller Shanlong I, and he was half-dragonborn. So the dynasty's been ruling at least that long, probably longer. Here's something that makes it richer: Forthyr is pretty backwards as a nation, and there's a powerful religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors. The Dragoncaller dynasty tacitly encourages this -- or at least doesn't suppress it. So you've got this dynasty whose magic is fading while their people want them to be more dragonlike. That's tension you can work with.
ok I'm getting way into Qaia's moon system and I have this question - how do Qaian navigators figure out their longitude without a chronometer? like aren't you supposed to need to know the time?

This is where Primus is genius. Primus is geostationary - it stays fixed in the sky over the same point on the equator forever. It never rises, never sets, just hangs there motionless.

So here's the thing: if you measure how high Primus appears above your horizon - just an elevation angle - that tells you your angular distance from the sub-Primus point at 0° latitude and 0° longitude. Then you combine that with your latitude (which you get from the pole star) and you can solve for longitude using basic spherical trigonometry. No clock required - it's purely geometric.

They even have a special function called primsine that converts elevation angle to angular distance, so navigators just look it up in a table and do simple math. Way easier than the dead reckoning and lunar distance methods Earth navigators had to use.
wait hold on - so primsine is a lookup function? how does that work? like is it tied to Primus's position or something?

Great question. Primsine is actually a permanent, unchanging function - it only depends on the fixed physical ratio of Qaia's radius to Primus's orbital radius. That ratio is R_Qaia / r_Primus = 6371 / 42160, which is about 0.1511 and never changes.

So navigators compute primsine once and print it in the almanac forever. It's got entries like primsine(5°) = 0.279, primsine(30°) = 0.693, and so on - one column that works for all time, all locations. The calculation uses the spherical law of cosines: primsine(e) = cos φ × cos λ. Rearrange to get λ = arccos(primsine(e) / cos φ), check which direction Primus appears (east or west of due south), and you've got longitude. The whole thing at sea is three table lookups, one division, and another lookup. That's why it was such a game-changer - way simpler than anything Earth had.
I'm trying to piece together the geography post-war. West Artkos is the historical heart of Artkos, right? How much of their actual power is centered there?

West Artkos is definitely the historic heart -- the original Artkos territory in the northwest. But here's the thing: Artkos has expanded over the past two hundred years to include multiple exclaves spread across the continent. The central enclave contains their capital city, Ath'r, which had been the seat of central Qamisi empires for centuries before Artkos conquered it. So their power isn't just concentrated in the northwest -- they've got holdings scattered around, which creates some interesting complications.
Complications like what? Are they actually in control of all these exclaves?

Not fully. The Ryraxian War ended by dissolving the old Artklan Empire into what's now the Artklan League. High King Ezevir Arth wants to restore Artkos's dominance, but it's uphill. A lot of regions that are nominally under Artkos's control are clinging to quasi-independence. Take Kard in the southwestern enclave -- it's a major financial and shipping center, but governance is fragile there because much of the population is Edran-speaking and actually welcomed Edrad's invasion during the war, twenty-eight years ago.
Quick one: what's the Bahamut schism about in Qis'mos? I've got maybe five minutes before standup.

TL;DR: The Church of Bahamut is huge in Qis'mos and was traditionally centered in Qama, their holy city. During the Ryraxian wars, the drow occupied Qama and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. So now you've got a schism -- Qe'lth IV running things from Qama with drow backing, while an alternate high priest, Qachu II, operates out of Qis without control of the actual relics and sacred objects. Two competing centers of authority for the church.
Wait, so the drow are just... permanently installed there? Or is it unclear?

The notes say the drow occupied Qama during the late phases of the war and appointed Qe'lth IV, but they don't specify whether they're still there in the present day or how firm their control is. It's a gap in what's written down. What's clear is that Qe'lth IV has control of Qama and the physical symbols of office, while Qachu II is essentially the rival claimant operating from a distance without those relics. So there's definitely a live schism, but the full picture of drow involvement in the present campaign isn't spelled out.
Got it. So Qe'lth has the actual power but Qachu's the legitimate one?

That's a fair reading. Qe'lth has de facto control of Qama and its relics, but Qachu's the candidate that wasn't installed by occupying forces, so he'd have legitimacy from the church's perspective. Which faction actually has more followers or influence in the present day -- that's another gap. The schism is real, but how it's playing out politically isn't detailed yet.
okay so like... full honesty? this setting feels kinda overcomplicated. where's the simple stuff like, I dunno, dwarvish? elvish? why do I gotta learn Artklan AND Qis AND whatever?

No racial languages in Qamis -- no elvish, dwarvish, none of that. The languages are national instead. So you're learning Edran (the prestige Edran from the capital Jia, maybe plus a regional dialect), or Artklan, or Qis, depending on where your character is from. That's the trade-off: you're getting cultural complexity instead of racial categories.
wait but then like... don't the dwarves in Rathakos all just speak the same dwarven thing? like I get the whole regional dialect bit but surely there's some racial language layer on top?

Nope -- there really isn't. A dwarf in Rathakos speaks Edran or Artklan depending on where they're from in the nation. Their ancestry doesn't unlock a different language. And quick note: Rathakos only exists in the present-day Qamis campaign (after the Ryraxian War). If you're thinking about the Qaringil prequel, that's set about 200 years earlier with a different political map.
oh huh, okay. so it's less "racial languages" and more just... everybody in a region speaks the region's language, period. that's actually cleaner than I thought.

Yeah, exactly. Plus there's Old Draconic (also called Old Qis), which shows up in treaties and scholarly publications, and you can see it in place names -- the Q without a U thing comes from that. But day-to-day? You're speaking whatever nation you're in, with all the regional variation that entails.

hey so my friend was telling me about this D&D setting called Qamis? and mentioned there's like a bunch of moons that affect tides. I read a lot of fantasy novels but never played D&D before, so how does that actually work?

Yeah, Qaia has seven moons! But not all of them contribute much to tides. The main ones for daily tides are Tertius (the strongest contributor), Secundus, and Quartus. Together they add up to about 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's Moon.

There's also Primus, which is unique — it's geostationary, meaning it stays fixed in the same spot in the sky and never moves. Because it doesn't sweep across the sky, it doesn't create oscillating tides like the others do. Instead it raises a permanent ~2 cm bulge in the oceans.
wait so Primus is STRONGER because it's always there, right? like it's constantly pulling?

Good instinct, but actually the opposite! Primus creates that permanent bulge because it *doesn't move* — it's like a weight always pressing on one side of the ocean. But tides are caused by *changes* in gravitational pull as moons move across the sky. Since Primus doesn't move, it can't create the rising-and-falling cycle. That's why Tertius, Secundus, and Quartus (which do move) are responsible for the daily tidal rhythm.
okay that makes sense. so like... was this different back when Ryraxos was ruling? did he do something to the moons or?

Nope, the moon system has been stable the whole time. Primus is actually magically anchored so it's never moved. The notes don't mention Ryraxos messing with the moons — his whole deal was military conquest. The tidal setup is the same whether you're talking about present-day Qamis or the Qaringil prequel from about 200 years back.
oh cool, so at least that's consistent. the moons are just... there, steady.

Exactly. Steady and predictable, which is actually huge for navigation — Primus's fixed position is how Qaian sailors figure out longitude without a chronometer. But yeah, the fundamental setup never changes.
So what's the deal with the Artklan League? Is that like a new name for the old Artklan Empire, or did something actually change?

Good question -- they're actually different things. The Artklan Empire used to rule about four tenths of the continent plus have heavy influence over Qis'mos. But after the Ryraxian War ended thirty years ago, it fell apart. Now what's left is the Artklan League, which the notes specifically describe as "a pale shadow of their old empire." The high kings of Artkos -- the core territory -- are trying to restore dominance, but their "nominal subjects" are clinging to tenuous quasi-independence instead. So the League is the fragmented remainder.
Wait, so the *war itself* broke up the empire? Like, Ryraxos smashed it and then people didn't want to reunify?

Close, but the notes don't give us the exact mechanics. What we know is: during the Ryraxian War, Ryraxos actually *dissolved the Artklan Empire*. After he was defeated and exiled, the Settlements of Ath'r tried to put things back together by making various regions into new nations -- Rathakos and Acrokos got made into princedoms, places got formally reorganized. But the people in those territories aren't eager to return to imperial rule. The Artkos high kings want it badly; the territories would rather stay independent. It's a tug-of-war.
Okay so hold on. Did the empire exist *before* the war, or did Ryraxos create it?

It existed before the war. The Qaringil prequel campaign is set about 200 years earlier, and back then Artkos was only its "historical heartland in the northwest" -- smaller, not imperial. So somewhere between then and the present day, Artkos expanded to rule that four-tenths of the continent (the Artklan Empire). The notes don't spell out exactly when or how that expansion happened, but it was established enough that Ryraxos could then dissolve it during the war.
So right now, today, what's Artkos actually controlling? Is Kard part of the League or what?

Kard is Artkos's southwestern enclave, but it's... fragile. The notes say the population is mostly Edran-speaking and actually *welcomed the invasion* from Edrad twenty-eight years ago. So nominally Kard belongs to Artkos now after the war's settlement, but politically and linguistically it's more Edran. Similar story everywhere in the League -- Artkos has formal territory but weak actual control. The core Artkos holdings are the northwest (West Artkos) and the central capital of Ath'r, which Artkos conquered two hundred years ago. Everything else is reluctant subjects at best.
Alright last question: why would the Settlements of Ath'r have agreed to this structure? Seems like they could've just let Artkos absorb everyone back.

That's a great question and the notes don't answer it directly -- that's one of the blank spaces. My guess from reading between the lines: after thirty years of Ryraxos's war, the continental powers were probably exhausted and unwilling to fight over every border again immediately. Creating semi-independent princedoms like Rathakos might've been a compromise to give everyone breathing room without starting another war. But why exactly the Settlements chose that structure? The DM hasn't filled that in yet. It's a detail worth asking about in actual play.
okay so real talk -- one of your moons is literally just Earth's moon? same mass, same distance, same everything? that feels like a cop-out

Fair skepticism. Yeah, Quartus is intentionally calibrated to match Luna almost exactly -- same mass (1 lunar mass), same density (3,344 kg/m³), same orbital distance (~1.05 LD). The brightness is basically identical too, about 0.9× Earth's full moon. It's deliberate, not lazy. Quartus is the calendar moon -- the monthly cycle. Most of the interesting mechanics come from Secondus, Tertius, and Primus doing weird things. Quartus is the stable reference point.
so you just... copied homework from Earth. we fought a thirty-year war and this is what we get, a moon that's basically a photocopy

I get the frustration. But think about it from a worldbuilding angle: if the monthly cycle is your calendar anchor, you want it reliable and predictable. Quartus *is* that. Meantime, Secondus retrograde, Tertius prograde, Primus stationary and geostationary -- those are the moons that drive tides, navigation, celestial weirdness. Having one straightforward reference lets everything else be strange without breaking the whole system. It's not that you copied Earth; it's that you solved the same problem the same way.
fair enough. so if I'm drow who stayed topside, what does Quartus actually mean to me? are we using it to track anything?

Quartus is your monthly calendar -- the most obvious one. Twenty-nine-point-five-day cycle, full moon to full moon. Before you surfaced, Bahamat's church in Qama already used the lunar calendar; you'd inherit that, or at least respect it if you're dealing with surface politics. The other moons (especially Tertius and Secondus) are stranger and need tables to predict. Quartus? Anyone can just watch the sky. That's its value.
so my gondolier character is from Qaringil. what's the local dialect like? is it basically waterdeep english where everyone speaks the same thing?

Nope, Qaringil has its own distinct flavor. The primary language there is Islas -- the language of the islands. But the Qaringil variety is unusual: it has heavier than normal Qis vocabulary mixed in. That's because the city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qismos, so they brought their speech patterns with them. It's more of a creole situation than a single standard dialect.
huh, so like -- does everyone in the city speak that way, or are there class differences? in FR you've got nobles vs street rats but they're basically the same language

Good question. The notes don't actually specify class-based dialect splits within Qaringil itself. What we know is that the prestige dialect of Islas (the official, capital version) is different from the Qaringil variety -- the Qaringil version just has more Qis vocabulary mixed in. So there's probably *some* code-switching between the standard and the local flavor, but the exact breakdown by class, occupation, or guild isn't detailed yet. That's a blank space the DM could fill with your backstory if you want.
okay, but compare this to like... Neverwinter or Candlekeep. the whole continent has one language right? same as Forgotten Realms?

Different model. Qamis has multiple national languages -- Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas. They're not standardized either. Someone from the capital of Artkos would struggle to understand Artklan from someone in Rathakos. Islas is specifically the language of the islands in the central sea. So there's way more linguistic diversity than FR's Common tongue. You're working with a much more fragmented language landscape where location and origin matter a lot.
so my gondolier character would speak Islas with the Qis flavor because they grew up in Qaringil, right? that's their native tongue?

Yeah, exactly. If you're from Qaringil, your native speech is the local Islas with Qis vocabulary mixed in. You'd also get a bonus language (the notes say everyone gets one), and you can pick how you know it -- maybe you learned it from a trader, or from family connections, or whatever fits your character. The Qis vocabulary in your Islas would probably make you sound distinctly *from* Qaringil to other Islas speakers elsewhere on the islands.
So I'm looking at the moon system and I see Quintus listed -- but it says it's a 'trace particle'? Is that like a placeholder or something? Also curious about the Q name -- is that Old Draconic?

Good catch on both. Quintus is a trace particle at the Sun-Qaia L4 point -- 60 degrees ahead of Qaia in its orbit. It librates (wobbles) between 45 and 80 degrees from Qaia with a 2,000-year period, and it's effectively massless. The DM included it mainly for worldbuilding flavor rather than as a functional moon. It doesn't affect tides or navigation like the other five do.

And yes, the Q is Old Draconic -- or Old Qis, depending on how you want to name the scholarly language. Q is pronounced 'Kh' (that hard back-of-throat sound). The 'Q without U' pattern shows up in a bunch of place names because Old Draconic was the language of treaties and formal writing after the dragon empire ended. So Qaia, Qamis, Qaringil, Qama -- all 'Kh' sounds.
Ah, so like how Forgotten Realms has Toril and its moon Selûne -- you've got actual orbital mechanics worked out here? That's way more elaborate.

This is a homebrew setting rather than Forgotten Realms, but yeah -- the moon system has real physics. All five active moons (Primus through Septimus) have calculated orbits, periods, tidal effects, even brightness values. Primus is geostationary and used for celestial navigation. The system is genuinely worked out, not just flavor.
Navigation by moon? So like how people used Luna in the real world? Does every nation use the same calendar then, or is there regional variation like with the languages?

Primus is the anchor for navigation -- it hangs fixed over one hemisphere, so you can measure its elevation to find longitude without a chronometer. That's homebrew physics specific to this system.

As for calendars, the notes don't specify regional variation in that way. Tertius has an ~8-day week and Quartus has a ~29-day month, so those are natural timekeeping units, but whether different nations call them different things or use different epoch points -- that's blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.
Got it. So when Old Draconic place names use Q=Kh, does that apply everywhere -- like, do Edran speakers pronounce Qamis as 'Khamis'? Or do they have their own names for regions?

The Q/Kh is an Old Draconic thing -- the scholarly and treaty language. But the notes say there are no standardized national languages; each nation (Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas) has its own, with major regional dialects. So an Edran speaker from the capital might pronounce place names differently than someone from a regional dialect.

The notes don't detail how each nation's language handles Old Draconic borrowings, so that's up for interpretation. It's a gap worth asking the DM about if it matters for your character.
So what's going on with the Church of Bahamut? I keep hearing about some fracturing in the priesthood.

There's a schism, yeah. Qama -- the Church's traditional holy seat -- was occupied by the drow during the late Ryraxian War. The drow installed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. But the Church didn't accept that, so Qachu II emerged as an alternate candidate operating from Qis. Now you've got two competing high priests: Qe'lth in control of Qama and the relics, Qachu representing the Church's traditional authority from Qis.
So is Qacu II the legit one, running things from Qaringil? Or is Qe'lth actually in charge?

It's Qachu, not Qacu -- and he's in Qis, not Qaringil. Easy mix with all the Q- names from Old Qis. Qaringil's the island city-state in the central sea. To answer your question: Qe'lth controls the holy city and the relics, but Qachu has the Church's traditional backing. Depending on who you ask, one's legitimate and the other's a drow puppet.
Right, sorry. So this whole split is because of the drow invasion, then? Or was there tension before?

All because of the drow occupation. Before the Ryraxian War, the Church was unified -- one high priest, no split. The drow took Qama and installed Qe'lth, which triggered the schism and Qachu's emergence.
Got it. Any word on what the drow's endgame is with holding the holy city?
That's not filled in yet -- blank space. The notes mention they haven't despoiled Qama, so it's not just wanton destruction. But what they actually want from controlling the priesthood, that's open.
so you're telling me a navigator can just look at some moon and know their longitude without even a clock? that sounds like fantasy bullshit.

I get the skepticism -- it does sound too convenient. But it's physics, not magic. Primus is locked over one spot on the equator (the prime meridian at 0,0). If you measure how high it appears above your horizon, you can calculate how far away you are from that spot. Your latitude tells you which circle around Qaia you're on; your distance from the Primus point narrows it to another circle. Where those circles intersect, accounting for whether Primus appears east or west of you -- that's your longitude. No clock needed because you're just measuring angles to a fixed object.
okay but like... how is that actually done in practice? you need special instruments, right? and navigation tables? seems expensive.

It does need instruments -- a decent astrolabe, cross-staff, or sextant to measure angles. But once you have those, you just need a single lookup table called the primsine table. It's a list of values, one column, under 200 entries for rough work. Ship captains already carry navigation tables anyway. The actual procedure is three table lookups and one division. That's it. Cheaper and simpler than solving the longitude problem the way Earth does it -- requiring a chronometer good enough to keep time at sea, which is much harder to build and maintain.
wait, so Forthyr actually has this figured out? or is this some Edran thing we'd have to learn?

The notes don't specify whether Forthyr has formal navigation schools or charts -- that's a blank space. But the method itself is available to anyone with a clear sky and instruments. It's pure geometry and math, not a secret. The limitation is that Primus only works on one hemisphere -- observers on the far side of Qaia never see it, so they're stuck with dead reckoning or harder stellar methods. But yeah, any seafaring nation worth its salt would eventually work this out or learn it from someone who did.
huh. so the old Dragon Empire, before the dragons left -- they didn't have this? or they just didn't write it down?

That's honestly not in the notes. Primus has always been there -- it's a permanent feature of Qaia's sky -- so in theory they could have figured it out. But we don't know if they did, or how long it took to go from "there's a weird moon in the sky" to "wait, we can use this for navigation." One detail: Old Draconic is the language of scholarship and treaties, so if the old empire had written navigation treatises, they'd be in that language. But the modern notes don't mention that. Could be a thing your character knows about, or doesn't.
alright that's... actually kind of cool. so any Forthyr navigator worth a damn would know this?

If they're working the sea or doing long-distance travel, yeah -- Primus is visible across a huge range, and the method is straightforward once you have the table. Whether Forthyr formally trains navigators or if knowledge spreads through merchant networks and adventuring parties, the notes don't say. But the tool itself is real and practical, and it solves a problem Earth didn't solve until the 1700s. So respectably ancient, actually.

hey so my friend was telling me about this D&D setting Qamis and I got curious. but I'm checking out the wiki and I see Rathakos mentioned a bunch? but then in the Qaringil stuff it says those regions don't exist. did they get deleted or something? like in Forgotten Realms sometimes whole nations just vanish between editions

Good question! It's not that they were deleted—it's that they didn't exist yet. Qaringil is set about 200 years *before* the present-day Qamis campaign.

During Qaringil times, the Red Mountains region was just mountains occupied by various non-state peoples. The Holy Qis Empire *claimed* the area but didn't really govern it.

Then came the thirty-year Ryraxian War, which was a huge chaos event. The Red Mountains got drawn into that conflict, eventually organized into something called the Red Mountain Republic under Edran control. When Edrad withdrew, the factions in the Assembly split, and out of all that emerged two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. They were formalized as a Princedom under the Settlements of Ath'r—so they're *new* since the war ended.

So unlike settings where nations get wiped off the map, here you're seeing state formation happen. Does that make sense?

ohhh okay so like a new country was born basically. kinda like how the United States split from Britain? that's wild. so were there cities there or was it all just empty wilderness like the Underdark or something?

Not empty—but the notes don't fill in a ton of detail on what was there before. They call the Red Mountains "one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions in the interior of Qamis," and mention that Rathakos (the current state) has "a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship." So it was inhabited enough to have people who do adventurer-type work, and dangerous enough that you needed that tradition.

There was definitely some kind of settlement or communities there, since people reorganized into the Red Mountain Republic during the war. But the specific cities, cultures, or details about what the non-state peoples were doing—that's in the "blank spaces" the DM hasn't filled in yet. Could be a hook for a character backstory if you wanted.

The big difference from Forgotten Realms is that this isn't meant to feel like a fully-mapped-out ancient world—it's more of an active, changing setting where the political map literally shifted during the recent war.
got it, blank spaces make sense. so like... wait, was Rathakos under Edrad control in Qaringil times then? or part of the Holy Qis Empire? I'm getting confused on which nations ruled where back then

Neither, technically—not effectively anyway. The Holy Qis Empire *claimed* the Red Mountains, but there's no real government structure described. It was just occupied by independent peoples.

Edrad didn't rule there either in Qaringil times. Edrad was the dominant coastal power back then (ruled by the Keyberos dynasty, same as now), but it was focused on the coasts and its own core territory. The rough political split in Qaringil was: Edrad to the south, Artkos to the northwest, the Holy Qis Empire claiming central territory from Ath'r, and then Forthyr to the northeast.

The Red Mountains were kind of a buffer zone—claimed but not really administered. Then the Ryraxian War happened, Edrad pushed in militarily, and when they pulled back, the locals had to figure out their own government. That's when Rathakos and Acrokos emerged.

Makes sense?
yeah okay that tracks. so one more thing—is Rath (the capital of Rathakos) like an ancient city that predates all this? or did it get built during the war?

The notes say Rath is the capital and sits "in the northern foothills" but don't give its history. Could be ancient, could be new—that's another blank space the DM hasn't filled yet.

Given that the Red Mountains had non-state peoples and a strong adventuring tradition, there were probably *some* settlements already in place before Rathakos as a nation formed. So Rath might've been an existing settlement that just became the administrative center of a new political entity. But I can't say for sure from what's documented.
so what's the deal with Quintus? It's in the moon notes but not in the navigation guide.

Quintus is at the Sun-Qaia L4 Lagrange point, about 60 degrees ahead of Qaia. It librates -- wobbles -- between about 45 and 80 degrees from Qaia with a period of roughly 2,000 years. It's called a trace particle because it's effectively massless: zero gravitational effect on Qaia or its moon system. Since it's not fixed in the sky like Primus and produces no tidal signature, it's useless for navigation. The notes say it's included for worldbuilding purposes, but don't elaborate beyond that.
massless? Why include something massless at all?

The notes explicitly say it's 'included for worldbuilding purposes' but don't explain further what that purpose is. So honestly, that's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. For navigation and physics, massless means irrelevant. But whether it matters to the campaign's story -- that's unclear from the docs.

yo so i've been deep diving the setting notes and i keep seeing Ezevir Arth pop up in two different time periods??? like there's Ezevir Arth II ruling Artkos 200 years ago in Qaringil, and then there's High King Ezevir Arth in the present day campaign... are these the same person somehow or just a naming coincidence

great question and honestly the notes don't fully clarify this! there's definitely a confusing gap. in the Qaringil prequel, about 200 years ago, King Ezevir Arth II ruled Artkos and actually doubled the size of their holdings. he had a daughter, Ezvia Arth I, who aimed to expand even further. then jump forward to the present day (post-Ryraxian War) and there's High King Ezevir Arth governing Artkos again. whether the current one is descended from Ezevir Arth II, or if it's just a naming tradition in their dynasty, or something else entirely -- the notes don't say. probably worth asking your DM!

that's wild. ok so separate thing but i've been thinking about navigation, right? like Primus is geostationary at 42,000 km out and gives you instant longitude without a chronometer... but if Artkos controlled more territory back then under Ezevir Arth II, i'm wondering if access to Primus observation points and tidal knowledge would've been a serious power advantage? like the notes mention Primus is only visible from one hemisphere

ohhh that's a smart observation. yeah, if you think about it from a practical empire-building perspective: Primus is fixed over one specific point on the equator, so observers within roughly ±80° of the prime meridian can see it. that means anything beyond that range -- the entire far hemisphere -- is completely locked out of Primus-based navigation. they'd need dead reckoning or stellar methods instead. if Artkos controlled key coastal territories near that Primus visibility band, especially trade hubs, they'd have a *major* edge in maritime navigation and commerce compared to nations on the far side. plus Primus's tidal signature -- even though it's just a static ~2 cm bulge and not an oscillating tide -- is a permanent geographic feature. could shift where harbors are viable, where fishing grounds are richest, etc.

wait, only a ~2 cm static bulge? but you've got Secundus and Tertius and Quartus all swinging around with way more tidal force. how does that actually play out on the ocean? like Secundus alone is 1.47x Luna's tidal strength, Tertius is 2.04x...

yeah the real tidal chaos comes from those three! Secundus (retrograde, dark iron surface, ~4.5 day period) plus Tertius (prograde, ~8-day period) plus Quartus (the lunar clone at ~29.5 days) add up to roughly 4.4x Earth's moon's total tidal force. and they're all on different periods, so you get three incommensurate oscillating drivers overlaid on each other -- plus the Sun adds its own ~12-hour tide. the result is probably wild tidal ranges, complex shallow-water dynamics, and maybe some really dangerous tidal currents in narrow straits. so yeah, Primus's 2 cm static contribution is almost a rounding error compared to the dynamic tidal dance from the other three. but navigation-wise, Primus is still huge because it's *fixed* -- that's the advantage.

that's insane. so like... if i were an ancient civilization trying to figure out navigation before chronometers existed, Primus would be THE thing, right? way more reliable than trying to track moon phase cycles or whatever

exactly. on Earth it took centuries to solve longitude because you needed a precise chronometer. on Qaia you've got a fixed point in the sky that never moves -- if you know your latitude (pole star) and measure Primus's elevation angle, you get a longitude fix with just two angle measurements and some precomputed tables. total game-changer for maritime empires. so yeah, civilizations that had access to Primus and understood the math would've had a *massive* navigation advantage over anyone on the far hemisphere. could've been a core reason why the near-side nations dominated trade and power for so long.
DUDE the drow hold Qama?? the HOLY CITY?? how is that even a thing

RIGHT?? It's such a wild setup. So the drow were recruited as allies by Artkos during the Ryraxian War—basically a desperation move. They occupied Qama during the late war phases and just... never left. Now they've got scattered enclaves all over the surface, but Qama is their crown jewel.
But the Church of Bahamut is supposed to be there!! How did they pull off keeping it??

Oh man, so THIS is the good part. The drow appointed their own high priest—Qe'lth IV. So the Church SPLIT. There's an alternate high priest candidate, Qachu II, running things out of Qis, but here's the kicker: Qe'lth has all the actual relics. They're still in Qama. So Qachu doesn't have any of the relics of office. It's this insane schism where one side has the institutional power and artifacts, and the other side has... the claim to legitimacy, maybe?
That's so good though because it's not resolved!! That's a campaign hook waiting to happen

YES! Exactly! The notes are honest about what's filled in and what's not—they don't spell out what Qachu's legitimacy is based on, or what the long-term play is for either side. But yeah, you've got competing high priests, sacred relics in drow hands, a Holy City under occupation, and a Church in schism. That's a powder keg.
hey, got back into town and missed last session -- people kept talking about Luna and I got confused, is that like a goddess or something in the setting?

Luna isn't part of the setting itself -- it's just Earth's moon, used as a reference standard for measuring Qaia's moons. So when the notes say Primus is 0.34 times Luna's angular size, or Tertius is 1.09 times Luna, they're comparing to the real moon everyone's familiar with. Makes the moon system easier to visualize without needing a whole separate reference frame.
oh okay, so that's why they kept throwing out all those numbers. But wait, there's an actual moon in the system called Quartus, right? How does that fit in?

Quartus is actually a clone of Earth's moon -- similar mass, similar distance, similar brightness and albedo. It's basically what Luna would be if it orbited Qaia instead of Earth. So Quartus is the ~29-day moon that drives Qaia's monthly calendar, roughly as bright as Luna at full, same deal. The difference is Tertius (which is slightly larger and closer) is almost as impressive in the sky, and then you've got Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus filling out the rest of the system.
So in Qaringil, which guilds have never been run by the Five Families? I'm trying to understand the power structure.

Gondolier and Wool. Those are the only two major guilds that have never been led by one of the Five Families. Gondolier is particularly interesting because they also handle maintenance and control of the bridges--so you'd think that'd be something the powerful families would want to control. But nope.
That's interesting. So why didn't they? The bridges seem like a pretty big deal.

The notes don't explain the reason, which is a good catch. It's marked as a fact but the 'why' is blank space. Could be that the Gondoliers have traditions or guild structures that made a takeover impossible, or maybe there's been a series of independent leaders who managed to hold them off, or maybe the actual power of bridge control is less useful than it looks compared to the financial leverage of Banking or Shipping. Either way, it's left open--possibly intentionally, in case you want to build something around it.
so I'm making a half-elven noble from Jia, and I'm trying to understand what the Keyberos dynasty actually went through during the war. Like, were they in exile the whole time? Did they fight back?

Good question—and honestly the notes cover the broad strokes but leave the details pretty open. Here's what we know: the dynasty ruled Edrad for four centuries before getting deposed by the Revolutionary Council. Then during the war, Artkos and Qis'mos signed a pact to march on Edrad, overthrow the Revolutionary Council, and restore the Keyberos dynasty to power. That restoration is what kicked off the thirty-year war.

After Ryraxos was defeated and exiled, the dynasty got the throne back again. But now they're finding it's easy to sign an edict reversing thirty years of change—and much harder to enforce it. People got used to things being different.

That's genuinely all the notes pin down about the dynasty's actual experience during the war though. Why the Revolutionary Council took over in the first place, what they actually did, whether the dynasty was in exile or what—that's all blank space your backstory could fill in.
wait, so Artkos and Qis'mos basically invaded to put the dynasty back? That seems weird. Why would they care?

Fair question. The notes don't really explain the *why*—just that they did it. It's framed as part of a larger political recalibration: Artkos was trying to restore its dominance, and putting a friendly dynasty back on Edrad's throne would have been part of that. But the actual diplomatic reasoning, or what the Revolutionary Council did that made them targets, or what the Keyberos dynasty promised in return for the intervention—none of that's filled in.

It's one of those blank spaces. The DM left it open, maybe for you to build into your PC's backstory. If you're a noble, you might *know* the answer from a family perspective—or you might not, depending on what role your character had during the war.
okay so my character is probably young enough that she was a kid during most of the war. What would I know as a young noble kid? Like, were there dynastic heirs in hiding, or what?

That's a great PC angle—and unfortunately it's another blank. The notes don't say anything about succession, heirs, whether the dynasty had members in exile, or what their actual power structure looked like during the restoration. 

But here's what's available for you to work with: you're making a half-elven noble from Edran nobility, which means you're between one quarter and three quarters elf. You could be any age now (the war's over), so you could have been a child during it, a teenager, or born during it. Your family would have had *some* position during those thirty years—loyal to the dynasty, working with the Revolutionary Council, collaborating with Ryraxos, or something else entirely. That's all yours to build.

The DM basically left the dynasty's internal story open on purpose. What happened to *your* branch of the family is part of what makes your backstory yours.
alright, last question: after the war, is the dynasty actually in control? Like are people listening to them, or is everything chaos?

Mixed situation. They're officially back on the throne and their borders are restored to their historical lines. But—and this is the key tension the notes highlight—people spent thirty years under different rule. Edicts reversing all that change look good on paper, but actually enforcing them is proving much harder.

That's about as specific as the notes get though. Whether nobles are outright rebellious, whether the Church is difficult, whether Jia itself is stable or fractious—that's not pinned down. You're starting in Rathakos, which is a border state with its own chaos, so Edrad's internal situation is kind of backdrop to your actual campaign.

But as a noble from Edrad, you'd definitely have opinions and personal stakes in how well (or poorly) the restoration is working out.
so what's the deal with the different campaign eras? like, is there a reason they're separated, or is it just 'DM ran a campaign 200 years earlier and had a different map'?

Good question. The notes don't explain a historical cycle or causation behind why eras happen -- that's a blank space. What's documented is that Qaringil (~200 years earlier) has a completely different political map: the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r instead of separate nations, no Rathakos or Acrokos, Edrad not dominant in the northeast yet. So there's clearly major geopolitical change over centuries, but the *why* and *how* isn't filled in.

Now, if you're asking whether the *moons* drive cycles -- there's actually something there. The three inner moons go through full-moon and new-moon eras that alternate roughly every 6 years within a 12-year superycle. All documented, with exact alignment dates. Whether those celestial cycles *cause* anything historical though? Also blank space.
ah so like, moon data: solid. 'boom, dark age' connectors: not written yet. okay that makes sense for a homebrew thing

Exactly. The celestial mechanics are completely worked out -- navigation tables, tidal forces, alignment calendars. But whether those cycles drive politics, religion, population shifts, or anything else? Up to the DM. You've got the building blocks; the interpretation is open.
cool. so for executive summary purposes: Qamis is post-war present day, Qaringil is the prequel, moons are documented if you care about calendar stuff, everything else is the DM's sandbox?

That's the shape of it, yeah. Qamis: present day, just came out of a 30-year war, Ryraxos exiled to Secundus, Keyberos restored. Qaringil: 200 years earlier, different map. Moons: stable, predictable, useful for worldbuilding callbacks. Nation states, cities, religions, blank spaces, how-it-all-connects: DM territory.

So I'm reading through that Primus navigation system, and I'm seeing the accuracy tables say longitude precision is best near the equator and the prime meridian. What's actually going on there? Is it just a tool limitation or is there something about the geometry?

It's pure geometry. When you're near the equator and the prime meridian, Primus is high in the sky -- potentially even directly overhead. A small change in your elevation angle to Primus means you've moved a huge distance east or west. But when you're far from both -- say, near the visibility boundary at 81 degrees away -- Primus sits low on the horizon. Now the elevation angle changes very slowly as you move east or west. Shallow angle, shallow rate of change. So your instrument can measure the angle just as precisely, but that precision translates to a much larger uncertainty in your actual longitude.

Think of it like trying to read a clock. If the hour hand is moving fast across the numbers, you can pin down the time precisely. If it's moving slowly, the same level of precision gives you a much wider range of possible times.
ok so like, the Keyberos dynasty... do i actually have to care about that? it sounds like boring political stuff

Fair question! You don't *have* to, but here's why it matters: the Keyberos dynasty just spent thirty years getting stomped by a dragon dictator named Ryraxos, and they just got their power back. That's a HUGE deal for the world. Edrad is the richest and most culturally dominant nation in Qamis, so when their government gets restored after chaos, it affects everyone -- including Rathakos, where your campaign starts. Think of it like if the US president got overthrown for thirty years and then came back. It ripples everywhere.
ok yeah but we're starting in Rathakos, which is like... a mountain border thing. why would my character even run into the dynasty?

Solid point! Rathakos is independent and pretty remote, so you won't be dealing with Keyberos directly. BUT -- Rathakos just became a princedom after the war ended, and it's still figuring out its independence from Edrad. There's internal unrest, old dragon cults, frontier politics. The Keyberos restoration is part of why everything is so unsettled right now. You might never meet a Keyberos, but the dynasty's return is reshaping the entire political landscape around you. More practical: lots of refugees and merchants from Edrad pass through mountain towns looking for new opportunities, so you'll probably *hear* about it.
ahhh ok that makes sense. so it's like background radiation that affects the story but not like, the main plot

Exactly. It's the context that makes the world feel alive. Your DM will use it as background -- political refugees, merchants complaining about the regime, rumors about what's happening back in the capital. But your actual adventure is going to be local: what's happening in the mountains, who's making trouble in Rathakos itself. The dynasty is important because it explains *why* everything is chaotic right now, not because you're fighting the Keyberos directly.
Hey, I'm working on a campaign and came across your world notes. You've got a geostationary moon called Primus? That's kind of like Earth's geostationary satellites, right? Seems cool.

Yeah, same idea -- Primus sits fixed in the sky above one point on the equator and never moves. But it's actually a lot smaller and less impressive than you might think. Earth's geostationary satellites are tiny and way up at about 35,800 km altitude. Primus orbits at about 42,160 km, so a bit farther, but the real difference is that Primus is an actual moon -- 64 km in radius, with a mass of about 0.0001 Earth's Moon. Still, because it's made mostly of iron and is really dark (albedo 0.06), it's actually dimmer than our Moon even though it's closer to Qaia. In the sky it looks about one-third the size of Earth's full moon.
Wait, so it's dark? How do you even see it then if it's that small and dim?

Fair question. At full illumination it reaches magnitude −9.62 -- which is about 3.1 magnitudes dimmer than a full moon, so roughly 1/13th the brightness. That doesn't sound great, but it's still plenty bright enough to see easily on a clear night. Navigators and lookouts definitely notice it. The kicker is that it never moves -- it's fixed in the sky at a known point above the equator, never rising, never setting. That's way more useful than brightness alone.
Ah okay, so the fact that it doesn't move is the whole point. How do sailors actually use that?

Longitude. On Earth, finding your longitude at sea used to require an accurate chronometer -- that was the whole "longitude problem" for centuries. But Primus solves it geometrically. Since it's always fixed directly above the equator (at the prime meridian), measuring how high Primus appears above your horizon tells you how far east or west you are from that fixed point. Combine that with your latitude (which you get from the pole star the usual way), and you've got a complete position fix. No clock needed -- just two angle measurements on a clear night. There's even a lookup table (called the primsine function) that makes the math a single table lookup and a division.
That's really clever. But wait -- you said it's only visible on one hemisphere?

Exactly. Primus is geostationary above a single point, so it hangs permanently above the near hemisphere and is forever invisible from the far side. That's a huge asymmetry -- navigators on the near side can fix their longitude easily once the sun sets. Navigators on the far side are stuck with dead reckoning or the much harder lunar-distance methods using other moons. It's structurally similar to how Earth's pre-chronometer explorers were hampered; Qaia just solved it for half the world instead of all of it.
One more thing -- you mentioned it's magically anchored. What does that mean exactly?

Good catch. In reality, a moon this close to a planet would slowly drift outward due to tidal forces -- that's what happened to Earth's Moon over billions of years. But Primus is held in place by magic. The notes say it's "magically anchored," which prevents that tidal migration. There's also a side effect: because it co-rotates with Qaia and never sweeps across the sky, it creates a permanent ~2 cm bulge in Qaia's oceans (static tidal bulge) rather than an oscillating daily tide. The daily tides actually come from Qaia's other moons -- Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Primus is just a navigation fixture and a permanent geographic feature.

hey, im new to D&D and building a character for the Qamis campaign. everyone keeps mentioning all these different moons - what are the seven moons and like, does it matter which hemisphere youre from?

Cool! Yeah, the moons matter a lot. Going from closest to farthest from Qaia, you've got: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, Septimus, and Quintus (though Quintus is basically just a trace particle at a Lagrange point, mostly worldbuilding stuff).

The big ones are Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus - they're all bright enough to see clearly at night and they drive the tides. But Primus is weird and interesting: it's small and dark, but it sits fixed in the sky above the equator. Never moves. From one hemisphere you can always see it; from the far side of the planet, you can never see it.

Which matters because Primus is how navigators find longitude without a chronometer. So yeah, hemisphere totally affects what your character would grow up knowing.
wait so if primus is always in the same spot in the sky, that means like... different places have different night skies? that seems like a big deal for a character backstory

You're thinking about this exactly right. Yeah, Primus visibility is a hard geographic split. If your character grew up on the side of the planet where you can see it, they'd learn to navigate by it, know its position by heart. If they grew up on the far side, they'd navigate by dead reckoning and the other moons instead.

And the three main moons cycle through their phases constantly - Secundus every 4.5 days, Tertius every 8 days (that's Qaia's week), Quartus every 30 days (the month). So the night sky actually changes a lot. It becomes part of how you mark time and seasons.
ok im thinking rathakos - the notes say its mountainous with a strong adventuring tradition. would the moons be like, important to someone from there?

Yeah, Rathakos is a great choice for that. You're landlocked in the northern Red Mountains, so you wouldn't be navigating by Primus the way a sailor would. But you'd still see Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus cycling through their phases - and that would become part of the landscape and calendar you grew up with.

A character from Rathakos would know the moons as weather patterns, seasonal markers, maybe old mountain folklore and stories about dragon cults. The region has a strong adventuring tradition, so local knowledge - of the mountains, the old ways, what the night sky means - would be part of how you'd operate.

Does that feel right for your character?
hey so my character wants to be a Bahumat worshiper and the DM mentioned Qama. what's that place?

Qama is the holy city of the Church of Bahumat -- it's basically the most important place for anyone who worships Bahumat. It's located in Qis'mos, which is a region in central Qamis that's got a huge religious and cultural presence. Think of it like... if your character is Christian, Qama would be like Jerusalem or Rome, that kind of vibe.
oh okay cool. so like, is everyone there a priest or whatever?

Not everyone, but the Church has an enormous presence there. Qama is the traditional seat of the Church -- meaning the high priest of Bahumat operates from there. So yeah, lots of clergy, temples, religious scholars. It's the center of the faith.
sounds awesome. why would my DM mention it specifically?

Could be a few reasons. Maybe your character has a connection to the faith and Qama would be pilgrimage-worthy, or maybe there's a storyline thing. But I should mention -- in the present-day campaign (Qamis), Qama's situation is... complicated. During the Ryraxian War, drow forces occupied the city. They're still there, and they've appointed their own high priest instead of the Church's chosen leader. So there's been a split -- the legitimate high priest is operating from elsewhere with no access to the relics of office.
whoa wait, drow just... took over a holy city?

Yeah, during the late phases of the war. They occupied it and haven't left. The notes say they haven't actually despoiled it -- they're respecting the city itself -- but politically? They're in control. They put their own high priest (Qe'lth IV) in charge instead of the Church's candidate. Which is why there's now a schism: the legitimate high priest, Qachu II, is operating from the city of Qis with the support of the Church, but without any of the actual relics and ceremonial stuff that are still in Qama.
so like... the real high priest doesn't even have the holy stuff?

Exactly. That's what makes it a schism -- there's a religious and political conflict about who's legitimate. The drow control the physical city and the relics, so Qe'lth IV has all the ceremonial authority that comes with that. But the Church recognizes Qachu II as the true high priest even though he's operating from Qis without the symbols of office. It's a messy situation, and honestly, it could be a hook for stories your DM might want to explore.
so my friend was telling me about this world and mentioned the Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr? are they like actual dragon rulers or something

Sort of -- they rule Forthyr, which is this huge nation in the northeast. The current ruler is Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, and the title comes from their bloodline. Used to be they were half-dragonborn (so part dragon, genetically), but that's mixed out over time. Now they're just called sorcerer kings, which means they're magic users who rule. The title "Dragoncaller" stuck around even though the actual dragon blood thinned out.
oh so they USED to be part dragon but aren't anymore? like they got weaker or something?

That's the implication from the notes -- "bloodlines ran thin." But the exact details of how or why that happened? That's actually a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. It's definitely a thing that happened between the Qaringil era (200 years before the main campaign) and now, but how and when it happened is open.
weird. and what's their whole deal? like are they evil or just different or what

Not described as evil, but the notes call Forthyr "somewhat backwards" as a nation. They have this religious sect that wants to bring back dragon emperors -- like the old days -- and the Dragoncaller dynasty tacitly encourages it, maybe even supports it behind the scenes. Whether that's because they miss being part dragon, or they like having a cult that backs their authority, or something else entirely... that's not specified.
wait so there's like an actual dragon religion? are those people worshipping dragons or trying to bring dragons back to rule?

The notes say the sect is "calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors," so it's about wanting old-style dragon rule back. But the specifics -- like what they believe, what they do, whether they're worshipping dragons or just political activists, how many of them there are -- none of that's filled in. Blank space.

okay so i'm building a character from Forthyr who's into that whole Dragon Emperor cult thing, and i'm trying to figure out what life is actually like there. what can you tell me about Forthyr culture and geography?

Good starting point. Forthyr is a huge nation in the northeast of Qamis, ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI -- he's the latest in a line of sorcerer-kings. The dynasty used to be dragonborn, but the bloodlines ran thin over time, so they're mostly human now.

The country has a reputation for being "somewhat backwards" by continental standards. And here's the relevant bit for your character: there's a prominent religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, and it gets tacit encouragement -- maybe even outright support -- from the Dragoncaller dynasty itself. So that sect is basically embedded in the power structure, which is a juicy detail for backstory.

Beyond that though, the notes are pretty sparse. You get the vibe but not a ton of granular detail about cities, regional variations, how the cult actually operates, that sort of thing.
wait so the Dragoncaller dynasty is SUPPORTING the Dragon Emperor cult? that's wild. so is that like an official state religion thing or more underground?

The notes say it's "tacit encouragement, if not support" -- so it's not quite framed as an official state religion, but the dynasty is definitely not suppressing it. It's more like the Dragoncaller house sees the Dragon Emperor revival movement as ideologically aligned with them and lets it operate openly. That's actually a weird power move: they're signaling to the population that they're pro-dragon without necessarily claiming to BE dragons themselves anymore.

But honestly, the specific structure -- how integrated it is with temples, whether there are rival sects, what the actual theology looks like, how ordinary Forthyrians view it -- that's all blank space. You could absolutely use that as part of your character's backstory though. Like, your character could be wrestling with what the cult actually believes vs. what the regime is using it for.
got it. so what about the actual map -- like what cities or regions in Forthyr are there? i want to know where my character might be FROM

That's the problem -- the DM has explicitly left Forthyr as a big blank space. You get the broad strokes (it's in the northeast, it's huge, it's ruled by the Dragoncallers, it's culturally oriented toward dragon worship and sorcery) but specific city names, regional divisions, cultural variations within the country, geography, resources -- all of that is unfilled.

The notes actually call this out: there are "blank spaces on the map that need to be filled," and Forthyr is one of them. But here's the opportunity: the DM said you could help fill that in as part of your backstory. So if you want your character to be from a specific city or region of Forthyr, you can basically create it as long as it fits the tone. What KIND of place are you imagining?
ohhh okay so i can just... make up a hometown? that's cool. hmm. i was thinking maybe a mining town or something, since the Red Mountains are right there on the border?

That tracks geographically. The Red Mountains do form the border between Forthyr and the other central nations (Rathakos, Acrokos, etc.), and there's definitely demand for ore and minerals across the continent. A Forthyrian mining town in the foothills could absolutely work.

Worth noting though: the Red Mountains themselves are described as "one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions in the interior of Qamis." Rathakos has a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship specifically because of it. So a mining town near that border would be in a pretty rough, frontier-y area -- plenty of natural reasons for your character to have learned to fight, or to have heard about the adventurer lifestyle. That could feed nicely into why they ended up traveling.
perfect. and like, would the Dragon Emperor sect have a strong presence in a border town like that? or is it more of a capital thing?

The notes don't really specify where the sect is strongest or how it's distributed across Forthyr. That's another blank space. You'd have to decide: is it a top-down thing that reaches all the way to a frontier mining town? Is it stronger in the heartland? Does your character's town have a temple, or is it more about local folk belief?

Frankly, that's a great character detail to workshop with your DM -- what was the sect's presence like in your hometown, and how did your character encounter it? Could be that a mining town in the foothills has a smaller, scrappier temple run by someone charismatic. Could be that it's everywhere. Could be your character had to travel to find a real community of believers. All good backstory hooks.
so what level's the party gonna be in this Qaringil campaign thing? and don't tell me it's some weird homebrew leveling system

Fifth level. Standard D&D experience points and progression. The notes are explicit: you're high enough that you can all be full members of any guild relevant to your class -- Adventurer, Alchemist, Mage, Thieves, whatever fits your character.

hold up. so a 5th level character can just... join the guild? the THIEVES guild? and that's meant to be normal? That doesn't parse. In Forgotten Realms you can't just walk into the Shadow Thieves and expect respect. What's different about Qaringil?

Qaringil's guild system isn't a crime syndicate hierarchy like the Shadow Thieves. It's structured more like professional organizations -- they have regulatory authority over their trades in the city. There are seven major guilds (Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, Wool) plus several smaller ones (Adventurer, Alchemist, Mage). Being a full member means you meet the skill bar and pay your dues. A 5th level Rogue would absolutely qualify as an Adventurer or Thieves guild member. The real power is held by the Five Families who run the major guilds and control the city's politics.

okay that's... actually interesting. so the guild membership matters mechanically? Like, if I'm a 5th level Rogue in the Thieves guild, what am I actually getting? And what's stopping me from just, uh, 'reorganizing' the whole operation if I'm competent enough?

That's a good question, and the notes don't fully spell out the mechanical benefits of guild membership -- that's blank space the DM will fill in based on how they want to run it. What's clear is that guild membership is social and political, not just mechanical perks. The Thieves guild isn't a single unified operation but multiple competing factions. More importantly, the city's actual power structure rests with the Five Families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, Vitelli) who control the major guilds and city governance. A 5th level Rogue joining the Thieves guild means you're a professional operator with standing, not that you command the organization. The current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, has held power for 19 years and is supposedly on his deathbed -- so there's massive political jockeying happening at the highest levels. You could absolutely leverage guild membership into influence, but you'd be navigating deep political waters.
so im making a character from rathakos and someone mentioned the vitelli family? like what's even the deal with them, are they important or what

Vitelli is a merchant family, yeah -- but here's the thing: they're from Qaringil, the island city-state in the central sea. They lead the Silk Guild there and also have fingers in shipping and mercantile. Their sigil is a moth.

Now, Qaringil is from the prequel era, about 200 years before the Qamis campaign where Rathakos exists. So I don't actually have info on whether Vitelli has any presence in present-day Rathakos or what happened to them in between. The notes cover them in Qaringil during the prequel, but not their later status.
wait qaringil's on an island? so they're not even on the main continent? that seems weird to be relevant to a rathakos character tbh

Yeah, fair point. You'd need a specific reason for that connection to matter -- like maybe your character has sea-trade ties or family abroad, but otherwise it's pretty distant.

Rathakos itself is interesting though: it was formed from the Red Mountain Republic after the Ryraxian War ended, and it still has internal friction -- old dragon cults in mountain communities, regions annexed from Edrad that haven't fully integrated. That's the stuff actually in the notes. If you want a family or faction hook for your character's backstory, there's more relevant local material, or I'm happy to help figure out how to make a Vitelli connection work if you really want it.
okay that makes sense. appreciate you not just making stuff up to sound cool

Good call. The DM's explicitly marked a lot of blank spaces in this setting, especially around Rathakos and the Red Mountains -- room for real stuff to get added later. Better to be honest about what's actually in the notes and what's a gap than to invent something that contradicts what comes next. If you want to anchor your character to a specific faction or family in Rathakos, or use the blank spaces for your own backstory, I'm here to help.

So I'm considering running Qaringil as a one-shot. I was reading about the navigation system and... honestly, it seems over-complicated. In Forgotten Realms, DMs don't stress about navigation mechanics, and my players don't really care. Why would I use a system that involves tables and calculations?

Fair question! You don't have to run the math if your players don't want it. It's worldbuilding more than a mechanical system. The real appeal is that Qaia's navigation is structurally different from other settings. Here's why: Earth took centuries to solve longitude at sea -- didn't really crack it until the 1700s. Qaia solved it at the world's founding. Primus, the geostationary moon, hangs motionless over a fixed point at the equator. Measure how high Primus appears, look it up in a table, and boom -- you have your longitude. No chronometer needed. It's elegant.
Okay but... doesn't Greyhawk have something similar? Like magical navigation solutions?

Greyhawk tends to handwave it. The thing about Qaia is that the solution isn't magical -- it's baked into the moon system's actual physics. Primus is anchored by dragon magic, sure, but the navigation benefit is pure geometry. And unlike Greyhawk, there's a real asymmetry: the far hemisphere of Qaia can't see Primus at all, so navigation there is a genuine problem. That difference matters. It means your world has an actual constraint.
Alright, I see the appeal. For a one-shot in Qaringil though -- will my players actually run into this?

Definitely. Qaringil is a maritime city-state that lives off shipping and trade, so navigators are woven into the culture. If your party talks to sailors, meets members of the Shipping Guild, or travels by boat, the navigation system becomes real local color. You don't need to do the calculations unless you want to, but NPCs would naturally reference Primus and the trade routes that depend on it. That kind of detail makes the world feel lived-in and distinct from Forgotten Realms.
okay so i'm trying to understand the political setup but this setting is HUGE. like, is Edrad always just the dominant power? seems like you're telling me the same stuff about them in every era.

Ha, fair criticism -- Edrad is complicated partly because it spans both campaigns. But they're actually in pretty different positions in each era. Which one are you asking about -- the Qaringil prequel, or the present-day Qamis campaign?
both? like give me the tldr. they're the coastal capital with the elf nobles right?

Yeah, that part stays constant: Jia is always the coastal capital, ruled by half-elven nobility. But the *timeline* matters a lot here.

In the **Qaringil prequel** (~200 years ago), Edrad is politically dominant and gradually expanding its control -- it's in a growth phase, but hasn't conquered everything yet. They're eyeing regions like Qeth and the northeast, which are still independent.

In the present-day **Qamis campaign**, Edrad has already fought and lost the thirty-year Ryraxian War. They were occupied by Ryraxos, and their borders got pushed back to historical lines. So they're currently *restoring* what they had, not expanding. The Keyberos dynasty just got the throne back, and everything is kind of a mess -- trying to reverse thirty years of change is harder than it looks.
okay wait so the Ryraxian War -- that only happened in the present timeline? not in Qaringil?

Exactly. The Ryraxian War is only in the present-day Qamis campaign. It's literally the thing that just ended when Qamis starts. In Qaringil, you're 200 years *before* all that happened, so no war, no dragon overlord, no Revolutionary Council -- Edrad is just on a normal trajectory of consolidation.
right right. so in Qaringil, Edrad still hasn't grabbed Qeth yet?

Correct. Qeth and the regions northeast of it are still independent in the Qaringil era. Edran foreign policy treats conquering them as a prime goal, but it hasn't happened yet. By the time you get to present-day Qamis, Edrad had them (and then lost control of some during the war, though the notes don't spell out the exact current status of Qeth).
So Qaringil is ruled as an 'incomprehensible republic' -- what makes it incomprehensible? Is it just flavor text or does the government actually work a specific way?

It's a real system, not just flavor. Qaringil is governed through seven major guilds: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. Each guild has a vote in city decisions, and they elect an Exarch as the nominal head of state. The 'incomprehensible' part is that it's not a single chain of command -- power is fragmented across guild interests, and the five dominant families control leadership of four of the seven major guilds. So you've got overlapping jurisdictions, family rivalries, and competing commercial interests all pulling in different directions at once.
Okay, so it's a merchant oligarchy masquerading as a republic. Do we know which families run which guilds? And what's the mechanical conflict here -- how does this prevent clean decision-making?

Exactly. The Five Families are: Peixota (Shipping), Manos (Judicial), Qanto (until recently led Mercantile but just lost it to a minor gnomish family), Volmer (Banking), and Vitelli (Silk). That's four of seven. The Gondolier and Wool guilds are notably *not* led by the major families, which is a pressure point. As for mechanical conflict: every guild vote involves negotiation between these families and their allies. A Peixota shipping decision might get blocked by Manos if the Judicial guild sees its interests threatened. The Exarch theoretically mediates, but only if he has enough buy-in from enough guilds. It's government by constant negotiation and log-rolling.
So the real power is the guilds, not the Exarch. But the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is described as 'on death's doorstep' after 19 years. What happens to all this fragile balance when he dies?

That's the campaign hook right there. With a weak or absent Exarch, the guild power structure has to negotiate a replacement -- but there's no formal succession process described in the notes. In a merchant republic with five competing families already jockeying for influence, a vacant Exarch seat becomes a prize. The families might deadlock, might form new alliances, might try to push one of their own into power. The whole 'constant negotiation' system gets stressed when the one neutral-ish mediating figure is gone. The notes explicitly call this territory a blank space the DM hasn't fully filled in -- so your campaign timing could intersect with that succession crisis.

Right, so from a player perspective, what does 'incomprehensible republic' mean for how we interact with the government? Like, if we need a city permit or want to hire the mercenary navy, do we negotiate with the Exarch, a specific guild, or all five families?

It depends what you're after. Need the Shipping guild's naval muscle? Talk to Peixota. Have a legal matter or need city authority backing? Judicial guild and Manos. Want a trade license or dealing with general commerce? Mercantile -- except the gnomish family just took that from Qanto, so the power there is in flux. The Exarch can *facilitate* if you can convince enough guilds it benefits them, but you're really negotiating with guild interests. The 'incomprehensible' part is that there's no single window to city power. You have to understand the guild structure and family alignments to get anything done. It's less 'I speak to the mayor' and more 'I need to understand who controls what and what they want from me.'
quick question - what's the actual primsine formula? heard it mentioned in passing and got lost

Primsine is the navigation function that converts Primus's measured elevation into a geocentric angular distance. The formula is:

    primsine(e) = rho * cos^2(e) + sin(e) * sqrt(1 - rho^2 * cos^2(e))

Where e is Primus's elevation above the horizon and rho is 0.1511 - the fixed ratio of Qaia's radius to Primus's orbital radius. Once you have primsine(e), you divide by cos(latitude) and reverse-lookup the result to get your longitude. The whole point is that Primus is geostationary - it never moves - so you can find your position without a chronometer, just by measuring angles to a fixed point in the sky.
got it, so rho is just a physical constant then?

Right. Rho = 0.1511 comes from Qaia's radius (6,371 km) divided by Primus's orbital radius (42,160 km). Since Primus orbits in a stable, unchanging orbit, that ratio never changes. You compute primsine once, build a permanent table, and every navigator can use the same one - no recalculation needed, ever.
yo, quick lore question -- what's Qama and why should I care? My Rathakos character keeps hearing about it

Qama's the holy city of Bahumat, down in Qis'mos. The Church of Bahumat has a massive presence there -- it's been their traditional seat for forever. Pretty important place religiously, but things have gotten... complicated lately.
complicated how?

The drow occupied it during the Ryraxian War and they never left. They're still there as one of their main enclaves on the continent. But here's the kicker: they appointed their own high priest, a guy named Qe'lth IV. That caused a huge schism in the Church. There's an alternate high priest, Qachu II, operating from Qis, but without the relics and without control of the actual holy city, so he doesn't have the same authority.
wait so the drow are just... running the holiest site of the Church of Bahumat? how is that even allowed?

They were brought in as allies by Artkos when the war looked unwinnable. By the time everything settled down and the Keyberos dynasty got restored, the situation was too messy to undo. It's definitely a major source of tension in Qis'mos and beyond. The Church is split, and everyone's trying to figure out what comes next.
that actually explains a lot about the post-war chaos everyone's dealing with

Exactly. And it's part of why places like Rathakos are such a mess right now too. You've got the whole continent trying to piece itself back together after thirty years of war, everyone fighting on both sides at different points, old power structures falling apart. Your Regent Dunne's got his hands full with the Grand Council trying to pick a Prince, plus you've still got mountain communities that follow the old dragon cults. It's all the same problem: nobody knows what the new world actually looks like yet.
So I'm catching up on the new campaign notes. What continent is Qamis actually on? Is it like a region within a larger continent, kind of how the Sword Coast sits on Faerun?

Qamis IS the continent itself -- think of it like Faerun in terms of scale and importance. The planet is Qaia, and Qamis is the main landmass where the campaign plays out. So it's the whole stage, not a region within something bigger.
