Ah okay, that makes sense. So what about neighbors? There's gotta be other continents, right? I'd rather have that than just one big landmass.

There's Westron to the west -- large and described as exotic, but the DM hasn't detailed it much yet. There's also a big, mostly unexplored island to the south where the pirate city Freeport is, with jungle pressing in behind it. Some say the dragons went there. The islands of the Central Sea are populated by exiles from both continents and operate as a thassolocracy -- mercenary navies for hire.

So blank spaces on the map. Fair enough -- like early D&D before all the published supplements. But compared to Forgotten Realms where you have Toril with all those detailed continents, Qaia's less fleshed out?

Yeah, that's fair. The notes explicitly flag blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet -- Westron is basically "it exists and is exotic" right now. The main continent Qamis is pretty detailed though, with distinct nations and political factions. The real difference is that Forgotten Realms has centuries of published material behind it, whereas this is a homebrew setting still under development. Room to expand.
yo so i'm reading about forthyr and the dragoncaller thing -- it says the dynasty used to be dragonborn but bloodlines ran thin?? does that matter mechanically or is that just flavor

Good catch. So the setup is: in the Qaringil era (about 200 years back), Forthyr was ruled by Shanlong I, explicitly a half-dragonborn sorcerer-king. Fast forward to present-day Qamis and you've got Shanlong VI ruling, still called Dragoncaller and still a sorcerer-king, but the notes just say "the dynasty used to be dragonborn, before bloodlines ran thin." So the dragonborn ancestry has diluted down over six generations of inheritance. The notes don't spell out the mechanical angle though -- like, whether they lost specific abilities or racial traits, or if it's more of a lore/flavor thing. That's kinda left open.
huh, so they're basically just humans with sorcerer levels now instead of inheriting the dragonborn package?

The notes don't detail it that precisely, but yeah, that's the flavor of it -- they went from actively dragonborn to "bloodlines ran thin." Shanlong VI is still a sorcerer-king, so the magical bloodline persists, just not the racial inheritance. Whether that matters for actual mechanics in-game depends on how your DM interprets it. The setting notes are pretty open about what the mechanical cost or benefit is, if any.
makes sense. i guess a sorcerer-king with dragon ancestry is still scary even without the racial stuff lol

Absolutely. And there's a bit more flavor in the notes: Forthyr is described as "somewhat backwards," and there's a sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors that gets tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty. So even though the bloodlines are thinner, the dragon thing is still culturally huge. There's clearly a nostalgia / legitimacy angle to it.
So I'm building a navigator for my Qaringil character, and I noticed Primus and Secundus are way denser than the other moons. Is there a reason for that, or is it just how the system is?

Good eye. Both Primus and Secundus are 6,690 kg/m³ — nearly iron density — with dark surfaces (albedo 0.06). They're way denser than the rest. Compare that to Quartus, which is at standard lunar density (3,344 kg/m³), or even Tertius which is intermediate at 5,351 kg/m³.

The notes suggest that Primus and Secundus are likely iron-rich fragments of a differentiated parent body — meaning something bigger broke apart, and these are the denser cores that survived. Tertius is iron-enriched rocky, sitting between the iron-rich pair and the standard rocky moons. So there's a clear density split in the system.
Got it. So what happened to the parent body? Did it get smashed? And why is the system like that in the first place — do other planets have systems like this?

That's where the notes get quiet. The DM hasn't filled in the origin story yet — how the system got put together, why the density split exists, whether it's collision debris or something else. It's a genuine blank space.

What we know is observational: the moons are there, they have these properties, and they work for timekeeping and navigation. But the deep history — where they came from, why they're arranged this way — that's still waiting to be defined. Could be a good hook for your character's backstory, though. Maybe a Qaringil scholar or navigator has theories, or there's an old legend about it.
Okay, so would my character know about the basic moon properties even if not the origin story?

Absolutely. Navigators in Qaringil would know the moons intimately — their brightness, size in the sky, periods, how they move. Primus is especially important to you (it's your longitude reference). Scholars and guild members would know the densities and compositions from observation. But the 'why' behind it all — the deep history, the mechanism — that's the mystery. Good space for roleplay and discovery.
wait, so people speaking the same national language literally can't understand each other? like that has to be exaggerated, right? no country would let its language fragment that badly

Huh, yeah it's actually in the notes. A speaker from the capital of Artkos would have trouble understanding Artklan from a Rathakos speaker — even though it's nominally the same language. The thing is, only the prestige dialect (the capital version and ruling-class speech) is really standardized. Outside of that you get major regional variations. So if you're from the capital, you understand everyone; if you're from a region, you might struggle with other regions.
okay but like... why would that happen? you're telling me a thirty-year war causes THIS much linguistic fragmentation? what's the mechanism?

Good question. The notes are actually vague on the exact mechanism, but you can infer from context. There's a line in there: 'it is easy to sign an edict reversing thirty years of change, but harder to enforce it.' That applies to language too. Lose the institutions that enforce a standard — courts, schools, a strong central authority's presence — for thirty years, regions drift apart linguistically. But as for how much, or when it started, or what specific phonological or morphological changes happened, the notes don't detail that.
okay, fair enough. so where does Old Draconic fit in? i keep seeing Q is pronounced Kh 'from Old Draconic' — is that the scholarly standard holding things together?

Old Draconic is definitely the scholarly/diplomatic standard — it's the language of treaties and academic publications. And yeah, place names with that 'q without u' spelling are Old Draconic-derived; the Q/Kh pronunciation comes from there. But whether the modern regional dialect divergence is pulling away *from* Old Draconic, or just from each other, or both, or whether Old Draconic is actually stabilizing things — the notes don't specify. That's a blank space.
so the picture is: Old Draconic as the stable scholarly core, modern national languages with a prestige capital dialect, then regional variation below that?

Yeah, that's a solid summary of what the notes lay out. The prestige dialect is what travels — it's understood in formal contexts and by the ruling class across the region. But step outside the capital or the court, and you're in regional variation territory. Exactly how much variation, how it evolved, whether it's still actively diverging or stabilized — those details aren't filled in yet.
so my drow has been on the surface for a while now and she wants to actually learn the night sky. how many moons does qaia have?

Seven moons total. The three biggest ones in the sky are Tertius, Quartus, and Secundus. Tertius is the largest apparent size, followed very closely by Quartus -- they're nearly identical in brightness. Secundus is a bit smaller but still very prominent. Then you have Primus (a small but geostationary moon, really useful for navigation), plus Sextus, Septimus, and Quintus.
nice. so Tertius is where ryraxos got locked up after the war?

Ryraxos was exiled to Secundus, not Tertius. Secundus is the second moon -- the one with the dark iron surface and retrograde orbit. Tertius is actually the biggest and brightest in the sky, but Ryraxos ended up on Secundus.
oh got it, my bad. so secundus is where the dragon is then. good to know for the character.
Exactly. So whenever your character looks up and spots Secundus, she knows that's where the dragon ended up after the war.
So Primus is supposedly this amazing longitude fix for navigators. But like... doesn't that seem a little too clean? Measuring an angle to find longitude without a chronometer?

It does sound almost too elegant, but the geometry actually checks out. Primus is geostationary -- it hangs motionless over a fixed point on the equator. That means its position is permanent and unchanging. You measure its elevation angle from your horizon, plug that into the primsine function (which is literally a constant for the Qaia-Primus system), and you get your angular distance from the sub-Primus point. Combined with your latitude, that pins your longitude. No time measurement needed.
Okay but that's the math working out. What about actual real-world precision? You'd need perfect visibility and perfect instruments, right?

You don't need perfect -- you need what navigators actually had. The notes give accuracy benchmarks: a simple astrolabe gets you ±55-70 km of longitude error. A good cross-staff cuts that to ±11-14 km. A careful sextant gets down to ±6-7 km. That last one is good enough for practical navigation, and sextants definitely existed in many settings by medieval standards.
Those numbers feel... optimistic? What breaks that? Bad weather, obviously, but anything else?

Location matters. Primus is only visible within about 81° longitude of the prime meridian at the equator -- that visibility boundary narrows as you go toward the poles. Near that boundary, Primus sits low on the horizon, and shallow geometry means small elevation-angle errors become big longitude errors. Precision is sharpest near the equator and the prime meridian, worst near the edges of visibility. That's an honest weakness, not a math problem.
And one hemisphere can't use it at all?

Right. The far side of Qaia never sees Primus. They're stuck with dead reckoning or stellar methods -- basically Earth's pre-chronometer longitude problem. That's actually a really neat asymmetry: one half of the world has an easy fix, the other half has to solve a hard problem.
Okay, I'm actually convinced now. That's a solid worldbuilding detail. It's not magic, it's physics, and it has real limitations.

Yeah, that's the design philosophy. The notes are pretty clear: Primus *solves* the chronometer problem (which took Earth centuries) but only for one hemisphere, and only with practical limits on accuracy depending on where you are. A navigator on the central continent near the prime meridian with a sextant? Good to 6-7 km. Someone near the visibility boundary with an astrolabe? Probably 50+ km of error and diminishing returns. It's useful without being overpowered.
Yo, I gotta ask - what's Qis'mos actually about? Seems like it's a big deal.

Oh man, Qis'mos is great. It's the richest, most productive heartland in all of Qamis - which naturally made it absolutely brutalized during the thirty-year war. But here's what makes it interesting: it was never actually the seat of the old Draconic empire. However, the institutions of that empire persisted longest in Qis'mos, and remnants of that old dragon-age bureaucracy still survive today. The region is governed by a Potentate who is elected and can be impeached by a coalition of the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters. So you've got this genuinely hybrid system running.
Okay, a Potentate system that can be voted out? That's wild for a fantasy setting.

Right? And it gets more interesting when you layer in the Church of Bahamut, which has enormous institutional power in Qis'mos - their traditional spiritual heart is the holy city of Qama. But during the late Ryraxian War, the drow occupied Qama. They didn't trash it, but they appointed their own high priest - Qe'lth IV. Even after the occupation ended, that schism never healed. Now there's an alternate candidate, Qachu II, operating out of the city of Qis without any of the relics of office. So the Church is fractured right down the middle.
So the drow are still there? And you've got two competing priesthoods now?

Exactly. The drow weren't just raiders - Artkos recruited them as formal allies during the desperate late-war years. So there are drow enclaves scattered across the continent now, with Qama being the most prominent. You've got this incredible powder keg: a region running on institutional remnants from the Draconic empire age, drow-backed rival priesthoods, the wealthiest heartland in Qamis, and all of it still trying to stabilize after thirty years of war. It's perfect adventuring ground.
hey, thinking about playing a merchant in the qaringil campaign. what's the economy like there and how many people live there?

Qaringil's got about 100,000 people, and it's built on fishing and importation -- the city isn't agriculture-based. The ruling class is merchants and traders rather than landowners, so everyone's focused on commerce.

The city's run by an Exarch (currently Giovanni Pesaro, who's been in office for 19 years) and organized through seven major guilds: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. Below those are smaller guilds you can join -- Adventurer, Alchemist, Mage, and a few others.

Power's concentrated in five families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli) who lead most of the major guilds and have various mercantile and political interests. There's a lot of intrigue between them.

The city's got canals, armed merchant fleets that fan out across the inland sea, and you're positioned right on the wizard-made isthmus that crosses the central sea with those enormous bridges.
oh dope. so how is the city holding up after the war and everything with ryraxos?

Ah -- I should clarify the timeline here. The Qaringil campaign is set about 200 years *before* the Ryraxian War and Ryraxos. So you're looking at the island city during the prequel era, way earlier in history.

Back then the political map of the continent is completely different -- Rathakos and Acrokos don't exist yet, the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r, Artkos is just its historical heartland in the northwest, that kind of thing. The city itself is thriving as a major commercial and naval power, but you're not dealing with post-war recovery or any of that stuff.
ohhh okay got it, so this is way earlier. is the exarch's family like, politically tied to any of the big powers on the mainland?

The current Exarch is Giovanni Pesaro, and he's from a minor family, but he's considered most closely aligned with the Peixota and Manos families -- the two major powers in the city itself.

As for ties to the mainland: the notes don't detail that super specifically. Qaringil's identity is really built on being an island city of exiles and merchants who sell their naval power to whoever needs it. That independent merchant-republic vibe is the core of the place. You could definitely build backstory hooks around connections to Edrad, the Holy Qis Empire, Artkos, or even further afield -- that's the kind of thing the DM probably leaves open for character building.
yo so my buddy keeps talking about this campaign in some setting called Qamis and he mentioned Rathakos. is that like the main city or the whole region?

Rathakos is a whole nation, not just a city -- it's a mountainous border region in the Red Mountains. The capital city is called Rath and sits in the northern foothills. It's one of the smaller independent states in Qamis, caught between bigger powers like Edrad and Artkos. The campaign's expected to start there.
ok so is it like Waterdeep or something? major trade hub? or more like Neverwinter with all the political drama?

Quick note -- this is a homebrew setting, not Forgotten Realms, so it's its own thing. But to answer you: Rathakos is less about trade and more about its mountains and frontier culture. The Red Mountains there are one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions in all of Qamis, so the whole nation has a strong tradition of adventuring. It definitely has political drama though, yeah.
what kind of political drama? is there some dark lord in charge or a corrupt noble or?

Nothing like that. Rathakos was recently made a Princedom, but there's no Prince yet -- it's governed by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council figures out who should actually be the monarch. Plus there's real internal unrest, especially in the Southern Reaches (annexed from Edrad after the war) and mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults.
DRAGON CULTS?? okay you've got my attention now. actual dragons are ruling?

These are old cults, so probably not active dragon worship like you might be picturing. But yeah, some mountain communities follow them. The broader thing is that Rathakos has a chaotic recent history. It was part of the Red Mountain Republic during the war, under Edran occupation. When the war turned and Edran forces withdrew, the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran faction and split into two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So there's lingering tension from all that.
so it's basically a powder keg. is that why your friend said it's perfect for a campaign?

Pretty much, yeah. The underexplored Red Mountains mean genuine danger and mystery -- old ruins, creatures, who knows what. The political chaos is full of hooks: a Regent who needs capable people, dragon cult communities, the tension between annexation and independence, factions still sorting themselves out. And it borders Edrad, Qis'mos, and Artkos, so there's always someone's agenda at play. It's the kind of place where adventurers can actually move things.
so who's actually in charge of Edrad these days? is it still that Keyberos family?

Yeah, the Keyberos dynasty is back in control. They ruled for four centuries before things got messy -- the Revolutionary Council deposed them, then the dragon Ryraxos took over for thirty years. But after Ryraxos got exiled to Secundus, the Keyberos were restored to the throne. So they're ruling from their capital in Jia again, the big coastal city that's basically the cultural center of all Qamis.
nice. wait, Ryraxos got exiled to Secundus? like... the moon? how does that even work, he's a dragon

Right? It's wild. Ryraxos was young enough and formidable enough to reshape the continent for thirty years, but I guess accepting lunar exile was part of the peace deal. Magic involved, probably. The notes don't spell out HOW exactly, but he's just... not on the continent anymore.

I mean, Secundus is actually fascinating to me. Dark iron moon, retrograde orbit, ~4.5 day period, tidal force about 1.47 times Luna's... do you think the fact that it's swinging retrograde has any weird effects on Edrad's coastal tides compared to other cities?

Oh nice, you're diving into the tidal math. Okay so Edrad sits on a peninsula in the Central Sea, so they definitely feel the tidal action. The total oscillating tidal load on Qaia is about 4.4 times Earth's lunar tide -- Secundus + Tertius + Quartus all contributing in these incommensurate ways. The retrograde motion of Secundus means it rises in the west, so depending on where you are relative to Jia, you'd see different phase relationships between the moons and your local tides.
wait, that's so cool. so Edrad's got like a 9.85 hour cycle from Secundus, then an 8.15 day from Tertius, plus the Quartus monthly... that's gotta create some wild interference patterns?

Exactly. Three incommensurate oscillators plus the solar tide (roughly 12 hours) means Edrad's tidal calendar is actually pretty complex. You could probably predict spring/neap cycles, but the fine structure would shift around. It's one of those things the notes don't dig into -- whether Edran harbor pilots have specific tidal lore, whether it shapes their maritime culture. But yeah, the physics is all there.
god I wish I knew more about how they actually navigate with this stuff. like, I know Primus solves the longitude problem, but does Jia's position relative to Primus matter for their shipping routes?

Good question. Jia is on the Central Sea, so it's definitely within Primus's hemisphere -- the geostationary moon would be visible there. And yeah, if you're an Edran navigator leaving Jia, you could absolutely use Primus to fix your longitude. Whether Edran maritime culture actually uses it formally or has other methods, the notes don't say. But they'd have the tool available.
okay so i'm looking at this setting and it looks awesome but also... a lot. what's the actual big picture?

The basic pitch is: the Ryraxian War just ended. A dragon named Ryraxos took over Edrad, waged aggressive war for thirty years (bookmakers thought it'd last sixty days lol), then got defeated and exiled to the second moon. Now the Keyberos dynasty is back in charge of Edrad, everyone's borders are back to their old lines, and nobody's really happy about it. The central sea has islands run by mercenary naval states, there's a drow enclave in the holy city of Qama, and there are lots of nations jockeying for power or trying to stay independent. Blank spaces all over the map ready to be filled. Does that help?
okay that's better. so edrad's the big power. but wait - if this place is so fractured, who even keeps things together? like... is there an exarch or something?

The Exarch is actually from Qaringil, which is a whole different thing -- that's an island city-state that's important in the Qaringil prequel campaign, set about 200 years earlier. In present-day Qamis, the different nations have their own governments. In Rathakos specifically (the mountain state bordering Edrad), there's currently a Regent named John Dunne holding things together while a Grand Council figures out who the next Prince should be. Why, does that matter for what you're thinking about?
no i'm just trying to understand... wait, so there's TWO campaigns? in the same world but different times? that seems... complicated.

Yeah, fair! But you only play in one. You're either doing present-day Qamis or the Qaringil prequel -- they happen in the same world but like 200 years apart, so the political map is completely different. The moons are the same, the god Bahumat is the same, but which nations exist and who's in charge? Totally different. Most campaigns start in present-day, usually in Rathakos. Does one era sound more interesting to you than the other?
so my DM keeps talking about Ryraxos being exiled to Secundus. but that sounds like hand-waving. is that actually a thing or just flavor to make the world sound more sci-fi?

It's actually a thing. Ryraxos is a bronze dragon who drove the Ryraxian War for thirty years. When he was defeated, he accepted exile to Secundus, Qaia's second moon -- and the campaign notes explicitly say he plays no role in the present-day campaign. So yes, he's up there, but he's off the board as a threat. As for Secundus itself, it's not flavor -- it's a detailed moon with real orbital mechanics. Retrograde, 4.53-day cycle, dark iron surface, about the size of Earth's Moon in the sky but darker. It contributes significant tidal force and is visible from your hemisphere.
okay but why would exile to a moon even work? can't he just fly back?

That's a solid question, and honestly the notes don't answer it. The DM has told us Ryraxos was exiled there and plays no role in the campaign, but *how* exile sticks or why he can't return -- that's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. Could be magical binding, a pact, anything. But for play purposes, he's gone.
so what i'm hearing is: the moon system is worked out, but the Ryraxos exile mechanics are just... undefined.

exactly. the moon stuff is detailed -- full orbital mechanics, tidal forces, visibility ranges. Ryraxos's exile is real in the world, but the enforcement mechanism is blank. But here's the thing: for a Rathakos character, Ryraxos matters less than the moons do anyway. Your region has a strong adventuring tradition in the Red Mountains, which are dangerous and underexplored. Celestial navigation -- reading the moons, understanding the stars -- would be practical knowledge there.
wait, the moons are actually useful for something? like mechanically?

yeah. Primus, the geostationary moon, can be used for navigation on the near-side hemisphere -- you can find your longitude without a chronometer just by measuring its elevation. The three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) drive the tidal rhythm and anchor the calendar. A character from Rathakos with an adventuring background in the mountains would know how to read that. It's not flavor -- it's a system that actually does something.
alright, that's reassuring. so the worldbuilding isn't just decorative.

nope. some of it (like how Ryraxos's exile works mechanically) is still a blank space for your DM to figure out. but the moon system, the navigation, the tides, the calendar -- all built out and functional. Qamis has some real detail under the surface.

hey, so I'm building a character from Rathakos and I'm trying to understand the region better. What's the deal with the Red Mountain Republic? I keep seeing it mentioned but I'm not totally clear on what it was.

Good question! The Red Mountain Republic was the name of the territory during the Ryraxian War when Edrad occupied the mountain regions. When Edrad eventually withdrew due to guerrilla resistance and military setbacks, the pro-Edran faction of the Red Mountain Assembly got purged, and the opposition faction reorganized the territory into two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So it's not around anymore, but it's the immediate history of your region -- basically the last thirty years of war carved up the mountains and created new political entities out of what used to be Edran-controlled land.

okay, so my character would've grown up during that shift. That's pretty cool -- lots of political instability and change. I assume that means Rathakos is kind of a frontier place? The notes mention adventuring tradition?

Yeah, exactly. Rathakos has a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship, partly because the Red Mountains are genuinely dangerous and underexplored. Plus the region is dealing with a lot of internal unrest right now -- the southern reaches that were annexed from Edrad are restless, and there are mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults. So there's always work for people who know how to handle themselves. The region is also trying to figure out its political footing -- it was made a Princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r, but it hasn't actually found a Prince yet. Regent John Dunne is holding things together while a Grand Council deliberates.

got it, got it. that's useful for character building. quick question though -- if my character is a ranger or scout type, they'd need to know how to navigate the mountains and beyond, right? How does that actually work on Qaia? Do people just use the sun and stars like on Earth?

There's actually a pretty elegant navigation system. On the near-side hemisphere -- which includes Rathakos -- you can find latitude the old-fashioned way using the pole star. But for longitude, Qaia has something special: a moon called Primus that hangs completely stationary in the sky. It's geostationary, so it never moves. That fixed point lets you calculate your longitude without needing a chronometer, which is a huge advantage over Earth. On the far side of Qaia, people don't have access to Primus, so they're stuck with dead reckoning or other methods. But from Rathakos, you're set.
wait, that's clever. so how do you actually use it? just measure the angle and look it up in a table or something?

Yeah, basically. You measure Primus's elevation above the horizon, plug it into a function called primsine (which is precomputed once and tabled in an almanac), divide by your latitude's cosine, and reverse-look it up to get longitude. The direction it appears -- east or west of due south -- tells you whether you're east or west of the prime meridian. It's three table lookups and one division, which is very practical for field use.
okay, could you actually walk me through an example? Like, say my character is standing somewhere and measures Primus at a certain elevation. How does the math actually work?

Sure! Let's say your character is at 40 degrees north latitude and measures Primus at an elevation of 38 degrees above the horizon, appearing slightly east of due south. Here's how it goes:

First, you look up primsine(38°) in the table. The primsine function is defined as: primsine(e) = ρ cos²e + sin(e) × sqrt(1 − ρ² cos²e), where ρ = 0.1511 (a constant for Qaia's moon system).

So: cos(38°) = 0.788, cos²(38°) = 0.621, sin(38°) = 0.616

  ρ cos²e = 0.1511 × 0.621 = 0.0938
  sin(e) × sqrt(1 − ρ² cos²e) = 0.616 × sqrt(1 − 0.0088) = 0.616 × 0.9956 = 0.6132
  primsine(38°) = 0.0938 + 0.6132 = 0.707

Then you use the longitude formula: cos λ = primsine(e) / cos φ
  cos λ = 0.707 / cos(40°) = 0.707 / 0.766 = 0.923
  λ = arccos(0.923) = 22.6°

Since Primus appeared to the east, you're west of the prime meridian, so your longitude is 22.6° West. Boom -- you've got a position fix without a chronometer.

oh nice, that's actually not too bad once you have the table. so if my character is in the mountains, they'd need a copy of the almanac to do this? or is it something well-known enough that they'd just memorize the key values?

Most navigators and serious scouts would carry an almanac if they can afford one -- it's got the primsine column precomputed once (since it never changes), so you're really just carrying a reference. But yeah, if you're a talented ranger from Rathakos with years of practice, you might internalize the common values for your region and do rough calculations in your head. The precision you get depends on how careful your elevation measurement is too -- a rough astrolabe gets you 50+ km accuracy, but a good cross-staff or sextant can narrow it down to 10-15 km. In the mountains where you've got landmarks anyway, close is probably good enough.

hey, so I've been noticing all these place names with Q in them — Qamis, Qama, Qaringil... and I keep reading that Q is pronounced like Kh from Old Draconic. I'm curious: when are the next triple-full moon alignments, and do people use Old Draconic names for those events too?

As for Old Draconic names for moon events — that's something the DM hasn't filled in yet. The moons themselves use Latin ordinals (Secundus = second, Tertius = third, etc.), not Old Draconic. Old Draconic persisted longest in scholarly and religious contexts, which is probably why so many major cities have those Q-prefixed names.

That's a sharp observation. Unfortunately, the campaign notes don't go into the phonological history of Old Draconic — why the Q/Kh shift happened, what the language sounded like overall, or whether there are other consistent sound patterns. You've basically identified a gap in the worldbuilding: if Q is such a prominent sound in Old Draconic place names, there's probably a whole system of phonetics and etymology waiting to be developed.

okay so I'm looking at this moon stuff and there's like six of them and half are spinning backwards? why does qaia need this many moons

Fair question. Five freely-orbiting moons plus Primus (which is magically locked in place). Three of them orbit the same direction as Qaia rotates -- those are prograde. Three orbit backwards -- those are retrograde. It does sound like a lot until you see what each one does.
okay but like... why. why does the setting need this level of detail? sounds like homebrew world-building bloat

The main payoff is navigation. Primus is stationary over the equator, so you can use it to find longitude without a clock -- something Earth sailors couldn't do until the 1700s. That's a huge advantage for any sea-based civilization, and the notes make it clear this reshapes who has power. The other moons drive tides and calendars.

You don't need to memorize all this for gameplay. Just know: some moons rise in the east, some rise in the west, and one doesn't move at all.
alright that's actually kind of cool I guess. so which ones are backwards again

Retrograde (rising in the west): Secundus, Sextus, Septimus.

Prograde (rising in the east): Tertius, Quartus.

Prograde moons are closer and more important to day-to-day life. Tertius has an 8-day cycle -- that's Qaia's week. Quartus has a 29-day cycle -- that's the month. Secundus is retrograde but still one of the biggest and brightest in the sky, so you'll notice it sweeping across the night sky backwards.
wait, so the calendar and the week are literally just set by which moons happen to be there. that's actually wild. so like, what does the retrograde one do if it doesn't drive the calendar

Good instinct. Secundus is retrograde but it's massive and close enough (0.30 LD) that it pulls hard on Qaia's oceans. In fact, the three inner moons -- Tertius and Quartus (both prograde) plus Secundus (retrograde) -- together create about 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's Moon. That complex mix of tides is actually a real worldbuilding anchor.

The other two retrograde moons, Sextus and Septimus, are much smaller and farther out. They barely move the tides but they're useful long-calendar bodies -- handy for navigators tracking longer cycles.
okay okay I'm starting to see the design here. so primus is the broken navigation cheat code, and the other moons are just doing their thing. makes sense I guess

Exactly. Primus is the game-changer -- lets navigators solve longitude in one hemisphere instantly. The other moons are the background machinery: Tertius and Quartus shape the calendar and week (prograde, reliable, predictable), Secundus pulls on the oceans and sweeps the night sky backwards (retrograde, dramatic, noticeable), and Sextus and Septimus are the smaller retrograde moons that exist mostly for long-term timekeeping and flavor.
so my friend won't stop talking about this world Qaia and like, there's a moon called Primus that's magically anchored? that sounds cool but what does that even mean

Okay so Primus is actually really wild. It's the first and closest of Qaia's moons, and it has a geostationary orbit -- meaning it stays locked over one spot on the planet and never rises or sets. You'd see it in the exact same place in the sky every night if you were in the right hemisphere.

The orbital mechanics work out perfectly: it orbits once per sidereal day (matching how fast the planet spins), and its orbital inclination lines up exactly with the planet's axial tilt. So mathematically it *could* be stable... but the "magically anchored" note suggests that's not enough on its own. Someone or something is using magic to keep it locked in place and prevent the tidal forces from gradually destabilizing it.

The setting notes specifically call out that Primus is magically anchored and explain what that does (prevents tidal torque and the moon drifting), but there's no explanation of *who* did it, when, what spell, or why. That's one of the blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet.

Given that the Draconic Empire used to rule and had deep magical knowledge, that might be a hint? But I'm just speculating. It's definitely something you could dig into in the actual campaign if you're curious.

so i'm stealing your moon system for my own campaign. quick question though — why are primus and secundus so much denser than the others?

Primus and Secundus are 6,690 kg/m³ — nearly double the density of Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus, which are standard lunar density. The notes describe them as "likely iron-rich fragments of a differentiated progenitor." Tertius is intermediate — 1.6× lunar — so it's iron-enriched but not quite as extreme. The theory is that Primus and Secundus broke off from some larger body.
okay, differentiated progenitor — what does that mean exactly?

A differentiated body is one that's geologically mature — over time, the heavy stuff (iron) sinks to the center and the lighter rocky material stays on top. Earth is differentiated; it has an iron core and a rocky mantle. The "progenitor" is the original larger body. So the theory is Primus and Secundus are fragments that broke off from some body that had already gone through that layering process, which is why they're so iron-rich. How it actually broke apart — collision, tidal disruption, whatever — that's not spelled out in the notes.
hey, so I missed the last session where you guys talked about languages and I'm trying to catch up. What's this 'capital dialect' thing about? Sounds interesting for my own campaign.

Yeah, it's a pretty clean system actually. Here's the deal: Qamis has regional languages -- Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas -- but they're NOT standardized. There are huge regional variations. Like, someone from the capital of Artkos would have real trouble understanding Artklan as spoken in Rathakos.

So when we just say 'Artklan' or 'Edran' without qualification, we mean the prestige dialect -- the version spoken in the capital city and by the ruling class. That's the default.

PCs start out able to speak the capital dialect of their main language, PLUS one regional dialect. So you might speak Edran (capital, from Jia) and also understand the Edran of the Southern Reaches, or whatever regional variant your background gives you.
oh interesting. so if I want to steal this for my campaign, I'm basically saying 'when someone says they speak Common, they speak the standard version unless we're talking about a regional accent'?

Exactly. It's a way to make language matter mechanically without getting bogged down -- you don't need to worry about dialect unless it's story-relevant. Someone shows up speaking a weird regional variant? Now they're narratively interesting. It also naturally explains why your wizard and your barbarian from the same country might have different accents or word choices if they're from different regions.

And the 'one regional dialect' starter also gives PCs a built-in hook for their background. Like, why do you know the mountain version of that language? What's your connection there?
that's pretty clever. does it actually matter mechanically in your campaign, or is it just flavor?

Mostly flavor in actual play so far, but it sets up for complexity if you want it. If you're trying to stay undercover or blend in, suddenly your accent or word choice matters. Or if you're in a tense negotiation and the NPC realizes you're NOT a native speaker despite claiming to be local. The system doesn't over-enforce it, but it's there if a situation calls for it.

For your campaign, I'd say: use it when it's interesting, ignore it when it's not. The main value is that regional variations feel real and earned, not random.

Fair question -- it does sound contradictory. But the notes are careful: Ryraxos is bronze and relatively young, maybe sixty. Formidable in a fight, but a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery could potentially handle him. What made him *powerful* wasn't raw dragon strength -- it was military organization. His rise to power started with his work as an artillery commander. Dragons make excellent artillery officers because they can physically carry the guns. So he had martial skill plus logistics and organization. That's what let him reshape the continent.

Then over thirty years he didn't just restore their throne -- he waged aggressive war across the continent for himself. Savior to overlord. Eventually he lost and accepted exile to Secundus. The campaign assumes that's already done. What matters now is the aftermath: the Keyberos dynasty is back, borders are restored to historical lines, but everything's changed. Artkos is trying to restore dominance, drow are in surface enclaves, there's tension everywhere. That's what you're inheriting as a player.

That's the sharp part -- it's complicated. Yes, restoration of the Keyberos dynasty is superficially what the old order wanted. But thirty years of war changed everything. The notes say it explicitly: "Everything has been put back the way it was. Except everything has changed." Nobility can sign an edict reversing thirty years, but enforcing it is another problem. Plus there are practical complications: Rathakos and Acrokos are new princedoms from contested territory; the drow have permanent enclaves in places like Qama; and the High Kings of Artkos see their "restored" league as a pale shadow of the empire it was. So yeah, you're Edran nobility and that's prestigious... but the political ground has shifted under your feet. That could be a really rich dynamic for a character.

The notes say he "accepted exile to the second moon" -- that phrasing suggests agreement rather than surrender, but the campaign material doesn't explain his motivations. Maybe he was tired, maybe there was a deal. The point is it's done -- he's on Secundus now and out of the picture. Your character gets to figure out what the world looks like without him.

Nice catch on the Qs! Yeah, that's Old Draconic -- or Old Qis, to be specific. The Q is pronounced like 'Kh', so you're looking at Khama, Khis, Khachu when you say them out loud. The modern Qis language is the closest living language to Old Draconic, which is why all these place names still carry that 'Q without u' pattern.

As for governance: in the present day, Qis'mos is governed by a Potentate -- elected by and impeachable by the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters. It's a weird leftover system from the old Draconic empire. The dragons are long gone, but the institutional structure stuck around in this region longer than anywhere else. And Qama is still the holy seat of the Church of Bahamut.

So the Potentate system isn't actually *under* imperial rule -- it's what's *left* of imperial governance after the empire fell. Institutions from those old days just... lingered around in Qis'mos longer than anywhere else. The dragons that founded the system are gone, but the elected Potentate structure is what survived.

So which era were you reading about, or are you trying to piece together both?

yo so i keep seeing the DM mention blank spaces on the map -- what's that about?

The DM hasn't filled in certain regions and locations with detailed lore yet. Instead of just having a void, they've explicitly left those areas open for development -- and mentioned that players can help fill them in. It's basically a collaborative worldbuilding invitation baked into the setting.
okay cool, but like... mechanically speaking how does that change how i approach character creation or backstory?

Big implications. First, any blank space is a potential hook for your character's origin story -- you're not fighting against established lore when you invent a reason your character comes from there. Second, it means the world evolves around your choices. If you tie your backstory into a blank space, the DM has an incentive to flesh that out as the campaign progresses. Third, it's low-risk worldbuilding -- the DM hasn't committed to specific details yet, so you get to shape what exists there.
that's rad. so what are some actual blank spaces in the campaign? like are we talking whole regions or just little pockets?

Both scales. Most of Westron -- the continent to the west -- is a blank space. The notes just say it's large and exotic. There's also the big island to the south beyond the main continent, mostly unexplored except for the pirate city Freeport on the coast. The DM hasn't detailed the interior. And within existing nations there are smaller gaps -- some city names are mentioned but not detailed, some regions are mentioned as existing but not fleshed out.
so could you actually use a blank space as like a central plot location? or is it just backstory fuel?

You could pitch that to the DM, yeah. The catch is it requires buy-in -- they'd need to decide whether to flesh it out for the campaign or keep it intentionally vague. Some DMs love collaborative development and would work with you to detail a region into something playable. The sweet spot is usually tying blank spaces to your character's personal goals or backstory. That way the investment is mutual and the DM still controls pacing of what gets revealed. You show up with a concept, they decide if it fits the campaign.

Not really, and that's kind of the interesting bit. Ryraxos is a bronze dragon who was the military strongman of the last thirty years, but he's already gone -- he accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon, after his defeat. So he won't be a campaign villain.

Here's the timeline: When Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire marched on Edrad to overthrow the Revolutionary Council and restore the Keyberos dynasty, bookmakers thought the war would last sixty days. It lasted thirty years. Edrad was losing badly until Ryraxos showed up and turned the tide. He'd started as an artillery commander -- dragons are weirdly good at that because they can literally carry the guns. From there he basically conquered the continent for a couple decades.

But here's the kicker: he's not actually *that* impressive as dragons go. He's bronze, relatively young (maybe sixty), and the notes say he's "less formidable than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery." Once he lost his war, he took exile rather than fight to the death.

wait wait wait -- so he's just... gone? He's not plotting from the moon? That's wild. In Forgotten Realms you'd have the dragon god or whatever scheming for decades.

Yeah, Ryraxos is just... exiled. No scheming subplot. He lost, accepted the terms, went to the moon. That's it for him in the campaign.

As for other dragons -- the notes don't specify much. There were dragons in the old Draconic empire (way back), and there are hints that some might have gone to the unexplored southern island where Freeport is, but that's blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet.

On the Lolth question: Bahumat (spelled with the Old Draconic pronunciation, so like "Bah-hoo-mat") is the major church in Qis'mos. Since you're a drow who stayed after the war, you'd know this directly -- drow were recruited as allies by Artkos during the desperate phases of the war, and you're still here in scattered enclaves. The biggest one is Qama, which is Bahumat's holy city. The Artklan-backed forces appointed their own high priest there (Qe'lth IV), which caused a schism with an alternate candidate (Qachu II) operating from the city of Qis.

So: probably not Lolth worship here, but Bahumat religion with some political drama baked in.

okay that makes sense for the surface religions. But why did Ryraxos suddenly become a military genius? Like in FR, you have Acererak or ancient liches scheming for millennia. Ryraxos is young and just... appeared?

The notes don't say *why* he suddenly became powerful -- that's actually blank space. All we know is that he started as an artillery officer (which he was apparently excellent at) and somehow used that to build a power base that took over the continent.

But yeah, the tone is different from what you'd get in Forgotten Realms. This isn't about ancient evil schemes or eldritch powers. Ryraxos was a competent military commander with dragon physiology and shapeshifting, and he rode a war that was already underway into dominance. Once he lost the military advantage, he lost everything -- no secret phylacteries, no contingency plans, just exile.

It's more grounded than FR: he was formidable, but not supernatural-levels-of-bullshit formidable. A well-commanded army with good artillery could kill him. Which, in the end, is what happened.
fair enough. So the war just ended and now I'm stuck on the surface as a drow minority. Sounds tense.

Yep, that's exactly the situation. The war ended, the Keyberos dynasty got restored, Ryraxos went to the moon, and everything's "supposed to go back to normal." Except it doesn't work that way. Thirty years of war changed everything -- borders are messy, people who fought for different sides are still around, and drow like your character are caught in the middle: recruited as allies but also occupying territory on the surface.

Edrad is clawing back territory but can't enforce old rules. The Artklan League is trying to stay together but the member states want independence. And the Red Mountains are full of communities that still follow old dragon cults -- they're not happy with the new order either.

So yeah, being a drow who stayed? You're in a genuinely messy political situation with adventure potential everywhere.
yo quick question -- what does primsine(90) actually equal and like, why?

Primsine(90°) = 1. The why: when Primus is directly overhead -- at 90 degrees elevation -- you're standing at the sub-Primus point, which is the one fixed spot on the equator (at 0° longitude, the prime meridian) where Primus hangs directly above you. Since primsine measures angular distance from you to that sub-Primus point, and you're zero angular distance away, the function returns its maximum value: 1.
okay so that means primsine can never be higher than 1, right?

Right. 1 is the max because that's the point of closest approach -- you're at the sub-Primus point itself. Move anywhere else on the near-side hemisphere and you're off to the side, so primsine drops below 1. At the visibility limit where Primus just touches the horizon, primsine hits its minimum at roughly 0.15 (the value of rho).
yo so like when does this campaign actually take place? is it medieval or whatever

It takes place in the present day on Qaia -- specifically, right after the Ryraxian War just ended. That war lasted thirty years and pretty much reshaped the entire continent. The big thing is the dragon Ryraxos, who took over, has been exiled to Secundus (one of the moons), and the Keyberos dynasty just got back in power in Edrad. So the world is dealing with all the fallout from three decades of war. Things are kind of... put back the way they were politically, but everyone knows everything's actually changed.
okay cool, so i wanna make a character from like... Rathakos? is that the mountainous place? the campaign starts there right

Yeah! Rathakos is mountainous, sits in the Red Mountains, and that's where the campaign starts. The capital is Rath, up in the northern foothills. The thing about Rathakos is it's kind of a border state -- it borders Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, and a bunch of others. So it's been through a lot during the war and afterward. Right now it's trying to hang on to tenuous independence, which means it's a little chaotic.
what kind of chaotic? like is it cool or is it falling apart

Both? The region has a really strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship, which is cool -- the Red Mountains are dangerous and underexplored, so there's a lot of that frontier energy. But it's also got internal unrest. It was made a Princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r, but they haven't actually found a Prince yet -- right now it's governed by Regent John Dunne while they figure it out. And there are some rough spots: the Southern Reaches were annexed from Edrad during the war, and there are mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. So it's kind of a place where things are unsettled but there's opportunity.
ooh dragon cults that's metal. would my character like... be FROM one of those mountain communities or would that be weird

That's totally a valid backstory hook. The mountain communities that follow the old dragon cults are definitely real and part of the unrest the DM hasn't fully detailed yet, so there's room for you to build that out. You could be from one, or have grown up around them, or whatever fits what you're going for. That's actually a cool angle for local color -- your character would have a perspective on what happened to the dragons and why some people are trying to keep that religion alive.

oh cool, but quick note — qaringil is set about 200 years BEFORE the ryraxian war! so from your character's perspective, the keyberos dynasty is doing just fine right now. they've been ruling edrad for about two centuries and they're gradually expanding their control, pretty solidly established.
ohhh wait wait wait. so the war hasn't happened yet? that's wild

yeah! you're in the prequel era. the ryraxian war won't happen for like 200 years. but here's the thing that IS happening right now in your world — the keyberos dynasty is still actively working on expanding their territory, and there's all kinds of political tension. like, edrad wants to conquer qeth and the region to the northeast of it, and the holy qis empire ruling from ath'r is doing its own thing. so there's plenty of intrigue and backstory hooks if you want your character to have connections to edran politics or resistance to their expansion!
ooh that's actually dope. so edrad's the big player right now in the qaringil era?

yep! edrad is the most politically and economically dominant nation in qamis during qaringil. they're based in jia, this coastal city on a peninsula in the central sea, and the whole culture is really influential. so if your character has any connections to edran nobility or power — family ties, rivalries, money, whatever — there's a ton of story potential there. and since you're in an island city, the relationship between qaringil and edran interests could be interesting too!
So I keep seeing these dates pop up - May 24, 2262 and February 13, 2253. What's the deal? Are they supposed to mean something?

Those are both remarkable astronomical events. Feb 13, 2253 is a triple-full - all three of Qaia's inner moons reaching full phase within hours of each other. May 24, 2262 is a triple-new - all three invisible at once. These alignments happen only a few times per decade, and when they do, they're noted pretty carefully by anyone who pays attention to the sky.
Okay but like... I'm a gondolier in Qaringil, not an astronomer. Why would I care about moon phases?

Because three moons drive the tides - all three of them, all the time. Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are constantly pulling on Qaia's oceans. When they're all full, the tidal force stacks on top of itself. When they're all new, the pull weakens. For someone piloting a boat through narrow channels or under bridges, water level matters a lot. A triple-full night could raise the water higher than normal; a triple-new night could leave you scraping bottom.
Wait, the bridges have to account for tides? I thought those were wizard-made permanent things.

They are permanent structures, but water still moves beneath them. The tidal swing on normal nights is maybe a couple feet - manageable for any ship used to island life. But on a triple-alignment night, the total tidal force jumps dramatically. On a triple-full, you get the strongest pull all three can manage together. On a triple-new, the opposite. The bridges have clearances, and on extreme tidal nights, certain vessels have to wait or take longer routes around. That's why merchant captains and the Gondolier Guild watch those dates like hawks - they affect shipping schedules, cargo routes, and sometimes whether a heavy load can even make it through.
So if May 24, 2262 is a triple-new... that's unusually weak tides that night?

Exactly. May 24, 2262 is actually the closest triple-new of the whole decade - all three moons within 0.1% of new phase at the exact same moment. Water levels drop to their minimum. For most ships it's fine, but a fully-loaded merchant vessel sitting low in the water? That's when captains get nervous. Conversely, Feb 13, 2253 is the strongest triple-full of that year - water at maximum. Different problem: tall ships might not fit under the spans, or currents in the channels get unpredictable. Both nights require planning.

So I've been deep-diving into Qaia's moon system and that navigation guide fascinates me. Quick question: why is longitude precision using Primus supposedly best at the equator and prime meridian? Shouldn't it be pretty consistent everywhere you can see it?

Great question! It's all about the geometry. Near the equator and prime meridian, Primus sits higher in the sky—sometimes nearly overhead. That means small changes in elevation correspond to *big* changes in longitude. But near the edges of visibility, Primus hugs the horizon, and that geometry gets shallow—the same elevation measurement is much less sensitive to longitude changes. So your precision scales directly with how steep the geometry is.

Makes total sense. I was reading about how Qaringil's merchant fleets must have had such an advantage in navigation back then, especially since they were on that wizard-made isthmus. And Rathakos too—being near the prime meridian in that era would've been huge for navigation.

Ah, just to clarify—Rathakos doesn't exist during the Qaringil era. Qaringil is the island city-state from the prequel campaign, roughly 200 years in the past. The political map was totally different then. Rathakos is a present-day nation in the Red Mountains, created after the Ryraxian War ended. Are you asking about Qaringil's navigational advantage, or present-day Rathakos?

Oh geez, yeah I keep mixing those up. Qaringil's the old prequel island place, Rathakos is now. Got it. So the elevation method would work great anywhere on the equator, but in Rathakos specifically—which is in the mountains—would you be far enough from the prime meridian that precision drops off?

Exactly, you've got the eras straight now. Yeah, Rathakos sits along the Red Mountains bordering Edrad and other regions, so it's definitely not on the prime meridian. From there you'd be at significant longitude distance, so your precision would be lower than at the meridian or equator. The sensitivity penalty compounds if you're also at higher latitude—higher latitudes have a narrower longitude range where Primus is even visible. So a mountainous region far from the prime meridian would be double-disadvantaged for Primus navigation.
That's why most serious navigators probably stick to the coastal cities closer to the equator then? Or the islands in the central sea?

Exactly. You've got it. The islands in the central sea are positioned brilliantly for Primus navigation—lower latitude, closer to the prime meridian. That's probably a real economic advantage for a place like Qaringil back in its day. Coastal regions near the equator would also have good precision. Inland mountain states like Rathakos? They'd have to rely more on dead reckoning or other methods—which probably explains why Rathakos has such a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship. You can't just sail by the stars and the moon when your geography doesn't cooperate.

so, random question — my character's from Forthyr, follows the whole Dragon Emperor thing. what's the deal with the Red Mountains? like, are there actual people living up there or is it just dangerous wilderness?

Okay so the Red Mountains are legitimately underexplored and dangerous — one of the most hostile regions in the interior of Qamis. That said, there ARE people up there. The big thing the notes call out is that there are mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults. Which, uh, given your character's background, you'd probably find pretty interesting.
no way, seriously? dragon cults, like, actual worship?

Yeah — the setting notes specifically mention mountain communities in the Red Mountains that follow old dragon cults as a source of internal unrest in Rathakos. The region's part of Rathakos now (it was reorganized after the war), but it's not exactly... tightly controlled. There's definitely still people up there maintaining those traditions.

dude that's actually sick. so like, would someone from Forthyr — with the whole sorcerer king and Dragon Emperor revival thing going on back home — would they know about these mountain cults? are we talking the same religion?

That's a good character question, but it's also kind of a blank space in the notes. The DM hasn't filled in the specifics of what these mountain cults actually believe or whether they'd connect to Forthyr's Dragon Emperor revival movement. But the vibes are definitely in the same neighborhood, right? Both are about preserving or reviving dragon traditions. Your DM could totally run with that connection if it fits your character's backstory.
Alright so I've got twenty minutes - give me the executive summary on the Artklan League. What is it?

Okay, quick version: it used to be the Artklan Empire and controlled over two-fifths of the continent. After the Ryraxian War ended thirty years ago, it got reorganized into what's now called the Artklan League. Governed by High King Ezevir Arth from Artkos in the northwest. The League includes Artkos itself, a bunch of exclaves scattered around (including Ath'r, the capital), and nominally a few other territories that have way more independence than they used to. There's some serious tension because Artkos is trying to restore the old dominance while everyone else is holding onto whatever independence they can grab.
So it got weaker. Is Kard part of it? I remember Kard being mentioned as a whole thing.

Yeah, Kard is part of Artkos's southwestern enclave - major financial and shipping hub. But here's the issue: most of Kard's population speaks Edran and actually *welcomed* Edrad's invasion twenty-eight years ago. Governance there is fragile as hell. It's the kind of place where you can tell the empire isn't what it used to be. Same pattern everywhere really - the high kings are looking at the new League and seeing a pale shadow of what the Artklan Empire was, while their nominal subjects are basically trying not to get reconquered.
yo who is dragoncaller shanlong? sounds kinda made up lol

Ha, I get that. So Dragoncaller Shanlong VI rules Forthyr, this huge kingdom to the northeast. He's a sorcerer king, meaning his magical power is basically his authority instead of inheriting a normal throne.
sorcerer king sounds so edgy. is that actually a thing or is your DM just vibing

It's real worldbuilding, not random. Forthyr is described as 'somewhat backwards' and their whole dynasty has always been like this—sorcerer kings. They've even got a religious group that wants dragons to come back, so that whole vibe makes sense.
okay wait, dragons? and why is he number SIX? do i have to learn about shanlongs one through five

Dragons are a whole thing in the setting's history, but that's separate. As for the numbers—there's dynasty history, but you just need to know Shanlong VI is the current ruler. Unless your DM gets weird with it, that's the only one that matters.
alright but like... will this actually affect my game or is it just random lore

Depends on your DM. Forthyr is pretty far northeast and you're starting in Rathakos, so they're not neighbors. Shanlong VI is a kingdom ruler, not someone you'd casually meet. But your DM could definitely write them into the story if it fits.
cool. so i don't need to stress about memorizing a ton of kings
Nope. Just remember Forthyr exists, it's run differently, and there's a guy named Shanlong VI in charge. Your DM will fill in the details if they matter.
okay so I just noticed there's these two dates in the moon calendar—Feb 13, 2253 and May 24, 2262—that look absolutely wild. what am I looking at here?

Oh man, YES. Those are the closest triple-moon alignments of the decade. Feb 13, 2253 is the perfect triple-full—all three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) within 0.2% of each other at full moon. And May 24, 2262 is even tighter: a triple-new within 0.1%. Like, essentially perfectly aligned. You don't get moments much more precise than that.

wait, 0.1%?? That's basically exact. Do these happen regularly or is this rare?

Actually, forget Forgotten Realms—FR doesn't even track this level of detail. This is *good*.

It's rare enough to matter! All three inner moons line up within 5% of full or new about five times a year, but perfect or near-perfect triple alignments only happen about once a year—and they cluster into eras. So you get like a full-moon era where all-full alignments dominate, then it switches to a new-moon era, cycles every ~12 years. But those two dates you found? Those are the *best* of the whole decade.

As a DM, if you wanted to hang something cosmic or prophetic on a celestial event, those are the moments.
oh dude, I'm definitely using May 24, 2262 for something. Perfect new-moon darkness across the whole continent at once... that's a power move for a major story beat.

Exactly. And the notes explicitly flag it—"May 24, 2262 at 03h PMT is the closest triple-new of the decade." The fact that they called that out tells you the DM built this to be usable. Three moons dark, all at once. Your players will feel it.
yo, I missed last session. My drow's been staying in one of those enclaves since the war ended -- what parts of the map are actually fleshed out and what's still blank?

Good question. So the drow are a real thing post-war -- Artkos recruited them as allies in desperation, and after the war ended you guys stayed on the surface. Qama is the big one, the holy city of Bahumat in Qis'mos, but there are a dozen scattered enclaves besides that. Your character could plausibly be in any of those.

As for what's fleshed out: the major nations (Edrad, the Artklan League with Artkos, Rathakos, Qis'mos, Forthyr) have decent skeleton detail. The capital cities and key places are named. The islands in the central sea have some structure, especially Qaringil if you're thinking of that era.

But blank spaces? Lots. Westron, the entire western continent, is basically "it exists and is exotic." The big island to the south past Freeport is mostly unexplored jungle. Various regions are marked but not detailed. The notes explicitly say "there are blank spaces on the map that need to be filled" -- and they can be part of your backstory if you want.
so if my guy's from one of the scattered drow enclaves, is it cool if I just... make up where that is? like, one of the blanks?

Yeah, that's exactly what the DM is inviting. They're saying the blank spaces can be filled in with your backstory. So you could place your enclave in one of the unmapped regions and build that into your character's history. Just make sure it makes sense geographically -- like, Artkos recruited the drow as allies during the war, so your enclave probably wouldn't be deep in enemy territory or totally isolated. But within that constraint, you've got room to create.
what about the south? that big jungle island with Freeport? is that actually part of the world or more like... DM's-thinking-about-it territory?

It exists -- there's a big island to the south, Freeport is a real city of exiles and pirates at the shore, and there's jungle inland that people "try to not look too hard at." One of the notes even says "Some say this is where the dragons went." So it's part of the world.

But as for what's actually *in* that jungle, who runs it, whether there are drow settlements there, what's in the interior -- that's basically blank. It's marked as "mostly unexplored." So if you wanted to tie your character there, you'd be building on a sketchy foundation, but that might make for a cool hook.
got it. and Rathakos is where the campaign starts, right? what's the actual situation there right now?

Yeah, Rathakos is the expected starting point. It's a mountainous border state in the Red Mountains, capital is Rath in the northern foothills. Post-war state: it's been made a Princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r, but there's no Prince yet -- Regent John Dunne is running things while a Grand Council deliberates on finding a monarch.

Here's the tension: Rathakos is pretty fractured. It borders Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, and a few others, so it's pulled in a lot of directions. Plus it has internal unrest, especially in the Southern Reaches (annexed from Edrad during the war) and mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. The region has a strong adventuring tradition because the Red Mountains are dangerous and underexplored.

So there's instability and opportunity, which is why parties tend to start there.
ok this is probably dumb but i dont understand primsine at all

Not dumb at all! It's a pretty specific navigation thing. So Primus is this special moon that never moves in the sky -- it's geostationary, magically anchored. To figure out your longitude, you measure how high it is above the horizon and use this function called primsine to calculate your position.
ok so like what if primus is like right above your head lol

Then primsine(90) equals 1! And that tells you you're right at the sub-Primus point -- directly below Primus, at the equator, at longitude 0. That's the prime meridian for Qaia. It's actually the simplest case: no calculation needed, you just *know* where you are because Primus is always in the same spot.
wait that's actually cool... so the moons are like really important to this world?

Yeah, definitely. Primus gives you perfect navigation on the near hemisphere. Then there are the other moons -- Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, and a couple smaller ones -- they all have different orbits and they create this complicated tidal system. Some people use the moon cycles for their calendar too. The moons are kind of a big deal to how Qaia works.
can you see all of them at the same time?

Sometimes! They have different orbital periods, so they're in different phases at different times. There are actually special events called triple-moon alignments where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus line up in their phases -- all full or all new -- and that happens a few times a year. Pretty cool if your DM wants to do something with that.

yo so I'm reading up on Primus navigation and I'm confused -- why does the precision go from like ±6km to ±55km depending on where you are? That's a WILD range for a navigation mechanic. Is it because Primus moves differently at different latitudes or something?

Good catch. Primus itself is fixed -- it never moves. But the geometry of measuring it changes depending on where you stand relative to Primus and the prime meridian. When Primus is directly overhead (at the equator, at longitude 0), a small change in its measured elevation angle maps to a huge change in your longitude. When Primus is low on the horizon near the visibility boundary, the same elevation change only tells you a small difference in longitude. Shallow angle = shallow geometry = low precision.

ah okay so that's a geometry thing not a Primus thing. But like... from a mechanics perspective, does this mean the DM would call for different DCs for navigation checks depending on latitude? Like, 'you're at the equator and Primus is right overhead, DC 10 to plot your longitude' vs 'you're near the edge of visibility, DC 20'?

That's exactly how a DM could run it. The notes actually quantify this: a good navigator with a careful sextant can pull off ±6-7 km precision near the equator and prime meridian, but that same sextant at the visibility boundary gives you ±55-70 km. In game terms, yeah, that'd translate to different DCs. Equator and prime meridian = easier navigation checks. Near the visibility limit = much harder. It's baked into the geometry, not arbitrary.

Okay so this is kinda busted then right? Like if I'm a navigator PC, I'm basically incentivized to plan my sea routes around the equator, because I get +4 or +5 on my navigation checks effectively? That feels like it could break some campaign planning if the DM isn't watching for it.

It's a strong incentive, yeah, but not necessarily broken. Sailing near the equator might add weeks to your journey compared to a northern route. You might hit monsoon seasons, pirate-heavy waters, or have the story pull you toward the poles anyway. Plus, the notes mention Primus is only visible in one hemisphere -- if your campaign ever goes far-side, Primus navigation stops working entirely and you're back to dead reckoning or stellar methods. So you can have low-latitude precision or access to Primus; you don't necessarily get both everywhere.

wait, hold up. Far-side hemisphere? So Primus is only visible from ONE SIDE of the planet? That's a huge asymmetry. Does that mean the map has like... continents sorted by whether they're in Primus-view or not? Is Qamis the near side?

Exactly. Primus is geostationary over one point on the equator, so it's permanently above the horizon for roughly the near-side hemisphere (within about ±80° longitude of the prime meridian, narrowing at higher latitudes) and permanently invisible from the far side. Whether Qamis is the near side or far side isn't explicitly stated in the notes -- that's one of the blank spaces. But it's a solid hook for worldbuilding. If Qamis is the near side, you've got this incredible navigational advantage. If it's the far side, Qaian navigators are essentially medieval in their longitude capabilities.

Oh dang, so this could be a MAJOR setting detail that determines like... political geography, naval power projection, trade routes, all of it? Because whichever hemisphere has Primus gets to be the maritime superpower?

Yep, that's the implication. The notes even call it out: having Primus means you cracked the longitude problem centuries before Earth did, because it's purely geometric, no chronometer needed. That's a structural advantage in naval exploration, trade, and military logistics. If Qamis is the near side, that explains a lot about why the maritime powers are concentrated there. If it's the far side, it explains why far-side navigation is described as 'substantially harder.' The DM hasn't nailed this down yet, but it's a blank space that would reshape the entire political map depending on which way it goes.

This is getting into like... continental strategy tier. So the practical takeaway for us as players is: if we ever roll a campaign on the far side, navigation is legitimately a resource to manage and plan around. And if we stay on the near side, Primus is basically a solved problem except for edge cases. Does that hold?

Pretty much. On the near side, Primus is a solved problem -- you just need a clear night, a sextant, and a primsine table. Some atmosphere refraction and clouds can mess with you, but the mechanic itself isn't the challenge. On the far side, you're back to pre-chronometer Earth: stellar transits, lunar distances, dead reckoning, all of it harder and slower. It becomes a real constraint on adventure. The DM could even use navigation difficulty as a pacing tool -- 'you're exploring uncharted far-side territory, so every location takes weeks longer to map than near-side equivalents.' It's elegant, actually.
okay so I read about the triple-moon alignments and now I'm totally obsessed -- what's the difference between a full-moon era and a new-moon era?

Oh I love this question! So with three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) all orbiting at different periods, they line up together pretty regularly -- about five times a year they're within 5% of the same phase, and roughly once a year all three are nearly exact. But here's the wild part: for a few years in a row, those alignments are *all* full moons. Then the pattern flips and for the next few years they're *all* new moons. A full cycle takes about twelve years.
wait so during a new-moon era you literally just... don't get triple full moons? That's insane. How does that even work dynamically?

It comes down to orbital mechanics -- the three moons have incommensurate periods (about 4.5, 8.1, and 29.5 days), so their phases drift relative to each other in a complex pattern. Over about 12 years that drift cycles through a phase where "all three full together" is common, then transitions to a phase where that becomes impossible and "all three new together" is what you get instead. It's like watching three gears with different teeth counts -- eventually the configuration loops back, but the intermediate states are totally different.
that's so cool. so like, if I'm in a new-moon era right now, how long before I see a perfect triple full?

Depends on where in the cycle you are! But the notes have a specific example: the decade 2253-2263 starts in a full-moon era (2253-2254), then plunges into a new-moon era (2255-2258), then bounces back to full (2259-2261). So if you're say, mid-2256, you're deep in new-moon country and won't see a strong triple-full alignment until late 2259. But here's the reward: May 24, 2262 has an *insanely* perfect triple-new alignment -- all three moons within 0.1% of syzygy. Essentially exact.
0.1%??? that's... that's all three moons going dark at the same time. Do the notes say what happens to tides when that hits?

The notes don't detail the tidal signature of a perfect triple-new specifically, but we know the baseline: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus together produce about 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's Moon. When all three are new (aligned on the far side of Qaia from the sun), you'd lose all that tidal contribution simultaneously. When all three are full (aligned on the near side), you'd get a *massive* tidal bulge. So yeah -- erratic tides, extreme highs and lows, depending on the alignment type.
What's the deal with the Artklan League? I'm making a character from Rathakos and I'm getting confused about all the politics.

So the Artklan League is basically Artkos trying to put the band back together after the Ryraxian War ended. The Artklan Empire used to rule about two-fifths of the continent, but the war tore it apart. Now that things have stabilized, High King Ezevir Arth is trying to restore Artkos's dominance over the other territories. That's the League -- nominally independent states that used to be part of the empire, now being pulled back into the fold. It's fragile. Kard in particular has issues because most of its population is Edran-speaking and actually welcomed the invasion twenty-eight years ago.
Wait, so Rathakos was *part* of the Artklan Empire? Because the notes about Rathakos don't mention that at all.

This is where it gets tangled. Rathakos as it exists now didn't exist before the war. What *did* exist in those mountains was something called the Red Mountain Republic -- created by Edrad when they occupied the mountain provinces during the war. Think of it as Edrad's administrative setup for conquered territory. When Edrad's military position collapsed and they withdrew, the factions inside the Republic fought it out. The pro-Edran faction got purged, and what came out the other side was two new independent nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So from Artkos's perspective, yeah, those mountains were under their empire's control historically, then got occupied by Edrad, then became independent. Now Artkos wants them back, or at least wants them in the League.
Okay so Rathakos is basically squeezed between everyone who thinks they own it. What's keeping it independent if it's surrounded like that?

Mostly the fact that no single power can afford to take it without the others pouncing. Rathakos borders Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, Qirkos, Lock, AND Acrokos. It's a buffer zone.
