The ancient Draconic empire that once ruled Qaia is ancient history now -- the notes don't go super deep into that era, but the institutions persisted longest in Qis'mos. There's also a popular rumor about the big unexplored island far to the south, near Freeport: 'Some say this is where the dragons went.' As for Rathakos specifically, you'd know the mountains still have isolated communities clinging to old dragon cults, which creates real tension in the region -- especially between the old dragon-worshippers and whatever the newer authorities are trying to establish.
huh ok so are the dragon cults just nostalgia or is there actual lore to them? like did something go wrong when the empire fell?

The notes don't detail exactly why the cults exist or what went wrong when the empire fell -- that's kind of a blank space for the DM to fill in. But here's the flavor: the dragon cults are real enough that Rathakos is dealing with 'internal unrest in many places, especially in the Southern Reaches annexed from Edrad and from mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults.' So they're not just wistful nostalgia -- they're a present political problem. Your character would absolutely encounter people who believe the dragons were better rulers, or that dragons will return, or that dragon worship is the true way. Whether there's something to those beliefs, or whether the cults are just romanticizing a lost era, is something the campaign could explore.
okay that tracks. so when you say the red mountains are dangerous and underexplored -- is that because of the cults or is it just a general frontier thing?

Probably both, but the notes frame it more as a general frontier thing. Rathakos has 'a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship' specifically because 'the Red Mountains remain one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions in the interior of Qamis.' So yeah, it's rough terrain, hard to map, probably has monsters and natural hazards. The dragon cults add a layer of political weirdness on top of that -- there's unrest because different factions in the mountains have different loyalties (Edrad, the new Rathakos government, old dragon traditions). But the core reason the mountains matter for adventurers is just that they're wild and unexplored.
yo wut was that about the continent again, i totally blanked

you're on Qamis -- the continent where everything's happening. it's on the planet Qaia. after thirty years of the ryraxian war, Ryraxos the bronze dragon is exiled to the second moon and the continent is basically in chaos as everyone tries to figure out the new power structure.
ok cool. so like geographically, what's around Qamis? other continents or

yeah, there's stuff around. to the west is another continent called Westron -- it's huge and exotic but the dm hasn't really prepped it yet. to the south is a big island that's mostly unexplored jungle with a pirate city called Freeport on the shore. and there are islands in the central sea that are independent city-states run by mercenary naval powers.
wait the islands are completely independent? like they're not part of any nation

right, they're basically their own city-states called thassolocracies -- ruled by naval power. they were founded by exiles from both continents and now they're independent, making money off mercenary contracts and trade. they control the shipping lanes through the central sea, so they're a pretty big deal.
huh, so if we wanted to sail somewhere we'd probably have to deal with them

basically. there's a wizard-made isthmus with enormous bridges that crosses part of the central sea, so travel isn't completely locked down, but the island cities definitely control the major trade routes. any serious sailing will probably involve them.
ok so i'm making a half-elven Edran noble, thinking about the Keyberos restoration angle. but the whole 'drow on Qamis' thing feels made up? like that can't actually be lore in the setting

nah it's definitely canon. the notes say 'the drow, recruited as allies by Artkos in a desperation move, remain on the surface in a dozen scattered enclaves.' they're explicitly still on the continent. most prominent enclave is Qama, the holy city of Bahumat.

ok but that still doesn't make sense. why would the Keyberos dynasty—who just got restored and want to rebuild Edran dominance—just let a drow occupation force hold the religious capital of the continent?

that's a really fair question, and the notes don't actually explain that part. it's blank space. what *is* concrete: during the late phases of the war the drow occupied Qama and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. the Church immediately responded by setting up a rival candidate, Qachu II, in Qis with the actual relics of office. so you don't have a stable occupation—you have active religious schism and competing claims to authority.
so as an Edran noble, this is like... a real political powder keg i'd have to navigate?

exactly. you've got a fractured Church, a drow-appointed high priest in the holiest site, and the Keyberos dynasty freshly restored but unable to immediately resolve it. that's simultaneously a threat (foreign occupation, religious chaos) and an opportunity (prove Edran strength, restore Church unity, exploit the schism). the situation is definitely there; what you do about it is your character's angle.
so im making a character and i want to get a sense of the world. whats the planet called? and like what continent are we on?

The planet is Qaia, and the continent is Qamis. The campaign takes place in the present day -- right after the thirty-year Ryraxian War just ended. The dragon Ryraxos is exiled to Secundus, which is the second moon, and the Keyberos dynasty just got restored to power in Edrad.

There's also a prequel campaign set about 200 years earlier based in the island city of Qaringil, but that's a different era with a different political map.
ok cool. so i noticed a lot of the place names have Q in them without U after -- like Qaringil, Qamis, Qama. is that a language thing?

Yes! Q without U comes from Old Draconic -- also called Old Qis. That's the scholarly and treaty language, the prestigious one used in formal writing and diplomacy. The Q is pronounced "Kh" (kind of like the Hebrew chet or German Bach). So Qaringil is pronounced "Kharingil."

The modern nations have their own languages -- Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas -- but Old Draconic stuck around as the academic lingua franca after the dragons left.
that's rad. so if im from somewhere that speaks Qis, would i naturally use Old Draconic words in my everyday speech? or is it more like... Latin?

More like Latin, yeah. Old Draconic is formal -- you'd encounter it in books, treaties, inscriptions, scholarly stuff. In everyday conversation you'd use your national language. Though the Qis language is actually the closest modern language to Old Draconic, so speakers of Qis would probably have an easier time picking it up.

And if you're from Qis'mos -- the region where the Church of Bahamut is strongest -- you might naturally code-switch into Old Draconic when talking about religious or formal matters.

ooh i like that angle. so if i made my character from Qama, the holy city of Bahamut, would that make sense for them to have Old Draconic as a bonus language? im thinking someone who spent time in the temples

Absolutely. Qama makes sense for that -- it's the traditional seat of the Church of Bahamut, so religious scholars there would definitely study Old Draconic. That's a natural fit for a temple-trained character.

One wrinkle though: Qama was occupied by drow during the late phases of the war, and they appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. So if your character's been around the temples lately, there's been some disruption -- and an alternate candidate, Qachu II, is operating from the city of Qis without any of the relics of office. That could be interesting backstory material if your character was caught in that schism.
hm ok interesting. so is Qama in present day or prequel? and wait, the drow are just... on the surface now?

That's all present day -- post-war Qamis. The drow were recruited as allies by Artkos during the desperation phase of the Ryraxian War, and they didn't leave. They have a bunch of scattered enclaves across the continent, with Qama being the most prominent one. They haven't despoiled the city, but yeah, they've put down roots.

Qaringil (the prequel campaign) is about 200 years earlier, so no drow on the surface then.
wild. ok so if im from Qama and trained in the temples, what language would i speak natively? Qis?

Qis, yes -- that's the regional/national language of Qis'mos. You'd speak the prestige dialect (the one used in the capital and by the ruling class), plus one regional dialect. Then Old Draconic on top of that from your temple training.

One note: there's no standardization in these languages, so regional dialects can be pretty different. Someone from the capital of Artkos would have real trouble understanding the Artklan spoken in Rathakos, for example. But for your PC, speaking the prestige Qis and one regional variation would make sense.
ok cool. so what would the regional variant be if im from Qama specifically?

That's actually a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet -- the notes mention regional variation exists but don't detail what makes Qama's dialect distinct from other Qis'mos speech. You could workshop that with your DM or work it into your character as a detail they can use. Like, maybe your character has a particular accent or some archaic temple phrasing that locals recognize.
What's the mechanical angle on the full-moon vs new-moon eras in the triple-moon alignments? Or is it just flavor? And what's the cycle length?

Great question. So Qaia has three inner moons -- Secundus (4.5-day orbit), Tertius (8.1-day orbit), and Quartus (29.5-day orbit) -- and because their periods don't align evenly, their simultaneous-full and simultaneous-new events happen in predictable bursts. The notes define a 'full-moon era' as a multi-year period where all-full alignments happen frequently and all-new alignments are absent or rare, and vice versa for new-moon eras. These alternate with a full cycle of roughly 12 years. The alignment calendar shows the exact dates when these rare triple-syzygies occur. Mechanically though? That's entirely your DM's call. The notes just track the pattern and provide the data; what it does (if anything) for spellcasting, tides, lunar magic, whatever -- that's house rules. But the infrastructure is there if you want to use it.
Got it. So what year does the campaign actually start in?

The alignment calendar covers 2253-2263, which shows two full-moon eras (2253-2254 and 2259-2261) bracketing a new-moon era (2255-2258). But the notes don't explicitly state what year the Qamis campaign opens in, so I can't pin down which era you're starting in. That's probably something your DM will clarify -- or weaponize as a detail. The data's there either way.
okay so i'm reading about primus and it says it's magically anchored. what's that about? like, why does it need magic to stay geostationary?

Good question! Primus sits in a truly geostationary orbit -- it never rises or sets, just hangs in the same spot in the sky above one point on the equator. The problem is, without magic, that orbit is *unstable*. Tidal forces from Qaia would gradually pull it inward or outward (tidal migration), destabilizing the orbit over time. The magic locks it in place, preventing that drift entirely. It's the only way to keep something as small as Primus in a perfect geostationary orbit indefinitely.
but like... how much tidal force are we talking about? is the magic doing the heavy lifting or just a small nudge?

Pretty heavy lifting, actually. Secundus alone exerts 1.47 times the tidal force Earth's Moon does. Add in Tertius at 2.04x and Quartus at 0.87x, and Qaia's tidal load is around 4.4 times Earth's. Primus is tiny -- only 0.0001 times the Moon's mass -- so those tidal forces would gradually spiral it inward. Without the magic holding it steady, Primus would be long gone. It's a permanent feature of the Qaia system specifically *because* something magically anchors it.
that's wild. so is the magic like... actively correcting its orbit, or is it more that the magic sealed its orbit at creation and just... holds it?

The notes don't specify the mechanics of *how* the magic works -- that's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. All we know is that Primus is magically anchored and therefore experiences no tidal torque and no migration. Whether it's an active correction, a sealed orbit, or something else entirely is up to your DM to decide. Pretty interesting question to ask them though, especially given how precise the geostationary geometry has to be!
wait why is qaringil's government described as incomprehensible lol, that's such a funny way to put it

It's a genuinely awkward system! So Qaringil is a republic run by guilds and five major families, but instead of having a clear hierarchy, you've got all these competing power centers negotiating constantly. The Exarch is the top leader, but they're elected by the nobility, clergy, AND guildmasters combined -- and they can be impeached by the same groups. So basically every major decision requires navigating multiple factions with different interests. Add in that it's this maze of canals instead of being organized geographically, and you get a city that's almost designed to be politically confusing. It works because everyone involved is focused on making money, not grand ideology, but it's... not a system you can reduce to simple rules.
okay but HOLD ON they have actual armed merchant fleets?? like they just send battleships out to sell mercenary services??

YES! That's the core of Qaringil's whole thing. The city can't feed itself -- it survives on fishing and importing food -- so instead of being vulnerable because of that, it weaponized it. The major guilds, especially the Shipping guild, control these powerful armed merchant fleets. They don't just trade: they sell their navies as mercenaries to whoever's willing to pay on the mainland. It's how a tiny island city-state stays independent and actually powerful. You've got this cool inversion where their biggest weakness (no farmland) turned into their biggest strength (controlling sea trade and naval power).
so uh... everyone keeps talking about how great Jia is. why's it such a big deal?

Jia's the capital of Edrad, sitting on a peninsula that sticks out into the Central Sea. It's described as by far the most culturally influential city in all of Qamis -- basically the trendsetter. And Edrad is historically the most politically and economically dominant nation on the continent, so that probably helps. The city's ruled by the half-elven Keyberos dynasty, who've been in power for four hundred years.
okay but like... what *is* there that makes it culturally influential? it's not just 'big city = culture city', right?

That's actually a great question, and the notes don't spell it out -- that's one of the blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. But connecting the dots: Jia is the capital of the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis, it sits on the coast for trade and cultural exchange, and it's where the Keyberos dynasty holds court. Those facts together probably explain why it's the cultural center. If you're trying to understand Edran culture or find where the power is, Jia's the place.
okay so I totally missed last session and now everyone keeps talking about Primus and I'm lost... something about how only half the planet can see it?

Yeah! Primus is the smaller moon that's geostationary -- it orbits Qaia in exactly one sidereal day, which means it's always directly above the same point on the equator. It never rises, never sets, just sits there in the sky. That's why only the near-side hemisphere can see it -- the far side of the planet never gets a view.
Wait wait wait. It stays in the exact same spot in the sky? How is that even possible? Shouldn't it like... I dunno, drift or fall or something?

Good question. For that to work, it has to be at just the right distance from Qaia, moving at just the right speed so its orbital period matches Qaia's rotation -- one day. That distance works out to about 42,160 km from Qaia's center. And here's the kicker: according to the lore, Primus is magically anchored in place, so it maintains this perfect geostationary orbit. No natural forces pulling it out of alignment.
Okay so half the planet sees it, half doesn't. Is that literally a hard line -- like you cross from seeing it to not seeing it?

Not quite a hard line, but close. Primus is visible to observers within roughly 80 degrees of longitude from the prime meridian at the equator -- but that range narrows as you move toward the poles. So from the equator you've got this band of visibility that's almost 160 degrees wide, but someone at 60 degrees north latitude would have a much narrower range. The exact visibility limit is about 81.3 degrees away from the point directly below Primus.
Oh so near the edge of where you can see it... it'd be like super low on the horizon?

Exactly. Right at the visibility boundary, Primus would be basically sitting on your horizon. Which actually matters practically -- atmospheric refraction is significant down there, and it becomes harder to get an accurate measurement. The navigator's tables mention that precision degrades near the visibility edge. It's still usable, but you lose some accuracy.
Huh. So like... why does the DM keep emphasizing this? Are we supposed to use Primus for something in the campaign?

Oh yeah, this is the cool part. Primus is the basis for celestial navigation on Qaia. Because it's fixed in the sky above the equator, you can measure its elevation angle and use that to calculate your exact longitude -- without needing a chronometer or knowing the time. You just need to know your latitude from the pole star, measure Primus's elevation, and do a quick lookup in a navigation table. It's called the primsine function. Basically, Primus solves the longitude problem that plagued Earth navigation for centuries.
Oh damn, that's actually clever. So... what about the people on the far side? How do they navigate?

That's the thing -- they can't use Primus at all. The far-side hemisphere has to rely on dead reckoning, stellar observations, or much harder methods like lunar-distance measurements using other moons against a star catalog. It's substantially harder and less accurate. So there's this huge asymmetry baked into the world: navigators on the near side have a massive advantage. It's analogous to Earth's pre-chronometer era -- when maritime nations struggled with longitude problems for centuries. Except on Qaia, half the planet still can't easily solve it.

so I'm looking at Qamis for some inspiration and I'm seeing this Bahamut schism thing with two high priests... isn't that just going to confuse players? Like, in Forgotten Realms that's always a total mess.

It's a fair worry, but it's actually pretty clean in Qamis. The setup is: Bahamut's main temple is Qama, which is the holy city in Qis'mos. During the Ryraxian War, the drow occupied Qama and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. That caused a schism—the legitimate religious authority stayed in Qama with Qe'lth, but a rival candidate, Qachu II, set up in Qis without the relics of office.

So from a player perspective it's actually simple: one guy has the relics and the city, the other guy doesn't. There's no ambiguity about who's "really" in charge. It's more about politics than theology.

okay but like... the drow *appointed* a high priest? That doesn't feel very grimdark. Isn't that the kind of thing that would cause way more religious upheaval? In FR you'd have adventurers marching on temples.

Good question. The notes say the drow "have not despoiled" Qama, which suggests they're being relatively respectful occupiers. It's less "we control your church now" and more "we're administering this territory and we're going to have a say in it."

The real tension isn't a holy war—it's that Qachu II in Qis is the legitimate religiously-sanctioned candidate, but Qe'lth IV has the actual relics and is sitting in the actual holy city. So you've got a schism of legitimacy. It's political friction more than theological upheaval, which honestly might be more interesting for players than another "raid the temple" arc.
hmm, I guess that makes sense. Is this something players would actually run into in a normal Qamis campaign, or is it more background worldbuilding?

The notes don't specify how active this conflict is right now or how players would encounter it. That's kind of a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. It could be background color, or it could be a hook—like maybe someone hired them to investigate what's *really* going on at Qama under drow watch, or to work with one of the high priests. Depends on where your campaign is based and what your players care about.
okay cool. honestly I might steal something like this. The drow-occupied territory thing is cleaner than the FR "demon cult has infiltrated everything" vibe.

Yeah, it's a solid narrative hook without needing paranoia mechanics. And you get the benefit that the drow aren't playing weird games—they're just there, administering, which feels less soap-opera and more like actual geopolitics. Steal away.
hey, trying to get up to speed on Qaringil before we start -- who actually lives there? what races are we talking about?

Qaringil's run by five major families who hold most of the power guilds. They're all mostly human, though all of them have some elf ancestry mixed in. Beyond the families, there's at least one prominent gnomish family -- Tiddwar, who leads the Mercantile Guild and are known for their gears sigil. The city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qismos, so there's that cosmopolitan trader vibe. Past that, the DM hasn't detailed out the full racial makeup of the general population, so that's blank space you could fill in with backstory if you wanted.
so it's basically human-dominated with gnomes and elves sprinkled in?

That's a fair read for an exec summary, yeah. The families that actually run things are human-majority with elf ancestry, Tiddwar shows gnomes have real pull in commerce, and the city's melting-pot origin from Qismos settlers suggests diversity. But beyond that snapshot, the campaign notes don't pin down racial percentages or community breakdowns. You'd have room to develop that detail yourself depending on your character.
hi, just reading about the setting. why is Q pronounced 'Kh'? that seems weird

Good question! Q is pronounced Kh — it comes from Old Draconic (also called Old Qis), which is the language of scholarly publications and treaties across Qaia. You see it all over place names: Qamis, Qaringil, Qis'mos, Qama — that 'q without u' pattern is a signature of Old Draconic.
got it. so the Qis Empire uses Old Draconic for official documents and stuff like that?

You're on the right track, but let me gently clarify — are you asking about the Qaringil prequel era (~200 years ago), or the present-day Qamis campaign? The political setup is pretty different between the two.

In Qaringil, yes, the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r and uses Old Draconic for scholarly and diplomatic work. But by the time of present-day Qamis, things have shifted significantly.
wait, those are two different things? I thought the Qis Empire and Qis'mos were the same entity

Easy mix-up! They're related historically but quite different. In Qaringil (the prequel), the Holy Qis Empire is a unified political power ruling from Ath'r. That's the entity you're reading about.

In present-day Qamis, ~200 years later, that empire has fractured. Qis'mos is now a geographic and cultural region — still the richest heartland in Qamis and the seat of the Church of Bahumat — but it's politically fragmented, governed by locally-elected Potentates rather than ruled as an empire.

So the Holy Qis Empire was a specific historical entity; Qis'mos is what that region evolved into. Old Draconic stayed the scholarly language in both eras, but the political structure is entirely different.
ohhh, that makes sense. so both campaigns share the same moon system and the language heritage, but the nations are completely different?

Exactly! Both campaigns are set on planet Qaia with the same seven moons and the same astronomical/navigational system. Old Draconic persists as the scholarly language in both eras. But yes — two centuries of history, wars, and political upheaval mean the map and power structures are significantly different. The Qaringil prequel (early era) is based in the island city-state of Qaringil, while Qamis present-day (post-Ryraxian War) is where things kick off in Rathakos. Different worlds, same cosmology.

So I've been hearing navigators and merchants talk about how the moons work for figuring out where you are at sea. There's like, seven of them right? Can you break down what they are and which order they're in?

Yeah, there are seven moons orbiting Qaia. Here's the lineup from closest in to farthest out: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, Septimus, and way out there's Quintus -- though Quintus is basically a speck. The first six are the real players. Primus is unique because it's geostationary -- it hangs in one fixed spot in the sky and never moves. Secundus and Septimus orbit backwards (retrograde). Tertius and Quartus go the normal direction. And Sextus also goes backwards.
Okay, so if I'm out on the canal at night, which ones am I actually gonna see? I figure some of them must be super dim or far away.

You'll see most of them if you have clear skies. Tertius is the biggest and brightest -- it's huge, almost the size of Earth's moon but slightly dimmer because its surface is darker. Quartus is basically an Earth-moon clone, nearly identical in brightness and size to Tertius. Secundus is almost as large as Tertius but darker, so it's dimmer -- still impressive though. Then there's Primus, which is small and faint, only about one-third the size of Earth's moon and much dimmer. Sextus and Septimus are tiny -- visible if you know where to look, but they're no match for the big three.
I keep hearing navigators talk about using Primus to figure out their longitude without needing a chronometer or anything. How does that actually work? That seems impossible.

It's actually elegant. Because Primus is always fixed in the same spot in the sky -- hanging above the equator at longitude zero -- you can use its elevation angle from any location to calculate how far east or west you are. You measure how high Primus appears above the horizon, plug that into a lookup table called primsine, divide by your cosine of latitude (which you already know from the pole star), and you get your longitude. No time measurement needed, just geometry.
Okay I'm starting to get it. But what's this primsine thing exactly? When I hear navigators talking about it, they make it sound like some complicated formula. Walk me through it.

Primsine is just a lookup function, not that scary. The formula is: primsine(e) = ρ cos²(e) + sin(e) × sqrt(1 - ρ² cos²(e)), where e is Primus's elevation angle and ρ is the ratio of Qaia's radius to Primus's orbital distance, which is a fixed constant: 6371 km divided by 42,160 km, or about 0.1511. Once you have primsine(e), you use the formula cos(λ) = primsine(e) / cos(φ), where φ is your latitude and λ is your unknown longitude. Then reverse-lookup λ from the cosine table. In practice, navigators just use precomputed tables instead of doing the math every time.

All this navigation stuff is fascinating, but I work with water every day. What about the tides? We've got all these canals in Qaringil, and the lock-keepers are always talking about tidal schedules. How does having three big moons plus Primus affect the water levels?

This is where it gets complex. Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all orbit freely and pull on Qaia's oceans, creating an oscillating tide about four times stronger than Earth's single moon. They have different orbital periods -- Secundus takes 4.5 days, Tertius about 8 days, Quartus about 29 days -- so their tidal effects interfere and reinforce in complicated rhythms. Primus is different: because it's geostationary and never moves, it doesn't create a daily tidal cycle. Instead it raises a permanent static bulge in the oceans, about 2 centimeters higher sea level along the line from Qaia's center through Primus. So the canal water levels you see are driven by three incommensurate oscillating tides plus one static offset.

I was reading an old ship captain's log I found in the Shipping Guild archives, and he kept mentioning waiting for a 'triple full' -- said it only comes around once a year or so and it's huge for navigation or something. What's he talking about?

A triple full is when all three of the main moons -- Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus -- are simultaneously full or nearly full at the same time. It's rare because their orbits are incommensurate. But the system has long-term cycles: there are full-moon eras when triple-fulls happen pretty frequently, then it switches to new-moon eras where triple-news happen instead. The whole cycle repeats every dozen years or so. Within a single era, you can get alignments so precise that all three moons are within a fraction of a percent of perfect syzygy -- like, within minutes of each other. The closest one in this decade happens around 2262 in May, where all three align within one-tenth of a percent simultaneously. For navigation purposes, that kind of alignment creates unique lighting conditions and tide patterns that can help you orient yourself.

The dragonborn thing could be flavor, could be a plot hook, could be something else entirely.

Yep, that's basically it. Same dynasty, same obsession with dragons, same sorcerer-king ruling it -- just the magic in the bloodline isn't as strong as it used to be. Whether that becomes a plot point depends on your DM.

Peixota leads Shipping, Manos leads Judicial, Volmer leads Banking, and Vitelli leads Silk. oh right, Tiddwar. so they're one of the Five?

hey, so I'm thinking about running Qaringil as a one-shot and I keep seeing Rathakos mentioned. What's the deal with that place? Is it a good setting for a prequel campaign?

Ah, good catch -- this is actually a key thing to keep straight. Rathakos doesn't exist in the Qaringil era. Qaringil is set about 200 years before the present-day Qamis campaign, and Rathakos only comes into being after the Ryraxian War. In Qaringil's time, that whole region is part of the Red Mountains, which are occupied by various non-state peoples but nominally claimed by the Holy Qis Empire. No Rathakos yet.
Oh okay, so what's actually in the Red Mountains during Qaringil? Are they just blank space or is there something there?

The Red Mountains exist and are definitely inhabited by non-state peoples -- mountain communities, local factions, that kind of thing. The Holy Qis Empire claims them but doesn't really administer them effectively. Beyond that, it's honestly marked as a blank space in the campaign notes that the DM hasn't fully filled in yet. So there's room for you to add your own content if you want to set a one-shot there, or you could focus on the islands or cities that are more developed.
Got it. So when does Rathakos actually get established? What happens between Qaringil and now that creates it?

During the Ryraxian War, when Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire, the former mountain provinces that were under Edran occupation got reorganized into something called the Red Mountain Republic. After Edran forces withdrew later in the war, the political factions in the republic split -- the pro-Edran faction got purged, and the opposition established two new nations from the wreckage: Rathakos and Acrokos. They're both mountainous border regions along the Red Mountains.
And what's Rathakos like now? Is it stable enough to actually be a campaign starting point?

Stable is... generous. It got made a Princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r, but they haven't actually found a Prince yet -- it's governed by a Regent named John Dunne while a Grand Council deliberates on a monarch. There's real internal unrest: the Southern Reaches that were annexed from Edrad are restless, and mountain communities still follow old dragon cults. That said, it borders Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, and a few other nations, plus the Red Mountains are one of the most dangerous and unexplored regions on the continent. It has a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship. So yeah -- unstable, but interesting.
okay so when's the next great triple-new alignment? also this setting is incredibly complex and you're going to have to explain why i should care about moons

Fair criticism! But the next really tight one is coming up fast: May 24, 2262, just a few months away. All three inner moons -- Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus -- will be new within 0.1% of each other. That's about as close as they get.

As for why you'd care: triple-new (or triple-full) alignments mess with tides, which affects coastal life and navigation. They're also considered auspicious or ominous depending on the culture. And if you're building a character, knowing what the sky looked like when they were born, or what calendar event shaped a memory, grounds them in the world.
okay but like. i'm trying to build a PC from Rathakos and i don't really care about tides. what's the actual vibe of the place right now? the war JUST ended right?

Yeah, the war ended not that long ago. Rathakos is a mountainous border state in the Red Mountains, and it's... messy right now. It was just made a princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r, but they haven't found a prince yet -- Regent John Dunne is running things while a Grand Council deliberates.

The tension is real: there's internal unrest in the Southern Reaches (the territory annexed from Edrad during the war, so the population is mixed and fractious) and mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults. The place has a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship, so it's not quiet.

If your character is from there, they're either from a place trying to figure out who it is after thirty years of chaos, or they've got some stake in the internal fights.
okay so it's tense. dragon cults, you said -- like Ryraxos people, or is that a different thing? and what would a normal person actually do for work there?

Different thing. These are old cults that predate Ryraxos -- the mountain communities have their own deep traditions. Ryraxos was the crisis; these are the underlying fault lines.

As for work: Rathakos is mountainous and relatively poor compared to the coastal cities. The tradition is adventuring and frontiersmanship -- so mercenary work, exploration, monster hunting, guard contracts. The Red Mountains are genuinely dangerous and underexplored, so there's steady work for people willing to risk it. You'd also have the normal subsistence: farming in the valleys, shepherding, mining, that sort of thing.

If your character isn't an adventurer, they might be involved in managing the post-war aftermath -- there's a lot of local governance chaos happening right now. Or they could have ties to the factions pulling on the Grand Council. It's not a stable place to just live quietly.

alright that helps. so like, the southern reaches -- those are the ex-Edran territories. what does that actually look like? would people there even want to be part of Rathakos, or is it all resentment

The notes don't spell out the texture of that specifically -- like, whether the southern Reaches population leans loyalist, resentful, opportunistic, or mixed. That's the kind of blank space your DM might want you to help fill in with your character's backstory.

What we know: they were under Edran occupation, then Edran withdrew, and the Red Mountain Assembly (which had rival factions) purged the pro-Edran side and created the new nations of Rathakos and Acrokos. So there's definitely been real violence and fracture, and people have recent loyalties that don't align with the new borders.

If your character is from the Southern Reaches, that political fracture is probably part of who they are -- whether they're navigating it, leaning into it, or trying to escape it.

Ha, totally fair question. Seven moons sounds wild until you realize most of them barely matter. Here's the practical version: three moons are important for the calendar and tides — Tertius, Quartus, and Secundus. Primus is smaller but useful because it never moves in the sky (you can use it to figure out where you are). Then Sextus and Septimus are tiny and dim. Quintus is basically a decorative point in space. So really you only need to think about four or five of them, and only two or three are going to come up in actual play.

oh okay got it. so like, are these moons the same in qaringil or is that different

Good question — the moon system is actually the same. Qaringil is set about 200 years earlier, but it's the same planet (Qaia), same world, same seven moons orbiting the same way. So Primus would work for navigation in either era, the calendar would be based on the same moons, all that. The moon system doesn't change — what changes is the political map and which nations are in power.
Who's Regent John Dunne? I'm drowning in all these ruler names.

John Dunne is the regent of Rathakos, a mountainous border region in the present-day Qamis campaign. He's governing while a Grand Council deliberates on finding a prince. Rathakos is dealing with a lot of internal unrest -- old dragon cults in the mountains, plus the Southern Reaches that were annexed from Edrad during the war and still have sympathizers.
Wait, isn't Rathakos like... an island city-state? Like the Exarch place with the canals? Pesaro or whatever?

Close, but you're mixing two different eras. The island city-state you're thinking of is Qaringil from the Qaringil prequel campaign -- set about 200 years earlier. Giovanni Pesaro is the Exarch there, and yeah, it's criss-crossed with canals. Rathakos is on the mainland, in the mountains, and that's the present-day setting. Easy to conflate when there's this much history.
So the political map is just completely different depending on when you're playing?

Yep. In the Qaringil prequel, the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r and Rathakos doesn't even exist as a nation yet. Fast-forward 200 years to present-day Qamis and you've got a totally redrawn map after the thirty-year Ryraxian War. Rathakos and Acrokos are new princedoms that carved out of the old Red Mountain Republic. Same world, radically different politics. Helps if you focus on one era at a time.
yo, what's the deal with Qaringil being an 'incomprehensible republic'? sounds chaotic lol

Ha, it does sound chaotic! Qaringil is an island city-state that runs on a system of Seven Great Guilds -- Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. Five major families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli) control four of those seven guilds and have stakes in many others. On top of all that is an Exarch -- currently Giovanni Pesaro, who's held the position for 19 years and is supposedly on his deathbed. What we know is Pesaro is aligned with the Peixota and Manos families, which presumably gives him political backing. As for how he's survived 19 years while reportedly on his deathbed, the notes don't explain that either. It's an interesting detail that's left as a blank space for the DM to develop.
Alright, so I missed last session. What's the deal with the Peixota family? DM kept going on about them and I was half asleep.

Peixota are one of the Five Families in Qaringil -- that's the island city-state in the central sea. They control the Shipping Guild and have their fingers in mercantile and judicial stuff too. Their sigil is a dolphin, which makes sense for a shipping family.
OK so this is actually established in your setting notes, not the DM just winging it and making up random noble families?

Yeah, it's in the campaign material. Qaringil is built on five major families -- Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, Vitelli -- and the notes spell out who leads which guild and what their sigils are. They're real enough that they've got competing interests and shifting alliances. Not random filler.
Fair enough. So shipping is their main power base then?

Shipping is their primary leverage. Controlling the Shipping Guild in a city that depends on fishing and imports for food means you've got serious economic clout. They also have connections in mercantile and judicial, so they're not just one-note -- typical for an old money family that's been around long enough to build networks across multiple interests.

Okay so I'm reading the Qamis setting notes and there's this whole technical section about a moon called Primus that's used for navigation. It says geostationary? I'm trying to figure out if this is actually clever worldbuilding or if I'm missing something.

It's genuinely clever. Primus is a tiny moon -- 64 km radius, about a third the size of Earth's Moon -- in a geostationary orbit anchored over the equator. Because it's magically held in place and its orbital plane aligns with Qaia's equatorial plane, it appears motionless in the sky from the near hemisphere. It never rises, never sets.

That fixed, visible reference point is what makes it useful for navigation. A navigator can measure how high Primus appears above the horizon, and that tells them their longitude without needing a chronometer. Earth's geostationary satellites do the same thing, but they're way too faint to see with the naked eye.
Oh, so Qaia's navigators solved the longitude problem earlier than Earth did, basically? That's a significant technological advantage.

Exactly. On Earth, finding longitude at sea required either a very accurate chronometer (expensive, hard to build) or complex lunar-distance calculations using the Moon against star charts. Qaian navigators just need a simple angle-measuring tool and a table of pre-calculated values. It's a much more accessible solution.

There's one catch though: Primus is only visible from the hemisphere it hangs over. Navigators on the far side of Qaia can't use it -- they're stuck with traditional methods like dead reckoning and stellar observation. So the navigational advantage is hemispheric, not global.
That's a really interesting asymmetry. Does that play into the campaign setting at all, like geopolitical divisions or trade routes?

The notes don't explicitly connect Primus navigation to the geopolitics, but the asymmetry is definitely a worldbuilding detail worth considering. You have one hemisphere with a major navigational advantage -- potentially easier ocean travel, better maritime trade networks, more precise mapmaking. The other hemisphere has to rely on older methods. That could create some interesting divergence in how maritime cultures develop, or where the major naval powers emerge.
hey! so I'm building a character from Rathakos and I'm confused about the bonus language thing. How does that work?

Oh nice, a Rathakos character! So the language rule is pretty straightforward: everyone gets one bonus language on top of your two starting ones. Your starting two are the capital dialect (that's Artklan, the prestige version from Ath'r) plus one regional dialect of your choice. Then for the bonus, the DM just wants to know how you learned it -- like, there has to be a story reason, not just "I picked it from a list."

Then when Edran forces withdrew, the opposition faction purged the pro-Edran side and eventually you get Rathakos and Acrokos as independent nations. If your character grew up in that period, you'd have lived through *all of that* -- occupation, the Republic, the purge, the independence. That's huge.
right?? so like maybe they grew up speaking Edran because of the occupation, and then after the purge they had to learn Artklan to fit in with the new regime?

YES. That's perfect. You'd have Edran as a regional dialect (makes sense for someone from the Southern Reaches or that era), Artklan as your capital dialect, and then maybe Qis as your bonus language if you had contact with Qis'mos traders or scholars. Or you could even lean into the dragon cult angle -- there's old dragon cults still active in mountain communities, so maybe you picked up some Old Draconic from that? Your backstory would basically write itself.
ohhh I like the Old Draconic angle a lot actually. That fits the whole vibe of Rathakos still having this wild frontier thing. Okay I think I've got it!

Hell yeah! That's a killer combo -- someone shaped by occupation, independence, and mountain traditions all at once. And Old Draconic from dragon cult stuff is so much cooler than just picking a language off a list. You're gonna have an awesome character.

so my drow just got to Qaringil and I'm trying to figure out if the Gondolier Guild would even let me join, or what they actually care about besides boats. The notes say they run maintenance on the bridges too but like... that's it?

That's actually all the notes specify. The Gondolier Guild maintains and controls the bridges (the two enormous wizard-made bridges that cross the central sea), and obviously runs the gondolas around the city. But as for what else they do -- guild structure, economic interests beyond transport, how they actually wield political power in Qaringil -- that's blank space. The notes do flag one interesting detail though: they've never been led by one of the major families, unlike the Shipping, Judicial, Banking, Silk, and Mercantile guilds. That suggests they might be more independent or follow different rules.
Huh. So they're not captured by the Five Families. Do the notes say anything about whether they actually have money or influence, or are they like... just a workers guild?

Not covered. The notes don't describe their economic standing, their internal hierarchy, or how much political weight they carry in Qaringil's decision-making. What you know is that controlling the bridges is probably important -- those are critical infrastructure -- but whether that translates to guild power, wealth, or just responsibility is up to your DM to flesh out. Same question for your drow character's chances of joining: the notes don't say what the guild's membership policy is or whether they're selective.
okay, so basically I should ask my DM. But thinking out loud -- if they maintain the bridges, they probably have engineers, right? Or masons? Are there other guilds handling that?

Good instinct. The notes list seven major guilds (Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, Wool) and mention several minor ones including Adventurer, Alchemist, and Mage. But there's no detail on whether Gondolier owns the bridge maintenance work directly, whether other guilds do skilled trades, or how specialization works between them. That's another blank space your DM would need to fill in. It's the kind of detail that matters a lot for what roles are actually available in the guild, though.
Right. Last question: do the notes mention anything about tensions between Gondolier and the families, or just that they've never been led by one?

Just that they've never been led by one of the Five Families. No detail on whether that's because they actively resist family control, because the families don't care about them, or for some other reason. The Wool guild is in the same boat -- also never led by a major family -- but the notes don't explain that either. Could be a hook for your DM though.
Who is Regent John Dunne? Is he relevant to the Qaringil prequel at all?

Regent John Dunne is a present-day Qamis figure only—he won't appear in the Qaringil prequel, which is set about 200 years earlier. He's the current governor of Rathakos while a Grand Council searches for an actual Prince to rule it. Rathakos itself is a post-war nation, so it didn't exist back in the prequel era. In present-day Qamis, he's dealing with serious unrest: the Southern Reaches (taken from Edrad) might still have Edran sympathies, and mountain communities are clinging to old dragon cults.
Got it. So in the prequel, Rathakos hasn't been established yet at all?

Right. Rathakos and Acrokos only came into being after the Ryraxian War, carved out from what used to be the Red Mountain Republic. In the Qaringil prequel era, the Red Mountains are just held by various non-state peoples and claimed but not really administered by the Holy Qis Empire. Totally different political map.

Hey, I'm working on a separate campaign and want to borrow some inspiration from Qamis. How has Edrad changed between the Qaringil era and your present day? They seem to be dominant in both but I'm curious about the specifics.

Good question. Edrad's *position* as the dominant nation is basically constant across both eras, but the path it took to get there diverged pretty hard. In Qaringil, about two centuries ago, Edrad was already politically and economically dominant, ruled by the half-elven Keyberos dynasty from Jia. At that point they were actively expanding royal control, trying to conquer territories like Qeth to their northeast. Fast forward to the present day in Qamis, and Edrad is *still* the dominant power, still ruled by the Keyberos dynasty from Jia—but they've been through the wringer. The Ryraxian War lasted thirty years, disrupted everything, and the dynasty actually lost the throne for a while. Now they're restored, but their borders have been pulled back to their 'historical lines,' which is honestly a step backward from where they'd pushed to.
Wait, they lost the throne? What happened there? And this Ryraxos character—he was the dragon overlord, right?

Exactly. So during the Ryraxian War, the Keyberos dynasty got deposed by the Revolutionary Council first. The Council was basically a radical government that took over. But that didn't last long either—Ryraxos, a bronze dragon, stepped in and took control. Here's the wild part: Ryraxos actually *saved* Edrad from certain defeat in the war initially. He won battles they were losing. Then he... just kept fighting. For about two and a half decades, he waged aggressive war across the entire continent as a dragon overlord. Finally got defeated, accepted exile to the second moon, and that's when the Keyberos dynasty got restored to power. So the throne went: Keyberos → Revolutionary Council → Ryraxos → Keyberos again.
The dynasty got restored after a dragon overlord. That's got to come with some... complications. Are they actually in control, or are they struggling?

Both, honestly. They're back in power and they're the legitimate authority on paper. But the notes are pretty clear that restoring the *form* of the old government was way easier than actually *enforcing* thirty years of reversals. The high kings of Artkos, their neighbors, see the new Artklan League as a pale shadow of what it used to be. Everywhere, nominal subjects are clinging to a tenuous quasi-independence. And Forthyr, which had been ignoring the southwest for decades, now finds it impossible to ignore. It's a power vacuum disguised as a restoration—they've got the throne but not the control they had before.
That's actually a really interesting setup. What about Edrad's territory specifically? Did they lose ground during the war, or did they gain it?

They lost ground. The notes specifically say their borders got 'driven back to their historical lines.' In Qaringil, Edrad didn't control Qeth and was actively trying to conquer it—that was a goal of their foreign policy. By the present day, we know Edrad controls cities like Etops and Qeth, so they eventually got some of what they wanted. But it sounds like during the war years, especially under Ryraxian control or the Revolutionary Council, they lost territory, and the post-war settlement restored them to some earlier baseline. So it's a partial recovery—they got territory back, but maybe not everything they'd gained in the two centuries between Qaringil and the war.
This is great. One last thing: from a campaign perspective, what would you say is the most interesting angle for a DM stealing from Edrad's story? Like, what's the *hook*?

A few threads stand out. First, there's the legitimacy problem: the Keyberos dynasty is back, but after thirty years of someone else ruling, how does that actually work? What does restoring a dynasty *really* mean when everyone underneath has gotten used to other power structures? Second, the drow—they were recruited as allies by Artkos during the war and they're *still there*, in scattered enclaves, including occupying Qama, the holy city of Bahamut. That's a massive religious and political tension point. Third, the whole "borders driven back to historical lines" is potentially unstable. A century ago Edrad was actively conquering. Do they want to try again? Is that even possible? Any of those could fuel a whole campaign—internal instability, foreign occupation of sacred sites, or an expansionist recovery project.
hi! so for my character I'm thinking somewhere Primus isn't visible. what are the actual visibility limits? and is Old Draconic a thing because of all those Q place names?

Good questions. Primus is geostationary over the near hemisphere—from the equator you can see it out to roughly ±81 degrees longitude from the prime meridian. That range narrows the farther north or south you go (at 60 degrees latitude, only about ±72 degrees). The entire far side never sees it. As for the Q names: yes, Q is pronounced 'Kh', from Old Draconic, which was the language of scholarship and treaties in the old dragon era. You see that 'q without u' pattern everywhere in place names—Qama, Qis, Qeth, and so on. It's the prestige scholarly language.
cool! so if I place my character in Qis'mos or around Qama, that's deep Old Draconic country then? What's the actual vibe there right now, like culturally?

Qis'mos is the heartland—incredibly rich agricultural region, fought over heavily during the war. The Church of Bahamut dominates spiritually, centered on Qama, the holy city. Here's where it gets interesting for backstory: Qama was occupied by drow in the late Ryraxian War, and they installed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. They didn't trash the place, but it created a religious schism. There's a rival claimant, Qachu II, still holding court in Qis with all the traditional relics of office. So if your character is from Qama or that region, you've got real political and religious tension to play with.
oh that's perfect actually. if my character grew up in Qama during the drow occupation, would they recognize both high priests as legitimate, or is it more complicated than that?

The notes don't specify exactly how people on the ground navigate that schism—that's one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. But the setup is definitely there: you've got an imposed authority (Qe'lth IV and the drow) and a traditional claimant (Qachu II with the historical relics and legitimacy). How your character relates to that—whether they see the drow-backed priest as illegitimate, or complicated, or pragmatic—is something you could workshop with the DM as part of your backstory. It's good ground for personal conflict.
So I'm working on an Edran noble and the setting notes mention Lock. What's the deal with that place?

Lock is a kingdom to the northeast, and it's got a burgeoning industrial presence -- so economically it's growing faster than a lot of the continent. The country as a whole is a kingdom, but here's the interesting part: the city of Lock itself and a couple other major cities are "free cities." That means they operate with some autonomy from the crown.
Free cities? Like what, they do their own thing? That seems weird for a kingdom.

Yeah, it's built on historical precedent. In the Qaringil era -- about two hundred years back -- Lock was one of several river cities that had ancient privileges, and they were locked in what the notes call a "cold conflict" with the Holy Qis Empire over their perogatives. Fast forward to now, and that independence streak persists. The notes don't spell out exactly how much autonomy these free cities have or how that relationship works day-to-day in the present, but the core idea is they've managed to keep enough power that they're not under full royal authority.
Okay. So as an Edran noble, why should I care? Does Edrad have issues with Lock, or...

Good question. The notes don't actually spell out Edrad's specific relationship with Lock in the present day -- that's a blank space your DM hasn't filled in yet. But here's what's worth thinking about: Lock's got growing industrial power, and in a post-war world where everyone's repositioning themselves, that matters. Edrad's trying to maintain its position as the most politically and economically dominant nation, so a rising industrial kingdom on your borders is worth paying attention to. Whether that's trade, competition, alliance opportunities, or something else -- that's probably worth asking your DM about or building into your character's history.
So is Qaringil just Venice? Like, I'm prepping for a campaign and I don't want to learn an entire world that's just "Italian city-state but fantasy."

It definitely has Venice vibes -- canals, merchant fleets, guild-based governance, thalassocracy. But it's not a reskin. The economy is actually pretty specific: it's a city-state of about 100,000 people that survives *primarily on fishing and importation*, not trade in the Venice sense. That's a real constraint. The elites are commercial rather than landowners -- they made their wealth from shipping and commerce, not feudal holdings. There are seven major guilds (Shipping, Silk, Wool, Mercantile, Judicial, Banking, Gondolier) with internal politics, and five families control most of the power. And the city was originally built by exiles and adventurers from Qismos, which gave it a specific cultural flavor.

Okay, that's more detailed than I expected. But does the DM actually flesh out *how* the economy works? Like, where do they import from, what's the fish trade look like, is there actual friction between the guilds or is it just window dressing?

Good question, and I'll be honest: those are blank spaces. The notes tell you the structure (fishing + importation, guild-based, five families, specific guild sigils and leadership) but don't spell out the specific trade routes, which regions supply what, or whether guild conflicts are active or historical. That's exactly the kind of thing a DM would develop based on the campaign that actually happens -- or that players could fill in with backstory. You get enough detail to run it, but not a full economic history.
Huh, okay. At least that's honest. So if I'm the tech lead asking for an executive summary before committing to this world, what's the actual hook? Why should I care about Qaringil specifically?

Two things. First: it's an island city-state in a civil-war prequel (about 200 years before the main campaign), so you're watching power consolidate in real time. The current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, has run the city for 19 years and is supposedly dying soon -- so the succession is actually in play. That's immediate, organic conflict. Second: you've got five families controlling the major guilds, and there are underground criminal organizations (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses) competing for power. So if you're a player, your faction choices actually matter. It's small enough that you see the consequences.
Fair. That actually sounds less like window dressing than I thought. What's the vibe for the party, though -- are we expecting intrigue, or is it more swashbuckling merchant-navy stuff?

The notes leave that pretty open. You've got the structural pieces for intrigue (succession crisis, family factions, underground guilds) and the pieces for adventure (armed merchant fleets, fishing industry, frontier-adjacent setting). The DM probably isn't going to force one or the other -- it depends on what the party leans into and which families or guilds they align with.
okay so quartus — isn't that just earth's moon? like, identical?

yep, pretty much. same mass, same radius, same density (3,344 kg/m³, lunar standard), same albedo at 0.12, same 29.5-day period. it's functionally identical to ours. the notes are explicit about it — "a clone of earth's moon at roughly the same distance and mass."
lmao so the dm just... didn't design it? feels lazy

i get that instinct, but it's actually deliberate. quartus is the calendar body — that stable 29.5-day period anchors the month. you need *something* filling that role, and inventing new orbital mechanics just for novelty's sake would be pointless. the dm spent the weirdness budget elsewhere: secundus retrograde and eccentric, tertius iron-enriched at 8.15 days, sextus and septimus tiny and far out. making quartus the boring one makes the whole system coherent.
but secundus and tertius are way more mechanically interesting. couldn't quartus have been weird too?

sure, technically. but then what? you'd have four competing weird moons and no stable way to tell time. having one reliable reference actually lets everything else shine — the complex tidal patterns from three incommensurate oscillators, the alignment eras where all three inner moons syzygy together, the fact that primus is geostationary but stuck to one hemisphere. quartus being "boring" is what makes the system *work* as a system.
okay that actually... makes sense. so it's functional minimalism?

exactly. plus a meta benefit: anyone reading already has a mental model for quartus. that frees up brain cycles to understand the weird stuff instead of learning new orbital mechanics for something that doesn't need to be exotic.
yo i missed last session, what's the deal with everyone talking about Dragon Emperors?

So there used to be a Draconic empire way back, and while it wasn't centered in Qis'mos, that's where the institutions from it lasted longest after the dragons departed. There's a religious sect in Forthyr that wants the Dragon Emperors to come back, and the Dragoncaller dynasty that rules there actually gives them tacit support.
wait, the Dragoncallers support people who want dragons back? don't they literally descend from dragons?

Yeah, the Dragoncaller dynasty used to be dragonborn, but the bloodlines ran thin -- now they're just sorcerer-kings, not actual dragons anymore. As for why they'd back a sect calling for the return of Dragon Emperors, the campaign notes don't really spell out their motivation, but it does fit with Forthyr being described as 'somewhat backwards' compared to the rest of the continent.
YO so I bailed on last session -- what's the deal with languages in this world? There's no elvish or dwarvish?

Correct! Qamis has no racial languages at all -- no elvish, dwarvish, nothing. The languages here are all national: Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, Islas. You speak whatever language your nation speaks, regardless of race.
Okay wait that's actually such a cool design choice. So a half-elf from Edrad just... speaks Edran like everyone else?

Exactly! For character creation you pick the capital dialect -- the prestige version spoken in the seat of power -- and one regional dialect. Plus one bonus language, but you have to tell the DM how you learned it. But yeah, a drow in Qama, a human, a dwarf, all speaking the same language because that's where they're from.

That actually reframes everything. It means culture and identity are about nationality and displacement, not bloodline. The Edran nobility are half-elf but that's a completely separate thing from language.

Right! Edran nobility has to be between one quarter and three quarters elf -- that's a political bloodline requirement. But linguistically they're just Edran speakers like everyone else in that nation. It makes the setting's history way richer: language is about where conquest and war shaped you, not about what species you're born as.
so wait, what's the difference between the Artklan League and the Artklan Empire? i missed last session and i'm trying to catch up

The Artklan Empire is what it used to be -- it ruled over about forty percent of the continent and had serious influence over Qis'mos too. But that was before the thirty-year Ryraxian War tore everything up.

Now it's the Artklan League, and... well, it's not the same thing. The high kings of Artkos see it as a pale shadow of what they had. The territories that used to be firmly under imperial control are now clinging to tenuous quasi-independence. They're nominally part of the League, but it's fragile.
okay but like, what happened during the war? why is everything so fragmented now?

The Ryraxian War lasted thirty years and basically rewrote the political map. When it started, the bookmakers thought it'd be over in sixty days -- they were very wrong. By the time it ended, Ryraxos (a bronze dragon who'd become the dominant military power) got exiled to the second moon, and the Keyberos dynasty was restored to the throne in Edrad.

But here's the thing: reversing thirty years of change with an edict is easy. Actually enforcing it is hard. The territories that make up the League now -- they got pulled in different directions during the war, they experienced different occupations and rulers. Getting them to just... accept Artkos's dominance again? That's proving really difficult. So yeah, the League exists on paper, but it's weak and everybody's basically trying to stay independent if they can get away with it.
which territories are part of it, just so i have the geography straight

The core is Artkos itself -- governed directly by High King Ezevir Arth. It's split into pieces though. West Artkos is the historic heartland in the northwest, but Artkos also has exclaves, including a central one with the capital city Ath'r. And then there's Kard down south, which is a major financial and shipping center -- that one's particularly fragile because a lot of the population is Edran-speaking and actually *welcomed* the Edran invasion twenty-eight years ago.

As for the other League members... the notes don't spell out the full roster, but the region's definitely fractious. There are also places like Rathakos and Acrokos in the mountains that were formed out of the old Red Mountain Republic after the war ended. It's all pretty unstable right now.
so I'm working on my gondolier character and I'm reading about this Primus navigation thing -- why is the precision best right at the equator and the prime meridian? doesn't that seem weird?

It's actually elegant geometry. When Primus is directly overhead (which is only at the equator, prime meridian), a tiny change in its elevation angle -- even half a degree -- translates to a huge change in your longitude. But when Primus is low on the horizon near the edge of visibility, you have to move dozens of kilometers to shift the angle by half a degree. It's the same measurement problem Earth navigators have with celestial bodies.
Hm, ok, but like -- in Forgotten Realms you can just, I dunno, use a sextant and the stars and whatever and get good precision anywhere? Why is Qamis different?

Forgotten Realms doesn't have a geostationary moon like Primus. Primus never moves in the sky -- it's locked above one point. That's what makes it so powerful for navigation: you measure its height to find your distance from that fixed point. But that power only works when the geometry is steep. Near the equator, Primus swings high overhead as you move east or west, so elevation changes fast. Near your Qaringil latitude, Primus hangs lower, so you have to move farther to see a noticeable change in height.
wait so like -- if I sail south from Qaringil toward the equator, my navigation gets *better*? that's the opposite of how spellcasters in FR talk about going into different regions

Yeah, exactly. In Qamis, sailing south toward the equator is actually a gift to any navigator using Primus. The math works out that you go from roughly 6-7 km precision (good sextant work from Qaringil's latitude) to maybe 1-2 km right on the equator, because the elevation angle of Primus swings through a wider range for the same change in longitude. It's pure geometry: cos λ = primsine(e) / cos φ. That cosine term in the denominator gets bigger as you move away from the equator, which means you need more precision to solve for the same longitude difference.
ok that math checks out but like -- I feel like in Waterdeep or Neverwinter they don't talk about zones of precision like this. seems like they just... have navigation? is this just a Qaia thing?

It's a Qaia-specific consequence of how the moon system works here. Primus is geostationary and dark (only about 1/18th as bright as a full moon), and it exists nowhere else. If Forgotten Realms had a stationary moon, navigators there would deal with the exact same geometry problem: best precision at the equator, worse toward the poles. It's not exotic -- it's just math. Qaian navigators figured this out and built tables for it.

okay so my friend won't stop talking about this Qaringil campaign and I'm thinking of joining. They mentioned something about the Five Families? I'm not really a tabletop person but I read a lot of fantasy stuff, so I'm trying to understand the political scene.

Qaringil is a city-state on an island in the middle of a sea, and the Five Families are basically the old money that runs everything. They control most of the major guilds and have their fingers in a lot of pies. Each family has a sigil and a specialty, but they all try to stay involved in power wherever possible.

Here's the quick version: Peixota (Dolphin) runs shipping, Manos (Hand) runs the courts/judicial stuff, Qanto (Dove) used to run trade but recently lost that power, Volmer (Crow) controls banking, and Vitelli (Moth) runs the silk trade. They're all mostly human with some elf ancestry.

The city is actually run by an elected Exarch -- right now that's Giovanni Pesaro, who's been in power for 19 years and is basically on his deathbed. Nobody knows which family will back the next one, which makes things... tense.
okay that's wild. so if I wanted to play someone from one of these families, what would that even look like? Like, what would day-to-day life be like?

Depends which family, but basically you'd grow up surrounded by power politics. Qaringil is a merchant republic, so these families are commercial dynasties, not landowners -- they're fighting over trade routes, shipping contracts, guild seats, that kind of thing.

If you're Peixota, you probably grew up around ships and docks, with your parents talking about which routes their fleets were running. Vitelli? Surrounded by silk merchants, textile networks, probably some cultural pretension. Volmer's got banking intrigue and money. Manos is courts and law and leverage through the judicial system.

The Dove sigil (Qanto) is interesting because they *lost* the Mercantile Guild recently to a minor gnomish family that's more aligned with the Exarch. So a Qanto character right now would be dealing with that family tension -- they still have judicial and banking influence, but they got outmaneuvered on their main power base.
wait, so there's a Sixth family? This gnomish family that took over the Mercantile Guild? Or are they not in the Five?

Not in the Five, no. There's also a notable gnomish family called Tiddwar (Gears sigil) that's definitely rising in power -- they *lead* the Mercantile Guild now. But the Five Families are the old guard. Tiddwar's newer money, more aligned with the current Exarch.

There are lots of other families in Qaringil; there's a whole bunch of minor guilds too. But the Five are the ones with the historical weight and established power bases.

gotcha. So if I played someone from Peixota who wasn't like... the main heir or whatever, what would be realistic? Like could I be a younger kid who actually wants to explore or adventure instead of running shipping routes?

Totally. That's honestly a pretty classic backstory. Second or third kid who didn't get the shipping empire, or maybe you're the right age to inherit but you're sick of it. Peixota's got enough money and guild power that they could absolutely afford to let a younger sibling off the leash to have an "adventuring phase" -- especially if you're bringing back useful connections or information from your travels.

You could even frame it as doing low-key work for the family while you're out adventuring: making contacts in other cities, picking up trade rumors, that kind of thing. Keeps one boot in the family world while you actually get to do stuff.

One thing to keep in mind though -- Qaringil is built on trade and fishing. It's criss-crossed with canals, it's all ships and fortifications and guild halls. So even as someone trying to escape that world, you'd probably be shaped by it. Maybe you navigated by the moons growing up, or you know shipping law inside and out, or you speak the Islas language *plus* Qis because your family dealt with merchants from mainland Qis'mos.
ooh, the languages thing is cool. I didn't know Qaringil had its own dialect. So like, if I grew up there, what would that sound like?

The city originally got built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, so the version of Islas they speak there has more Qis vocabulary mixed in than you'd find elsewhere on the islands. So imagine the baseline island language (Islas) but with Qis words and phrases bleeding through -- especially in contexts related to trade, law, and scholarship.

If you're from a merchant family like Peixota, you'd probably grow up bilingual or trilingual anyway. You'd have the local Qaringil Islas dialect, standard Islas for dealing with other island traders, and probably Qis for dealing with mainland Qis'mos merchants and written contracts and formal guild stuff.

So if you're playing a Peixota kid, you'd naturally have that mix baked in. You might throw in Qis words even when you don't mean to.

Perfect. I think I'm getting a feel for this. One last thing -- if someone *wasn't* from one of the Five Families, could they still have interesting political angles? Like, what if I was from Tiddwar or some other family?

Absolutely. Tiddwar's actually a great angle -- they just took the biggest power move of the decade by pulling the Mercantile Guild seat from Qanto. Playing a younger Tiddwar with that family momentum at your back would be really different from playing old-guard Five Families money. You'd have ambition, recent success, less historical baggage.

Or you could play someone from a completely minor family, or no family at all -- a guild member who worked their way up, or an adventurer from the mainland, or someone involved in the underground guilds (thieves, bandits, cutpurses) if you want that angle. The Five Families are the power structure, but Qaringil's got 100,000 people. Plenty of room for non-Five characters.

But if you want the political intrigue and the built-in connections, the Five Families (or Tiddwar) give you that immediately. Which direction are you leaning?
okay so i keep hearing about the Ryraxian War and honestly i have no idea what actually went down. like, was it really a war, or was it just Ryraxos steamrolling everyone?

Oh it was genuinely a war -- and a LONG one. Bookmakers actually set the over/under on how long it would last at sixty days. Sixty days! It went thirty years.

So here's how it started: Qis'mos and what was left of the Artklan Empire signed a pact to march on Edrad, kick out the Revolutionary Council, and restore the Keyberos dynasty to the throne. That was the plan. But then Ryraxos -- this bronze dragon -- showed up and absolutely turned the whole thing around. Edrad looked like they were about to lose, and he delivered them from defeat. Then he basically just... kept fighting. Waged aggressive war across the continent for about two and a half decades.

The war just ended. Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus -- Qaia's second moon -- and the Keyberos dynasty is back on the throne in Edrad.
wait wait wait. a dragon. was on the side of Edrad? but the Artklan Empire and Qis'mos were fighting for Edrad?? that doesn't make sense

Okay yeah, the phrasing is confusing! Let me untangle it. Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire allied together with the GOAL of marching on Edrad -- they wanted to depose the Revolutionary Council and restore the Keyberos dynasty (who used to rule Edrad). So technically they were fighting FOR the Keyberos restoration, but they were doing it by attacking Edrad's current government.

BUT when they actually started fighting, Ryraxos sided with Edrad and basically defended them long enough that the Keyberos faction won. So by the end, Ryraxos had been instrumental in the Keyberos restoration -- but he and his dragon faction also ended up controlling huge amounts of territory across the continent for decades.

It's a mess of conflicting interests and shifting allegiances. Basically everyone except Artkos and Edrad fought on both sides at different times depending on who was winning.
hold on, so Ryraxos is young right? like i heard he's only like 60? so how is he powerful enough to do all that??

YES okay so this is the wild part -- Ryraxos isn't actually as cosmically powerful as you'd think for a dragon overlord. The notes specifically say he's "relatively young (perhaps sixty)" and "less impressive than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery."

Here's his secret: he got really good at artillery. Like, his rise to power literally began as an *artillery officer*. Dragons make excellent artillery officers because they can help carry the guns around! So he wasn't winning through raw dragon power so much as through military tactics and logistics.

That's honestly kind of brilliant. He scaled up a competent artillery command into continental dominance.
A DRAGON. CARRYING GUNS. that's the most ridiculous thing i've heard all week and i love it

RIGHT?? It's such a practical solution too. Most dragons are just kind of... flying around doing dragon things. But Ryraxos figured out "oh wait, I'm really strong, I can help move heavy artillery" and turned it into a military career. The engineering mindset is undefeated.

So now he's chilling in exile on Secundus (second moon) and the political map is completely reshuffled. The Keyberos dynasty is back in power in Edrad, the Artklan League is trying to reassert itself, and basically nobody is where they started except Edrad got what they wanted. It's kind of a mess politically.
So I'm rolling a Qaringil character and I'm confused about the moons. Is Secundus like the main moon? The Selûne equivalent?

Qaringil's actually in a very different situation from Forgotten Realms. Qaia has five main moons orbiting it, not just one. Secundus is the second one, but there's also Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus. Plus Primus, which is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit so it never moves in the sky. So no single moon dominates -- it's a whole system.
Wait, FIVE moons? That would make the tides insane compared to FR. How does that even work?

It is complicated! Secundus has a tidal effect about 1.5 times stronger than Earth's moon, and Tertius and Quartus add more on top of that.
