The three of them together create a tidal load about 4.4 times what a single moon does on Earth. For a seafaring city like Qaringil, that means complex, powerful tides -- the kind you'd absolutely need to understand for sailing and harbor management.
OK so what does Secundus actually look like? Like is it as bright as the moon we see from Earth?

Not quite. It has a dark iron-rich surface, so even though it's nearly Earth's moon-sized in the sky -- and gets even bigger near periapsis -- it ends up about 1 magnitude dimmer. At full phase, it's roughly 40 percent as bright as Earth's full moon. And since it's retrograde, you'd watch it rise in the west rather than the east, which is probably disorienting if you're used to FR.
Hey, so my friend was telling me about this D&D setting and mentioned Regent John Dunne. Who is he?

John Dunne is the current ruler of Rathakos, a mountainous border state in the Red Mountains. After the Ryraxian War ended, Rathakos was made into a Princedom, but they haven't actually found a Prince yet. So while a Grand Council is deciding on who the permanent ruler should be, John Dunne is running things as Regent.
So he's like a temporary king waiting for the real one to show up? What's his actual story -- like where is he from?
That's a blank space, actually. The setting notes don't cover his background or how he ended up as Regent. That's something the DM hasn't filled in yet.
Okay, but like... is he doing a good job? Is Rathakos stable under him?

Not really. The notes say Rathakos is suffering from internal unrest in a lot of places. There's especially trouble in the Southern Reaches that were annexed from Edrad, and in mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults. But whether that's because John's not a great regent, or just because the situation is inherently messy, or both -- that's not specified. Could be material for the campaign.
So basically he's a holding pattern character with a troubled region and we know nothing about what he actually thinks or wants.

Pretty much, yeah. The notes establish his job title and that the region has problems, but his personality, competence, goals, or any of his actual history -- all open. Depending on the campaign, the DM will fill those in, or your party might discover them through play.
real quick -- what's a thassolocracy and which places in Qamis run that system?

A thassolocracy is a government built on naval power -- ruled by the sea instead of a traditional monarchy or council. In Qamis, the islands of the central sea are the clearest example. They're populated mostly by exiles from both continents, and they operate as thassolocracies ruled by nautical robber barons. Their whole economic model is selling powerful mercenary navies to the mainland powers -- so instead of fighting for territory like Edrad or Artkos, they're mercs-for-hire with fleets.
okay but game-wise, what does that mean for my character? are they hostile to mainland powers, transactional, or what? and how do we actually interact with them at the table?

It's mostly transactional -- they're not at war with the mainland, they're selling military services to whoever pays. So you're more likely to encounter them as mercenary fleets you can hire, neutral trading ports, or factions with their own agendas. A nautical-focused character could easily have roots there or crew connections. The notes don't drill into specific individual island states yet (those are blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in), but the core idea is solid: you're dealing with sailor cultures and merc crews operating on supply-and-demand, not land-based feudalism.
hey, quick question—what's rho in the primsine navigation stuff?

Rho (ρ) is the ratio of Qaia's radius to Primus's orbital distance. Specifically, it's 0.1511. You calculate it as R_Qaia / r_Primus = 6371 / 42160. It's a fixed physical constant of the Qaia–Primus system, and it shows up in the primsine formula because it determines how the geometry of Primus's geostationary orbit relates to the observer's position on Qaia's surface.
okay but like... why does that number matter? Isn't it just math that navigators use? Does it actually change how navigation works?

It's not just math—it's the physics constraint that shapes the entire Primus-navigation system. Because rho is about 0.15, it sets the visibility limit. The maximum angle from the sub-Primus point where Primus is still above the horizon is arccos(ρ), which is roughly 81 degrees. That means you can only use Primus for longitude from within about 81 degrees of the prime meridian. Outside that arc, Primus drops below the horizon no matter what. That's a HARD geographic asymmetry.
wait, so only half the world can actually use Primus navigation? That seems insane for the global power structure.

Right—the near-side hemisphere (within ~81 degrees of the prime meridian) gets perfect geostationary longitude fixes with just angle measurements. The far-side hemisphere? They're stuck with dead reckoning or lunar-distance methods against star catalogues, which is way harder. Whoever controls access to Primus-based navigation tables has a massive trade advantage. And the prime meridian itself is anchored to whatever is at the sub-Primus point—0 degrees on the equator, fixed forever.
so does that mean the Keyberos dynasty's whole dominance is literally built on controlling this geographic advantage? Like, is Edrad powerful because it owns the prime meridian hemisphere?

That's a really sharp question. The notes don't explicitly confirm it, but it's plausible worldbuilding—navigation advantage becomes trade advantage becomes wealth becomes military power. Edrad being coastal with Jia as the cultural capital, and having natural access to perfect longitude measurement, would make it the obvious hub for continental commerce. But whether the Keyberos dynasty's four-hundred-year reign is *fundamentally* rooted in the Primus advantage, or just enhanced by it? That's blank-space territory. You might ask your DM how much of Edran power is meant to flow from navigation.
okay so if my PC is half-Edran nobility, does that mean I'm basically born knowing how to navigate by Primus? Is it like a class privilege that gets taught to nobles?

The notes don't say. It makes logical sense that Edran merchants and nobility would have early access to primsine tables and navigation know-how—but whether it's restricted to the upper class, taught formally, or something everyone knows, isn't specified. That's a character-building detail worth asking your DM about. You could definitely justify your noble PC growing up with navigation training if that fits your character.
last one—could someone on the far side of Qaia somehow work around the limit? Like, is it actually impossible or just really hard?

Impossible. It's pure geometry. The visibility boundary is arccos(ρ) ≈ 81.3 degrees from the sub-Primus point—that's a hard physical limit. Once Primus falls below the horizon, it's gone. No technology, no angle measurement, no workaround changes that. The far side never sees Primus, period. It's baked into the moon system itself.
so who's this Ryraxos character? I keep seeing his name everywhere in the setting notes

Ryraxos is a bronze dragon who basically ran the continent for thirty years. The Ryraxian War started when Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire teamed up to restore the Keyberos dynasty to the throne in Edrad—bookmakers gave it sixty days, tops. Ryraxos saved Edrad from what looked like certain defeat, became incredibly powerful, and then spent about two and a half decades waging aggressive war across Qamis. When he finally lost, he accepted exile to Secundus—the second moon—so he's out of the picture for the campaign.
okay, but how did a dragon just... become a warlord? That seems like a big leap

Right, so the notes are pretty specific about this: his rise started with him being an artillery officer. And it turns out dragons are really good at that job because they can physically carry the guns around. That's kind of the whole thing—he had competence in a valuable role, leveraged it into power, and snowballed from there.
wait, dragons carrying artillery. that's actually hilarious. so is he like, super powerful as dragons go?

Not really, weirdly enough. The notes describe him as bronze and relatively young—maybe sixty years old. Formidable, sure, but apparently less impressive than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery. So he wasn't dominating through raw dragon power; it was more about being in the right place, having useful skills, and playing the political game well during a really chaotic war.
hiya, language question -- what are we working with in Qamis?

No racial languages -- everyone speaks from the same national pool. Five main ones: Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, and Islas. Plus Old Draconic (aka Old Qis) for scholarly stuff and treaties.

Character-wise you pick one national language in the capital prestige dialect plus one regional variant, and get a bonus language to justify however you want. Fair warning: the regional variants are really different -- capital speakers can't understand speakers from their own language's other regions.
Interesting. So no language families or branches -- just five independent nationals?
Exactly. The notes don't drill into linguistic families or how they relate to each other. Just the five nationals and Old Draconic as the shared academic language. Everything else is a blank.
Makes sense. Quick follow-up: beyond those six, are there any niche languages? Trader argots, sacred languages, anything?

Not mentioned. Might exist in the setting, but the DM hasn't documented them yet. That said, you could build one into your character's background -- the bonus language slot is there for exactly that kind of thing.
Got it. Last question: are the islands linguistically their own thing, or do they use the continent languages?

Islas is the main island language across the central sea city-states. In Qaringil, an island city that's the setting for the prequel campaign, Islas has picked up heavier Qis vocabulary because it was originally settled by adventurers from Qismos.

But the broader picture of how island languages relate to continent languages, or how standardized or varied Islas is by region -- that's not in the notes. Blank space the DM can fill in.
so the drow control Qama? how'd they end up holding a major holy site like that?

The drow were recruited as military allies by Artkos during the Ryraxian War -- a desperation move when things were looking bad. They occupied Qama during the late phases of the conflict and have stayed put. The city itself is intact, unspoiled, but the occupation created a major religious schism. The drow installed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, while the broader Church recognizes Qachu II as the legitimate high priest. Qachu's got institutional backing but is operating out of Qis without access to the actual relics and symbols of office -- those are in Qama under drow control. So from a faction perspective you've got military control and formal religious authority on one side, institutional legitimacy on the other. As for whether it's permanent, the notes don't explicitly confirm it, but given the drow are now established in multiple surface enclaves with Qama as the most prominent one, they seem to be staying.
wait, so the drow just appointed a new high priest unilaterally? what about the actual Church hierarchy?

Yeah, they did, and the Church hierarchy didn't recognize it -- that's the schism. You've got Qe'lth IV with military backing and control of the seat of power in Qama, and Qachu II with institutional legitimacy but operating out of Qis without the relics or symbols of office. It's a real split rather than just a puppet installation. De facto control versus de jure authority -- the drow own the ground but not the institutional legitimacy.

I get the primsine formula and how navigation works with Primus, but looking at the political map... there's like a dozen nations crammed in here. Artkos, Edrad, Qis'mos, Rathakos, Acrokos, Lock, Forthyr, the islands... how is this supposed to make sense? Isn't it just going to be confusing?

It's actually messier than confusing right now, which might be more helpful than you think. The Ryraxian War just ended thirty years later than anyone expected, and the Keyberos dynasty was just restored in Edrad. Borders got redrawn, occupation forces pulled back, but thirty years of fighting doesn't just un-happen. Nations are trying to restore themselves or assert new independence, and it's not clean. That's by design. The good news: you're starting in Rathakos, a border state in the Red Mountains that's actually a great vantage point. It borders Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, and several other regions, so you'll naturally learn the landscape by dealing with its immediate neighbors. And honestly, the DM has also left some blank spaces on the map that haven't been filled in yet -- the setting isn't fully locked down, so you're not supposed to memorize a detailed political atlas. Focus on your region and the adjacent powers, and the rest will come into focus as the campaign develops.
So Rathakos is where I anchor. What's its deal -- is it stable enough to be a home base?

Stable is probably too generous. Rathakos is trying to hang on to tenuous independence after the war -- it's a Princedom now but hasn't found a Prince yet, so it's being run by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council figures out who should rule. There's internal unrest in some places, especially in the Southern Reaches annexed from Edrad and in mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. But here's the good part: the Red Mountains are one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions on Qamis, so Rathakos has a strong tradition of frontiersmanship and adventuring. Unstable borders and unexplored mountains beat "everything is settled." You'll have no trouble finding trouble.
hey, missed last session. someone mentioned a bahamut schism and i heard families are involved? what'd i miss
Which campaign are you in? The five families and Exarch politics are from Qaringil (the prequel), but the Bahamut schism is present-day Qamis - different eras.
we're present-day. so families don't matter for the schism?

Not according to what's in the notes. The schism is between two high priests: Qe'lth IV, who was installed in Qama by the drow during the war and has the relics and the holiest seat, versus Qachu II, who operates from Qis and represents the traditional lineage but without institutional backing.
why'd the drow install their own priest? are they like, still occupying qama?

The notes say the drow occupied Qama during the late Ryraxian War and appointed Qe'lth IV, but don't detail whether they still have a garrison there or what their current relationship is. The fact that Qe'lth is described as drow-backed suggests drow influence is real though - that might be a blank space your DM is developing.
interesting. from a religious perspective, who actually has legitimacy? like theologically speaking

The notes don't go into Church of Bahamut doctrine, unfortunately. You've got institutional legitimacy on one side (Qe'lth has the role, the city, the relics) and doctrinal legitimacy on the other (Qachu has the lineage claim). The real answer to who's legitimate is probably something your DM will explore as the campaign develops.

okay so primus is geostationary, stuck over 0° E... but i'm not seeing how that avoids needing a chronometer. on earth, the moon's position is also fixed if you're stationary — you still need the time to know where it 'should' be. what's the fundamental difference?

the difference is the moon moves across the sky and primus doesn't. on earth, the moon has an orbital period of ~29 days, so at any given moment its position depends on the time. to extract longitude, you need to know what time it is to know where the moon should be. primus has an orbital period of exactly 1 sidereal day — same as qaia's rotation — so it's always above the same spot. it never rises, never sets, never moves. measuring its elevation is measuring a pure geometric relationship to a fixed landmark.

got it — so you're saying the sub-primus point is at 0° E, 0° N, and my elevation to it tells me my angular distance from it. then with latitude from the pole star, the spherical law of cosines gives me longitude. but that's what primsine does, right? the formula cos(λ) = primsine(e) / cos(φ) — primsine is just encoding that angular distance?

exactly. primsine(e) is the angular distance from you to the sub-primus point, expressed so you can directly plug it into the cosine formula. because primus is fixed, measuring its elevation tells you that distance with no time computation involved — pure geometry. that's why there's no chronometer in the chain: the sub-primus point is a fixed known location, not a moving target that requires time-tracking.
hey, so I'm reading through the Qaia navigation docs and I keep seeing this 'primsine' function mentioned. Is that actually based on something real or is it just worldbuilding flavor?

Great question. It's based on something real -- the haversine. Both functions exist for basically the same reason: to turn a complex navigation problem into a table lookup.

Haversine is used for finding great-circle distances on Earth using latitude and longitude. Primsine does the same job for Qaia, but instead of converting lat/lon to distance, it converts Primus's elevation angle into your longitude.

The elegance is that you measure one thing (angle), plug it into a pre-printed table, and out comes a number you can use to solve for position. No complex trigonometry at sea.
Okay, that makes sense. So haversine is the real Earth version, and primsine is the Qaia version. But why does that work? What's the actual math connection?

They both encode a physical ratio into a single function.

Haversine bundles Earth's radius into its definition, so when you measure angles and plug them in, you get distances. Primsine does the same thing with Qaia's radius and Primus's orbital radius -- the ratio ρ = 6371 / 42160 = 0.1511. Once that ratio is defined, the function is permanent. It never changes, so you print it once in the almanac.

The formula for primsine is: ρ cos²e + sin(e) × sqrt(1 − ρ² cos²e). It looks ugly, but the point is that ρ is a constant, so the whole thing reduces to "given elevation e, look it up."
That's actually brilliant design. So the DM basically built a working navigation system that would actually function if someone wanted to use it for real?

Exactly. The NAVIGATION.md docs walk through a worked example: observer at 30° N latitude, Primus measured at 42.2° elevation, and it calculates out to 30° East longitude. The math is tight.

What makes it elegant from a worldbuilding perspective is that it solves a real problem -- how do pre-industrial navigators find longitude without a chronometer? -- by using Qaia's specific moon system. Primus is geostationary and visible from one hemisphere, so it becomes a natural prime meridian. The primsine function makes it navigable.
okay so I'm making a gondolier from Qaringil and I keep hearing about Primus -- is that something I'd actually see from home?

Yeah, you'd see it. Primus is geostationary -- it's anchored over a fixed point on the equator at the prime meridian and just stays there forever. From Qaringil you're well within the visibility zone. The rule is Primus is visible to observers within roughly plus-or-minus 80 degrees of the prime meridian at the equator, narrowing toward higher latitudes. Since Qaringil is an island city-state in the central sea, you're definitely in range.
wait, so it literally doesn't move? like it's always in the same spot in the sky from Qaringil?

Right. It never rises, never sets, never drifts. Just hangs there permanently fixed. That's actually why it's so useful for navigation -- gondoliers and merchants learn to use it to figure out longitude. You measure how high Primus is above the horizon and that tells you how far east or west of the prime meridian you are. No chronometer required, which is a huge advantage at sea.
that's actually perfect for a character concept. so what does it actually look like in the sky? is it bright or tiny or what?

It's noticeable but not huge. About 10.4 arcminutes across -- roughly one-third the apparent size of Earth's moon. And it's got a dark iron surface, so it's dimmer than a full moon, around magnitude negative 9.6. You'd definitely spot it if you're looking for it, especially at night, and it'd be a familiar landmark if you grew up sailing around Qaringil. But it's not gonna light up the water the way Quartus does at full -- it's more of a navigation reference point.
hey so i missed last session, where are we starting again?

You're starting in Rathakos, a mountainous region along the Red Mountains that borders Edrad, Qis'mos, and Artkos. The capital is the city of Rath, up in the northern foothills. It's supposed to be a really good home base for adventurers.
ok but like why is it a good spot for adventuring

Couple reasons. First, the Red Mountains themselves are one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions on the continent, so there's definitely work for adventurers. Second, Rathakos as a nation has a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship -- it's kind of part of the culture. And third, things are kind of chaotic there right now, which means opportunities.
chaotic how

The Ryraxian War just ended, and Rathakos is trying to hold onto independence while surrounded by bigger powers. It's being run by a regent right now because they haven't picked a prince yet. Plus there's internal unrest in a lot of places -- especially in the southern regions that used to be controlled by Edrad, and in the mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults. So yeah, unstable.
wait dragon cults? like they worship dragons?

The notes don't say a ton about what those cults actually believe or do, just that they exist in the mountain communities and are part of why things are unsettled. Sounds like something your DM might expand on though.
okay so my friend who runs D&D in this world mentioned the Revolutionary Council and I have no idea what that is. Was that like... a faction or something?

Yeah, the Revolutionary Council was the government of Edrad right before the big thirty-year war. They're described as "squabbling" -- which is not a great look for a governing body. Basically, Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire made a pact to march on Edrad, kick out the Council, and put back the Keyberos dynasty on the throne. That kicked off the whole Ryraxian War.
Wait, who were they though? Like, actual people? And how did they even take power in the first place if the Keyberos dynasty had been ruling for four hundred years?

That's... actually not covered in the campaign notes. That's one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. All we know is that they were in charge of Edrad at some point, and they were dysfunctional enough that two major powers decided "hey, let's invade and restore the old dynasty." But the actual history of who they were, how they got power, what they wanted -- all that's open.
So they just got yeeted and we never found out what happened to them?

Basically, yeah. The notes focus on the war and what happened after -- the Keyberos dynasty back in power, Ryraxos the dragon causing chaos, his exile to the second moon. But what became of the Revolutionary Council members themselves? Whether they were executed, exiled, executed, scattered -- that's blank too. Could be a decent backstory hook if your character had a reason to care about them.

hey, I'm thinking about running Qaringil as a one-shot before we start Qamis. But I'm confused about Edrad — is it still dominant in that era? I remember the Keyberos dynasty was pretty shaky after the wars...

Just to clarify — Qaringil is set about 200 years *before* Qamis, so there's no Ryraxian War yet in that era. The Keyberos dynasty is actually pretty solid in Qaringil. They've been ruling for about two centuries and are actively expanding their control. It's the present-day Qamis where they're dealing with recovery after the thirty-year war with Ryraxos.
Oh right, I got the timeline backwards. So in Qaringil Edrad is expanding. Does that mean they control Qeth?

Nope — Qeth is actually one of Edrad's prime targets in the Qaringil era. The notes specifically say Edrad doesn't control Qeth yet, but conquering it remains a goal of Edran foreign policy. By the time we get to present-day Qamis (200 years later), they do control Qeth, so presumably they succeeded in that conquest at some point in between.
That makes sense. So what else shifts between the two eras for Edrad?

A few things: In Qaringil, the dynasty is in expansion mode — politically and economically dominant, actively pursuing territorial goals. By present-day Qamis, they've been ruling for four centuries and just emerged from the thirty-year war where the dragon Ryraxos temporarily deposed them (there was a Revolutionary Council in between). So in Qamis they're focused on recovery and reasserting control over their territories. Also, the half-elven nobility structure stays the same — Edran noble families have to be between one quarter and three quarters elf in both eras. Jia remains the cultural heart either way.
Got it. So Qaringil is basically Edrad at their peak, before everything falls apart.

Exactly. Qaringil is Edrad in growth mode, before the upheaval. By the time Qamis rolls around, they've had a rough thirty years, and even though the war is over and the Keyberos are back on the throne, the notes mention it's 'easy to sign an edict reversing thirty years of change, but harder to enforce it.' So yeah, Qaringil gives you a very different political vibe — more about ambitious expansion, less about recovery.
hey, so I'm making a character from Rathakos and I'm trying to understand the world better. What's the deal with Secundus? Is it like Selûne or something from Forgotten Realms?

This is a homebrew setting, so the moon system is pretty different from Forgotten Realms. Secundus is Qaia's second moon -- retrograde, which means it rises in the west instead of the east like most of the other moons. It's also got a dark iron surface, so even though it's fairly close and fairly large in the sky, it's noticeably dimmer than you'd expect. One important detail for your Rathakos character: Ryraxos, the bronze dragon who was defeated at the end of the Ryraxian War, is exiled there. He's not coming back.

Wait, dragons can live on moons? Like, is it habitable? I'm trying to figure out if Rathakos characters would have any connection to what goes on up there. Also how does that compare to, like, Oghma's realm or wherever the gods keep exiles in FR?

Good question, but the notes don't actually specify whether Secundus is habitable or what Ryraxos's exile looks like up there -- just that he accepted it and plays no role in the campaign. The DM's left that as a blank space. As for how it compares to Forgotten Realms theology and the divine realms, that's outside the scope of this setting. Qamis has its own religious landscape, but I don't have details on god-specific mechanics like that. For Rathakos flavor, though: Rathakos was formed after the Ryraxian War ended and the Red Mountain Republic dissolved. It's a mountainous border state trying to hang onto independence while figuring out who should actually rule it. The culture there has a strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship.
That's helpful. So like, Rathakos is basically the equivalent of the Sword Coast or something -- frontier adventuring. And the Red Mountain Republic was the government while the war was happening?

Close -- the Red Mountain Republic was specifically the entity that governed the mountain provinces when they were under Edran occupation during the war. When the Edrans withdrew and Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire, the Assembly in that region purged the pro-Edran faction and established two new nations out of its territory: Rathakos and Acrokos. So it wasn't the long-standing government of the area -- it was a wartime administration that didn't survive the peace. Rathakos now is still figuring itself out: it's currently a Princedom without a Prince, governed by a Regent (John Dunne) while they figure out who should rule. There's unrest in the Southern Reaches (the Edran-annexed parts) and in mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. So yeah, frontier and instability -- good ground for adventuring, and plenty of hooks for a character.

so like... what do we actually know about westron's religious setup? i'm trying to understand the bahamut thing but it feels like everyone's arguing about a split in *one* faith. does westron have a completely different theological tradition or what?

okay, fair question, but i have to be honest: westron is almost entirely a blank space. the notes say it's a large continent to the west, that it's exotic, and that there are 'religious differences, with them mostly not being bahamut worshippers.' and then... that's it. the DM hasn't filled in the material yet. so yeah, there probably *is* a different theological tradition over there, but what it actually looks like? no idea.
wait, so you're telling me the entire western continent is just... undefined? that seems like a pretty big gap if religious stuff is supposedly important.

it does feel like a gap, and your skepticism makes sense. but here's the thing: the campaign notes are explicit about what's blank space and what isn't. westron is flagged as unfilled-in, which actually means *you* could help fill it as part of a character backstory if you wanted. that's how the DM structures it.

that said, the bahamut schism is *not* blank space. in qis'mos, it's a live, detailed problem: the drow appointed their own high priest (qe'lth IV) in qama during the war, which split the church. there's an alternate candidate (qachu II) running a counter-church from qis. that's material tension with specific names and locations—not invented, grounded in the actual politics of the post-war settlement.
okay, i'll bite. so the schism is real qis'mos politics, not just theology. is it actually about doctrine, or is it just... drow occupation drama?

the notes don't specify the theological angle. what they *do* say is that the drow (recruited by artkos as allies during the war) are still garrisoned in a dozen enclaves after it's over, including qama. the church was split by that occupation—qe'lth iv was appointed by artkos's military authority, while qachu ii claims the legitimate succession from qis. so structurally it's political (who has authority, how the occupation happened) but whether it's also about doctrine, ritual disputes, or interpretation of bahamut's will? that's unfilled. you'd have to ask the DM or work it out with them as part of your character's faith.
at least that's concrete. so if i'm a cleric from one side or the other, there's actually a live conflict i could be part of?

yeah, exactly. qis'mos is dealing with occupation aftermath, a fractured priesthood, and the schism is right there in the capital religious structure. whether you're loyal to qachu ii's underground church in qis, caught between the two, or even sympathetic to qe'lth iv's position (which the notes don't evaluate), there's real political and religious meat to dig into. westron's unknowns are unknowns, but the schism is playable conflict.
hey! so i'm writing a half-elven edran noble character and jia keeps coming up. what's it actually like as a place?

Jia's the capital of Edrad and the seat of the Keyberos dynasty -- they've ruled for four hundred years (except during the thirty-year Ryraxian War that just ended). It's built on a peninsula jutting into the Central Sea, so there's definitely a maritime element. The notes specifically call it "by far the most culturally influential city in Qamis", which is significant when half-elven nobility from there have shaped the whole region.
right but like... what does culturally influential actually mean? are there famous institutions, great families, specific things jia's known for? what does court look like?

That's where it gets thin. The notes say Jia is culturally central and holds the dynastic seat, but they don't actually detail what that consists of -- the court structure, major noble houses, what institutions define the city, what kind of architecture or art or scholarship it's known for, the economy. It's one of the explicit blank spaces the DM flagged.

What's solid: capital city, peninsula location, Keyberos dynasty rules from there, half-elven nobility must be between one quarter and three quarters elf. Beyond that -- the actual texture of Jian court life, specific families, what makes people culturally defer to it -- that's open territory.
so like, i could propose a family noble house or a cultural thing jia's known for, and run it by the dm?

Yeah, exactly. The blank spaces are blank partly because the DM invites players to fill them in through backstory and character choices. You could establish your family as patrons of something, keepers of a tradition, stewards of an institution -- whatever fits your character concept, as long as you coordinate with the DM so you're not inventing something that conflicts with what's already canon.

You've got the geographic and political bones: peninsula city, dynastic power, maritime position. Everything beyond that is collaborative space.

So I'm building a half-elven Keyberos heir for the Qamis campaign, and I noticed in the moon reference there are these specific dates called out as "closest alignments" -- Feb 13, 2253 and May 24, 2262. What makes those special?

Those are perfect triple-moon alignments. On Feb 13, 2253 at 18h, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all reach full moon simultaneously and are nearly identical in phase -- 100.0%, 100.0%, and 99.8%. May 24, 2262 is the flip side: all three reach new moon at once, within 0.1% of each other. The notes call them "the closest triple-full of the decade" and "the closest triple-new of the decade" respectively. They're rare perfect events.
Okay, but like... do these actually *mean* anything in the setting? Are they just cool sky phenomena, or does Qaian culture treat them as significant?

That's a blank space -- the notes don't specify. The alignment calendar exists and these dates are explicitly marked as the closest events, so the DM clearly thought they were important enough to calculate precisely, but whether they have magical significance, religious meaning, or cultural traditions attached to them isn't documented yet. For a noble backstory though, that's potentially a hook to pitch to your DM.
Right, so -- would it make sense for the Keyberos dynasty to track these? Like, could the dynasty use them for something? Legitimacy claims, prophecies, magical rites, that kind of thing?

The notes don't connect the alignments to dynasty practice specifically, but Edran nobility being half-elven and maintaining 400 years of continuous rule suggests they're invested in long-term patterns and astrological knowledge. The Keyberos just got restored to the throne after the Ryraxian War, so there could easily be coronation rites, prophecies tied to celestial events, or renewal ceremonies your family practices. That's totally in your court to pitch to the DM as part of your character's background.
What about the Church of Bahamut? They're huge in Qis'mos, and we know Bahamut's tied to dragons and ancient Qaian history. Could these alignments be religious calendar markers?

The Church of Bahumut is indeed enormous in Qis'mos and occupies the holy city of Qama, but the moon reference doesn't explicitly connect the alignments to Bahamut worship or ritual calendar. That said, the Church does have institutional memory going back to the dragons' time, so if anyone tracks these cycles for religious purposes, they'd be candidates. Again, worth asking your DM whether Feb 2253 or May 2262 line up with feast days, prophecy cycles, or events your character's family cares about.

Wait -- the present day is post-Ryraxian War, right? So we're living through this alignment calendar. Is May 2262 in our future? Like, is that an event that's about to happen in the campaign timeline?

Yes. The Ryraxian War was thirty years long and just ended, so the present day of Qamis is sometime after that (the exact current year isn't specified in the notes, but it's post-war). The alignment calendar spans 2253-2263. Depending on when exactly the campaign kicks off, May 2262 and the other marked dates could be upcoming events. If your family tracks these celestially -- whether for magic, prophecy, or dynasty ritual -- you've got a ticking clock. That's solid character-hook material to discuss with your DM.
so how did they actually manage to take down Ryraxos? last time we played was before all this went down

The short version: he got defeated and accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon. But here's the thing -- Ryraxos was actually less of a powerhouse than you might expect from a dragon overlord. The notes specifically say he's less impressive than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery.
wait, seriously? so how did he end up running the whole continent for thirty years then

That's the interesting part. Ryraxos is bronze, relatively young -- only about sixty years old. His power came from being an exceptional *military commander*, not from sheer dragon strength. The notes call out that he started his rise as an artillery officer. Dragons make excellent at that job because, well, they can carry the guns. So his thirty-year dominance was more about tactical genius and military organization than being an unstoppable force of nature.
and he just... agreed to go to the moon? that seems weird

According to the notes, yeah -- he accepted exile to Secundus upon his defeat. The notes don't spell out the exact circumstances of how that negotiation happened, so that's still a blank space the DM hasn't filled in. But the important part for the current campaign is that it happened, he's gone, and he plays no role in present-day Qamis.
so the Keyberos dynasty is back in charge now? is it just back to normal?

Technically yes -- Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire made a pact to march on Edrad, depose the Revolutionary Council, and restore the Keyberos dynasty. And the dynasty has been ruling Edrad from the coastal capital of Jia for four hundred years, so restoration happened. But the notes capture something really important: "It is easy to sign an edict reversing thirty years of change, but harder to enforce it." Everything's been *put back* the way it was, but everything's *changed*. The political situation is pretty tangled.
tangled how?

Several ways. The Artklan League -- which is supposed to be united under High King Ezevir Arth from Artkos -- is fracturing. Artkos sees the League as a pale shadow of the old empire, but the nominal subjects (places like Rathakos, Kard, the free cities) are clinging to a tenuous quasi-independence they didn't have before the war. Rathakos especially is a mess: it's a new Princedom with no Prince yet, currently run by Regent John Dunne while a council debates a monarch. There's internal unrest, especially in regions recently annexed from Edrad and in mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. And there's a complication nobody expected: the drow, recruited as allies by Artkos near the end of the war, are still on the surface in scattered enclaves. Most notably, they occupied Qama, the holy city of Bahumat, and they've appointed their own high priest instead of respecting the traditional one. That's split the Church.
okay so Rathakos is where the campaign starts, right? that tracks given all the instability

Yep, exactly. Rathakos sits in the Red Mountains bordering Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, and a couple of other territories -- it's a natural crossroads and a pressure cooker right now. Strong tradition of adventurers and frontiersmanship there too because the Red Mountains are still one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions on the continent. Lots of room for a party to make a name for themselves.
so is the Artklan League just the old empire rebranded, or actually different?

Actually different, though the exact mechanics of how it changed aren't detailed in the notes. The old Artklan Empire ruled about four tenths of the continent and had major influence over Qis'mos. The current Artklan League is noticeably smaller, and the high kings of Artkos -- who are the dominant power -- describe it explicitly as a 'pale shadow' of what they used to have.
but what actually caused the shift? did they lose a war?

The Ryraxian War just ended, which lasted thirty years and tore the continent apart. Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire fought together to overthrow a dragon overlord and restore the Keyberos dynasty to Edrad. But a thirty-year war is brutal -- borders got redrawn, territories changed hands multiple times. The notes don't spell out the exact political settlement, but something clearly shifted from 'empire' to 'league' in the process.
so they're basically powerless now?

Not quite. Artkos directly governs its core territories under High King Ezevir Arth, so they're still a major regional power. But a lot of the land nominally part of the League isn't really under their control anymore. Kard, for example, is a major trading hub, but its governance is described as fragile -- a lot of people there are Edran-speaking and actually welcomed Edrad's invasion during the war. Those aren't the kind of places that will happily return to being firmly under Artklan rule.
ohh so the empire era they actually *had* unified control, but now it's just fragmenting?

Yeah, that seems to be it. The old empire was a real unified thing -- controlled forty percent of the continent. What exists now is more like a core power (Artkos) with territories that nominally belong to the League but are increasingly independent-minded. Thirty years of war, changes in borders, people switching sides -- that tends to break down loyalty to the old hierarchy.
So when exactly does the Qamis campaign take place? Like, is this ancient history, current day, future stuff?

It's basically the present day. The campaign starts right after a massive war just ended -- the Ryraxian War, which lasted thirty years. Before that ended, bookmakers in Kard were betting it would only last sixty days, so... it went way longer than anyone expected. But as of now, it's over.
Thirty years?? That's insane. What was even happening that took that long?

So a bronze dragon named Ryraxos basically turned the war around when Edrad was about to lose, then spent the next two and a half decades just... conquering across the continent aggressively. He's actually not as powerful as you'd think for a dragon overlord -- probably in his sixties, still relatively young for a dragon. But yeah, he dominated the continent for decades until he was finally defeated. After that he accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon, so he's gone from the picture.
Wait, he accepted exile? Just like that? And how does a moon work as exile?

The notes don't elaborate much on those details, honestly -- that's one of those things the DM hasn't fully filled in. But yeah, Secundus is Qaia's second moon, it's actually pretty substantial (about 40% as bright as our Moon), so it's not like banishing him to nowhere. The fact that he's gone means the present-day campaign doesn't have to deal with an actual dragon overlord, which changes everything politically.
Gotcha. So everything just... snapped back to normal after he left? The Keyberos dynasty is back in charge?

On paper, yeah -- the Keyberos dynasty is restored in Edrad, which had ruled for four centuries before the chaos. But that's the thing: the notes actually make a point about this. It's easy to sign an edict reversing thirty years of change, but way harder to actually enforce it. A lot of the other nations don't want to go back to the old power structure, and there are still drow occupying cities they moved into during the war. So everything looks like it snapped back, but... it really hasn't underneath.
Ooh, okay. So there's tension baked in. That makes sense. Is there older history you can go back into, or is this the earliest the world goes?

There actually is! There's a prequel campaign called Qaringil set roughly two hundred years *before* Qamis. It's based in an island city-state with the same name, and the political map is completely different -- the Holy Qis Empire rules from a place called Ath'r, and some of the nations that exist in present-day Qamis don't exist yet. Rathakos and Acrokos, for instance, are brand new nations that came out of the post-Ryraxian war reorganization. So you've got two centuries of history connecting the prequel to the present day.
So this is a whole shared universe across both campaigns? That's cool. Does Qaringil the city still exist in present day?

The notes don't actually say -- they're silent on whether Qaringil the city makes it through those two hundred years or what happens to it. That's probably a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. The campaign itself is a worldbuilding-heavy setting, and there are a bunch of areas marked as intentionally open for players and DMs to fill in with their own backstories, so you'd probably have to ask.

hey! so i was reading through the navigation stuff and i'm trying to wrap my head around how this actually works. in our old games, we had magic compasses and stuff. how do qaian sailors figure out where they are without all that?

It's actually pretty elegant -- and no magic required at all. You've got two basic tools: the pole star for latitude (elevation above the horizon gives you your north-south position), and Primus for longitude. Primus is the geostationary moon, so it hangs in exactly the same spot in the sky forever. You measure its elevation, run it through a calculation, and boom -- you know your longitude. No chronometer, no magical items.

wait, how does a moon that doesn't move help you find longitude? in Forgotten Realms they've got the entire Selune thing and even then navigators need all kinds of tools. you're telling me Qaia just... solves it with geometry?

Yeah, that's the payoff. Primus is geostationary because its orbital period matches Qaia's rotation exactly, AND its orbit sits perfectly in the equatorial plane. So from the near hemisphere, it's always above the same spot -- longitude 0, on the equator, never moving. That fixed point is the key. You measure how high it is above your horizon, and the spherical geometry tells you how far east or west you are from that fixed point. It's similar to how latitude works with the pole star, just sideways instead of vertical.
okay that actually makes sense. so what happens if you're on the far side of the planet? do you just... can't navigate?

Exactly. Primus is permanently below the horizon on the far hemisphere. So yeah, navigators there are stuck with dead reckoning, stellar transits, lunar-distance methods using other moons -- basically all the hard stuff that Earth explorers had to do before the chronometer. It's a real asymmetry in navigational capability. The notes describe it as "a substantially harder problem, exactly analogous to Earth's pre-chronometer longitude difficulty."

huh, so there's an actual limit to what one side of the world can do compared to the other. that's wild compared to stuff like forgotten realms where you can just teleport or scry or whatever. is the calculation itself complicated or is it manageable with tables?

Totally manageable with tables. The DM calls it the "primsine function" -- basically a permanent lookup column because it depends on a fixed physical constant (the ratio of Qaia's radius to Primus's orbital distance). Once you've got primsine(elevation) from a table, you're doing one division and maybe a reverse cosine lookup. Ship captains would carry an almanac with this stuff printed once and use it forever. You can get rough positioning (~100 km) with a simple table, or much better precision if you want more entries.
Hey, I'm prepping a campaign in a different setting but I love your dragon lore. What's the deal with the Dragon Emperors? Are they a faction, a historical thing, or what?

Good question -- it's definitely historical. There was a Draconic empire way back, but the dragons departed at some point. The details are honestly fuzzy. What persisted longest was the institutional legacy in Qis'mos, where remnants of the old empire's government structures hung around longer than anywhere else. The Church of Bahamut has a massive presence there, which tracks with the dragon worship angle.

The kicker is that even 200 years ago (during the Qaringil era), the old Draconic empire was already gone. So we're talking deep history.
So they're not around anymore -- got it. But you mentioned a sect wanting them back? Who's pushing for that, and why would anyone care in the present day?

Yeah, this is where it gets interesting for your purposes. There's a religious movement in Forthyr -- the nation in the northeast -- that's actively calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors. And here's the wild part: it gets tacit support from the Dragoncaller dynasty that rules Forthyr.

The dynasty is named for dragons and used to be dragonborn lineage, but the bloodlines have "run thin" over time. So you've got a ruling class that's literally descended from dragons, governing a nation where a popular sect wants dragons to come back. That tension feels ripe for a campaign.
Huh -- so the Dragoncallers are actively supporting a return? Or is it more like they're not stopping it because it serves their interests?

The notes say "tacit encouragement, if not support" -- so it's ambiguous whether they're actively backing it or just... not suppressing it because it benefits them politically. Either way, there's a weird symbiosis: a ruling class losing its dragon heritage, a religious sect wanting dragons back, and mutual interest in the idea. Whether that means secretly working to restore dragons, or just riding the political energy, isn't spelled out.
This is gold. Do we know anything about what the Dragon Emperors actually were? Like, was it a government, a council, individual rulers? And where did they go?

That's a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet. The notes mention the dragons "departed" but don't say when, why, how organized they were, or where they went. There's one tantalizing line though: "Some say this is where the dragons went" about a big unexplored island to the south with a pirate city called Freeport on its edge and jungle pressing up against it. But that's pure speculation.

For your campaign, this is actually perfect -- you've got religious fervor, a ruling dynasty with a mysterious connection to dragons, institutional memory in Qis'mos, and a whole mystery about what happened to an ancient empire. The DM left room for you to build it into whatever you want.

okay so I'm looking at this language stuff and there's all this talk about 'capital dialect' and 'regional dialect' -- are you seriously telling me I have to care about this? isn't the setting already complicated enough?

Fair pushback. The basic idea is simple though: each national language has major regional variations. So when you pick a language, you get two dialects by default -- the prestige dialect spoken in that nation's capital (and by its ruling class), plus one regional dialect. The rule exists to reflect that someone from the capital of Artkos would actually struggle to understand someone from Rathakos, even though they're both nominally speaking Artklan.
right but like... if they can't understand each other, how does *anything* work? commerce? diplomacy? and more importantly, how am I supposed to roleplay a dialect I've never heard of?

Both good questions, and the notes don't fully answer either one. On the first: it's silent on how mutual intelligibility actually works in practice, or how traders/diplomats handle it. That's a blank space. On the second: the notes don't give you examples of what a Rathakos accent or dialect actually sounds like, so you'd need to either invent it or ask the DM for guidance.

so the DM put in a rule about regional variation but... didn't actually specify which regions have which dialects or what they sound like. that tracks. what about the capital dialects -- is there a list of what the prestige dialects even are?

The notes name five main national languages: Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, and Islas. It's implied each has a capital prestige form -- so standard Edran is spoken in Jia, standard Artklan in Ath'r, etc. But there's no guide to what these sound like or how they differ from each other. For character creation purposes, you just pick one national language and you automatically get the capital dialect plus a regional one, and the DM presumably figures out the mechanics in play.
and if I pick a regional dialect as my main language instead of the capital version, like, what happens?

That's not covered in the notes. The rule assumes you start with capital + one regional, not that you'd swap them. Whether the DM would even allow picking a regional dialect as your main language is a blank space -- you'd have to ask.
What's West Artkos? Is it contested or does Artkos have solid control there?

West Artkos is the historic heart of Artkos -- the original northwestern core of the kingdom. But the capital's now Ath'r, which Artkos conquered about two hundred years ago. Ath'r had been the seat of major Qamisian empires before that, so the shift to it as capital is politically significant. Whether West Artkos still has real power or leverage against the center, or if that's a source of tension -- the notes don't fill that in yet. Could definitely be a hook for your campaign though.
so i keep seeing 'thassolocracy' mentioned in the qamis stuff. what even is that, and does qamis actually have any?

A thassolocracy is a state where power flows from control of the sea -- naval strength and maritime trade basically replace traditional land-based governance. And yeah, Qamis definitely has them. The islands in the central sea are described as thassolocracies populated by exiles from both continents. They're run by nautical robber barons who basically sell their powerful mercenary navies to whoever can pay. It's less 'honorable republic' and more 'whoever controls the shipping controls the politics.'
right, but which islands specifically? is qaringil one of them?

The notes don't name every island, but Qaringil is probably the clearest example. It's on an island in the central sea, criss-crossed with canals, and its armed merchant fleets fan out across the sea. It's governed as a republic -- though described as 'incomprehensible' -- by competing guilds and families. The Shipping Guild controls the flow of goods, the mercenary navy is integral to keeping the trade routes open. So yeah, Qaringil functions as a thassolocracy even if the notes don't use that exact label.
okay so in qaringil, which families actually control the shipping power? like, how does that translate to political muscle?

The Peixota family -- their sigil is a dolphin -- leads the Shipping Guild. That gives them direct control over maritime routes and presumably the mercenary contracts. But it's not that clean, because the other major families have shipping interests too. Volmer (the Banking Guild) and Vitelli (Silk Guild) both dabble in shipping on the side. So Peixota has the most explicit naval power, but the real politics is the tension between families with overlapping stakes in the sea trade. Control of shipping doesn't equal absolute power -- it's one lever among several guild interests.
that's interesting. so compared to something like edrad or artkos, which are built on land control and nobility, qaringil's whole power structure is fundamentally different?

Completely different. Edrad has the Keyberos dynasty -- hereditary half-elf nobility controlling land. Artkos has the High King and territorial expansion. But Qaringil? Power shifts with guild control and merchant family interests. You can't inherit absolute power the way Edran nobles do; you have to maintain guild interests, maintain trade relationships, keep your mercenary navy flush with coin. It's more meritocratic and more cutthroat at the same time. The Exarch (the elected leader) serves at the pleasure of the guilds and families. That's a fundamentally different political machine -- success comes from trade dominance and naval reach, not bloodline or territorial claim.
hey, quick question. my Forthyr character follows the Dragon Emperor revival sect. the DM keeps talking about 'blank spaces' — what's that about?

Good question! Blank spaces are basically parts of the setting the DM hasn't detailed yet — open territory for players to help fill in, often through backstory.

For Forthyr specifically, the notes don't say when the old Dragon Emperors ruled, what they actually were, or what happened to them. Also blank: the exact beliefs and practices of the revival sect itself. Those are open hooks for you to develop.

There are also big unexplored regions — Westron (the continent to the west) is almost entirely undocumented, and the big southern island with Freeport is mostly a mystery. The DM's explicitly left those as blank spaces too.
ok got it. so like, the current Dragoncaller Shanlong — he's descended from the old Dragon Emperors, right? is he dragonborn?

Close, but not quite! Dragoncaller Shanlong VI is a sorcerer king, but he's not dragonborn — at least not anymore. The dynasty *used to be* dragonborn generations ago, but the bloodlines have run thin over the centuries. He's the sixth Dragoncaller, so there's a long history, but the dragonborn ancestry has mostly faded.

That said, the sect is definitely tied to the Dragoncaller dynasty — the notes say the dynasty tacitly encourages it, maybe even openly supports it. So the sect and the rulers are allied or sympathetic.

But whether the old Dragon Emperors were actual dragons, dragonborn, something else entirely, or how they relate to the current dynasty — that's all blank space right now.
wait hold on. are you talking about the same Shanlong from like 200 years ago? i thought the Dragoncallers were around back then too?

Good catch! The Dragoncaller *dynasty* does go back 200 years, but it's different individual rulers. This is where era matters.

**Qaringil** (the prequel, ~200 years before present): Ruled by **Dragoncaller Shanlong I**, who was half-dragonborn.

**Qamis present-day** (the main campaign): Ruled by **Dragoncaller Shanlong VI** — six generations later, and the dragonborn blood has diluted way down by now.

Same dynasty, same focus on sorcerer-kings and Dragon reverence, but very different individual rulers. Which campaign are you in? That'll help me say more about the current situation with the sect.
heyyy, quick question -- I'm building a campaign on a different world and want to borrow some ideas. How bright is Primus in the sky? Like, can you actually see it?

Oh yeah, definitely visible -- it's just not as dramatic as you might expect. Primus has an apparent diameter of about 10.4 arcminutes, which is roughly one-third the size of Earth's full moon. As for brightness, it maxes out at magnitude −9.62, which is about 3.1 magnitudes dimmer than a full moon. So it's bright enough to be unmissable in the night sky, but it's definitely a minor fixture, not a dominant one. Its dark iron surface (albedo 0.06) keeps it from being as brilliant as it might otherwise be.
okay so small and dim. what does that actually *look* like? is it like... would you notice it glancing up? or is it more of a 'point it out to someone' kind of thing?

You'd definitely notice it if you were looking at the sky, especially at night. At 10.4 arcminutes it's clearly a small disc, not a point -- you can see its shape. But because it's dimmer than Luna, it won't jump out at you the way a full moon does. The notes actually describe it as looking roughly the size of 'a large asteroid seen up close,' which captures the feel: distinct, visible, but not overwhelming. The real atmospheric hook is that it *never moves*. From the near-side hemisphere, Primus hangs in exactly the same spot in the sky every night -- it's geostationary, so it neither rises nor sets. That strangeness would be the thing people notice more than the brightness.

ohhh that's cool, it's a landmark. so if you're building a character from that side of the world, you'd grow up with this thing just *there* in the same place in the sky always. what'd that feel like for navigation or just... living there?

Exactly! It's so deeply baked into daily life that it becomes a navigation tool. Because it hangs over the equator at a fixed point, seafarers use its elevation above the horizon to find their longitude without needing a chronometer -- pair that with the pole star for latitude and you've got a full position fix just from angles. That alone would shape Qaian culture pretty heavily: this small dim moon is more useful than a compass for open-water navigation.

For a character's home life, imagine growing up in that hemisphere knowing there's always a fixed reference point in the sky. It's not bright enough to read by, not like Earth's full moon, but it's *there* every clear night. Constant, reliable, part of the geography as much as any landmark. Sailors and merchants would build their whole lives around it. The far side of the world never sees it at all, which creates a real divide in practical knowledge and capability.
nice, that's the kind of local color I needed. so just to pin down atmosphere: dim enough that you wouldn't read by it, but bright enough to definitely see? like, not a 'faint speck' situation?

Right, not a speck. It's roughly 1/18th the illumination of Earth's full moon -- so if a full moon gives you maybe 0.1-0.3 lux for working by, Primus gives you a dimmer twilight glow. You can see it clearly, see the disc shape, know exactly where it is. But you're not reading a map by it or working metalwork under its light. It's more like the way you'd notice Venus in a daylight sky -- undeniably there, obvious if you know where to look, but not intrusive.

hey so im brand new to d&d and joining the qamis campaign. the dm said i gotta pick starting languages but im confused. in forgotten realms everyone just speaks common right? whats the deal with qamis

good question — qamis is actually pretty different there. theres no racial languages, no elvish or dwarvish stuff. instead youve got national languages: edran, artklan, qis, forthrian, and islas. scholars and diplomats also use old draconic for treaties and academic stuff.

when you pick your starting languages, you get the capital dialect — the prestige version spoken in the ruling class and the capital city — plus one regional dialect. so you might speak capital artklan and also mountain artklan from rathakos, for instance.
okay but isnt that basically like common plus one accent? feels simpler than forgotten realms honestly

heres the thing though — the regional variations are *really* big. these arent standardized languages. most speakers from the capital of artkos would actually have serious trouble understanding artklan from rathakos. same language nominally, but the dialect differences are substantial enough that theyre not following each other very well.
wait what, so theyre literally not understanding each other? isnt that kind of a problem for like, governing a nation? why would artkos let that happen

thats the rub — the setting assumes that qamis nations *arent* super culturally unified the way you might expect. after the 30-year ryraxian war, borders got redrawn, populations moved around, regions that were under different rulers for decades are getting absorbed back. those language divisions are part of what makes that messy. its a fractious, post-war political landscape.
okay so if my character is from jia or wherever the capital is, and i go to like a mountain village, the locals might not understand my fancy capital accent?

yep, exactly. youll probably communicate the gist, but youre gonna sound foreign. thats why the dm has you pick a regional dialect too — if you know mountain artklan, youd fit in way better in rathakos. its a nice detail: where youre from actually matters when you travel.

yo, so I'm building a rogue for this campaign and I want to figure out the guild mechanics. Are the smaller guilds like Alchemist or Mage worth the feat slots or multiclassing investment, or is it more of a roleplay tax?

That's a smart optimization question. The notes mention smaller guilds - Adventurer, Alchemist, Mage - along with some underground criminal organizations styled as guilds (Thieves, Bandits, Cutpurses), but those details are specific to Qaringil, the prequel campaign set about 200 years before present-day Qamis. The notes say fifth-level party members can be full guild members of anything relevant to their class.

Which setting are you playing in - Qaringil or present-day Qamis (starting in Rathakos)? That changes what guild options are on the table mechanically.
Rathakos. So like, the Judicial Guild there - can I leverage that for social checks, or is that more of a Qis'mos thing? I remember the Manos family running it, seems like a good faction hook.

Ah, I need to gently correct - the Judicial Guild and the Manos family (one of the five families) are both Qaringil-specific, the island city-state in the prequel. Rathakos, in present-day Qamis, is a different place entirely - a mountainous border region with a tradition of adventuring.

The notes actually mark Rathakos's detailed faction and guild setup as a 'blank space' - so the specific guild structure, whether a Judicial Guild exists there, and what mechanical benefits faction membership would give are all things to ask your DM about. Rathakos is designed to be a place the DM can fill in, potentially with your character's backstory.
oh right, yeah that makes sense. so those guild mechanics are really an island thing then?

Exactly. Qaringil is an island city-state with a detailed republic structure, seven major guilds with specific families leading them, and all that faction interplay. That's where the guild mechanics are spelled out.

Rathakos and the rest of present-day Qamis are less detailed in the setting notes so far - plenty of room for your DM to build out. Good news for character building though: you can work with your DM to tie your guild affiliation to whatever factions they're creating for Rathakos.
ok i missed last session, whats the deal with Ryraxos? and why does this setting have to be so convoluted lol

Fair question. Ryraxos is a bronze dragon who basically caused the entire mess. He's young for a dragon, maybe sixty, and started as an artillery commander -- like, literally directing cannons, and he was good at it because dragons can carry the guns. When Edrad was losing to the Artklan Empire, Ryraxos showed up and turned the war around. But then instead of being chill about it, he went full conqueror and waged aggressive war across the continent for thirty years.

The war is finally over now. He lost, accepted exile to the second moon (Secundus), and isn't coming back. So for the actual campaign, he's not a factor.

I get the complexity complaint though. The setting is doing a lot.
wait, he's not THAT impressive? and he lost? feels weird that this one dragon would dominate for three decades then just... get exiled

Yeah, the notes are pretty explicit about this: Ryraxos isn't actually that impressive by D&D standards. He's less formidable than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery. The thirty-year war wasn't just him -- he was part of a bigger conflict where Artklas and Qis'mos allied to march on Edrad.

As for how he got exiled: the notes don't go into the details of his actual defeat, just that it happened and he accepted exile as the consequence. That's one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet, honestly.
...and now hes on the moon. with a bunch of other stuff the DM half-wrote. cool cool

Yeah, pretty much. The moon system is fully worked out -- obsessively so, there are like orbital mechanics. But a lot of the actual political consequences and blank map spaces? That's for the campaign to fill in as you go, or for your backstories if you want. It's a weird balance but that's the vibe.

so i keep hearing merchants in qaringil talk about qis'mos like it's some big deal. but honestly? it sounds made up or something. is qis'mos even a real unified place or just a bunch of squabbling city-states?

Qis'mos is real and unified under a Potentate -- kind of. It's the heartland of Qamis, actually the richest and most productive land on the continent. That's part of why merchants are talking about it -- it's hugely important for trade. The reason it might *feel* scattered to you is that it's complex governance-wise: the Potentate is elected by and answerable to the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters. But it's not a loose confederation. It's a single administered region.
okay but come on. it was totally destroyed during the war, right? how can it be the "heartland" if it got ravaged for thirty years?

You're right that it was devastated -- it's one of the most fought-over regions from the whole Ryraxian War. But being "richest and most productive" means it *can* recover, and more importantly, it's valuable *because* of that capacity. The fertility, the infrastructure, the institutions that survived there. That's why both sides wanted it so badly.
fair. so what's the actual situation there *now* then? like, is it stable enough for a trader to work with?

That's where it gets tricky. There's a major complication nobody might tell you in Qaringil: Qama, the holy city of Bahamut, was occupied by the drow during the late war. They didn't destroy it, but they put their own high priest in place -- a drow named Qe'lth IV. The Church of Bahamut is *huge* there, so this created a schism. There's now an alternative high priest, Qachu II, operating from the city of Qis, but without any of the actual relics of office. So there's a religious and political split that's still live. It's not chaotic, but it's complicated.
wait, the drow appointed a priest? and nobody's fixed that yet? that seems like a pretty big deal.

It *is* a big deal, and yeah, it hasn't been "fixed" in the sense of the drow being removed. The drow have actually settled in enclaves across Qamis as allies during the war, and they haven't left or been expelled. Qama just happens to have the most prominent one. The schism is real and ongoing -- it's a fault line in Qis'mos politics and religion right now. So if you're dealing with Qis'mos merchants or institutions, you'd want to know which side of that split they're on.
alright, that's actually really useful. so it's not "qis'mos is a disaster" but more "qis'mos is stable but has some serious tensions under the surface."

Exactly. Rich, important, governed, but with real complications -- especially around the Church schism and the drow presence. That's why merchants care about it and why you should too if you're in the trade world.
hey, who's this Dragoncaller Shanlong character? Keep seeing the name in Forthyr lore and I'm curious.

Dragoncaller Shanlong is the current ruler of Forthyr, a huge nation in the northeast. He's the latest in a line of sorcerer-kings, all of whom carry the title "Dragoncaller." The dynasty has deep ties to draconic heritage—which is interesting given the current state of things. Want to know more about the current one specifically, or the history?
Wait, current one? So there's been more than one Dragoncaller Shanlong? How do you even track that?

Yeah, the current one is Dragoncaller Shanlong VI—the sixth in the line. In the Qamis present-day campaign, that's who's ruling. But if you go back to the Qaringil prequel (about 200 years earlier), you get Dragoncaller Shanlong I, who was explicitly half-dragonborn. The full genealogy beyond that isn't detailed yet, but the line clearly goes further back. The numbering system keeps them straight.

Got it. But I'm stuck on something linguistic—is "Dragoncaller" a title or a name? And I've noticed the Q in place names like Qaringil and Qama—does that connect to Old Draconic somehow? Like, is "caller" related to draconic language roots?

Good instinct! "Dragoncaller" is a title, not a family name—Shanlong is the actual name. And yes, you're onto the Q thing. The "Q" in place names like Qaringil, Qis, and Qama comes from Old Draconic—it's pronounced like the German "ch" in "Bach," that harsh throat sound (Kh). Old Draconic is the scholarly language for treaties and academies. As for whether "Dragoncaller" itself derives from Old Draconic, the notes don't say, but given the kingdom's draconic obsession, it wouldn't surprise me. The title really does emphasize the sorcerer-kings' connection to dragons.
So Shanlong I was half-dragonborn 200 years ago. What happened? Why does the present Shanlong VI have "bloodlines ran thin" instead of actual draconic heritage?

That's where the notes get sparse. In Qaringil, Shanlong I is half-dragonborn with actual draconic ancestry. By present-day Qamis, the text says the dynasty "used to be dragonborn, before bloodlines ran thin"—so somewhere over those two centuries, the draconic heritage faded out. Could be natural dilution, deliberate marriage strategy, something else entirely. The DM hasn't filled that in yet. It's a blank space, and honestly a pretty loaded one given what happens next.
What do you mean 'given what happens next'? Does the dynasty lean into the draconic thing despite losing the blood, or distance themselves from it?

They lean *hard* into it, which is the weird part. In Forthyr there's a prominent religious sect that literally calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors—the old draconic rulers. And the notes say this sect receives "tacit encouragement, if not support, from the Dragoncaller dynasty." So Shanlong VI is actively supporting a movement for dragon rule even though he himself isn't dragonborn anymore. That contradiction is fascinating: fading draconic blood, but ideology of draconic supremacy.
That's got to be unstable. Is he just hoping the sect never actually restores a Dragon Emperor, or does he have some endgame where he hands power to a dragon? How does that even work out?

You've nailed the tension. The notes don't say what Shanlong VI's actual endgame is—whether he genuinely believes in Dragon Emperor restoration, is using the sect for legitimacy, or is betting it'll never materialize. Forthyr is described as "somewhat backwards," which might suggest the whole thing is less politically calculated than Edrad or Artkos, but that's reading between the lines. The core contradiction is planted and waiting: a sorcerer-king with thin draconic blood championing draconic supremacy. Could detonate, could simmer for centuries. The DM clearly knows it's loaded.
hey, how do qaian navigators actually find their longitude? seems complicated

Actually, it's pretty elegant. They use Primus, one of Qaia's moons -- it's in a geostationary orbit, which means it's fixed at the same spot in the sky for the entire near-side hemisphere. It never rises or sets. To find your longitude, you measure Primus's elevation angle, look up a value called primsine in a table, and calculate your exact east-west position from the prime meridian. No chronometer needed. Paired with a latitude fix from the pole star, you've got your full position.
oh interesting! so did Giovanni Pesaro write about this in Qaringil? I'm curious about the original documentation

Good instinct to look for primary sources -- but here's the thing: Giovanni Pesaro is the Exarch of Qaringil in the *present* Qamis era, not the prequel. He's been in office for 19 years. The Qaringil campaign is set about 200 years earlier, so it would have had a different Exarch leading the city. The primsine method itself would've existed in both eras though, since Primus has always been geostationary.

oh duh, that makes sense. so the name 'primsine' -- is that from Old Draconic? I'm kind of obsessed with the Q/Kh shift in the language, and I'm wondering if the navigation terminology has that Old Draconic flavor

The Q sound is definitely Old Draconic -- that's where place names like Qaia, Qaringil, and Qis come from (all pronounced with that 'Kh' sound from Old Draconic). Old Draconic is also the language of scholarly publications and treaties, so it'd make sense for navigation terminology to have roots there. That said, the notes don't actually specify an Old Draconic name for the primsine function itself -- it's just called 'primsine' in the current documentation. That could be a cool detail to ask the DM about though.
So I just heard that in the prequel campaign, Qis'mos isn't even a unified nation? But everyone talks about it like it's the big important region. What's going on there?

Right, so Qis'mos is a geographic and cultural region in the prequel era, but it's not politically unified. Part of it—north of the river—is ruled by the Holy Qis Empire from the city of Ath'r. The southern parts have independent regions, each governed by a Potentate—a ruler elected by the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters who can be voted out if they lose support.

OK but that's just weird to me? I mean, in Forgotten Realms—like, I read the books—you have all these city-states and nobles, but there's still some kind of hierarchy. Waterdeep's connected to the larger realm, you know? Why would Qis'mos stay so split up? Wouldn't that make them weaker or easier to attack?

Actually, it's rooted in history. The Draconic empire left behind institutions when the dragons departed. Instead of those institutions falling apart or getting controlled by one new ruler, the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters all learned to share power. So you end up with independent Potentates in some regions and the Holy Qis Empire controlling others. The Church of Bahamut is huge in that region too—their holy city is Qama.

OK, so it's like aftermath of empire collapse where the institutions survived? That makes sense. But I still don't get why they wouldn't just consolidate eventually. Like, in every fantasy book I've read, if you've got similar institutions and the same religion, eventually someone unifies it all. Why wouldn't that happen here?

Actually, the structure itself explains some of it. The institutions distributed power among the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters—they all have a say in electing the Potentates and voting them out. That shared-power system is pretty different from a kingdom where one ruler consolidates everything. Whether the regions actively prefer staying independent or whether unification's just never happened, the notes don't say. But the system of checks and balances seems intentional.
hey, so my buddy's starting a new campaign in this world and keeps talking about Rathakos. I've never actually played D&D before, just read a lot of fantasy. What's Rathakos about?
