Rathakos is a mountainous region in the heart of Qamis—it sits in the foothills of the Red Mountains and borders a bunch of the major nations: Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, and others. The capital is the city of Rath. Your buddy probably mentioned it because the DM expects the campaign to start there.
Wait, so it borders all these major nations? Doesn't that make it kind of a war zone?

Could be, but not quite. Rathakos is actually trying to hang on to independence, which means it's caught in a pretty precarious position. The Red Mountains that run through it are some of the most dangerous and unexplored regions on the continent, which has built up a strong tradition of adventuring. There's definitely opportunities for work there.
Adventuring opportunities—so it's not stable? And who actually runs the place if everyone's trying to pull it in different directions?

Rathakos was recently made into a Princedom, but they haven't actually found a Prince yet. Right now it's governed by a Regent named John Dunne while a council figures out who should rule. There's also a lot of internal unrest—some areas were annexed from Edrad during the war that just ended, and mountain communities have their own traditions, some following old dragon cults. Not everyone's happy with the new government.
Dragon cults? What do you mean, like people worshipping dragons? And there was a war?

Yeah, a big one. It just ended—lasted thirty years and was driven by a dragon named Ryraxos. Rathakos got caught up in it and fought on both sides at different times, which left a lot of instability. The dragon cults are a legacy of that era; some mountain communities still follow them, and they're not thrilled with the new government trying to consolidate power. That's a lot of why there's unrest right now.

hey there!! so I'm building an Edran noble character and trying to understand the guild structure in Qaringil. what's the actual difference between the Silk guild, the Wool guild, and the Mercantile guild? in Forgotten Realms the merchant guilds kind of do everything...

Good question. In Qaringil they're more specialized than that. Silk handles both silk production AND trade in silk goods. Wool does the same for wool. The Mercantile Guild regulates trade in *other* commodities -- everything else. So if you're trading iron or grain or spices, Mercantile is your guild. But silk and wool carved out their own dedicated guilds because they're major enough industries in the city that they get vertical integration.

One thing worth noting: both Silk and Wool are led by major families (Vitelli leads Silk), but Wool is unusual in that it's one of the two major guilds that has *never* been led by one of the Five Families -- it's held by someone else. That gives it a different political dynamic.
okay got it, so they're product-specific guilds rather than catch-alls. is that unique to Qaringil, or is that how guilds work across Qamis in general? I want to make sure my backstory tracks.

That's a good instinct to check. Fair warning: the notes are pretty detailed about Qaringil (the island city-state in the central sea) and its guild structure, but they're pretty quiet about how guilds work in Edrad proper or the rest of Qamis. Qaringil is its own thing -- a commercial republic with a very specific power structure. Are you setting your noble character *in* Qaringil, or in Edrad itself? That'll change the answer.

ohhh right, Qaringil is an island, isn't it? no, I'm trying to build an Edran noble -- my character's family would be from Jia, the capital. so if guilds work differently there, that might matter for my backstory. what do we know about Edran guild structures?

The notes don't really cover Edran guilds in detail. What we know about Edrad itself is that it's ruled by half-elven nobility from Jia on the coast, the Keyberos dynasty holds the throne, and it's the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis. But the specific guild structure there -- if they even have guilds in the same way -- that's a blank space. Solid hooks for backstory though. You could define how your noble family's wealth works, whether they're merchant princes, landowners, or something else entirely.

gotcha. so Qaringil has this really specific merchant republic vibe with the Five Families and all that, but Edrad is just... a feudal nobility? or could it also have guild infrastructure I'm building myself?

Could be either, or something in between. Edrad's described as ruled by half-elven nobility, but that doesn't tell you whether the cities have guild systems, whether merchant families have guild seats, or what the economic structure looks like below the royal level. Jia is the most culturally influential city in all of Qamis, so it could plausibly have a sophisticated commercial structure. But the DM hasn't filled that in yet. If your character's backstory hinges on it, you might want to check in with them about what makes sense for your family's position.

Hey, so the rules say everyone gets one bonus language. I'm from Rathakos and I'm thinking of speaking the local dialect plus Old Qis from studying in Qama. Do those count as my start + bonus, or do I need to pick a third?

Good question. So your starting languages are: the prestige dialect (capital language of wherever you're from) plus one regional dialect. That's your baseline. The bonus language is the third one. If you're from Rathakos, you'd speak prestige Edran (the capital's dialect) plus a Rathakos regional variant as your starting pair. Then Old Qis is your bonus. That's three total. Though quick check -- are you in the Qamis present-day campaign? Rathakos is a fresh nation after the Ryraxian War, so if you're in Qaringil (the prequel ~200 years back), Rathakos wouldn't exist yet and we'd need to adjust your origin.

Oh, I didn't realize the campaigns were different. Yeah, I'm in the present-day one. So in that case, would my character speak the prestige Edran, the Rathakos dialect, then Old Qis? Do I have flexibility on whether that prestige dialect is Edran or Artklan since Rathakos borders both?

Exactly -- Edran prestige makes sense given the Southern Reaches annexed from Edrad, but honestly, Artklan prestige would work too depending on your character's background. The rule just says you start with a capital dialect and a regional one, and the DM approves the regional choice based on 'why.' For Rathakos, you could justify either Edran (from the south) or Artklan (from Artkos to the west). Just pitch it as part of your backstory: 'I grew up here, so I picked up the local Rathakos flavor,' or 'My family came from Edrad/Artkos, so I speak that prestige dialect.' Then Old Qis as the bonus. The DM has final say, but those are both solid narratives.
quick question -- what color dragon is Ryraxos? been hearing about him but never saw anyone describe what he actually looks like

Ryraxos is bronze. And here's the thing -- he's actually not the ancient, impossibly powerful dragon you might imagine from the war stories. He's only about sixty years old, which is relatively young for a dragon. The notes describe him as formidable, sure, but less impressive than a well-commanded artillery battery or even a competent adventuring party.
huh, that doesn't add up. if he's not that amazing on his own, how'd one dragon end up running the whole continent for thirty years?

That's the key detail -- Ryraxos made his name as an artillery commander, not as pure raw dragon power. Dragons are excellent artillery officers because they can physically help carry the guns, and his rapid rise to power started there. He combined military genius with draconic abilities, and that's what made him so dangerous. In the chaos of the Ryraxian War, that was enough to seize control.
so he was basically a brilliant tactician who happened to be a dragon. makes sense he'd be hard to stop. but he did get stopped eventually, yeah? what happened when he finally lost?

After his defeat, Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus -- that's Qaia's second moon. He's been off the continent ever since, so he has no role in the present campaign. The Keyberos dynasty was restored to Edrad's throne and everything returned to pre-war borders. As for the actual military defeat itself, that's one of the blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet -- we just know he lost, accepted exile, and that was that.
alright so I'm building a campaign in Artkos and I need some lore on High King Ezevir Arth. who is this guy?

Good timing to ask -- there's actually two. In the Qaringil prequel (~200 years ago), King Ezevir Arth II doubled Artkos's territory. In the present-day Qamis campaign, we have High King Ezevir Arth, the current ruler, trying to restore Artkos to its old dominance over the Artklan League.

Which era are you in?
present day. and i'm building a PC from West Artkos, old family type. what's the vibe there?

West Artkos is the historic heartland -- the original power base. An old family there would've grown up with stories of when Artkos actually ruled a huge chunk of the continent. Now High King Ezevir Arth is trying to restore that dominance, but the Artklan League is a shadow of the old empire. The war devastated everything.

So your character's family probably watched the empire fragment and is now watching it try to piece itself back together. That's good tension to build from.
okay, cool. what would they know about what's actually happening now? like, what's unstable?

An old West Artkosan family would know Artkos is holding a fractious League together. Places like Kard -- the southwestern enclave, a huge financial center -- are a nightmare. The population there is mostly Edran-speaking and actually welcomed the Edran invasion. So Ezevir Arth's governance there is fragile. Same with nominally-loyal states like Rathakos -- they're hanging on to independence by a thread.

Your character grew up watching the high king scramble to keep power, not actually ruling the way his predecessors did. That's the reality on the ground.
and what language would they speak? like, full prestige Ath'r style, or...

You'd speak Artklan -- the capital prestige dialect from Ath'r, plus a regional West Artkosan dialect. But here's the thing: West Artkos might resent Ath'r. That city was conquered and made capital 200 years ago, but the historic Artkos identity is in the northwest. So an old family might deliberately keep more of the regional flavor, as a statement that they're Artkosan-proper, not imperial-capital types.

That could be a nice character quirk -- they code-switch based on how much they respect who they're talking to.
ohhh i like that. so using the regional dialect is like refusing to fully assimilate?

Exactly. Speaking the regional Artkosan flavor rather than the prestige capital dialect is a quiet way of saying "this is real Artkos, not the newer imperial center." For an old noble family, that could be genuine pride in heritage -- or bitterness about the way power shifted. Either way, it's good character texture.
